topics.title: Excitable Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Excitable Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
An **Excitable Cell** is a specialized biological cell that is capable of generating and propagating electrical signals known as **action potentials** (or impulses) in response to a stimulus that exceeds a critical threshold value. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, excitable cells are the fundamental biological substrates that transduce, encode, and transmit information in the nervous, muscular, and endocrine systems. The three classical classes of excitable cells studied in biomedical signal processing are:

1. **Neurons (Nerve Cells)** – Primary signaling cells of the central and peripheral nervous system.
2. **Skeletal & Smooth Muscle Cells (Myocytes)** – Contractile cells that convert electrical excitation into mechanical force.
3. **Cardiac Muscle Cells (Cardiomyocytes)** – Specialized rhythmic cells of the heart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Excitable cells are characterized by a polarized **resting membrane potential (RMP)**, a finite **threshold potential**, a stereotyped **all-or-none action potential**, and distinct **refractory periods** that govern firing frequency.

### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Think of an excitable cell as a **leaky water balloon filled with two different concentrations of salt water**:
- **Inside the balloon**: A potassium (K⁺) rich "soup" (like salty seawater).
- **Outside the balloon**: A sodium (Na⁺) rich "soup" (like brackish water).
- **The balloon wall**: A *lipid bilayer* that is **impermeable** to ions, but pierced with tiny **gated channels** (think of them as little doors).

When the cell is at rest, these doors are mostly closed, and a small electrical "pressure" builds up across the wall (around **−70 mV**). When a stimulus comes along (a touch, a thought, a heartbeat signal), the **Na⁺ doors burst open**, sodium rushes *into* the cell, the inside becomes *less negative* (depolarization), and an **electrical wave (action potential)** sweeps down the cell membrane. Then the **K⁺ doors open** to let potassium rush *out*, restoring the original state. This electrical "wave" is exactly what your ECG, EEG, and EMG machines measure!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** The standard physiological **resting membrane potential** is approximately **−70 mV** for a typical neuron and **−90 mV** for a ventricular cardiomyocyte, while the peak action potential reaches roughly **+30 mV to +40 mV**.

---

### 1.3 Structural Anatomy of an Excitable Cell (Neuron)
A typical neuron consists of four functional regions:

| Region | Function | Signal Processing Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dendrites** | Receive incoming signals | Input summation zone |
| **Soma (Cell Body)** | Houses nucleus & integrates signals | Decision-making unit |
| **Axon Hillock** | Site of action potential initiation | Threshold detector |
| **Axon** | Propagates action potential | Signal transmission line |
| **Synaptic Terminals** | Release neurotransmitters | Output transducer |

---

### 1.4 The Lipid Bilayer & Ion Channels
The **plasma membrane** is a **5 nm thick** phospholipid bilayer that acts as an insulator (capacitor ≈ **1 µF/cm²**). Embedded within it are:
- **Leak Channels** – Always open, provide baseline conductance.
- **Voltage-Gated Channels** – Open/close in response to membrane voltage (e.g., Naᵥ, Kᵥ, Caᵥ).
- **Ligand-Gated Channels** – Open in response to chemical messengers.
- **Mechanically-Gated Channels** – Open in response to physical deformation.
- **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase Pump** – An *active* transporter that consumes **ATP** to maintain ionic gradients (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per cycle).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Resting Membrane Potential (RMP) — The "Baseline Bias"
The RMP exists because of an **unequal distribution of ions** maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump and the selective permeability of the membrane to K⁺ (which is ~25× more permeable at rest than Na⁺).

**Typical intracellular vs. extracellular ion concentrations:**

| Ion | Intracellular (mM) | Extracellular (mM) | Nernst Potential E_ion (mV) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| K⁺ | 140 | 4 | **−94** |
| Na⁺ | 12 | 145 | **+60** |
| Ca²⁺ | 0.0001 | 1.2 | **+130** |
| Cl⁻ | 4 | 120 | **−65** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Although the Na⁺/K⁺ pump transports **3 Na⁺ out** for every **2 K⁺ in**, it is *electrogenic* (net exports one positive charge per cycle), contributing roughly **−4 mV** to the RMP. The remaining ~**−66 mV** comes from passive K⁺ diffusion.

---

### 2.2 The Nernst Equation (Equilibrium Potential of a Single Ion)
The Nernst equation calculates the membrane potential at which there is **no net flux** of a particular ion.

The electrochemical potential equality condition:
$$\mu_{in} = \mu_{out}$$

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]_{in}} + zFV_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]_{out}} + zFV_{out}$$

Solving for the membrane potential $V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln{\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right)}$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm at body temperature **T = 310 K (37 °C)**:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{61.54}{z} \log_{10}{\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right)} \text{ mV}$$

Where:
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ — Universal gas constant
- $T = 310 \text{ K}$ — Absolute temperature
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ — Faraday's constant
- $z$ — Valence of the ion

---

### 2.3 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation (Multi-Ion Membrane Potential)
Because real membranes are permeable to **multiple ions** simultaneously, the actual RMP is a *weighted average* of the Nernst potentials of all permeant ions.

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln{\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)}$$

At 37 °C:

$$V_m = 61.54 \cdot \log_{10}{\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)} \text{ mV}$$

Where $P_K, P_{Na}, P_{Cl}$ are the **permeability coefficients** of the respective ions. At rest, $P_K \gg P_{Na} \gg P_{Cl}$, so $V_m \approx E_K$.

---

### 2.4 The Five Phases of the Action Potential

The action potential is a **stereotyped, all-or-none electrical event** that can be decomposed into 5 canonical phases:

1. **Resting State (Phase 4)** — Membrane is at RMP (~−70 mV for neuron). Voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels are **closed**.
2. **Depolarization (Phase 0)** — Stimulus reaches the **threshold (~−55 mV)**. Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open rapidly, Na⁺ flows *into* the cell, V_m shoots toward E_Na.
3. **Repolarization (Phase 1 & 3)** — Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (fast); voltage-gated K⁺ channels **open** (slow), K⁺ flows *out*, V_m returns toward RMP.
4. **Hyperpolarization / Undershoot (Phase 2 — After-potential)** — K⁺ channels close slowly, causing a brief dip below RMP (toward E_K).
5. **Return to Resting** — Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump restores ionic gradients; the membrane returns to −70 mV.

---

### 2.5 Refractory Periods (Frequency-Limiting Mechanism)

| Period | Mechanism | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Absolute Refractory Period (ARP)** | Na⁺ channels are inactivated; **no stimulus**, no matter how strong, can elicit another AP. | Sets the **upper firing limit** of the neuron. |
| **Relative Refractory Period (RRP)** | Some Na⁺ channels have recovered, K⁺ channels are still open; only a **suprathreshold** stimulus can trigger AP. | Encodes **stimulus intensity** via firing rate. |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Utility |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right)$ | Calculates single-ion equilibrium potential |
| 2 | Nernst (37 °C, base-10) | $E_{ion} = \frac{61.54}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}$ | Quick numerical estimation |
| 3 | GHK Voltage Equation | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out}+P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out}+P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in}+P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in}+P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | Realistic multi-ion resting potential |
| 4 | Membrane Time Constant | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | Determines how fast V_m changes |
| 5 | Cable Equation (Length Constant) | $\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Determines AP propagation distance |
| 6 | AP Conduction Velocity | $v \propto \sqrt{diameter}$ | Myelination increases v (saltatory conduction) |
| 7 | Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry | 3 Na⁺ out / 2 K⁺ in (1 ATP) | Electrogenic — contributes to RMP |
| 8 | Membrane Capacitance | $C_m \approx 1 \text{ µF/cm}^2$ | Universal constant for bilipid layers |
| 9 | F (Faraday's Constant) | $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ | Conversion factor charge ↔ moles |
| 10 | Threshold Potential (Neuron) | $V_{th} \approx -55 \text{ mV}$ | Critical firing boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always remember the numerical constant **61.54 mV** for physiological temperature (37 °C). At room temperature (25 °C), use **59.2 mV** instead. Examiners *love* testing this distinction.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
The mathematical framework of excitable cells is the **cornerstone of all bioelectrical signal processing**:
- **ECG (Electrocardiography)** — Measures summed action potentials of cardiac excitable cells.
- **EEG (Electroencephalography)** — Records synchronized post-synaptic potentials of cortical neurons.
- **EMG (Electromyography)** — Captures compound action potentials of skeletal muscle fibers.
- **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — Directly stimulate excitable cells to restore function.
- **Pharmacology / Drug Design** — Most neuroactive drugs target voltage-gated Na⁺, K⁺, or Ca²⁺ channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Nernst Equation

**Step 1 — Define the Electrochemical Potential.**
The total electrochemical potential of an ion on either side of the membrane has two components: a **chemical potential** (concentration gradient) and an **electrical potential** (voltage gradient).

$$\tilde{\mu}_{ion} = \mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]} + zF\psi$$

Where:
- $\mu^0$ = standard chemical potential (reference)
- $RT \ln{[Ion]}$ = chemical potential term (concentration dependent)
- $zF\psi$ = electrical potential term (charge × voltage)

**Step 2 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition.**
At electrochemical equilibrium, the net flux of the ion across the membrane is **zero**. This means the electrochemical potential on the *inside* must equal the electrochemical potential on the *outside*:

$$\tilde{\mu}_{in} = \tilde{\mu}_{out}$$

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]_{in}} + zFV_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]_{out}} + zFV_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the Standard Potential.**
The reference chemical potential $\mu^0$ is identical on both sides and therefore cancels:

$$RT \ln{[Ion]_{in}} + zFV_{in} = RT \ln{[Ion]_{out}} + zFV_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Group Terms and Rearrange.**
Move all chemical terms to the left and all electrical terms to the right:

$$RT \ln{[Ion]_{in}} - RT \ln{[Ion]_{out}} = zFV_{out} - zFV_{in}$$

$$RT \ln{\left(\frac{[Ion]_{in}}{[Ion]_{out}}\right)} = zF(V_{out} - V_{in})$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Membrane Potential.**
By the **biophysical convention**, the membrane potential $V_m$ is defined as $V_{in} - V_{out}$. Multiply both sides by $-1$ and divide by $zF$:

$$V_m = V_{in} - V_{out} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln{\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right)}$$

This is the **Nernst Equation**. ✔

**Step 6 — Convert to Base-10 and Physiological Temperature.**
Substitute $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$, $T = 310 \text{ K}$, and apply $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V} = 26.71 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_m = 26.71 \times 2.303 \times \frac{1}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{V_m = \frac{61.54}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Equilibrium Potential of K⁺

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{in} = 140 \text{ mM}$
- $[K^+]_{out} = 4 \text{ mM}$
- $T = 37 \,°\text{C} = 310 \text{ K}$

**Find:** $E_K$

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

$$E_K = \frac{61.54}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) \text{ mV}$$

$$E_K = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(0.02857) \text{ mV}$$

$$E_K = 61.54 \times (-1.5441) \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -95.03 \text{ mV}}$$

**Interpretation:** At −95 mV, the electrical pull pulling K⁺ *into* the cell exactly balances the chemical push driving K⁺ *out*. This is why $E_K$ is the dominant contributor to the resting membrane potential.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Equilibrium Potential of Na⁺

**Given:**
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145 \text{ mM}$
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 12 \text{ mM}$

**Solution:**

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right) \text{ mV}$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(12.083) \text{ mV}$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times (1.0821) \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +66.6 \text{ mV}}$$

This is the value that the membrane potential *transiently approaches* during the peak of the action potential when Na⁺ channels are wide open.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Excitable Cell Types

| Parameter | Skeletal Muscle | Cardiac Muscle (Ventricular) | Neuron (Typical) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Resting Potential** | ≈ −90 mV | ≈ −90 mV | ≈ −70 mV |
| **Threshold** | ≈ −55 mV | ≈ −70 mV | ≈ −55 mV |
| **Peak AP** | ≈ +30 mV | ≈ +30 mV | ≈ +40 mV |
| **AP Duration** | 1–2 ms | 200–300 ms | 1–2 ms |
| **Plateau Phase** | Absent | **Present (Phase 2)** | Absent |
| **Refractory Period** | Short | **Long** (prevents tetanus) | Short |
| **Pacemaker Activity** | None | SA/AV node (Phase 4 depol.) | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Action Potential Phase Diagram
The following Mermaid block renders the canonical five-phase action potential waveform as a block-functional architecture flow (signal processing stages):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 4: Resting State - Vm at -70 mV Na+ and K+ channels CLOSED Na+/K+ pump ACTIVE] --> B[Stimulus Arrives - Local current depolarizes membrane]
    B --> C{Vm reaches THRESHOLD -55 mV?}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D[Phase 0: Depolarization - Voltage-gated Na+ channels OPEN Na+ rushes IN Vm rises to +30 mV]
    D --> E[Phase 1: Na+ channels INACTIVATE - Fast inactivation gate closes]
    E --> F[Phase 3: Repolarization - Voltage-gated K+ channels OPEN K+ rushes OUT Vm falls back toward -70 mV]
    F --> G[Phase 2: After-hyperpolarization - K+ channels slow to close Vm dips to approx -80 mV]
    G --> H[Na+/K+ ATPase restores ionic gradients]
    H --> I{Refractory Period Active?}
    I -- Yes ARP --> J[ABSOLUTE REFRACTORY: No new AP possible Na+ channels still inactivated]
    I -- Yes RRP --> K[RELATIVE REFRACTORY: Stronger stimulus needed K+ channels still open]
    I -- No --> A
    J --> L[Refractory ends, full excitability restored]
    K --> L
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model of the Excitable Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Extracellular Space] -->|I_C = Cm dVm/dt| B[Membrane Capacitor Cm approx 1 uF per cm2]
    B --> C[Intracellular Space]
    A -->|I_Na via gNa| D[Na+ Channel Variable Conductance gNa gated by Vm]
    D -->|Nernst Battery E_Na plus 60 mV| C
    A -->|I_K via gK| E[K+ Channel Variable Conductance gK gated by Vm]
    E -->|Nernst Battery E_K minus 94 mV| C
    A -->|I_L via gL| F[Leak Channel Fixed Conductance gL]
    F -->|Leak Battery E_L| C
    A -->|Active Transport| G[Na+/K+ ATPase Pump consumes 1 ATP per cycle]
    G --> C
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Signal Processing Pipeline of an Excitable Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT: Stimulus Mechanical Chemical Electrical] --> B[TRANSDUCTION: Receptor or Synapse]
    B --> C[INTEGRATION: Dendrites and Soma perform spatial and temporal summation]
    C --> D[THRESHOLD DETECTION: Axon Hillock compares Vm to minus 55 mV]
    D --> E[ACTION POTENTIAL GENERATION: All or none firing at axon initial segment]
    E --> F[PROPAGATION: Passive cable conduction then saltatory at Nodes of Ranvier]
    F --> G[OUTPUT: Synaptic release of neurotransmitter at axon terminal]
    G --> H[POSTSYNAPTIC RESPONSE: Next excitable cell or effector organ]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: All-or-None Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Membrane at Rest Vm = minus 70 mV] --> B[Apply Stimulus of Amplitude delta V]
    B --> C{Vm plus delta V greater than or equal to Vth = minus 55 mV?}
    C -- YES depolarization crosses threshold --> D[ACTION POTENTIAL FIRES: Full amplitude AP approx plus 30 mV propagates]
    C -- NO subthreshold --> E[Subthreshold Depolarization: Local graded potential decays with distance constant lambda]
    E --> F[Signal does NOT propagate - decremental conduction]
    D --> G[Refractory Period - cannot fire again immediately]
    F --> A
    G --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define an excitable cell. Name any two types of excitable cells found in the human body.**

**Model Answer:**
An excitable cell is a specialized biological cell capable of generating and propagating electrical signals called action potentials in response to a stimulus that exceeds a critical threshold. Two types of excitable cells in the human body are:
1. **Neurons** — Found in the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves; they transmit electrochemical signals.
2. **Cardiomyocytes (Cardiac muscle cells)** — Found in the heart; they generate rhythmic action potentials responsible for the heartbeat.

*Other acceptable examples: skeletal muscle fibers, smooth muscle cells, pancreatic β-cells, and certain endocrine cells.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the significance of the Nernst equation in bioelectric signal processing?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nernst equation is fundamental to bioelectric signal processing because it:
1. **Quantifies the equilibrium potential** (reversal potential) of a single ion species across a semipermeable membrane.
2. **Predicts the direction of ion flow** when the actual membrane potential deviates from this equilibrium.
3. **Provides the basis for the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation**, which models the realistic multi-ion resting membrane potential.
4. **Enables the design of microelectrode recordings, ion-selective electrodes, and biosensors** by relating measurable voltages to ion concentrations.

The equation $E_{ion} = (61.54/z) \log_{10}([Ion]_{out}/[Ion]_{in}) \text{ mV}$ is used clinically in ion-selective potentiometry to measure blood electrolyte concentrations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Origin of the Resting Membrane Potential and the Nernst Equation**

**(a) Derive the Nernst equation for the equilibrium potential of a univalent ion. State the numerical value of the constant used at 37 °C.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The electrochemical potential of an ion combines a chemical and an electrical component:
$$\tilde{\mu} = \mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]} + zF\psi$$

**Step 2:** At equilibrium, the net ionic flux is zero, so:
$$\mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]_{in}} + zFV_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln{[Ion]_{out}} + zFV_{out}$$

**Step 3:** Cancelling $\mu^0$ and rearranging:
$$V_m = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln{\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$, $T = 310 \text{ K}$, and using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$V_m = \frac{61.54}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[Ion]_{out}}{[Ion]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Numerical Constant at 37 °C: 61.54 mV** (for $z = \pm 1$).

**(b) A nerve cell has intracellular and extracellular K⁺ concentrations of 140 mM and 4 mM respectively. Calculate the Nernst potential for K⁺ at body temperature (37 °C).** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $[K^+]_{in} = 140 \text{ mM}$, $[K^+]_{out} = 4 \text{ mM}$, $T = 37\,°\text{C}$, $z = +1$.

**Formula:**
$$E_K = \frac{61.54}{1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Substitution:**
$$E_K = 61.54 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) \text{ mV}$$

$$E_K = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(0.02857) \text{ mV}$$

$$E_K = 61.54 \times (-1.5441) \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -95.03 \text{ mV}}$$

**Interpretation:** The Nernst potential for K⁺ is approximately **−95 mV**. Since the actual resting membrane potential of a neuron (≈ −70 mV) is more positive than $E_K$, K⁺ ions continue to diffuse *out* of the cell at rest, which is the dominant ionic current responsible for maintaining the negative resting potential.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Action Potential Phases and the All-or-None Principle**

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the various phases of a neuronal action potential. Explain the underlying ionic mechanisms.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Phase | Voltage Range | Ionic Mechanism | Channel State |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase 4: Resting** | ≈ −70 mV | High K⁺ permeability; Na⁺/K⁺ pump active | Na⁺ & K⁺ channels **closed** |
| **Phase 0: Depolarization** | −70 mV → +30 mV | Rapid Na⁺ influx down electrochemical gradient | Na⁺ channels **open** (fast activation) |
| **Phase 1: Initial Repol.** | +30 mV → +10 mV | Na⁺ channel inactivation; transient K⁺ outflow | Na⁺ channels **inactivated** |
| **Phase 2: Plateau** *(cardiac only)* | ~0 mV | Ca²⁺ influx balances K⁺ efflux | Ca²⁺ channels **open** |
| **Phase 3: Repolarization** | +10 mV → −70 mV | K⁺ efflux dominates | K⁺ channels **open** (delayed rectifier) |
| **After-hyperpolarization** | −70 mV → −80 mV → −70 mV | Slow closure of K⁺ channels | K⁺ channels closing |

The complete waveform rises from **−70 mV to +30 mV** in <1 ms, then repolarizes over 1–2 ms. The **all-or-none principle** states that once threshold (≈ −55 mV) is crossed, the full-amplitude action potential will always occur; subthreshold stimuli produce only decremental local (graded) potentials.

**(b) Explain the absolute and relative refractory periods. Why are these periods important for signal processing in neurons?** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Absolute Refractory Period (ARP):**
- Coincides with the rapid depolarization and most of repolarization.
- Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are in the **inactivated state** (the inactivation gate is closed).
- **No stimulus**, regardless of intensity, can elicit another action potential.
- **Functional role:** Prevents backward propagation of the AP and sets the *maximum firing frequency* ($f_{max} = 1/\text{ARP duration}$).

**Relative Refractory Period (RRP):**
- Coincides with the after-hyperpolarization phase.
- A fraction of Na⁺ channels have recovered, but K⁺ channels are still partially open (membrane is hyperpolarized).
- A **supra-threshold stimulus** (stronger than normal) can trigger a new action potential, but of *reduced amplitude*.
- **Functional role:** Encodes **stimulus intensity** — stronger stimuli produce *higher firing rates* during this window.

**Importance in Signal Processing:**
1. **Unidirectional propagation** — The AP travels only *forward* (orthodromically) because the region behind it is refractory.
2. **Frequency encoding** — Firing rate carries information about stimulus strength.
3. **Temporal summation limits** — Prevents pathological hyperexcitation (e.g., epilepsy, tetanus).
4. **Signal fidelity** — Ensures discrete, non-overlapping impulses for downstream neural decoding.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{in} - V_{out}$ vs $V_{out} - V_{in}$:** The membrane potential is defined as $V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$ (inside minus outside). Reversing this sign will cost full marks on the Nernst derivation.
> 2. **Using 59.2 mV instead of 61.54 mV:** Examiners specifically test whether you used the correct physiological temperature (37 °C) constant. Forgetting to convert T = 310 K is a common 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the Na⁺/K⁺ pump's electrogenic contribution:** The pump is *electrogenic* (3 out : 2 in), not electroneutral. Always state it contributes ≈ −4 mV to the RMP.
> 4. **Confusing ARP with RRP:** ARP = no AP possible; RRP = AP possible with stronger-than-normal stimulus. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question.
> 5. **Confusing Nernst (single ion) with GHK (multi-ion):** Nernst is for one ion; GHK is for the realistic membrane with multiple permeant ions. Examiners test this contrast.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Excitable cells** = neurons, muscle cells, cardiomyocytes — generate action potentials.
- **Resting Membrane Potential** ≈ **−70 mV (neuron)**, **−90 mV (cardiac)**.
- **Nernst Equation:** $E_{ion} = (RT/zF) \ln([Ion]_{out}/[Ion]_{in}) = (61.54/z)\log_{10}([Ion]_{out}/[Ion]_{in}) \text{ mV}$.
- **GHK Equation:** Realistic $V_m$ considering permeability of multiple ions ($P_K, P_{Na}, P_{Cl}$).
- **Three numerical values to memorize:** $E_K \approx -94 \text{ mV}$, $E_{Na} \approx +60 \text{ mV}$, $E_{Cl} \approx -65 \text{ mV}$.
- **Threshold potential** ≈ **−55 mV** for a typical neuron.
- **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump:** 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, **1 ATP** consumed, **electrogenic** (net +1 charge out).
- **Action potential phases:** Resting (4) → Depolarization (0) → Repolarization (1, 3) → Hyperpolarization (2).
- **Depolarization** = Na⁺ influx; **Repolarization** = K⁺ efflux.
- **All-or-None Principle:** Subthreshold = nothing; Supra-threshold = full-amplitude AP.
- **ARP:** Na⁺ channels inactivated — no AP possible. **RRP:** Na⁺ channels recovering — stronger stimulus needed.
- **Membrane capacitance** ≈ 1 µF/cm²; membrane resistance ≈ 1000–10,000 Ω·cm².
- **Temperature constant:** 61.54 mV (37 °C) vs 59.2 mV (25 °C).
- **Faraday's constant** = 96,485 C/mol.
- **Cardiac AP has a plateau** (Phase 2) due to Ca²⁺ influx — *absent* in neurons and skeletal muscle.
- **Saltatory conduction** in myelinated axons → faster velocity.
- **Length constant** $\lambda$ and **time constant** $\tau_m$ govern passive signal spread.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell Membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cell Membrane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **cell membrane** (also called the **plasma membrane** or **biological membrane**) is a thin, semipermeable phospholipid bilayer, approximately **7 nm to 10 nm** in thickness, that envelops the cytoplasm of a cell and separates the intracellular fluid (ICF) from the extracellular fluid (ECF). It is composed primarily of a **lipid bilayer** (~50% by mass), **membrane proteins** (~40%), and **carbohydrates** (~10%), and functions as a selectively permeable barrier that regulates the transport of ions, nutrients, and signaling molecules.

In the context of **Biosignals and Signal Processing**, the cell membrane is mathematically modeled as a **parallel-plate RC circuit** with:
- **Membrane resistance (Rₘ):** typically **1000 Ω·cm² to 10000 Ω·cm²**
- **Membrane capacitance (Cₘ):** typically **0.5 μF/cm² to 1.5 μF/cm²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The cell membrane is the *fundamental source* of all bioelectric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG). Understanding its electrochemical properties is mandatory for Module 1.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the cell membrane as the **walls of a high-security city**:
- The **brick wall** (lipid bilayer) blocks almost everyone from entering.
- The **security gates** (ion channels) selectively allow specific people (ions like Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) to pass in/out.
- The **electrical grid** that powers the gates uses a **voltage difference** between the city interior and the outside world — this is the **resting membrane potential (~ −70 mV)**.
- When a gate is opened, ions rush in/out, causing a local voltage change — this is the **action potential**, the basis of every biosignal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value (Hodgkin-Huxley Squid Axon):**
> - Resting Potential = **−70 mV**
> - Intracellular [K⁺] ≈ **150 mM**, Extracellular [K⁺] ≈ **4 mM**
> - Intracellular [Na⁺] ≈ **15 mM**, Extracellular [Na⁺] ≈ **145 mM**
> - Temperature = **310 K (37 °C)** for human body

---

### Key Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | F | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Body Temperature | T | **310** | K |
| RT/F (Nernst Factor @ 310K) | — | **26.73** | mV |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Cell Membrane

The cell membrane follows the **Fluid Mosaic Model** (proposed by Singer & Nicolson, 1972). It consists of:

- **Phospholipid Bilayer**
  - Two layers of amphipathic phospholipid molecules.
  - Hydrophilic **phosphate heads** face outward (toward water).
  - Hydrophobic **fatty acid tails** face inward (away from water).
  - This structure is the basis of the **capacitive property** of the membrane (dielectric = hydrophobic core, εᵣ ≈ 2 to 5).

- **Integral (Transmembrane) Proteins**
  - Span the bilayer.
  - Function as **ion channels** (passive), **pumps** (active), and **receptors**.

- **Peripheral Proteins**
  - Loosely attached to the membrane surface.
  - Act as enzymes, structural anchors, or signal transducers.

- **Cholesterol Molecules**
  - Embedded in the bilayer.
  - Maintain membrane **fluidity** and reduce ion leakage.

- **Glycoproteins & Glycolipids**
  - Form the **glycocalyx**, important for cell recognition and signaling.

---

### 2.2 Electrical Equivalence

The membrane is modeled as:

- **Resistor (Rₘ):** due to ion leakage channels → ohmic current flow.
- **Capacitor (Cₘ):** due to the lipid bilayer separating two conducting electrolytes.
- **EMF sources (E_Na, E_K, E_Cl):** due to ionic concentration gradients.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Potential (single ion) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | mV (or V) | Equilibrium potential for ion X |
| 2 | Nernst Equation (log₁₀ form) | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | mV | Base-10 version (KTU-favored) |
| 3 | Nernst Factor @ 310 K | $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 61.54$ mV | mV | Used at 37 °C |
| 4 | Nernst Factor @ 310 K, z=1 | $E = 61.54 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$ | mV | For monovalent ions |
| 5 | Membrane Capacitance | $C_m = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Farads | Parallel-plate capacitor model |
| 6 | Specific Capacitance | $C_m/A = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$ | F/m² | Per unit area |
| 7 | Membrane Time Constant | $\tau = R_m C_m$ | seconds (s) | How fast voltage changes |
| 8 | Length Constant | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i + r_o}}$ | m | Passive signal spread |
| 9 | GHK Voltage Equation | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | V | Multi-ion resting potential |
| 10 | Membrane Current | $I_m = C_m \dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + \dfrac{V_m - V_{rest}}{R_m}$ | A | Hodgkin-Huxley current balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Question banks heavily test the Nernst equation (both natural-log and log₁₀ forms) and the Nernst factor numerical value at body temperature. Memorize **61.54 mV** and **26.73 mV** (the latter being the base-e version with factor 1/z).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG/EEG/EMG Electrode Design:** Membrane time constant dictates frequency response of bioelectric recordings.
- **Neural Prosthetics:** Depth and width of neural stimulation pulses are derived from membrane RC characteristics.
- **Defibrillators and Pacemakers:** Rely on precise understanding of action potential thresholds (~ −55 mV).
- **Biosensor Development:** Lipid bilayers are used as artificial membranes in FET-based biosensors.
- **Pharmacokinetics:** Drug action mechanisms (ion channel blockers like lidocaine) require understanding of membrane potentials.

---

### 2.5 Ion Transport Mechanisms

- **Passive Diffusion (Na⁺, K⁺ leak):** Down concentration gradient, no ATP.
- **Facilitated Diffusion (Ion Channels):** Down gradient via specific channels.
- **Primary Active Transport (Na⁺/K⁺ Pump):** Uses ATP, exchanges 3 Na⁺ out for 2 K⁺ in.
- **Secondary Active Transport (Co-transporters):** Uses existing gradients.
- **Bulk Transport (Endocytosis / Exocytosis):** For large molecules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a membrane permeable only to ion $X$ with charge $z$. The ion moves from a region of high chemical potential to a region of low chemical potential, generating an electrical potential difference.

**Step 1 — Chemical Potential of an Ion:**
The chemical potential of an ion of species $X$ in solution is given by:

$$\mu_X = \mu_X^{0} + RT \ln [X]$$

where $\mu_X^{0}$ is the standard chemical potential, $R$ is the gas constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $[X]$ is the molar concentration.

**Step 2 — Electrochemical Potential:**
When an ion carries charge $z$ and is in an electrical potential $V$, the total **electrochemical potential** is:

$$\tilde{\mu}_X = \mu_X^{0} + RT \ln [X] + zFV$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition:**
At equilibrium, the net flux of the ion across the membrane is zero. This occurs when the electrochemical potentials on both sides of the membrane are equal:

$$\tilde{\mu}_X^{out} = \tilde{\mu}_X^{in}$$

$$\mu_X^{0} + RT \ln [X]_{out} + zFV_{out} = \mu_X^{0} + RT \ln [X]_{in} + zFV_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**
The standard potential $\mu_X^{0}$ cancels on both sides. We are interested in the membrane potential $E_X = V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln [X]_{out} - RT \ln [X]_{in}$$

$$zF \cdot E_X = RT \ln \left(\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E_X$:**

$$E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln \left(\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Base-10 Logarithm:**
Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E_X = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the Nernst Factor at 310 K (Body Temperature):**
Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 310$ K, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96{,}485} = \dfrac{5929.4}{96{,}485} \approx 0.06154 \text{ V} = 61.54 \text{ mV}$$

Thus, for a monovalent ion ($z = \pm 1$):

$$\boxed{E_X = 61.54 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) \text{ mV at 37 °C}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Nernst Potential for K⁺

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM (extracellular)
- $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM (intracellular)
- $T = 310$ K
- $z = +1$

**Solution:**

$$E_K = 61.54 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{150}\right)$$

$$E_K = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(0.02667)$$

$$E_K = 61.54 \times (-1.5740)$$

$$\boxed{E_K \approx -96.84 \text{ mV}}$$

This matches the standard KTU/Hodgkin-Huxley reference value of **−97 mV**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Nernst Potential for Na⁺

**Given:**
- $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mM
- $T = 310$ K
- $z = +1$

**Solution:**

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{145}{15}\right)$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times \log_{10}(9.6667)$$

$$E_{Na} = 61.54 \times (0.9853)$$

$$\boxed{E_{Na} \approx +60.6 \text{ mV}}$$

This matches the standard reference value of **+61 mV**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Membrane Capacitance (Parallel-Plate Model)

**Step 1 — Model the Membrane:**
The phospholipid bilayer is modeled as a parallel-plate capacitor:
- Plate area = membrane area $A$
- Plate separation = bilayer thickness $d$ ≈ **7 nm = 7 × 10⁻⁹ m**
- Dielectric = hydrophobic core with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r \approx 3$

**Step 2 — Apply Parallel-Plate Capacitor Formula:**

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

**Step 3 — Calculate Specific Capacitance (per unit area):**

$$\dfrac{C}{A} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d} = \dfrac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(3)}{7 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\dfrac{C}{A} = \dfrac{2.656 \times 10^{-11}}{7 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\dfrac{C}{A} \approx 3.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Common Biophysical Units (μF/cm²):**

$$3.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F/m}^2 = 3.79 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2} \text{ F/cm}^2 = 3.79 \times 10^{-5} \text{ F/cm}^2 = 37.9 \text{ μF/cm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Real-World Values:** The calculated value (~38 μF/cm²) is the **geometric (structural) capacitance** assuming a uniform dielectric. In practice, the effective membrane capacitance is reduced to **~1 μF/cm²** because ion channels and proteins act as local short-circuits across the capacitor. **KTU examiners expect the value 1 μF/cm² as the "standard membrane capacitance" used in circuit models.**

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Membrane Time Constant

The membrane is modeled as an RC circuit. The time constant is:

$$\tau = R_m C_m$$

**Numerical Example (Squid Axon):**
- $R_m = 1000$ Ω·cm²
- $C_m = 1$ μF/cm²

$$\tau = (1000 \text{ Ω·cm}^2)(1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F/cm}^2)$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 1 \text{ ms}}$$

This means membrane voltage changes occur on a millisecond timescale — a critical parameter for **action potential propagation** and **frequency response of bioelectric signals**.

---

### 3.6 Derivation of Resting Membrane Potential using GHK Equation (Simplified)

**Step 1 — Starting Point (Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation):**

$$V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Assume Permeability Ratios:**
Typical relative permeabilities:
- $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**
- $[K^+]_o = 4$, $[K^+]_i = 150$
- $[Na^+]_o = 145$, $[Na^+]_i = 15$
- $[Cl^-]_o = 120$, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ (anions go inside)

**Numerator:**
$$(1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$

**Denominator:**
$$(1)(150) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(120) = 150 + 0.6 + 54 = 204.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$V_m = 26.73 \ln\left(\dfrac{11.6}{204.6}\right) = 26.73 \ln(0.0567) = 26.73 \times (-2.870)$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx -76.7 \text{ mV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result approximates the experimentally observed **resting membrane potential of −70 mV**. The small difference arises from neglected ion pumps (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) which actively contribute ~5 mV to the resting potential.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cell Membrane as an RC Circuit Model

    ECF --> R_Na --> nodeA
    nodeA((Node A)) --> R_K --> ICF

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define the term **resting membrane potential** and list the standard values of intra- and extracellular ion concentrations for K⁺ and Na⁺ in a typical mammalian cell.

**Model Answer:**

The **resting membrane potential** is the steady-state voltage difference (typically **−70 mV**, interior negative) across the plasma membrane of a quiescent cell, generated by differential ion permeabilities and the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump.

| Ion | Intracellular [mM] | Extracellular [mM] |
|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 150 | 4 |
| Na⁺ | 15 | 145 |

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for value, 1 mark for table.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
State the **Nernst equation** and compute its numerical coefficient (Nernst factor) at body temperature (T = 310 K) for a monovalent ion.

**Model Answer:**

The Nernst equation (log₁₀ form) is:

$$E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 310$ K, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $z = 1$:

$$\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96{,}485} \approx 61.54 \text{ mV}$$

(3 marks: 1 mark equation, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final value.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the equilibrium potential of a monovalent ion across a selectively permeable membrane. Show all steps clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the Nernst equation, calculate the equilibrium potentials for **K⁺ and Na⁺** at 37 °C given:
- $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM
- $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mM, $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation of Nernst Equation:**

1. Write the **electrochemical potential** of an ion on side 1 (out) and side 2 (in):
   $\tilde{\mu}_{out} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [X]_{out} + zFV_{out}$
   $\tilde{\mu}_{in} = \mu^0 + RT \ln [X]_{in} + zFV_{in}$

2. At equilibrium: $\tilde{\mu}_{out} = \tilde{\mu}_{in}$

3. Subtract and cancel $\mu^0$:
   $RT \ln [X]_{out} + zFV_{out} = RT \ln [X]_{in} + zFV_{in}$

4. Rearrange:
   $zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = RT \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$

5. Solve for $E_X = V_{in} - V_{out}$:
   $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$

6. Convert to log₁₀:
   $E_X = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

For K⁺ (z = +1):
$E_K = 61.54 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4}{150}\right) = 61.54 \times (-1.574) \approx -96.8$ mV

For Na⁺ (z = +1):
$E_{Na} = 61.54 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{145}{15}\right) = 61.54 \times (0.9853) \approx +60.6$ mV

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **electrical equivalent circuit model** of the cell membrane. Define membrane resistance, membrane capacitance, and the time constant, with their standard numerical values.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cylindrical axon of length 5 cm and diameter 20 μm has membrane resistance $R_m = 2000$ Ω·cm² and membrane capacitance $C_m = 1$ μF/cm². Calculate:
- (i) the membrane time constant,
- (ii) the membrane capacitance of the entire axon in Farads.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Equivalent Circuit Model:**

The cell membrane is modeled as a parallel combination of:
- A **resistor (Rₘ)** representing ion leakage through channels, with standard value **1000 to 10,000 Ω·cm²**.
- A **capacitor (Cₘ)** representing the lipid bilayer, with standard specific capacitance of **~1 μF/cm²**.
- **EMF sources** $E_{Na}$ and $E_K$ representing concentration gradients for the corresponding ions.

The **membrane time constant** $\tau = R_m C_m$ defines how quickly the membrane voltage responds to current injection (typically ~1 ms for neurons).

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

Given: Length $L = 5$ cm, Diameter $d = 20$ μm $\Rightarrow r = 10$ μm = $10^{-3}$ cm.

Surface area: $A = 2\pi r L = 2\pi (10^{-3})(5) = 10\pi \times 10^{-3}$ cm² $\approx 0.0314$ cm².

(i) Time constant: $\tau = R_m C_m = (2000)(1 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s = **2 ms**

(ii) Total membrane capacitance: $C_{total} = C_m \cdot A = (1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F/cm}^2)(0.0314 \text{ cm}^2) = 3.14 \times 10^{-8}$ F = **31.4 nF**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2.303** in the log₁₀ form: Many students write $E = \frac{RT}{zF}\log_{10}(\cdots)$ instead of $\frac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}(\cdots)$. This causes the answer to be off by 2.303×, costing 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing intracellular and extracellular concentrations** in the ratio — KTU requires the convention $[X]_{out} / [X]_{in}$. Reversing this flips the sign of the answer.
> 3. **Using 273 K (room temperature) instead of 310 K (body temperature)** in numerical problems — always check which temperature is specified in the problem.
> 4. **Forgetting the charge number $z$** for divalent ions (e.g., Ca²⁺) — the equation has $z$ in the denominator, so Ca²⁺ gives half the voltage of a monovalent ion.
> 5. **In the circuit model:** forgetting that the standard value **1 μF/cm²** is the *empirically measured* effective value, not the theoretical geometric value (~38 μF/cm²).
> 6. **Unit conversion errors** between cm², m², μF, and nF — practice dimensional analysis.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Cell membrane** = ~7–10 nm thick **phospholipid bilayer** with embedded proteins, cholesterol, and glycocalyx.
- [ ] Follows the **Fluid Mosaic Model** (Singer & Nicolson, 1972).
- [ ] The membrane acts as a **parallel-plate capacitor + resistor** in parallel — this is the basis of all bioelectric signal modeling.
- [ ] **Resting membrane potential ≈ −70 mV** (interior negative).
- [ ] Standard ion concentrations: **K⁺** (in = 150, out = 4), **Na⁺** (in = 15, out = 145), **Cl⁻** (in = 4, out = 120) — all in mM.
- [ ] **Nernst Equation (log₁₀ form):** $E_{ion} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$
- [ ] **Nernst Factor at 310 K** = **61.54 mV** (for z = 1).
- [ ] **Equilibrium potentials:** $E_K \approx -97$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +61$ mV, $E_{Cl} \approx -90$ mV.
- [ ] **Specific Membrane Capacitance** $C_m \approx 1$ μF/cm² (empirical); theoretical = ~38 μF/cm².
- [ ] **Specific Membrane Resistance** $R_m \approx 1000$ to $10{,}000$ Ω·cm².
- [ ] **Membrane Time Constant** $\tau = R_m C_m \approx 1$ ms (typical neuron).
- [ ] **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** gives the **resting potential** for multiple ions based on permeabilities.
- [ ] **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase Pump** actively transports 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP, maintaining the gradient.
- [ ] **Length Constant** $\lambda$ describes passive signal spread along an axon — critical for cable theory.
- [ ] All **biosignals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG)** are macroscopic summations of ion-channel-driven membrane potential changes.
- [ ] **Body temperature for human calculations:** always use $T = 310$ K unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Remember:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure. Nernst Potential - Resting Membrane Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structure. Nernst Potential - Resting Membrane Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Biological Cell Membrane – Formal Definition

The **cell membrane (plasma membrane)** is a thin, semi-permeable phospholipid bilayer (≈ **7–10 nm** thick) that separates the intracellular cytoplasm from the extracellular fluid. It functions simultaneously as a **biological capacitor**, a **selective ion-conducting barrier**, and a **signal transduction interface**. From a signal-processing perspective, the membrane is the fundamental site where every bioelectric signal (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) originates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Biosignals & Signal Processing, the membrane is treated as the **first-stage transducer** of every physiological signal. Without understanding its electrical structure, the analysis of ECG, EEG, and nerve-conduction signals is impossible.

**Composition of the membrane (Fluid Mosaic Model – Singer & Nicolson, 1972):**
- **Phospholipid bilayer** – hydrophobic core (tails) sandwiched between two hydrophilic phosphate-head layers.
- **Cholesterol molecules** – regulate membrane fluidity.
- **Integral (transmembrane) proteins** – ion channels, pumps, receptors.
- **Peripheral proteins** – cytoskeletal anchors and enzymes.
- **Glycoproteins / Glycolipids** – cell-recognition and immunological signaling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Impedance note:** Because the lipid core is a near-perfect insulator (~10⁹ Ω·cm² specific resistance), the intact membrane stores charge like a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance **C_m ≈ 1 µF/cm²**. This is a key KTU value to remember.

### 1.2 Nernst Potential – Formal Definition

The **Nernst Potential (E_ion)**, also called the **Equilibrium Potential** or **Reversal Potential**, is the precise transmembrane voltage at which there is **zero net ionic flux** for a given ion species. At this voltage, the electrical driving force pulling the ion in one direction exactly balances the chemical (concentration) driving force pushing it in the other direction.

Mathematically, the Nernst equation is written as:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{outside}}{[ion]_{inside}}\right)$$

where R, T, z, F are the universal gas constant, absolute temperature, ion valence, and Faraday constant respectively.

### 1.3 Resting Membrane Potential – Formal Definition

The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP), V_rest**, is the steady-state intracellular potential (with respect to the extracellular fluid) of an excitable cell when it is **not being stimulated**. It is determined by the **combined permeability and concentration gradients of all permeant ions** (chiefly K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻). For a typical mammalian neuron, **V_rest ≈ −70 mV** (sign convention: inside negative relative to outside).

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy 1 – The Hydroelectric Dam:**
Think of the cell membrane as a concrete dam. On one side (intracellular side) is a deep reservoir rich in K⁺ ions, on the other side (extracellular) is a lake rich in Na⁺ ions. Tiny gates (ion channels) in the dam allow some water (ions) to trickle through. When the gates are partially open, water flows until the water level on both sides reaches a balance — this final level difference is the **Nernst (equilibrium) potential** for that specific ion. The actual level difference observed when many such gates (for K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻) operate simultaneously is the **resting membrane potential**.

**Analogy 2 – The Water Tower & Pressure Valve:**
Imagine a building with a pressurized water system. The intracellular K⁺ is like the water tank — it has stored potential. The membrane's K⁺ channels are like small leak valves. The Nernst potential is the exact pressure at which the leak rate due to pressure exactly equals the rate of refilling by the pumps (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase). The **resting membrane potential is the stable, long-term operating pressure** maintained by this balance.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory aid for KTU exams:**
> - **Nernst Potential** = equilibrium for **one** ion species.
> - **Resting Membrane Potential** = equilibrium for the **whole cell** (all permeant ions considered together via the Goldman equation).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture of the Cell Membrane

The membrane is best understood as a **four-component composite system**:

1. **Lipid Bilayer (≈ 50% of mass):** Provides the high electrical resistance (R_m) and the dielectric that supports membrane capacitance.
2. **Transmembrane Proteins (≈ 25–30%):** Form ion-selective pores. Types:
   - **Leak channels** – always partially open; responsible for setting V_rest.
   - **Voltage-gated channels** – open in response to ΔV; responsible for action potentials.
   - **Ligand-gated channels** – open in response to chemical messengers.
3. **Peripheral Proteins & Cytoskeleton (≈ 20%):** Anchor and structurally support the membrane.
4. **Carbohydrate Coats (Glycocalyx, ≈ 5–10%):** Recognition sites, receptors, immunological markers.

### 2.2 The Three Ionic Gradients That Drive Bioelectricity

| Ion | Intracellular [mM] | Extracellular [mM] | Nernst Potential E (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K⁺ (Potassium)** | **140** | **4** | **−94 mV** |
| **Na⁺ (Sodium)** | **12** | **145** | **+60 mV** |
| **Ca²⁺ (Calcium)** | **10⁻⁴** | **2.5** | **+150 mV** |
| **Cl⁻ (Chloride)** | **4** | **120** | **−90 mV** |

> [!NOTE]
> The body invests enormous metabolic energy (~**70% of a neuron's ATP**) into the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** to maintain these steep concentration gradients. Without this pump, the gradients collapse in seconds and the cell dies electrically.

### 2.3 Why the Resting Potential is Closer to E_K Than to E_Na

Although the extracellular fluid contains both Na⁺ and K⁺, the resting membrane is **far more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺ (ratio ≈ **1 : 25 to 1 : 100**, depending on the cell). Because the cell's voltage is "tugged" toward the Nernst potential of the most permeant ion, V_rest (−70 mV) sits **closer to E_K (−94 mV)** than to E_Na (+60 mV).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$ | General Nernst equation (natural log) |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$ | Nernst equation in log₁₀ form |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$ mV at 37 °C | **Most used form in KTU exams** (T = 310 K) |
| 4 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation** |
| 5 | $C_m = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Membrane capacitance per unit area |
| 6 | $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ | Membrane time constant (signal-processing relevance) |

**Physical Constants (memorize for KTU):**
- **R** (Universal gas constant) = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **F** (Faraday constant) = **96,485 C/mol** ≈ **96,500 C/mol**
- **T** (Body temperature) = **310 K (37 °C)**
- **2.303·RT/F** at 37 °C = **61.5 mV**
- **2.303·RT/zF** at 37 °C, z = 1 = **61.5 mV**; z = 2 = **30.75 mV**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using the GHK equation, note that Cl⁻ appears **inverted** (i_o on the bottom, i_i on top) because Cl⁻ is a negative ion. Many students lose marks by writing Cl⁻ terms in the same orientation as K⁺ and Na⁺.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the Nernst potential is the foundation for:
- **ECG (Electrocardiography):** The cardiac action potential originates from the same Nernst/Goldman principles.
- **Nerve Conduction Studies (NCS) & EMG:** Diagnose peripheral neuropathies.
- **Microelectrode & Patch-Clamp Design:** Biomedical instrumentation depends on knowing the Nernst potential of the reference electrode.
- **Neural Prosthetics & Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Electrode-tissue interface modeling uses Nernst-derived junction potentials.
- **Biosensor Design:** Ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) used in clinical analyzers exploit the Nernst equation to convert ion activity into measurable voltage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (from Electrochemical Potential)

The Nernst equation is the cornerstone of bioelectricity. Below is the full KTU-board-style derivation.

**Starting Principle:** At electrochemical equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** (μ̃) of the ion must be equal on both sides of the membrane.

$$\tilde{\mu}_{outside} = \tilde{\mu}_{inside}$$

**Step 1 – Write the electrochemical potential on each side:**

For an ion of charge z, the electrochemical potential combines the **chemical potential** (concentration term) and the **electrical potential** (voltage term):

$$\tilde{\mu}_{outside} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[ion]_o + zF V_o$$

$$\tilde{\mu}_{inside} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[ion]_i + zF V_i$$

**Step 2 – Equate the two expressions:**

$$\mu^0 + RT \ln[ion]_o + zF V_o = \mu^0 + RT \ln[ion]_i + zF V_i$$

**Step 3 – Cancel the standard chemical potential (μ⁰):**

$$RT \ln[ion]_o + zF V_o = RT \ln[ion]_i + zF V_i$$

**Step 4 – Group voltage terms on one side and concentration terms on the other:**

$$zF V_o - zF V_i = RT \ln[ion]_i - RT \ln[ion]_o$$

**Step 5 – Factor the left-hand side and use the log property on the right-hand side:**

$$zF (V_o - V_i) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_i}{[ion]_o}\right)$$

**Step 6 – Define the Nernst potential E as the inside-referenced voltage (V_in − V_out):**

$$E = V_i - V_o = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$$

The sign flip from [ion]_i / [ion]_o to [ion]_o / [ion]_i is achieved by using the identity $\ln(a/b) = -\ln(b/a)$.

**Step 7 – Convert to base-10 logarithm for engineering convenience:**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$E = \frac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$$

**Step 8 – Plug in body-temperature constants (T = 310 K):**

$$E = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{z \times 96{,}485} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) \;\; \text{volts}$$

$$E \approx \frac{61.5 \times 10^{-3}}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) \;\; \text{volts}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{ion}\;(\text{mV}) \;=\; \frac{61.5}{z} \,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example – Nernst Potential of K⁺

**Given:** [K⁺]_o = 4 mM, [K⁺]_i = 140 mM, z = +1, T = 37 °C.

**Step 1 – Identify the ion valence:** z = +1 (potassium is monovalent).

**Step 2 – Write the equation:**

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right)$$

**Step 3 – Substitute the concentrations:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 – Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 5 – Take the base-10 logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.5441$$

**Step 6 – Multiply by 61.5:**

$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.5441) \approx -94.96 \;\; \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_K \approx -95 \;\; \text{mV}\;}$$

This matches the **standard accepted value of −94 mV** for the potassium equilibrium potential — a frequently asked numerical in KTU exams.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation

The GHK equation extends the single-ion Nernst idea to a membrane that is simultaneously permeable to multiple ions. The derivation uses the **constant-field assumption** (Goldman, 1943; Hodgkin & Katz, 1949), which assumes the electric field across the membrane is **linear and constant**.

**Step 1 – Nernst-Planck flux equation for ion j:**

$$J_j = -D_j \frac{d[C_j]}{dx} - \frac{z_j F D_j}{RT} [C_j] \frac{dV}{dx}$$

**Step 2 – Apply the constant-field assumption (dV/dx = −V_m/d, constant):**

$$J_j = -D_j \left[ \frac{d[C_j]}{dx} + \frac{z_j F V_m}{RT \, d} [C_j] \right]$$

**Step 3 – Integrate from x = 0 (inside) to x = d (outside) with boundary concentrations [C_j]_i and [C_j]_o:**

The standard integral (using separation of variables) yields the **GHK current density** for ion j:

$$I_j = P_j z_j^2 F^2 \frac{V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[C_j]_i - [C_j]_o \, e^{-z_j F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_j F V_m / RT}}$$

where **P_j** is the **permeability** of ion j (a constant-field lumped parameter).

**Step 4 – At steady state, the net membrane current is zero:**

$$I_{total} = I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$$

**Step 5 – Solve for V_m with the three ions (K⁺, Na⁺, Cl⁻) included, with z_{K,Na} = +1, z_{Cl} = −1:**

After algebraic rearrangement (see the standard textbook steps):

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{rest} = 61.5 \; \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right) \;\; \text{mV}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Cl⁻ flipped?** The negative valence of Cl⁻ reverses the direction of its driving force. The Cl⁻ terms therefore appear in **opposite orientation** to the cation terms — a critical KTU exam point.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example – Computing the Resting Membrane Potential

**Given (typical mammalian neuron):**

- [K⁺]_i = 140 mM, [K⁺]_o = 4 mM
- [Na⁺]_i = 12 mM, [Na⁺]_o = 145 mM
- [Cl⁻]_i = 4 mM, [Cl⁻]_o = 120 mM
- Permeability ratios: **P_K : P_Na : P_Cl = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45**

**Step 1 – Compute the numerator (outside concentration weighted by permeability):**

$$N = (1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)$$

$$N = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$

**Step 2 – Compute the denominator (inside concentration weighted by permeability):**

$$D = (1)(140) + (0.04)(12) + (0.45)(120)$$

$$D = 140 + 0.48 + 54 = 194.48$$

**Step 3 – Take the ratio:**

$$\frac{N}{D} = \frac{11.6}{194.48} \approx 0.05965$$

**Step 4 – Take the base-10 logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(0.05965) \approx -1.2245$$

**Step 5 – Multiply by 61.5:**

$$V_{rest} = 61.5 \times (-1.2245) \approx -75.3 \;\; \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{rest} \approx -75 \;\; \text{mV}\;}$$

This is the **classical GHK-predicted resting potential** for a mammalian neuron (closely matches the experimental value of **−70 mV**; the small difference arises from the contributions of active ion pumps and other minor ions not included in the simplified calculation).

---

### 3.5 Effect of Changing Permeability – Analytical Insight

The GHK equation reveals a powerful engineering insight: by selectively varying one permeability, we can shift the entire resting potential.

- If **P_Na → 0** (Na⁺ channels closed): V_m → E_K = −94 mV.
- If **P_K → 0** (K⁺ channels closed): V_m → E_Na = +60 mV (degenerate, unrealistic).
- **Action potential upstroke:** A sudden 600-fold increase in P_Na makes V_m shoot from −70 mV to +30 mV in 1 ms.
- **Repolarization:** A delayed increase in P_K restores V_m back to −70 mV.

This permeability-switching mechanism is the **physical basis of every bioelectric signal** — and hence the foundation of KTU Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – The Fluid Mosaic Model of the Cell Membrane

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EXT["EXTRACELLULAR SPACE (V_out = 0 mV reference)"]
        Naext["Na+ 145 mM"]
        Cleft["Cl- 120 mM"]
        Kext["K+ 4 mM"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["CELL MEMBRANE ~ 7-10 nm thick"]
        GL1["Glycoprotein / Glycocalyx"]
        CHOL1["Cholesterol molecule"]
        PHEAD1["Phosphate Head (hydrophilic)"]
        TAIL1["Lipid Tails (hydrophobic core)"]
        PHEAD2["Phosphate Head (hydrophilic)"]
        CHOL2["Cholesterol molecule"]
        GL2["Peripheral Protein (inside)"]
        NKP["Na+/K+ ATPase Pump"]
        KCH["K+ Leak Channel"]
        NACH["Na+ Leak Channel"]
        REC["Receptor Protein"]
    end

    subgraph INT["INTRACELLULAR CYTOPLASM (V_in approx -70 mV)"]
        Kint["K+ 140 mM"]
        Cllint["Cl- 4 mM"]
        Naint["Na+ 12 mM"]
        ANION["Large Anions A- (impermeant)"]
    end

    Naext -.flux via NACH.-> Naint
    Kint -.flux via KCH.-> Kext
    Naext -.pumped out.-> NKP
    Kext -.pumped in.-> NKP
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Equivalent Circuit of the Membrane (Signal-Processing View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vext["V_ext (reference node)"] --- Cm["C_m = 1 uF per cm2 (membrane capacitance)"]
    Cm --- Rm["R_m = 1 kohm per cm2 (membrane resistance)"]
    Rm --- Rk["R_K (K+ channel resistor)"]
    Rk --- Rna["R_Na (Na+ channel resistor)"]
    Rna --- Rcl["R_Cl (Cl- channel resistor)"]
    Rcl --- Vint["V_int (intracellular node)"]
    Ebat["Battery E_K = -94 mV"] --- Rk
    Ebna["Battery E_Na = +60 mV"] --- Rna
    Ebcl["Battery E_Cl = -90 mV"] --- Rcl
    Vint -. output .-> Vrest["V_rest approx -70 mV"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** This equivalent circuit shows that the resting membrane potential is simply the **weighted average of the Nernst batteries** of all permeant ions, where the weights are the **channel conductances (1/R)**. This is the linear-network (Wheatstone-bridge-like) interpretation of the GHK equation — a frequent KTU viva question.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Flow of Reasoning from Structure to Resting Potential

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Step 1: Cell Membrane Structure"] --> B["Step 2: Selective Permeability to Ions"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Concentration Gradients maintained by Na+/K+ Pump"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Single-ion Equilibrium gives Nernst Equation"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Multiple Ions - Constant Field Assumption"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation"]
    F --> G["Step 7: V_rest = -70 mV (mammalian neuron)"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Action Potential generation (next topic)"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**State and explain the Nernst equation. What is its significance in bioelectric signal generation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nernst equation gives the transmembrane potential at which an ion species is in electrochemical equilibrium (zero net flux):

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) \;\; \text{mV at } 37\,°C$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It defines the **driving force** behind every bioelectric signal — at the cellular level, all ECG, EEG, and EMG signals are macroscopic sums of these equilibrium voltages being perturbed.
2. It is the **basis for the GHK equation** that predicts the resting membrane potential.
3. It enables design of **ion-selective electrodes and biosensors** used in clinical analyzers.
4. It is essential in the **mathematical modeling of the Hodgkin–Huxley action potential**, which underpins modern neural engineering and BCI technology.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question]`
**What is resting membrane potential? Why is its value (≈ −70 mV) closer to the Nernst potential of K⁺ than to that of Na⁺?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The resting membrane potential (V_rest) is the steady-state transmembrane voltage of an excitable cell in the absence of stimulation. It is the weighted average of the Nernst potentials of all permeant ions, as given by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation**.

**Reason (2 marks):**
At rest, the membrane is **far more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺** (typical permeability ratio P_K : P_Na ≈ 1 : 0.04, i.e., K⁺ is ~25× more permeable). Since V_rest is "tugged" toward the Nernst potential of the **most permeant ion**, V_rest sits close to E_K = −94 mV. If the membrane were equally permeable to Na⁺, V_rest would shift toward E_Na = +60 mV. Additionally, the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump continuously expels 3 Na⁺ and brings in 2 K⁺, contributing a further ~ −4 to −10 mV hyperpolarization.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 marks, with internal choice)

---

### **Question A (14 marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation starting from the condition of electrochemical equilibrium. State clearly the meaning of each symbol used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mammalian cardiac cell has the following ionic concentrations:
- [K⁺]_i = 150 mM, [K⁺]_o = 4 mM
- [Na⁺]_i = 10 mM, [Na⁺]_o = 140 mM
- [Cl⁻]_i = 4 mM, [Cl⁻]_o = 120 mM
- Permeability ratios: P_K : P_Na : P_Cl = 1 : 0.05 : 0.4

Using the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation, compute the resting membrane potential. State the units clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) – Derivation of the Nernst Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Equilibrium condition (1 mark):**
At electrochemical equilibrium, the electrochemical potential μ̃ of the ion is equal on both sides of the membrane:

$$\tilde{\mu}_{outside} = \tilde{\mu}_{inside}$$

**Step 2 – Expression of electrochemical potential (2 marks):**
For an ion of valence z at concentration [C] and local electric potential V:

$$\tilde{\mu} = \mu^0 + RT \ln[C] + zF V$$

Apply to both sides:
- Outside: $\tilde{\mu}_o = \mu^0 + RT \ln[ion]_o + zF V_o$
- Inside: $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu^0 + RT \ln[ion]_i + zF V_i$

**Step 3 – Equate and cancel μ⁰ (1 mark):**

$$RT \ln[ion]_o + zF V_o = RT \ln[ion]_i + zF V_i$$

**Step 4 – Rearrange (2 marks):**

$$zF(V_i - V_o) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$$

$$E_{ion} = V_i - V_o = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$$

**Step 5 – Log base conversion & constants (1 mark):**

$$E_{ion} = \frac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right) \;\; \text{mV at 37 °C}$$

**Symbol meanings (1 mark included above):** R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) gas constant; T = absolute temperature; z = ion valence; F = 96,485 C/mol Faraday constant; [ion]_o, [ion]_i = external and internal ion concentrations.

#### Part (b) – GHK Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Write the GHK equation (1 mark):**

$$V_m = 61.5 \,\log_{10}\!\left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right) \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 – Compute the numerator (2 marks):**

$$N = (1)(4) + (0.05)(140) + (0.4)(4)$$

$$N = 4 + 7.0 + 1.6 = 12.6$$

**Step 3 – Compute the denominator (2 marks):**

$$D = (1)(150) + (0.05)(10) + (0.4)(120)$$

$$D = 150 + 0.5 + 48 = 198.5$$

**Step 4 – Take the ratio and logarithm (1 mark):**

$$\frac{N}{D} = \frac{12.6}{198.5} = 0.06348$$

$$\log_{10}(0.06348) = -1.1974$$

**Step 5 – Multiply by 61.5 (1 mark):**

$$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1974) = -73.64 \;\; \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{rest} \approx -74 \;\; \text{mV}\;}$$

This is consistent with the **experimentally measured resting potential of cardiac ventricular myocytes** (≈ −80 to −90 mV in vivo; the slight discrepancy is due to pump currents and other ions not in the simplified model).

---

### **Question B (14 marks) – Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 1]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the fluid-mosaic model of the cell membrane. Highlight the role of each structural component in determining the membrane's electrical properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the Nernst potential. Calculate the Nernst potential for **Ca²⁺** given that [Ca²⁺]_o = 2.5 mM and [Ca²⁺]_i = 10⁻⁴ mM at body temperature. Comment on the biological significance of this large positive value. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) – Fluid Mosaic Model (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):** The block diagram should show the phospholipid bilayer (heads + tails) with embedded proteins — ion channels, Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pumps, receptors, and glycocalyx. (See Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Component roles (4 marks):**
- **Phospholipid bilayer:** Provides the high resistance (R_m) and the dielectric that supports membrane capacitance (C_m ≈ 1 µF/cm²).
- **Cholesterol:** Regulates membrane fluidity and stabilizes channel function across temperature variations.
- **Transmembrane proteins (channels & pumps):** Conduct ions selectively; determine permeability ratios used in the GHK equation.
- **Peripheral proteins & cytoskeleton:** Maintain membrane shape and anchor ion channels in place.
- **Glycocalyx (glycoproteins/glycolipids):** Recognition sites for cell-cell signaling; clinically important in blood typing and immune response.

#### Part (b) – Nernst Potential of Ca²⁺ (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Nernst potential E_Ca is the transmembrane voltage at which the net flux of Ca²⁺ across the membrane is zero — i.e., the electrical driving force balances the concentration driving force.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify constants: z = +2, T = 310 K.

Step 2 — Apply Nernst equation:

$$E_{Ca} = \frac{61.5}{+2} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_o}{[Ca^{2+}]_i}\right)$$

Step 3 — Substitute values:

$$E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.5}{10^{-4}}\right)$$

Step 4 — Simplify the ratio:

$$\frac{2.5}{10^{-4}} = 2.5 \times 10^{4} = 25{,}000$$

Step 5 — Take the log:

$$\log_{10}(25{,}000) = \log_{10}(2.5) + 4 = 0.3979 + 4 = 4.3979$$

Step 6 — Multiply:

$$E_{Ca} = 30.75 \times 4.3979 \approx 135.24 \;\; \text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{\;E_{Ca} \approx +135 \;\; \text{mV}\;}$$

**Biological significance (1 mark):**
The very large positive equilibrium potential of Ca²⁺ reflects a **25,000-fold concentration gradient**, maintained by powerful Ca²⁺-ATPase pumps and Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchangers. This gradient is the **trigger for neurotransmitter release** at synapses, **muscle contraction** via calcium-induced calcium release, and **second-messenger signaling**. Even a tiny Ca²⁺ influx (during an action potential) produces a large electrochemical response, making Ca²⁺ an exceptionally sensitive intracellular signaling ion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Sign convention errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Confusing inside-referenced (V_in − V_out) with outside-referenced voltage. Always state clearly: **"V_m is the potential of the inside relative to the outside."**
> 2. **Wrong orientation of Cl⁻ in GHK (–2 marks):** The Cl⁻ terms MUST be inverted. Cl⁻ outside goes in the **denominator**, Cl⁻ inside in the **numerator** — opposite to cations.
> 3. **Forgetting the constant 61.5 mV (–1 mark):** State "at 37 °C" explicitly. If the problem is at 25 °C (room temperature), use **59.1 mV/z** instead.
> 4. **Forgetting the valence z for Ca²⁺ (–1 mark):** Always divide by z in the Nernst equation.
> 5. **Mixing log and ln (–1 mark):** Use $\log_{10}$ in engineering, $\ln$ in theoretical derivations — convert explicitly using 2.303.
> 6. **No units stated (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always write **mV** explicitly in the final answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU Module 1:**
> - **Nernst equation** is for **one** ion; **GHK equation** is for the **whole cell**.
> - At 37 °C, the **constant 61.5 mV** applies for monovalent ions (z = ±1); use **30.75 mV** for divalent ions (z = ±2).
> - **V_rest ≈ −70 mV** in neurons, **≈ −90 mV** in skeletal muscle, **≈ −50 mV** in smooth muscle.
> - **E_K ≈ −94 mV, E_Na ≈ +60 mV, E_Ca ≈ +135 mV, E_Cl ≈ −90 mV** (memorize these).
> - **Cl⁻ is inverted in the GHK equation** — a classic KTU trap.
> - The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** consumes ~70% of a neuron's ATP to maintain the gradients.
> - **Permeability ratio P_K : P_Na : P_Cl ≈ 1 : 0.04 : 0.45** at rest.
> - The **membrane time constant τ_m = R_m × C_m** controls how fast a bioelectric signal changes — relevant in ECG/EEG filtering.
> - The **Nernst potential** is the **open-circuit voltage** of an ion-selective membrane; the **GHK voltage** is the **closed-circuit operating point** with all channels active.
> - **Action potential** = transient flip of permeability from K⁺-dominant to Na⁺-dominant (Module 1 next topic).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goldman Hodgkin Katz Equation. Specialized Excitatory and Conductive System of Heart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Goldman Hodgkin Katz Equation. Specialized Excitatory and Conductive System of Heart # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation ### Formal Academic Definition The **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation** is a fundamental electrophysiological relationship that quantifies the **resting membrane potential (V_m)** of a biological cell by accounting for the simultaneous permeability of the cell membrane to multiple ion species. It is mathematically a direct extension of the Nernst equation into a multi-ionic system, derived from the constant field approximation (Goldman 1943, Hodgkin & Katz 1949). $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \left( \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o} \right)$$ where $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**), $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin (**310 K** at body temperature), $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96,485 C·mol⁻¹**), and $P_{ion}$ represents the permeability coefficient of the membrane to that specific ion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The GHK equation is the **cornerstone of bioelectric signal processing** for Module 1. It bridges the gap between single-ion Nernst potential (covered previously) and the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Voltage Equation** used to model cardiac, neural, and skeletal muscle resting potentials. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **multi-lane toll booth** on a busy highway. Each lane is reserved for a specific type of vehicle (cars = K⁺, trucks = Na⁺, buses = Cl⁻). The amount of "back-pressure" or "voltage" that builds up at the toll plaza depends not just on the number of each vehicle type, but also on **how wide each lane is open** (permeability) and **how many vehicles are queued on each side** (concentration). If K⁺ lanes are wide open and K⁺ is heavily concentrated inside, the toll plaza pushes the equilibrium potential strongly negative. The GHK equation is essentially the **mathematical bookkeeper** of how these competing lanes contribute to the net resting voltage. --- ## 1.2 The Specialized Excitatory and Conductive System of the Heart ### Formal Academic Definition The **Specialized Excitatory and Conductive System** of the heart is a unique network of **autorhythmic (pacemaker) cardiac muscle cells** that generate and propagate electrical impulses (action potentials) throughout the myocardium, thereby coordinating the synchronized sequence of **atrial contraction → ventricular contraction** required for effective blood pumping. This system comprises the **SA node, AV node, Bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibers**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the heart's electrical system as a **building's fire alarm and sprinkler network**. The **SA node** is the master alarm trigger (fires automatically ~70 times/min).
