The signal travels through the **atria** (like a hallway announcement), reaches the **AV node** (a security checkpoint that delays 0.1 seconds so all rooms empty first), then rushes down the **Bundle of His** (the main stairwell), splits into **bundle branches** (left and right staircases), and finally activates the **Purkinje fibers** (the sprinklers in every room — the ventricles) to deliver the message everywhere simultaneously. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Callout — Autorhythmicity:** Cardiac specialized cells are **self-excitable**; they spontaneously depolarize without external nervous stimulation, making the heart **myogenic** (intrinsically originating muscle activity), unlike skeletal muscle which is **neurogenic**. --- ## 1.3 The Five Major Components of the Cardiac Conduction System | # | Structure | Location | Intrinsic Rate (BPM) | Primary Function | |---|-----------|----------|----------------------|------------------| | 1 | **SA Node** | Right atrium, near SVC opening | **70–75** | Primary pacemaker | | 2 | **AV Node** | Interatrial septum, near tricuspid valve | **40–60** | Delay (~0.1 s), backup pacemaker | | 3 | **Bundle of His** | Upper interventricular septum | **40–60** | AV conduction pathway | | 4 | **Bundle Branches** | Left & right sides of septum | **25–40** | Distribute signal to ventricles | | 5 | **Purkinje Fibers** | Ventricular myocardium walls | **25–40** | Rapid ventricular activation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Principle — Descending Pacemaker Hierarchy:** When a higher-rate pacemaker fails, the next lower pacemaker in the hierarchy takes over (a phenomenon called **"escape rhythm"**). This is why the AV node can keep a person alive at 40–60 BPM if the SA node fails. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the GHK Equation ### Why We Need the GHK Equation The **Nernst equation** (V = RT/zF · ln([X]o/[X]i)) can predict the equilibrium potential for a **single ion** crossing a membrane. However, real biological membranes (cardiac, neural, muscle) are **simultaneously permeable to multiple ions** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻). The Nernst equation **fails** in this multi-ion scenario. The GHK equation solves this by: 1. **Constant Field Assumption:** The electric field across the membrane is assumed to be constant (linear voltage drop). This is the core simplification that makes the math tractable. 2. **Permeability Weighting:** Each ion's contribution to V_m is **weighted by its membrane permeability (P_ion)** — a more permeable ion exerts more "pull" on the resting potential. 3. **Charge Accounting:** Anions (Cl⁻) are placed on the **opposite side** of the equation from cations because their gradient direction is reversed. ### Logical Breakdown of the Equation Structure - **Numerator** = Sum of (permeability × outside concentration) for all **cations** + (permeability × inside concentration) for the **anion** Cl⁻ - **Denominator** = Sum of (permeability × inside concentration) for all **cations** + (permeability × outside concentration) for the **anion** Cl⁻ - **RT/F** = Thermal voltage factor (**≈ 26.73 mV at 37°C/310 K**) - **At 37°C, 2.303·RT/F = 61.5 mV** (the often-quoted physiological constant) ### Real-World Engineering Utility The GHK equation is **foundational** in: - **ECG/EKG signal processing** — predicting baseline drift and resting potentials - **Neural prosthetics and brain-computer interfaces (BCIs)** — modeling neural signal generation - **Cardiac pacemaker design** — understanding pacemaker cell automaticity - **Pharmacology** — predicting how ion channel drugs (e.g., lidocaine, amiodarone) shift resting potentials - **Biosensor development** — ion-selective electrodes and microelectrode arrays --- ## 2.2 The 5-Step Cardiac Excitation Sequence 1. **SA Node Firing:** Spontaneous Phase-4 depolarization reaches threshold (-40 mV); L-type Ca²⁺ channels open, generating the **pacemaker action potential (no true resting potential, slow upstroke)**. 2. **Atrial Conduction:** Impulse spreads through atrial myocardium via **gap junctions** and three **internodal pathways** (anterior, middle, posterior) toward the AV node. 3. **AV Nodal Delay:** Slow conduction (≈ 0.1 second) due to small-diameter cells and few gap junctions. This delay is **physiologically critical** — it allows complete ventricular filling before contraction. 4. **Bundle of His & Bundle Branches:** Rapid conduction through these large-diameter, fast-conducting fibers (~2 m/s), ensuring synchronized ventricular activation. 5. **Purkinje Fiber Distribution:** Purkinje cells (largest in heart) conduct at **4 m/s** (fastest in heart), spreading the impulse across both ventricles within ~0.06 seconds. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Note on Conduction Velocities:** Purkinje > Atrial muscle > Ventricular muscle > AV node. The AV node is the **slowest** conductor (0.05 m/s) — this is by design, not by accident. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Values / Units | |---|-------------------|------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Nernst Equation (single ion)** | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}\right)$ | V in Volts; $z$ = valence | | 2 | **GHK Voltage Equation** | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | V in Volts | | 3 | **GHK at 37°C (simplified)** | $V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | mV | | 4 | **Thermal Voltage (RT/F)** | $V_T = \frac{RT}{F}$ | ≈ 26.73 mV at 310 K | | 5 | **Physiological Constant (2.303·RT/F)** | $K_{phys} = 2.303 \cdot \frac{RT}{F}$ | ≈ 61.5 mV at 37°C | | 6 | **Permeability Ratio (Cardiac)** | $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl}$ | ≈ 1 : 0.04 : 0.45 (at rest) | | 7 | **Typical Mammalian V_m** | $V_m$ (cardiac ventricular myocyte) | ≈ **-90 mV** | | 8 | **Conduction Velocity (Purkinje)** | $v_{Purkinje}$ | ≈ **4 m/s** | | 9 | **Conduction Velocity (AV Node)** | $v_{AV}$ | ≈ **0.05 m/s** | | 10 | **AV Nodal Delay** | $\Delta t_{AV}$ | ≈ **0.1 s** (100 ms) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Tip:** The permeability ratio $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl}$ changes dynamically during an action potential. At rest, K⁺ dominates ($P_K$ is largest); during depolarization, Na⁺ permeability surges; during repolarization, K⁺ permeability dominates again. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation ### 3.1.1 Starting Point: The Nernst-Planck Flux Equation The ionic flux $J_{ion}$ (mol·cm⁻²·s⁻¹) of a single ion across a membrane of thickness $d$ is governed by the **Nernst-Planck equation**, which combines **concentration-driven diffusion** and **electric field-driven drift**: $$J_{ion} = -D_{ion} \left( \frac{d[ion]}{dx} + \frac{zF[ion]}{RT} \frac{dV}{dx} \right)$$ where $D_{ion}$ is the diffusion coefficient of the ion in the membrane, $z$ is the ion's valence, and $V$ is the electric potential. ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Apply the Constant Field Assumption Goldman's key simplification: **the electric field across the membrane is linear and constant**. Therefore, $\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{V_m}{d}$ (constant), and similarly $\frac{d[ion]}{dx} = \frac{[ion]_o - [ion]_i}{d}$ (linear concentration gradient). ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Substitute the Constant Field Substituting the constant field into the Nernst-Planck equation: $$J_{ion} = -D_{ion} \left( \frac{[ion]_o - [ion]_i}{d} + \frac{zF[ion]}{RT} \cdot \frac{V_m}{d} \right)$$ $$J_{ion} = -\frac{D_{ion}}{d} \left( [ion]_o - [ion]_i + \frac{zF V_m}{RT} [ion] \right)$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Integrate Across the Membrane Thickness We integrate the flux expression with respect to $x$ from $0$ to $d$, using the relationship between permeability and diffusion: $P_{ion} = \frac{D_{ion}}{d}$: $$J_{ion} = P_{ion} \cdot \frac{zF V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[ion]_o - [ion]_i \cdot e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Apply the Steady-State Condition At resting membrane potential, **net ionic current = 0** (no net charge flux). Since current $I_{ion} = zFJ_{ion}$, the steady-state condition requires: $$\sum_{ions} zFJ_{ion} = 0$$ For our three ions (K⁺ with $z=+1$, Na⁺ with $z=+1$, Cl⁻ with $z=-1$): $$F(J_K + J_{Na}) - F(J_{Cl}) = 0$$ $$J_K + J_{Na} = J_{Cl}$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Substitute the Flux Expressions $$P_K \cdot \frac{F V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[K^+]_o - [K^+]_i e^{-FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-FV_m/RT}} + P_{Na} \cdot \frac{F V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[Na^+]_o - [Na^+]_i e^{-FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-FV_m/RT}} = -P_{Cl} \cdot \frac{(-F) V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[Cl^-]_o - [Cl^-]_i e^{FV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{FV_m/RT}}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Simplify the Algebra Multiplying both sides by $\frac{RT}{F V_m}$ and the common denominator $(1 - e^{-FV_m/RT})$: $$P_K([K^+]_o - [K^+]_i e^{-FV_m/RT}) + P_{Na}([Na^+]_o - [Na^+]_i e^{-FV_m/RT}) = P_{Cl}([Cl^-]_o - [Cl^-]_i e^{FV_m/RT})$$ ### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Group Exponential and Non-Exponential Terms **LHS expansion:** $$P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o - e^{-FV_m/RT}(P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i)$$ **RHS expansion:** $$P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o - e^{FV_m/RT} P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i$$ ### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Isolate the Exponential Terms Rearranging to collect terms with $e^{-FV_m/RT}$: $$P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o - P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o = e^{-FV_m/RT}(P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i) - e^{FV_m/RT} P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i$$ Multiplying both sides by $-e^{FV_m/RT}$: $$e^{FV_m/RT}(P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o - P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o) = -(P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i) + e^{2FV_m/RT} P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i$$ ### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Final Algebraic Manipulation (using the identity $e^{FV_m/RT} = e^{2FV_m/RT} \cdot e^{-FV_m/RT}$) After further algebraic manipulation (substituting $e^{2FV_m/RT}$ in terms of the target exponential): $$e^{FV_m/RT} = \frac{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}$$ ### 3.1.10 Step 10 — Take the Natural Logarithm Taking $\ln$ of both sides: $$\frac{FV_m}{RT} = \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}\right)$$ ### 3.1.11 Step 11 — Final GHK Voltage Equation Inverting the logarithm by negating the argument (and absorbing the negative sign): $$\boxed{V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Constant for KTU Exams:** At $T = 310$ K (37°C), the conversion $2.303 \cdot RT/F = 61.5$ mV allows the GHK equation to be rewritten with $\log_{10}$ for ease of calculation: $V_m (\text{mV}) = 61.5 \log_{10}(\text{ratio})$. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style) **Problem:** Calculate the resting membrane potential of a cardiac ventricular myocyte at 37°C, given: - $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM - $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 10$ mM - $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM - $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ ### Step 1: Identify the Constant At 37°C, $\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = 61.5$ mV ### Step 2: Compute the Numerator $$\text{Num} = P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i$$ $$\text{Num} = (1)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)$$ $$\text{Num} = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Denominator $$\text{Den} = P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o$$ $$\text{Den} = (1)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(120)$$ $$\text{Den} = 140 + 0.4 + 54 = 194.4$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Ratio $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{11.6}{194.4} = 0.05967$$ ### Step 5: Compute the Logarithm $$\log_{10}(0.05967) = -1.2242$$ ### Step 6: Final Calculation $$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.2242) = -75.3 \text{ mV}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The calculated $V_m \approx -75$ mV closely matches the experimentally measured resting potential of a **ventricular myocyte** (≈ -90 mV). The small discrepancy arises because the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** actively maintains ionic gradients, contributing an additional ~15 mV — the GHK equation accounts only for **passive** ionic flux. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Cardiac Conduction System Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SA Node
Sinoatrial Pacemaker
Rate 70 to 80 BPM"] --> B["Internodal Pathways
Anterior, Middle, Posterior"] B --> C["AV Node
Atrioventricular Delay
Rate 40 to 60 BPM
Delay 0.1 second"] C --> D["Bundle of His
AV Bundle Conduction"] D --> E1["Right Bundle Branch
Right Ventricle Path"] D --> E2["Left Bundle Branch
Left Ventricle Path"] E1 --> F1["Purkinje Fibers Right
Velocity 4 m per s"] E2 --> F2["Purkinje Fibers Left
Velocity 4 m per s"] F1 --> G["Ventricular Myocardium
Synchronous Contraction"] F2 --> G G --> H["Blood Ejection
Cardiac Output"] A -.->|Backup if SA fails| C C -.->|Backup if AV fails| D ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: GHK Equation Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Ion Concentrations
Inside and Outside Cell"] --> B["Permeability Coefficients
P_K, P_Na, P_Cl"] B --> C["Constant Field
Assumption Applied"] C --> D["Steady State
Net Current equals 0"] D --> E["Nernst Planck
Integration"] E --> F["GHK Voltage
Equation Output V_m"] G["Temperature T
310 Kelvin"] --> F H["Universal Constants
R and F"] --> F ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Pacemaker Action Potential vs. Ventricular Action Potential ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Phase Comparison of Cardiac APs"] --> B["SA Node / AV Node
Pacemaker Cells"] A --> C["Ventricular Myocyte
Contractile Cell"] B --> B1["Phase 4: Spontaneous
Slow Depolarization via If funny current"] B --> B2["Phase 0: Upstroke via
L type Calcium channels"] B --> B3["Phase 3: Repolarization via
K efflux"] C --> C1["Phase 4: True Resting
Stable at minus 90 mV"] C --> C2["Phase 0: Rapid Upstroke via
Fast Na influx"] C --> C3["Phase 1: Transient K efflux
Notch"] C --> C4["Phase 2: Plateau via
Ca influx balanced by K efflux"] C --> C5["Phase 3: Repolarization via
K efflux"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of Cardiac Excitation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Input["Electrical Origin"] I1["SA Node Autorhythmicity
Inherent Instability of Membrane"] end subgraph Atrial_Stage["Atrial Stage"] S1["Atrial Depolarization
P Wave on ECG"] S2["Internodal Conduction
Gap Junction Mediated"] end subgraph Delay_Stage["AV Delay Stage"] D1["AV Node Slow Conduction
0.05 m per s"] D2["Delay 0.1 second
Allows Atrial Kick"] end subgraph Ventricular_Stage["Ventricular Stage"] V1["His Bundle and Branches
2 m per s"] V2["Purkinje Network
4 m per s"] V3["Biventricular Contraction
QRS Complex on ECG"] end subgraph Output["Mechanical Output"] O1["Ventricular Ejection
Stroke Volume Output"] end I1 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V3 --> O1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model] **Define the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation. Why is it preferred over the Nernst equation for predicting the resting membrane potential of cardiac cells?** **Model Answer:** The GHK equation is defined as: $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$$ It is preferred over the Nernst equation for cardiac cells because: 1. **Multi-ion accounting:** The Nernst equation only considers a single ion in isolation. Cardiac cell membranes are **simultaneously permeable** to K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻, all of which contribute to V_m. 2. **Permeability weighting:** The GHK equation includes the **permeability coefficient (P)** for each ion, accurately reflecting how membrane selectivity changes the resting potential. 3. **Realistic prediction:** In a typical ventricular myocyte, the GHK equation yields V_m ≈ **-90 mV**, matching experimental values, while the Nernst equation for K⁺ alone would give ≈ -98 mV and for Na⁺ alone would give ≈ +60 mV — neither matching reality. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model] **List the components of the specialized excitatory and conductive system of the heart in the order of impulse conduction.** **Model Answer:** The order of impulse conduction in the specialized excitatory and conductive system of the heart is: 1. **Sinoatrial (SA) Node** — primary pacemaker 2. **Internodal Pathways** (anterior, middle, posterior) 3. **Atrioventricular (AV) Node** — provides 0.1 s delay 4. **Bundle of His** (AV bundle) 5. **Right and Left Bundle Branches** 6. **Purkinje Fibers** — distributed throughout ventricular myocardium 7. **Ventricular Contractile Myocytes** > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students often list the Bundle of His before the AV node. Remember: the **AV node is anatomically and functionally first** — the impulse reaches the AV node **before** entering the Bundle of His. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model] **Q. (a)** Derive the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation from the Nernst-Planck flux equation using the constant field assumption. State clearly all assumptions made. (7 Marks) **Q. (b)** The following data is given for a mammalian cardiac ventricular myocyte at 37°C: - $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 150$ mM - $[Na^+]_o = 140$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM - $[Cl^-]_o = 110$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 5$ mM - Permeability ratio: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.05 : 0.5$ Calculate the resting membrane potential using the GHK equation. Compare your result with the typical experimental value and explain any discrepancy. (7 Marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — Derivation of GHK Equation **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. The membrane has a **constant electric field** across its thickness (Goldman's constant field assumption). 2. The system is in **steady state** — net ionic current = 0 at resting potential. 3. The membrane is **homogeneous** — permeability is uniform across the membrane thickness. 4. Only **three ion species** contribute significantly: K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻. **Derivation (6 marks):** **Step 1:** Start with the Nernst-Planck flux for a single ion: $$J_{ion} = -D_{ion} \left( \frac{d[ion]}{dx} + \frac{zF[ion]}{RT} \frac{dV}{dx} \right)$$ **Step 2:** Apply the constant field assumption: $\frac{dV}{dx} = \frac{V_m}{d}$ and $\frac{d[ion]}{dx} = \frac{[ion]_o - [ion]_i}{d}$ **Step 3:** Substitute and define permeability $P_{ion} = \frac{D_{ion}}{d}$: $$J_{ion} = P_{ion} \cdot \frac{zF V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[ion]_o - [ion]_i e^{-zFV_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-zFV_m/RT}}$$ **Step 4:** Apply steady-state condition (net charge flux = 0): $$F(J_K + J_{Na}) = F(J_{Cl})$$ (using $z = +1$ for K⁺, Na⁺ and $z = -1$ for Cl⁻) **Step 5:** Substitute flux expressions, multiply by $\frac{RT}{FV_m}$, expand, group exponential terms, and isolate: $$\frac{FV_m}{RT} = \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Invert the logarithm (negate the argument): $$\boxed{V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation **Step 1:** Use the converted form at 37°C: $V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{Num}}{\text{Den}}\right)$ mV **Step 2:** Compute the numerator: $$\text{Num} = (1)(4) + (0.05)(140) + (0.5)(5) = 4 + 7 + 2.5 = 13.5$$ **Step 3:** Compute the denominator: $$\text{Den} = (1)(150) + (0.05)(12) + (0.5)(110) = 150 + 0.6 + 55 = 205.6$$ **Step 4:** Compute the ratio and logarithm: $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{13.5}{205.6} = 0.0657$$ $$\log_{10}(0.0657) = -1.1825$$ **Step 5:** Final calculation: $$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1825) = -72.7 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 6: Comparison and Discrepancy (1 mark):** - Calculated: **-72.7 mV** - Experimental: **-90 mV** - **Discrepancy: ~17 mV** — caused by the **active Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** which the GHK equation does not account for (the GHK equation models only passive ionic flux). The pump extrudes 3 Na⁺ out and brings 2 K⁺ in per cycle, generating a net **electrogenic current** that hyperpolarizes the membrane by ~10–20 mV. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model — Alternative] **Q. (a)** Describe in detail the specialized excitatory and conductive system of the heart. Include a labeled functional diagram and discuss the intrinsic pacemaker rates of each component. (7 Marks) **Q. (b)** Explain the AV nodal delay. Why is this delay physiologically essential? What would be the consequence if this delay were absent? Relate your answer to the standard ECG waveform. (7 Marks) --- ### Model Solution Outline for Question B: #### Part (a) — Specialized Conductive System (7 Marks) **1. SA Node (Sinoatrial Node) — 1.5 marks:** - Location: posterior wall of right atrium, near opening of superior vena cava - Cell type: small, spindle-shaped **autorhythmic** cells with fewer contractile filaments - Mechanism: spontaneous Phase-4 depolarization via **"funny" current (I_f)** — a slow inward Na⁺ leak activated upon hyperpolarization - Intrinsic rate: **70–80 BPM** (fastest in the system) - Function: **primary pacemaker** — sets the heart's sinus rhythm **2. AV Node (Atrioventricular Node) — 1.5 marks:** - Location: posterior interatrial septum, near tricuspid valve opening - Cells: small-diameter with **few gap junctions** → slow conduction - Intrinsic rate: **40–60 BPM** (secondary/backup pacemaker) - Function: introduces **0.1 second delay** to allow complete ventricular filling **3. Bundle of His, Bundle Branches, and Purkinje Fibers — 2 marks:** - Bundle of His: originates at AV node, descends through interventricular septum - Splits into **right bundle branch** (to right ventricle) and **left bundle branch** (to left ventricle) - Bundle branches give rise to **Purkinje fibers** — the **largest cardiac cells**, with abundant gap junctions - Intrinsic rate: **25–40 BPM** (tertiary/backup pacemaker) - Conduction velocity in Purkinje fibers: **4 m/s** (fastest in heart) **4. Diagram Requirement (2 marks):** Must include a **flow diagram** showing SA → AV → His → Bundle Branches → Purkinje → Ventricles with rate annotations. --- #### Part (b) — AV Nodal Delay and ECG Correlation (7 Marks) **1. The Delay Mechanism (2 marks):** - AV nodal cells have **small diameter** → high internal resistance - AV nodal cells have **few gap junctions** → limited current flow - AV nodal cells lack fast Na⁺ channels — depolarization is mediated by **slow L-type Ca²⁺ channels** → slower upstroke velocity - Result: conduction velocity ≈ **0.05 m/s** (the **slowest** in the cardiac conduction system) **2. Physiological Importance (3 marks):** - The 0.1 s delay allows the **atria to fully contract and empty** their blood into the ventricles **before** ventricular contraction begins - This is called the **"atrial kick"** — it contributes ~**30% of ventricular end-diastolic volume** - Without this delay, the atria and ventricles would contract **simultaneously**, severely reducing cardiac output and causing ineffective pumping **3. ECG Correlation (2 marks):** - On the ECG, the AV nodal delay is represented by the **PR segment** (or the isoelectric portion between P wave and QRS complex) - **P wave** = atrial depolarization - **PR interval** (0.12–0.20 s normal) = conduction time from atria to ventricles, including AV nodal delay - **QRS complex** = ventricular depolarization - A **prolonged PR interval (>0.20 s)** indicates **first-degree AV block** — a clinical sign of AV nodal dysfunction - **Absent AV delay** would lead to merged P-QRS waves, indistinguishable atrial and ventricular activity, and drastically reduced cardiac output --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Forgetting to invert the Cl⁻ gradient:** In the GHK equation, $[Cl^-]_i$ goes in the **numerator** and $[Cl^-]_o$ in the **denominator** (opposite of cations). Students often place Cl⁻ the same way as cations, losing **3–4 marks instantly**. > > 2. **Using the wrong temperature constant:** At 37°C, the constant is **61.5 mV** (with $\log_{10}$), not 58 mV (which is for 20°C) or 26.7 mV (which is RT/F, the natural log constant). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Skipping the "active pump" explanation:** When asked to compare GHK-predicted V_m with experimental values, **always mention the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump's electrogenic contribution** (~10–20 mV). Examiners specifically look for this. Skipping it = -2 marks. > > 4. **Confusing the conduction velocities:** Purkinje fibers are the **fastest** (4 m/s); AV node is the **slowest** (0.05 m/s). Students frequently reverse these. Memorize the descending order: **Purkinje > Atrial muscle > Ventricular muscle > AV node**. > > 5. **Missing the constant field assumption:** A derivation question without explicitly stating the constant field assumption will lose **1 mark minimum** in KTU evaluations. Always state assumptions before starting the math. > > 6. **Incorrect units or no units:** Always state V_m in **mV** (not V) and concentrations in **mM** for clarity. Examiners penalize sloppy unit handling. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist #### Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation - ⭐ The GHK equation predicts resting membrane potential (V_m) in **multi-ion systems** - ⭐ The master formula is: $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ - ⭐ At 37°C: use **61.5 mV** with **log₁₀**; or **26.7 mV** with **ln** - ⭐ **Cl⁻ is placed oppositely** to cations in the equation (its concentration terms are swapped between numerator and denominator) - ⭐ The **constant field assumption** is the key mathematical simplification - ⭐ GHK accounts only for **passive** ionic flux — discrepancy with experiment explained by the **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** - ⭐ Typical cardiac ventricular V_m ≈ **-90 mV** (experimentally), ≈ -75 mV (GHK calculation) - ⭐ Permeability ratio at rest: $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} \approx 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ #### Specialized Excitatory and Conductive System of Heart - ⭐ The heart is **myogenic** — it generates its own electrical impulses (autorhythmic) - ⭐ Five components in order: **SA Node → AV Node → Bundle of His → Bundle Branches → Purkinje Fibers** - ⭐ **SA node** is the primary pacemaker (70–80 BPM); **AV node** is the backup (40–60 BPM) - ⭐ **Purkinje fibers** are the **fastest** conductors (4 m/s); **AV node** is the **slowest** (0.05 m/s) - ⭐ The **AV nodal delay (0.1 s)** is critical for the "atrial kick" — it allows ventricular filling - ⭐ On the ECG: **P wave = atrial depolarization; PR interval = AV nodal delay; QRS = ventricular depolarization; T wave = ventricular repolarization** - ⭐ Pacemaker cells lack a true resting potential — they have **Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization** via the **"funny" current (I_f)** - ⭐ Ventricular myocytes have a **5-phase action potential** (Phases 0–4) with a characteristic **plateau (Phase 2)** mediated by Ca²⁺ influx - ⭐ **"Funny current" (I_f)** is a mixed Na⁺/K⁺ inward current activated by hyperpolarization — it is the molecular basis of cardiac automaticity - ⭐ **Ectopic pacemaker:** Any non-SA node region generating impulses abnormally → arrhythmias > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** For Module 1 of Biosignals & Signal Processing, master the GHK equation numerically (expect computational problems in ESE) and conceptually (expect theory questions on the cardiac conduction system). The two are interconnected: both deal with bioelectric signal generation at the cellular and organ level.

topics.title: Rhythmicity - Ionic Basis of Cardiac Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rhythmicity - Ionic Basis of Cardiac Action Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cardiac Action Potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, regenerative change in the transmembrane electrical potential of a cardiac myocyte, characterized by five distinct phases (0–4), governed by the coordinated opening and closing of **voltage-gated ion channels** and the electrogenic activity of membrane pumps and exchangers. Unlike skeletal muscle or nerve, the cardiac AP exhibits a characteristic **prolonged plateau phase (Phase 2)** that is essential for the refractory period preventing tetanic contraction.

**Rhythmicity (Automaticity)** is the inherent ability of certain specialized cardiac cells (pacemaker cells of the SA node, AV node, and Purkinje system) to spontaneously generate rhythmic action potentials without external neural or hormonal input, due to a **slow diastolic depolarization (Phase 4 pacemaker potential)** that gradually brings the membrane potential to threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cardiac action potentials are the bioelectric foundation of the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)**. The sequence of depolarization and repolarization across the myocardium produces the P-QRS-T waveform. Understanding ionic currents is mandatory for analyzing arrhythmias, pacemaker design, and biopotential amplifiers used in patient monitoring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank on a hill (the ion gradient)** with a gate at the bottom (the ion channel). When the gate is opened, water rushes out (ions flow down their electrochemical gradient), and the water level in a lower basin (the cell interior) rises. Cardiac cells have *several* gates, each for a different ion (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺), and they open and close in a carefully choreographed sequence — this dance is the **action potential**.

For **rhythmicity**, picture a **leaky bucket (the pacemaker cell)**. Water is constantly trickling in (slow Na⁺ "funny" current). When the water level reaches a critical height (threshold ≈ **−40 mV**), an automatic valve opens and dumps the water out (depolarization via Ca²⁺). Then the bucket resets. The *rate* at which water leaks in determines how fast the bucket fills — this is exactly how the **SA node sets the heart rate at ~70–80 beats per minute**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Cardiac Metrics to Remember (Body Temperature 37 °C):**
> - Resting Membrane Potential (Ventricular myocyte): **−90 mV**
> - Threshold (Ventricular myocyte): **−70 mV**
> - Threshold (SA node): **−40 mV**
> - Overshoot: **+20 to +30 mV**
> - Total AP duration (ventricular): **200–300 ms**
> - Heart rate (SA node intrinsic): **70–80 bpm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of the Non-Pacemaker (Ventricular) Action Potential

The ventricular myocyte is the *workhorse* cell — it contracts forcefully but does not generate its own rhythm. Its action potential is divided into five phases:

- **Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization:**
  - *Trigger:* A neighboring cell depolarizes, bringing the local membrane potential to threshold (~−70 mV).
  - *Mechanism:* Voltage-gated **fast Na⁺ channels** open → massive Na⁺ influx down its steep electrochemical gradient.
  - *Result:* Membrane potential rapidly rises from −90 mV to about **+30 mV** in < 1 ms.
  - *Equation:* $I_{Na} = g_{Na}(V_m - E_{Na})$ where $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.

- **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization:**
  - *Mechanism:* Na⁺ channels inactivate (fast inactivation gate closes); a **transient outward K⁺ current (I_to)** opens.
  - *Result:* Brief repolarization "notch" from +30 mV back toward 0 mV.

- **Phase 2 — Plateau Phase (the cardiac signature):**
  - *Mechanism:* A balance between **inward Ca²⁺ current** through L-type (long-lasting) Ca²⁺ channels and **outward K⁺ current** (delayed rectifier, $I_K$).
  - *Result:* Membrane potential is held near 0 mV for ~200 ms — this prolonged plateau is what gives cardiac muscle its **long refractory period**, preventing tetanus and ensuring proper ventricular filling.

- **Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarization:**
  - *Mechanism:* L-type Ca²⁺ channels close; **delayed rectifier K⁺ channels (I_Kr and I_Ks)** remain open → strong K⁺ efflux.
  - *Result:* Membrane rapidly returns toward the K⁺ equilibrium potential.

- **Phase 4 — Resting Membrane Potential:**
  - *Mechanism:* Dominated by **inward rectifier K⁺ channels (I_K1)** which keep the membrane highly permeable to K⁺.
  - *Result:* Stable resting potential at **−90 mV**, maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, uses 1 ATP).

### 2.2 The Pacemaker (SA Node) Action Potential

The SA node cell is fundamentally different — it has **no true Phase 1 or Phase 2** and lacks a stable Phase 4. Instead, Phase 4 exhibits **spontaneous diastolic depolarization**, the cornerstone of rhythmicity:

- **Phase 4 — Pacemaker Potential (Slope of "Funny Current"):**
  - *Mechanism:* Three currents work together:
    1. **"Funny" current (I_f):** Inward Na⁺ current through HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated) channels — *this is the dominant pacemaker current*.
    2. **T-type Ca²⁺ current (I_Ca,T):** Activated late in Phase 4.
    3. **Decaying K⁺ efflux (I_K):** Outward K⁺ current progressively decreases.
  - *Result:* Membrane slowly drifts from maximum diastolic potential (~−60 mV) to threshold (~−40 mV).

- **Phase 0 — Depolarization (Ca²⁺-driven, NOT Na⁺-driven):**
  - *Mechanism:* SA node cells lack functional fast Na⁺ channels. Depolarization is mediated by **L-type Ca²⁺ channels**.
  - *Result:* Slower upstroke (~10 V/s vs ~200 V/s in ventricular cells).

- **Phase 3 — Repolarization:**
  - *Mechanism:* L-type Ca²⁺ channels close; delayed rectifier K⁺ channels open.

### 2.3 The "Funny Current" (I_f) — Molecular Basis of Rhythmicity

The HCN channel is gated by hyperpolarization (unusual — most channels open on depolarization) and is directly modulated by **cyclic AMP (cAMP)**. This provides a beautiful link to autonomic control:
- **Sympathetic (β₁) stimulation → ↑ cAMP → ↑ I_f → steeper Phase 4 slope → faster heart rate (positive chronotropy)**
- **Parasympathetic (M₂) stimulation → ↓ cAMP → ↓ I_f → flatter Phase 4 slope → slower heart rate (negative chronotropy)**

### 2.4 Hierarchy of Cardiac Pacemakers (Why SA Node Dominates)

Different parts of the conduction system have different intrinsic rates:

| Pacemaker Site | Intrinsic Rate (bpm) | Position in Hierarchy |
|---|---|---|
| **SA Node** | **70–80** | Primary (Normal Sinus Rhythm) |
| AV Node | 40–60 | Secondary (escape rhythm) |
| Bundle of His / Purkinje | 20–40 | Tertiary (idioventricular) |

The SA node fires fastest → it depolarizes subsidiary pacemakers *before* they can reach threshold on their own. This is called **"Overdrive Suppression"** — faster pacemakers continuously suppress slower ones.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Nernst Equation (general) | $E_X = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Equilibrium potential of ion X |
| Nernst at 37 °C | $E_X = \dfrac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Quick numerical eval (mV) |
| Na⁺ Nernst Potential | $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV | Phase 0 overshoot target |
| K⁺ Nernst Potential | $E_K \approx -94$ mV | Sets resting potential |
| Ca²⁺ Nernst Potential | $E_{Ca} \approx +130$ mV | Drives Phase 2 plateau |
| Chord Conductance Eq. | $I_X = g_X (V_m - E_X)$ | Single-channel current |
| GHK Voltage Equation | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Multi-ion resting potential |
| Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP | Electrogenic (net −1 charge) |
| Na⁺/Ca²⁺ Exchanger | 3 Na⁺ in : 1 Ca²⁺ out | Ca²⁺ removal during diastole |
| Heart Rate (BPM) | $HR = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} \times 60$ | From AP cycle duration |
| Refractory Period | $ERP \approx$ Plateau (Phase 2) duration | Prevents re-entry & tetanus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Understanding the Nernst and GHK equations is critical for designing:
> - **Pacemakers** (electronic implantable devices that mimic SA node I_f)
> - **ECG amplifiers** (high-input-impedance to avoid loading the ~mV bioelectric signal)
> - **Anti-arrhythmic drug classification** (Class I = Na⁺ channel blockers, Class II = β-blockers, Class III = K⁺ channel blockers, Class IV = Ca²⁺ channel blockers — Vaughan-Williams classification)
> - **Defibrillators** (reset all cardiac cells simultaneously by depolarizing the entire myocardium)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation from First Principles

The Nernst equation is derived from the equality of the **electrochemical potential** of an ion on both sides of a permeable membrane. The work done moving 1 mole of ions across the membrane by diffusion equals the work done by the electric field.

The chemical potential difference (per mole) for moving ion X from outside to inside:

$$
\Delta \mu_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}
$$

The electrical work moving 1 mole of ions with valence $z$ across a potential difference $(V_{in} - V_{out})$:

$$
\Delta \mu_{elec} = zF(V_{in} - V_{out})
$$

At equilibrium, the **net electrochemical potential** is zero, so $\Delta\mu_{chem} + \Delta\mu_{elec} = 0$:

$$
RT \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}} + zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = 0
$$

Solve for the equilibrium potential $E_X = V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$
zF \cdot E_X = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}
$$

$$
E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}
$$

Switching to base-10 logarithm using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$
E_X = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}
$$

Substituting constants at $T = 310$ K (37 °C): $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol:

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \frac{5935.5}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}
$$

Therefore the **practical Nernst equation at body temperature** is:

$$
E_X = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Calculating E_K for a Cardiac Cell

**Given typical cardiomyocyte ion concentrations:**

- Intracellular K⁺: $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM
- Extracellular K⁺: $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM
- Valence $z = +1$

**Step 1 — Identify the ratio:**

$$
\frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}} = \frac{4}{140} = 0.02857
$$

**Step 2 — Take the base-10 logarithm:**

$$
\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.544
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nernst equation:**

$$
E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \times (-1.544) = -94.95 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The K⁺ equilibrium potential is approximately **−95 mV**, which closely matches the observed resting membrane potential of **−90 mV** of a ventricular myocyte. The small discrepancy (5 mV) is due to small but non-zero permeabilities to Na⁺ and Cl⁻, accounted for by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Using GHK to Predict V_rest

**Given:**
- $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM
- $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM
- $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM, $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM
- Relative permeabilities: $P_K = 1.0$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$

**Step 1 — Numerator (extracellular side weighted by permeability):**

$$
P_K[K^+]_o = 1.0 \times 4 = 4
$$

$$
P_{Na}[Na^+]_o = 0.04 \times 145 = 5.8
$$

$$
P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i = 0.45 \times 4 = 1.8
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator (intracellular side weighted by permeability):**

$$
P_K[K^+]_i = 1.0 \times 140 = 140
$$

$$
P_{Na}[Na^+]_i = 0.04 \times 12 = 0.48
$$

$$
P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o = 0.45 \times 120 = 54
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 140 + 0.48 + 54 = 194.48
$$

**Step 3 — Compute log ratio and multiply by 61.5 mV:**

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{11.6}{194.48}\right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.05965)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(0.05965) = -1.2244
$$

$$
V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.2244) = -75.3 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Reality check:**

The GHK equation predicts ≈ **−75 mV**, closer to reality than pure Nernst for K⁺, but the *actual* ventricular resting potential of −90 mV is even more negative. This remaining discrepancy in cardiac cells is because the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump is electrogenic** (net exports 1 positive charge per cycle), pushing V_m closer to E_K.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pacemaker vs Non-Pacemaker

| Feature | SA Node (Pacemaker) | Ventricular (Non-Pacemaker) |
|---|---|---|
| Resting Phase 4 | Unstable, spontaneous depolarization | Stable at −90 mV |
| Phase 4 slope | Present (drives automaticity) | Absent |
| Phase 0 upstroke | Ca²⁺ (L-type) — slow | Na⁺ (fast) — very fast |
| Upstroke velocity | ~10 V/s | ~200–500 V/s |
| Overshoot | Lower (~+10 mV) | Higher (~+30 mV) |
| Plateau (Phase 2) | Absent | Prominent (~200 ms) |
| Threshold | ~−40 mV | ~−70 mV |
| Max diastolic potential | ~−60 mV | ~−90 mV |
| Fast Na⁺ channels | Few/None | Abundant |
| L-type Ca²⁺ channels | Present (T-type + L-type) | Present (L-type only) |
| HCN (funny) channels | Abundant | Sparse |
| Function | Initiate heartbeat | Pump blood |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Cardiac Conduction System & Pacemaker Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SA Node - 70 to 80 bpm Primary Pacemaker] -->|Internodal Tracts| B[AV Node - 40 to 60 bpm Secondary]
    B -->|Bundle of His| C[Bundle Branches Left and Right]
    C -->|Purkinje Fibers| D[Ventricular Myocardium 20 to 40 bpm Tertiary]
    A --> E[Phase 4 Funny Current If drives automaticity]
    E --> F[Threshold -40 mV reached]
    F --> G[L type Ca2+ Phase 0 Depolarization]
    G --> H[K+ Efflux Phase 3 Repolarization]
    H --> I[Cycle Repeats]
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ffa94d,color:#000000
    style D fill:#74c0fc,color:#000000
    style E fill:#b197fc,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Ionic Architecture of a Cardiac Action Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase4[Phase 4 Resting or Pacemaker]
        P4A[K1 Inward Rectifier K+ Channel] --> Vm1
        P4B[HCN Funny Channel If] --> Vm1
        P4C[T type Ca2+ Channel] --> Vm1
        P4D[Decreasing K+ Efflux] --> Vm1
    end
    subgraph Phase0[Phase 0 Rapid Depolarization]
        N1[Fast Voltage Gated Na+ Channel] --> Vm2
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2 Plateau]
        CA1[L type Ca2+ Channel] --> Vm3
        K1A[Delayed Rectifier K+] --> Vm3
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3 Repolarization]
        K2[Delayed Rectifier K+ Open] --> Vm4
    end
    Vm1[Vm -90 mV Ventricle or -60 mV SA] --> N1
    N1 --> Vm2[Vm plus 30 mV]
    Vm2 --> CA1
    CA1 --> Vm3[Vm near 0 mV]
    Vm3 --> K2
    K2 --> Vm4[Vm -90 mV Restored]
    style Vm1 fill:#ffe066
    style Vm2 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style Vm3 fill:#ffa94d
    style Vm4 fill:#ffe066
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Autonomic Modulation of Pacemaker Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SNS[Sympathetic Stimulation Beta1] --> cAMPup[cAMP Increases]
    PSNS[Parasympathetic Stimulation M2] --> cAMPdown[cAMP Decreases]
    cAMPup --> Ifup[Funny Current If Increases]
    cAMPdown --> Ifdown[Funny Current If Decreases]
    Ifup --> HRup[Heart Rate Increases Positive Chronotropy]
    Ifdown --> HRdown[Heart Rate Decreases Negative Chronotropy]
    HRup --> Out1[Pacemaker Slope Steepens]
    HRdown --> Out2[Pacemaker Slope Flattens]
    style SNS fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style PSNS fill:#74c0fc,color:#000000
    style HRup fill:#51cf66
    style HRdown fill:#fab005
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the phases of a ventricular myocyte action potential and identify the ionic basis of each phase.*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

1. **Phase 0 (Rapid Depolarization):** Voltage-gated **fast Na⁺ channels** open → massive Na⁺ influx → V_m rises from −90 mV to +30 mV.
2. **Phase 1 (Initial Repolarization):** Na⁺ channels inactivate; **transient outward K⁺ current (I_to)** produces a brief repolarization notch.
3. **Phase 2 (Plateau):** **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** allow sustained Ca²⁺ influx, balanced by **delayed rectifier K⁺ efflux** → V_m held near 0 mV for ~200 ms.
4. **Phase 3 (Rapid Repolarization):** Ca²⁺ channels close; K⁺ efflux dominates → V_m returns to −90 mV.
5. **Phase 4 (Resting Potential):** **Inward rectifier K⁺ channels (I_K1)** maintain high K⁺ permeability; **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** sustains ionic gradients.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the "funny current" (I_f)? Explain its role in cardiac rhythmicity and autonomic modulation.*

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

- The "funny current" (**I_f**) is an **inward Na⁺ current** carried by **HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated Cyclic Nucleotide-gated) channels** that is *activated by hyperpolarization* (atypical).
- It is the **dominant depolarizing current during Phase 4 of the SA node action potential**, producing the slow diastolic depolarization that drives **automaticity**.
- The slope of Phase 4 (rate of I_f-driven depolarization) directly determines **heart rate** — steeper slope = faster rate.
- **Sympathetic activation** (β₁ receptors) → ↑ adenylyl cyclase → **↑ cAMP** → ↑ I_f → ↑ heart rate (**positive chronotropy**).
- **Parasympathetic activation** (M₂ receptors) → ↓ cAMP → ↓ I_f → ↓ heart rate (**negative chronotropy**).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **ionic basis of all five phases of the ventricular cardiac action potential**. Compare and contrast it with the **SA node action potential**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of rhythmicity** in the SA node, with emphasis on the role of the funny current, T-type and L-type Ca²⁺ channels, and the modulation by the autonomic nervous system.

#### Model Solution A(a) — Phases of Ventricular AP and Comparison with SA Node (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** State the five phases and their voltage ranges:
- Phase 4: −90 mV
- Phase 0: −90 mV → +30 mV (rapid upstroke)
- Phase 1: +30 mV → 0 mV (notch)
- Phase 2: 0 mV plateau (~200 ms)
- Phase 3: 0 mV → −90 mV

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Ionic basis of each phase (already detailed in §2.1):
- Phase 0: Fast Na⁺ influx via voltage-gated Na⁺ channels (g_Na increases 600-fold).
- Phase 1: Na⁺ inactivation + I_to (transient outward K⁺).
- Phase 2: L-type Ca²⁺ current (I_Ca-L) balanced by I_Kr and I_Ks.
- Phase 3: I_Kr and I_Ks dominate; L-type Ca²⁺ closes.
- Phase 4: I_K1 (inward rectifier K⁺) + electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase (3:2 stoichiometry).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compare with SA node AP:
- No stable Phase 4; spontaneous diastolic depolarization.
- No Phase 1/2 plateau; briefer AP.
- Phase 0 driven by **L-type Ca²⁺** (not Na⁺) → slower upstroke (~10 V/s vs ~200 V/s).
- Threshold is more positive (~−40 mV vs ~−70 mV).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Functional significance — ventricular AP allows strong, sustained contraction; SA node AP allows rapid repetitive firing.

#### Model Solution A(b) — Mechanism of Rhythmicity (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define rhythmicity / automaticity — the ability of pacemaker cells to fire spontaneously without external input, due to **Phase 4 spontaneous depolarization**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Three ionic currents producing Phase 4 depolarization:
1. **"Funny" current (I_f):** Inward Na⁺ through HCN channels — the primary pacemaker current; activated at hyperpolarized potentials.
2. **T-type Ca²⁺ current (I_Ca,T):** Activated late in Phase 4; contributes the latter portion of diastolic depolarization.
3. **Decaying I_K (delayed rectifier K⁺):** Outward K⁺ current progressively decreases during Phase 4, allowing net inward current.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The slope of Phase 4 → threshold (−40 mV) determines heart rate. Threshold triggers L-type Ca²⁺ channels → Phase 0 depolarization.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Autonomic modulation:
- Sympathetic (β₁ → ↑ cAMP → ↑ I_f) → steeper slope → ↑ HR.
- Parasympathetic (M₂ → ↓ cAMP → ↓ I_f) → flatter slope → ↓ HR.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Nernst equation** starting from electrochemical equilibrium. Hence calculate the **equilibrium potential for K⁺** in a cardiac cell, given $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM at 37 °C.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **hierarchy of cardiac pacemakers** and the phenomenon of **overdrive suppression**. Why is the SA node the *normal* pacemaker of the heart?

#### Model Solution B(a) — Nernst Derivation + E_K Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Full derivation as in §3.1:
- Chemical potential: $\Delta \mu_{chem} = RT \ln([X]_{in}/[X]_{out})$
- Electrical potential: $\Delta \mu_{elec} = zF(V_{in} - V_{out})$
- At equilibrium, $\Delta\mu_{chem} + \Delta\mu_{elec} = 0$
- Solve → $E_X = (RT/zF) \ln([X]_{out}/[X]_{in})$
- At 37 °C → $E_X = (61.5/z) \log_{10}([X]_{out}/[X]_{in})$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute values for K⁺:
- $z = +1$
- $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Calculate:
- Ratio: 4/140 = 0.02857
- $\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.544$
- $E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.544) = -94.95$ mV

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Interpretation:
- $E_K \approx -95$ mV is close to the ventricular resting potential (−90 mV).
- The 5 mV difference is due to small but finite Na⁺ permeability (corrected by GHK equation) and the electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump.

#### Model Solution B(b) — Pacemaker Hierarchy and Overdrive Suppression (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the hierarchy and intrinsic rates:
- SA Node: 70–80 bpm
- AV Node: 40–60 bpm
- Bundle of His / Purkinje: 20–40 bpm
- Ventricular myocytes: do not normally fire spontaneously

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain why SA node dominates:
- It has the **fastest intrinsic rate** of spontaneous Phase 4 depolarization (largest I_f, steepest Phase 4 slope).
- It depolarizes *first* → spreads the action potential through the conduction system to the AV node and ventricles *before* the slower pacemakers can reach their own threshold.
- Result: **SA node sets the heart rhythm (Normal Sinus Rhythm)**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Overdrive Suppression mechanism:
- A pacemaker that has just fired is in its **refractory period** and has accumulated intracellular Na⁺ (reduced Na⁺ gradient).
- The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** must work harder to restore gradients → generates a hyperpolarizing current that **suppresses** spontaneous firing.
- If the SA node is suddenly blocked (e.g., SA exit block), the AV node "escapes" at its slower intrinsic rate (40–60 bpm) — this is the **escape rhythm**.
- The faster a pacemaker fires (overdriven), the more strongly it suppresses subsidiary pacemakers.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Clinical correlation:
- If SA node fails → AV nodal escape rhythm (40–60 bpm) — patient may be symptomatic but stable.
- If both SA and AV fail → idioventricular rhythm (20–40 bpm) — life-threatening, requires immediate pacing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write "Phase 0 of SA node is due to Na⁺ influx" — **WRONG.** The SA node has very few fast Na⁺ channels. Phase 0 of the SA node is **exclusively due to L-type Ca²⁺ channels**. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the equilibrium potential with the resting potential. Nernst gives $E_K = -95$ mV, but actual V_m = −90 mV because of small Na⁺ permeability (use GHK, not Nernst, for the real V_m).
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Saying "the funny current is carried by K⁺" — **WRONG.** I_f is an **inward Na⁺ current** (with some K⁺ component) through HCN channels.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that the **plateau (Phase 2)** is what gives the cardiac AP its long refractory period. Without it, the heart could tetanize — this is a high-yield clinical point.
> 5. **Mark Loss Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always include a **neat labeled diagram** of the action potential. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the sketch. Even a hand-drawn version uploaded in the answer script counts.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Five Phases of Ventricular AP:** Phase 0 (fast Na⁺), Phase 1 (I_to), Phase 2 (L-type Ca²⁺ + K⁺ = plateau), Phase 3 (K⁺ efflux), Phase 4 (K⁺ leak + Na⁺/K⁺ pump = −90 mV).
- ✅ **SA Node AP is fundamentally different:** No stable Phase 4, no plateau, **Ca²⁺-driven Phase 0**, **I_f-driven Phase 4**.
- ✅ **Funny Current (I_f):** Inward Na⁺ through HCN channels; *the molecular clock of the heart*; modulated by cAMP and autonomic nervous system.
- ✅ **Nernst Equation at 37 °C:** $E_X = (61.5/z) \log_{10}([X]_{out}/[X]_{in})$ mV.
- ✅ **Typical Nernst potentials:** $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV, $E_K \approx -95$ mV, $E_{Ca} \approx +130$ mV.
- ✅ **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase:** 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in — electrogenic, consumes ATP, maintains gradients long-term.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** SA (70–80) > AV (40–60) > Purkinje (20–40) bpm.
- ✅ **Overdrive Suppression:** Faster pacemakers continuously suppress slower ones; explains escape rhythms when SA node fails.
- ✅ **Autonomic Effects:** Sympathetic ↑ HR (↑ I_f via ↑ cAMP); Parasympathetic ↓ HR (↓ I_f via ↓ cAMP).
- ✅ **Plateau = Long Refractory Period = No Tetanus** (this is the critical physiological advantage of cardiac vs skeletal muscle).
- ✅ **Vaughan-Williams Anti-arrhythmic Classes** to remember: Class I (Na⁺ block), II (β-block), III (K⁺ block), IV (Ca²⁺ block).
- ✅ **ECG Correlation:** P wave = atrial depolarization, QRS = ventricular depolarization, T wave = ventricular repolarization. The long plateau prevents re-excitation during the QRS → ST segment.
- ✅ **Engineering Hooks:** Pacemaker design mimics I_f; defibrillators reset all cells; ECG amplifiers need high input impedance (>10 MΩ) and high CMRR (>80 dB) to faithfully capture these mV-level bioelectric signals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plateau Region
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plateau Region

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plateau Region (Phase 2 of Cardiac Action Potential):** The plateau region is the prolonged, relatively stable depolarization phase of the cardiac ventricular myocyte action potential, during which the membrane potential is sustained at approximately **0 mV to +20 mV** for an extended duration of **200–300 ms**. This phase is characterized by a dynamic equilibrium between the **inward L-type Calcium (Ca²⁺) current ($I_{Ca-L}$)** and the **outward delayed-rectifier Potassium (K⁺) current ($I_K$)**, producing an almost flat segment on the voltage-time plot.

In the context of **Biosignals & Signal Processing (OEEBT613)**, the plateau region is the single most critical morphological feature that distinguishes a **cardiac action potential (AP)** from a **neural action potential**, and it is the direct physiological origin of the **refractory period** that protects the heart from re-entrant arrhythmias.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Balloon-Deflating Analogy":** Imagine inflating a balloon (depolarization/Phase 0) and then putting your finger over the opening so the air leaks out very, very slowly. The balloon stays mostly inflated for a long time before it finally deflates (repolarization/Phase 3). That "stays mostly inflated" period is the **plateau**.
>
> In the heart, the *finger* is the **L-type Calcium channel** — it keeps pushing Ca²⁺ *in* (inflating the cell electrically) just enough to balance the K⁺ *leaking out* (deflating), so the voltage remains almost constant. Without this plateau, the heart would beat so fast it could not pump blood.

### 1.3 Standard Quantitative Metrics

| Metric | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Plateau voltage level | **≈ 0 mV to +20 mV** |
| Plateau duration (human ventricle) | **≈ 200 – 300 ms** |
| Inward current carrier (plateau) | **Ca²⁺ via L-type channels** |
| Outward current carrier (plateau) | **K⁺ via delayed-rectifier channels** |
| Driving force for Ca²⁺ at plateau | **≈ –60 mV (huge)** |
| Equilibrium potential for Ca²⁺ ($E_{Ca}$) | **≈ +120 mV** |
| Equilibrium potential for K⁺ ($E_K$) | **≈ –94 mV** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of a Ventricular Action Potential (Context Setting)

Before analyzing the plateau, we must situate it within the full action-potential timeline:

- **Phase 4 — Resting Potential (≈ –90 mV):** Dominated by the inward-rectifier K⁺ current $I_{K1}$. The cell is electrically quiet and mechanically relaxed (diastole).
- **Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization (–90 mV → +30 mV):** Fast voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels** open, producing a massive inward $I_{Na}$. The membrane "all-or-none" fires upward.
- **Phase 1 — Initial (Notch) Repolarization:** Na⁺ channels inactivate. A transient outward K⁺ current $I_{to}$ briefly pulls the voltage down. This is the small dip you see before the plateau.
- **Phase 2 — Plateau Region (≈ 0 mV, 200–300 ms):** The **key phase**. L-type Ca²⁺ channels open slowly, sustaining depolarization. Outward K⁺ currents balance inward Ca²⁺ currents. The membrane voltage is *almost constant*. This long depolarization drives **Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR)** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum → cardiac contraction.
- **Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarization (+20 mV → –90 mV):** L-type Ca²⁺ channels close (Ca²⁺- and voltage-dependent inactivation). Delayed-rectifier K⁺ currents $I_{Kr}$ and $I_{Ks}$ dominate, returning the cell to rest.

### 2.2 Why Does the Plateau Exist? — The Ionic Balance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The plateau is a **zero-net-current steady state**. At any instant on the plateau, the **algebraic sum of all ionic currents equals zero**:
>
> $$I_{Ca-L} + I_{Na\,(late)} + I_{Na/Ca} + I_K + I_{K1} + I_{to} + I_{pump} + I_{background} = 0$$
>
> During the plateau, the dominant inward current is **$I_{Ca-L}$**, and it is precisely offset by the dominant outward currents $I_K + I_{K1}$.

### 2.3 The Role of L-type Calcium Channels

L-type Ca²⁺ channels (DHPRs — Dihydropyridine Receptors) have three properties critical to the plateau:

1. **Slow activation kinetics** — they open 10–20 ms after the upstroke, reaching peak conductance around the start of the plateau.
2. **Long open time** — they remain open for hundreds of milliseconds.
3. **Ca²⁺-dependent inactivation** — the rising cytosolic [Ca²⁺] gradually inactivates the channel, eventually terminating the plateau.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS FOR KTU EXAMS — MEMORIZE THESE**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$$ | Ohmic ionic current (linearized) | $g$ = conductance (S), $E$ = Nernst potential (V), $V_m$ = membrane voltage (V) |
| 2 | $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[ion]_o}{[ion]_i}$$ | Nernst (reversal) potential | $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temp (K), $F$ = 96485 C/mol, $z$ = valence |
| 3 | $$V_m = \frac{\sum P_i [ion]_i}{\sum P_j [ion]_j}$$ | Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz voltage equation | $P$ = permeability (m/s), concentrations in mM |
| 4 | $$\tau_m \frac{dm}{dt} = m_{\infty}(V_m) - m$$ | Hodgkin–Huxley gating variable ODE | $\tau_m$ = time constant (s), $m$ = activation gate (0→1) |
| 5 | $$I_{Ca-L} = \bar{g}_{Ca} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot (V_m - E_{Ca})$$ | L-type Ca²⁺ current | $d$ = activation gate, $f$ = inactivation gate |
| 6 | $$\sum_k I_k = 0 \quad \text{(at plateau)}$$ | Steady-state current balance on plateau | Used to derive plateau voltage |
| 7 | $$E_{Ca} \approx +120 \text{ mV},\ E_K \approx -94 \text{ mV}$$ | Standard cardiac ion reversal potentials | At 37 °C, physiological concentrations |
| 8 | $$\text{APD}_{90} = t_{V_m \to -90\%\text{ of repol.}}$$ | Action Potential Duration (clinical metric) | Used to assess plateau length |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Pitfall:** When writing the Nernst or GHK equations in your answer script, do **not** use the symbol `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table — it breaks the rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The plateau region is not just a biological curiosity — it has direct engineering relevance:

- **ECG Signal Processing:** The QT interval on an ECG reflects the total action potential duration (Phases 0–3), and the **ST segment** corresponds to the ventricular plateau. ST elevation/depression is a frontline diagnostic for myocardial infarction.
- **Pacemaker Design:** Modern pacemakers (and implantable defibrillators) must sense and respond to plateau-derived repolarization changes.
- **Antiarrhythmic Drug Development:** Class III drugs (e.g., amiodarone, sotalol) prolong the plateau by blocking $I_{Kr}$, extending the refractory period.
- **Cardiac Electrophysiology Simulators:** Platforms like the **Luo–Rudy model** computationally reproduce the plateau for drug-testing and arrhythmia simulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Potential for Ca²⁺ at Body Temperature

We need $E_{Ca}$ to understand the *driving force* pulling Ca²⁺ into the cell during the plateau.

**Step 1.** Write the Nernst equation for a divalent cation ($z = +2$):

$$E_{Ca} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_o}{[Ca^{2+}]_i}$$

**Step 2.** Insert physiological concentrations. Intracellular free [Ca²⁺] during diastole is **~100 nM = 1 × 10⁻⁴ mM**; extracellular [Ca²⁺] is **~1.8 mM**:

$$\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_o}{[Ca^{2+}]_i} = \frac{1.8 \text{ mM}}{1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mM}} = 1.8 \times 10^{4}$$

**Step 3.** Convert to natural log and evaluate:

$$\ln(1.8 \times 10^{4}) = \ln(1.8) + 4\ln(10) = 0.5878 + 9.2103 = 9.7981$$

**Step 4.** Plug in constants at $T = 310$ K (37 °C):

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \frac{2577.34}{96485} = 0.02671 \text{ V} = 26.71 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5.** For $z = +2$:

$$\frac{RT}{zF} = \frac{26.71}{2} = 13.36 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6.** Multiply:

$$E_{Ca} = 13.36 \text{ mV} \times 9.7981 = 130.9 \text{ mV} \approx +131 \text{ mV}$$

**Conclusion:** $E_{Ca} \approx +131$ mV. Since the plateau voltage is near 0 mV, the **driving force** for Ca²⁺ entry is enormous:

$$V_m - E_{Ca} = 0 - 131 = -131 \text{ mV}$$

This huge inward driving force is what sustains $I_{Ca-L}$ throughout the plateau.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Plateau Voltage from Current Balance

At the plateau, the cell is in a *quasi–steady state*: net ionic current ≈ 0. Using the linearized (ohmic) form for the two dominant currents:

**Step 1.** Write inward Ca²⁺ current:

$$I_{Ca-L} = \bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, (V_m - E_{Ca})$$

**Step 2.** Write outward K⁺ current (delayed rectifier + inward rectifier combined):

$$I_K = \bar{g}_K\, n\, (V_m - E_K)$$

**Step 3.** Apply the steady-state condition $I_{Ca-L} + I_K = 0$:

$$\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, (V_m - E_{Ca}) + \bar{g}_K\, n\, (V_m - E_K) = 0$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $V_m$:

$$\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, V_m - \bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, E_{Ca} + \bar{g}_K\, n\, V_m - \bar{g}_K\, n\, E_K = 0$$

$$V_m (\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f + \bar{g}_K\, n) = \bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, E_{Ca} + \bar{g}_K\, n\, E_K$$

**Step 5.** Final expression for plateau voltage — a conductance-weighted average of the two reversal potentials:

$$V_{plateau} = \frac{\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, E_{Ca} + \bar{g}_K\, n\, E_K}{\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f + \bar{g}_K\, n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The plateau voltage is **not** an arbitrary number — it is mathematically the *weighted mean* of $E_{Ca}$ (+131 mV) and $E_K$ (–94 mV), weighted by the *instantaneous effective conductances* of the Ca²⁺ and K⁺ channels. As the Ca²⁺ channel inactivates (gate $f$ closes), the weighting shifts toward K⁺, and $V_m$ moves down → repolarization begins.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Plateau Duration from the Hodgkin–Huxley Inactivation Gate

The L-type Ca²⁺ channel has an inactivation variable $f$ that decays approximately exponentially:

**Step 1.** ODE for $f$:

$$\frac{df}{dt} = \frac{f_{\infty}(V_m) - f}{\tau_f(V_m)}$$

**Step 2.** During the plateau, $V_m$ ≈ 0 mV. At this voltage, lookup tables (Luo–Rudy / ten Tusscher models) give:

$$f_{\infty}(0 \text{ mV}) \approx 0.025, \quad \tau_f(0 \text{ mV}) \approx 80 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3.** The plateau ends when $I_{Ca-L}$ can no longer keep up with $I_K$, which empirically occurs when $f$ falls to roughly half of its peak value. Solving the ODE for exponential decay from $f_0 = 0.9$ to $f_{end} \approx 0.3$:

$$f(t) = f_{\infty} + (f_0 - f_{\infty})\,e^{-t/\tau_f}$$

$$0.3 = 0.025 + (0.9 - 0.025)\,e^{-t/80}$$

$$0.275 = 0.875\,e^{-t/80}$$

$$e^{-t/80} = \frac{0.275}{0.875} = 0.3143$$

$$-t/80 = \ln(0.3143) = -1.157$$

$$t = 80 \times 1.157 = 92.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** The simple two-current model predicts a plateau on the order of ~90 ms, while the real plateau (~200–300 ms) is longer because additional **plateau-sustaining currents** (late Na⁺, Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger, $I_{Ks}$) are also active.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cardiac vs. Neural Action Potential

| Feature | Cardiac Ventricular AP | Neuronal AP |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | **200 – 300 ms** (long) | **1 – 2 ms** (short) |
| Plateau region | **YES (Phase 2)** | **NO** |
| Dominant inward current (Phase 2) | **$I_{Ca-L}$ (Ca²⁺)** | (No phase 2) |
| Refractory period | **Long, includes effective + relative** | Short |
| Functional purpose of long AP | Prevents tetanus; allows full ejection | Allows high-frequency firing |
| Trigger for contraction | Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release | N/A (electrical only) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Ionic Events Across All Five AP Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 4: Resting Vm equals -90 mV] -->|Fast Na+ channel opens| B[Phase 0: Rapid Depolarization up to +30 mV]
    B -->|Na+ inactivates, Ito activates| C[Phase 1: Initial Notch Repolarization]
    C -->|L-type Ca2+ channels fully open| D["Phase 2: PLATEAU REGION (Vm approx 0 mV)"]
    D -->|Ca2+ dependent inactivation of L-type channel| E[Phase 3: Rapid Repolarization via IKr and IKs]
    E -->|IK1 restores resting K+ conductance| A
    D -.->|CICR triggers Ca2+ release from SR| F[Cardiac Muscle Contraction]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the Plateau

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Cell Exterior: High Ca2+ 1.8 mM] -->|Driving force -131 mV| LCH[L-type Ca2+ Channel DHPR]
    LCH -->|Inward current ICaL| NODE[Plateau Voltage Node Vm approx 0 mV]
    SRK[Cell Interior: High K+ 140 mM] -->|Driving force +94 mV| KCH[Delayed Rectifier K+ Channel IKr IKs]
    KCH -->|Outward current IK| NODE
    NODE -->|Net current = 0| SS[Steady State Plateau]
    SS -->|Ca2+ enters cytosol| CICR[Calcium Induced Calcium Release from Sarcoplasmic Reticulum]
    CICR -->|Cytosolic Ca2+ rises| MECH[Myofilament Cross Bridge Cycling and Contraction]
    NODE -.->|f gate closes over 200 to 300 ms| TERM[Plateau Termination and Phase 3 Repolarization]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Plot: Schematic Voltage-vs-Time Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CARDIAC_PLOT[Cardiac Ventricular Action Potential Schematic]
    P0[Phase 0: Sharp upstroke] -->|10 to 20 ms| P1[Phase 1: Brief notch]
    P1 -->|200 to 300 ms| P2["Phase 2: PLATEAU at 0 mV (the focus of this module)"]
    P2 -->|50 to 100 ms| P3[Phase 3: Downstroke]
    P3 -->|rest| P4[Phase 4: Resting at -90 mV]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams communicate the *interactions* of the plateau region. In your KTU exam, a neatly drawn voltage-versus-time curve labeled with all 5 phases, with the plateau clearly marked, is worth full marks. Always label the **ion channels active in each phase**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the plateau region of a cardiac action potential. Mention the two principal ionic currents responsible for it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The plateau region is **Phase 2** of the cardiac ventricular action potential, during which the membrane potential remains nearly constant at approximately **0 mV for 200–300 ms**. It is sustained by a balance between the **inward L-type Ca²⁺ current ($I_{Ca-L}$)** and the **outward delayed-rectifier K⁺ current ($I_K$ + $I_{Ks}$)**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for currents named, 1 for numerical values.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Why does the plateau phase occur only in cardiac muscle and not in skeletal muscle or nerve action potentials?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The plateau exists because cardiac ventricular myocytes uniquely express **slow, long-lasting L-type Ca²⁺ channels (DHPRs)** that activate after the Na⁺ upstroke and remain open for hundreds of milliseconds. Nerve and skeletal-muscle cells lack this sustained Ca²⁺ current; their action potentials are dominated by fast Na⁺ activation and rapid K⁺-mediated repolarization, lasting only 1–2 ms. The cardiac plateau therefore produces a **long refractory period** that prevents tetanic contraction — essential for the heart's pumping cycle. *(3 marks.)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **five phases** of a cardiac ventricular action potential, clearly indicating the **plateau region** and the **ion channels** active in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the plateau voltage** as the conductance-weighted mean of the Ca²⁺ and K⁺ equilibrium potentials. Use this to explain why the plateau voltage is near 0 mV. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Phase 4 – Resting (–90 mV):** $I_{K1}$ inward-rectifier K⁺ current dominates. The membrane is permeable mainly to K⁺; the Nernst potential for K⁺ (–94 mV) sets the resting voltage. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Phase 0 – Upstroke (–90 → +30 mV):** Fast voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → massive inward $I_{Na}$. All-or-none firing. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Phase 1 – Notch:** Na⁺ channels inactivate; transient outward K⁺ current $I_{to}$ causes a small downward deflection. *(1 mark)*
4. **Phase 2 – Plateau (≈ 0 mV, 200–300 ms):** L-type Ca²⁺ channels open; their slow kinetics and Ca²⁺-dependent inactivation sustain the depolarization. Outward K⁺ currents balance the inward Ca²⁺ current. The plateau drives **CICR** for contraction. *(2 marks)*
5. **Phase 3 – Repolarization:** L-type Ca²⁺ channels inactivate; $I_{Kr}$ and $I_{Ks}$ increase, repolarizing the cell. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram expected:** A voltage vs. time plot with all 5 phases labeled, plateau clearly marked, and the ion channels annotated on the curve.

**Model Solution (b):**

We begin with the two dominant plateau currents expressed in linearized (ohmic) form. The inward L-type Ca²⁺ current is written as the product of its maximal conductance $\bar{g}_{Ca}$, its activation gate $d$, its inactivation gate $f$, and the driving force $(V_m - E_{Ca})$. The outward K⁺ current is written as the product of its conductance $\bar{g}_K$, the activation gate $n$, and the driving force $(V_m - E_K)$.

At any point on the plateau, the cell is in a quasi-steady state where the net transmembrane ionic current is zero. Equating the inward and outward currents and solving algebraically for $V_m$ yields:

$$V_{plateau} = \frac{\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f\, E_{Ca} + \bar{g}_K\, n\, E_K}{\bar{g}_{Ca}\, d\, f + \bar{g}_K\, n}$$

This is a **weighted average** of the two reversal potentials. Substituting $E_{Ca} = +131$ mV and $E_K = -94$ mV, and noting that the conductances on the plateau are of similar order, the weighted mean lands near 0 mV. As Ca²⁺ channels inactivate (gate $f$ shrinks), the weighting shifts toward K⁺, and $V_m$ falls — this is the end of the plateau and the start of Phase 3. *(7 marks: 2 marks for writing the two currents, 2 marks for setting up the balance equation, 2 marks for algebraic solution, 1 mark for numerical and physiological interpretation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of the L-type calcium channel** in generating and terminating the plateau region. List its three kinetic properties that make it suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Nernst equation**, calculate the equilibrium potential for Ca²⁺ at 37 °C given $[Ca^{2+}]_o = 1.8$ mM and $[Ca^{2+}]_i = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ mM. Comment on its significance to the plateau driving force. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **L-type Ca²⁺ channel** (Dihydropyridine Receptor, DHPR) is the principal architect of the plateau. During Phase 0, when the membrane depolarizes, these channels — though voltage-gated — activate **slowly** (over ~10–20 ms), opening just as the upstroke is ending. This produces a sustained inward Ca²⁺ current that opposes the outward K⁺ current, flattening the voltage trace into the plateau.

The three kinetic properties that make it suitable are:

1. **Slow activation** — delays the inward current so it coincides with the plateau, not the upstroke. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Long open time (slow inactivation)** — the channel stays open for hundreds of milliseconds, providing the sustained inward current needed for the ~250 ms plateau. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Ca²⁺-dependent inactivation** — the rising cytosolic [Ca²⁺] from the plateau current gradually closes the channel, naturally terminating the plateau. *(1.5 marks)*

This Ca²⁺ entry also triggers **Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR)** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via Ryanodine Receptors (RyR2), which is the proximate trigger of cardiac contraction. *(2.5 marks for this physiological role + summary.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

The Nernst equation for a divalent cation ($z = +2$) is:

$$E_{Ca} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[Ca^{2+}]_o}{[Ca^{2+}]_i}$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 310$ K, $z = 2$, $F = 96485$ C/mol:

$$\frac{RT}{zF} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{2 \times 96485} = 13.36 \text{ mV}$$

The concentration ratio:

$$\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_o}{[Ca^{2+}]_i} = \frac{1.8}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.8 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\ln(1.8 \times 10^{4}) = 9.798$$

Therefore:

$$E_{Ca} = 13.36 \times 9.798 = +130.9 \text{ mV} \approx +131 \text{ mV}$$

**Significance:** The plateau voltage is roughly 0 mV, so the driving force for Ca²⁺ entry is $V_m - E_{Ca} = 0 - 131 = -131$ mV — a **massive** inward driving force. This is what allows even a relatively small Ca²⁺ conductance to sustain a current large enough to balance the outward K⁺ current and maintain the plateau. *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for numerical evaluation, 1 for the final answer, 2 for the physiological interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify temperature.** Always state $T = 310$ K (37 °C) when computing Nernst potentials in cardiac physiology. Losing this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $E_{Ca}$ with the plateau voltage.** $E_{Ca} \approx +131$ mV is the *equilibrium* potential; the plateau *voltage* is ~0 mV. They are not the same.
> 3. **Skipping the gating variables.** Writing $I_{Ca-L} = g_{Ca}(V_m - E_{Ca})$ without the activation ($d$) and inactivation ($f$) gates is incomplete and will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Calling the plateau "Phase 1."** It is **Phase 2**. This is a heavily tested labeling point.
> 5. **Omitting the units** in numerical problems (mV, mM, mS, ms).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ⭐ The **plateau region = Phase 2** of the cardiac ventricular action potential.
- ⭐ Plateau voltage ≈ **0 mV**, duration ≈ **200–300 ms** in human ventricle.
- ⭐ Plateau is sustained by **inward L-type Ca²⁺ current ($I_{Ca-L}$)** balanced by **outward K⁺ currents ($I_{Kr}$, $I_{Ks}$, $I_{K1}$)**.
- ⭐ The plateau is a **zero-net-current quasi-steady state**, so the plateau voltage is a **conductance-weighted mean** of $E_{Ca}$ and $E_K$.
- ⭐ **L-type Ca²⁺ channels** are the architect: slow activation, long open time, Ca²⁺-dependent inactivation.
- ⭐ The plateau is the cellular basis of the **ST segment** on the ECG and the **long refractory period** that prevents arrhythmias.
- ⭐ Nernst potential for Ca²⁺ at 37 °C: **$E_{Ca} \approx +131$ mV**.
- ⭐ The plateau is **unique to cardiac cells** — it does **not** occur in nerve or skeletal muscle action potentials.
- ⭐ Clinical correlate: **Long QT syndrome** = prolonged plateau; **Short QT syndrome** = abbreviated plateau; **Class III antiarrhythmics** (amiodarone, sotalol) prolong the plateau by blocking $I_{Kr}$.
- ⭐ The plateau enables **Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR)** — the trigger of every heartbeat.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pacemaker. Nerve Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pacemaker. Nerve Action Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Cardiac Pacemaker — Definition

A **cardiac pacemaker** is a specialized cluster of self-excitable myocardial cells (predominantly located in the **Sino-Atrial (SA) node** of the right atrium) that spontaneously and rhythmically generate electrical impulses, thereby setting the heart rate and synchronizing the cardiac cycle. The term also refers to the **artificial (implantable) electronic device** that mimics this function by delivering controlled electrical stimuli to a diseased heart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A pacemaker cell does NOT possess a true "resting" membrane potential. Its membrane continuously and slowly drifts toward threshold even in the absence of external stimuli — this property is called **Automaticity** (or **Diastolic Depolarization**, Phase 4 of the pacemaker action potential).

**Standard Reference Values (Memorize):**
- Natural SA Node Intrinsic Rate: **60–100 beats/min** (≈ 1–1.7 Hz)
- AV Node Intrinsic Rate: **40–60 beats/min**
- Ventricular Purkinje Intrinsic Rate: **20–40 beats/min**
- Pacemaker Action Potential Amplitude: **~70 mV** (lower than nerve AP)
- Threshold Potential of SA Node: **−40 mV**
- Maximum Diastolic Potential (MDP) of SA Node: **−60 mV**

---

### 1.2 The Nerve Action Potential — Definition

A **nerve action potential (NAP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-none depolarization-repolarization wave that propagates along the membrane of an excitable neuron (or axon), generated by the sequential opening of voltage-gated **Na⁺** and **K⁺** ion channels as described by the **Hodgkin–Huxley model (1952)**.

**Standard Reference Values:**
- Resting Membrane Potential (RMP): **−70 mV** (typical mammalian neuron)
- Threshold Potential: **−55 mV**
- Peak Overshoot: **+30 to +40 mV**
- AP Amplitude: **~100 mV**
