The study of signal generation is the **foundational first-principles layer** of biomedical signal processing. Without understanding *where* the signal comes from, it is impossible to:

- Design appropriate **front-end amplifier circuits** (e.g., choosing CMRR, input impedance, and bandwidth).
- Develop accurate **noise-removal algorithms** (e.g., removing 50 Hz powerline interference vs. genuine biopotential components).
- Build realistic **synthetic signal simulators** for testing devices.
- Understand the **clinical interpretation** of waveform morphology (e.g., why a QRS complex looks the way it does).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - **Resting Membrane Potential (V_m):** **−70 mV** (neurons), **−90 mV** (ventricular myocytes)
> - **Threshold Potential:** **−55 mV** (general excitable cells)
> - **Peak Action Potential:** **+30 mV to +40 mV**
> - **Nernst Potential for K⁺ (E_K):** **−94 mV**
> - **Nernst Potential for Na⁺ (E_Na):** **+60 mV**
> - **Nernst Potential for Cl⁻ (E_Cl):** **−65 mV**
> - **Body Temperature (T):** **310 K** (≈ 37 °C)
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How a Bioelectric Signal is Generated

The complete generation of a bioelectric signal can be partitioned into **five sequential physiological stages**, each corresponding to distinct electrical events that a biomedical engineer must understand:

#### Stage 1: Establishing the Ionic Gradients (The Cellular Battery)
- The **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump** (an active transport protein) uses ATP hydrolysis to pump **3 Na⁺ ions OUT** of the cell and **2 K⁺ ions IN** per cycle.
- This creates a **chemical concentration gradient**:
  - **High K⁺ inside**, **Low K⁺ outside** (Intracellular [K⁺] ≈ **140 mM**, Extracellular [K⁺] ≈ **4 mM**)
  - **Low Na⁺ inside**, **High Na⁺ outside** (Intracellular [Na⁺] ≈ **10 mM**, Extracellular [Na⁺] ≈ **145 mM**)
- The pump is **electrogenic** — it moves net +1 charge out per cycle, contributing directly to the **negative resting potential**.

#### Stage 2: Selective Permeability at Rest (The Closed Doors)
- At rest, the membrane is **selectively permeable** — primarily permeable to **K⁺** (via leak channels) and **Cl⁻**.
- K⁺ ions diffuse *outward* down their concentration gradient, leaving behind unbalanced negative charges (large anions like proteins that cannot cross).
- This creates the **resting membrane potential**, which closely approaches the **Nernst potential for K⁺** (E_K).

#### Stage 3: Local Depolarization and Threshold Crossing
- An external stimulus (synaptic input, mechanical stretch, pacemaker current) injects positive charge, raising V_m from −70 mV toward **−55 mV**.
- At threshold, **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open explosively (positive feedback: Na⁺ in → more depolarization → more Na⁺ channels open).

#### Stage 4: Action Potential (The Spike)
- **Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization:** V_m rises rapidly to **+30 to +40 mV** as Na⁺ rushes in.
- **Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization:** Fast Na⁺ channels **inactivate**; transient K⁺ current begins.
- **Phase 2 — Plateau (cardiac myocytes only):** Balance between Ca²⁺ influx and K⁺ efflux.
- **Phase 3 — Repolarization:** V_m returns toward resting value as K⁺ flows out via **delayed rectifier K⁺ channels**.
- **Phase 4 — Resting / Hyperpolarization:** Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase restores ionic gradients; brief after-hyperpolarization (AHP) occurs.

#### Stage 5: Propagation and Volume Conduction
- The local circuit currents flow along the cell membrane and through the surrounding **volume conductor** (tissue → extracellular fluid → skin).
- Surface electrodes placed on the skin detect these currents as **ECG, EEG, or EMG** signals.

### 2.2 The Equivalent Circuit Model of the Cell Membrane

The cell membrane, in conjunction with the intracellular and extracellular fluids, can be modeled as an **electrical circuit**. This is a **high-yield KTU topic** for 14-mark derivations.

The standard linear equivalent circuit consists of:
- A **variable capacitance (C_m)** in parallel with...
- A series combination of **variable resistors (R_Na, R_K, R_Cl)** each in series with their respective **EMF sources (E_Na, E_K, E_Cl)** representing Nernst potentials.

The total membrane current **I_m** is the sum of:
1. Capacitive (displacement) current: **I_C = C_m · (dV_m/dt)**
2. Ionic (conductive) current: **I_ion = Σ g_ion · (V_m − E_ion)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** This model is the **direct link between physiology and circuit design**. It justifies why biopotential amplifiers must have **high input impedance** (>10 MΩ), **high CMRR** (>80 dB), and **low input bias currents** (<10 pA) — all to avoid loading down these fragile high-impedance bioelectric sources.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|
| **Nernst Equation (Single Ion)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | $E_{ion}$ = Nernst potential (V), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K), $z$ = ion valence, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol |
| **Nernst Equation (Base-10 form)** | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | At 37 °C, $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} \approx 61.5$ mV |
| **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | $P_{ion}$ = membrane permeability for that ion. Used to compute resting V_m. |
| **Membrane Current Equation** | $I_m = C_m \dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + \sum_{ion} g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | Total membrane current = capacitive + ionic components |
| **Cable Equation (1-D propagation)** | $\dfrac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = r_m c_m \dfrac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + r_m i_m$ | $r_m$ = membrane resistance per unit length, $c_m$ = capacitance per unit length, $i_m$ = ionic current per unit length |
| **Conduction Velocity (Unmyelinated)** | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{r_m r_i} \cdot c_m}$ | $r_i$ = intracellular axial resistance |
| **Conduction Velocity (Myelinated, Saltatory)** | $\theta = d \sqrt{\dfrac{g_m}{2 r_i r_a}}$ | $d$ = internodal distance, $g_m$ = membrane conductance, $r_a$ = axial resistance per unit length |
| **Sodium-Pump Contribution** | $V_{pump} = \dfrac{I_{pump}}{g_m} \approx -4 \text{ to } -10$ mV | Direct electrogenic contribution to resting potential |
| **Length Constant (λ)** | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Distance over which V_m decays to 1/e of its value |
| **Time Constant (τ)** | $\tau = r_m c_m$ | Time for V_m to reach ~63% of its steady-state change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Nernst equation** and **GHK equation** are the cornerstones of *all* electrophysiology. They are used in:
> - **Pacemaker design** (predicting how device current affects cardiac cell excitability)
> - **Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES)** systems
> - **Drug development** (calculating how a new ion-channel blocker will shift the action potential)
> - **Neural prosthesis design** (estimating the strength of an injected stimulus needed to trigger an AP)

### 2.4 Hodgkin-Huxley Conceptual Framework (KTU-Level Depth)

Although the full Hodgkin-Huxley model is a **System of Four Coupled Nonlinear ODEs**, the KTU 2024 syllabus for "Generation" requires only the **conceptual understanding** of how it formalizes the equivalent circuit model.

The ionic currents are written as:
$$I_{Na} = \bar{g}_{Na} \cdot m^3 h \cdot (V_m - E_{Na})$$
$$I_K = \bar{g}_K \cdot n^4 \cdot (V_m - E_K)$$
$$I_L = \bar{g}_L \cdot (V_m - E_L)$$

Where:
- $m$ = **activation** gating variable for Na⁺ (rapid)
- $h$ = **inactivation** gating variable for Na⁺ (slower)
- $n$ = **activation** gating variable for K⁺
- $\bar{g}_{Na}, \bar{g}_K$ = maximum conductances
- $E_L$ = leak reversal potential (≈ −50 mV)

Each gating variable obeys a first-order kinetic equation: $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \alpha_x(1-x) - \beta_x x$, with voltage-dependent rate constants $\alpha_x$ and $\beta_x$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Nernst Equation (Full Thermodynamic Derivation)

This is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question** derivation. The derivation uses the principle of **electrochemical equilibrium**.

**Given Setup:**
Consider a membrane separating two compartments (inside and outside) containing a monovalent ion (e.g., K⁺) at concentrations $[K^+]_{in}$ and $[K^+]_{out}$. The membrane is permeable to K⁺ only.

**Step 1 — Define Electrochemical Potential:**

The total work (energy) per mole required to move an ion from outside to inside is the sum of:
1. **Chemical work** (concentration gradient): $\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln \dfrac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}}$
2. **Electrical work** (potential gradient): $\Delta G_{elec} = z F (V_{in} - V_{out})$

Therefore, the **total free energy change** is:
$$\Delta G = RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}} + z F (V_{in} - V_{out})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition:**

At electrochemical equilibrium, there is **no net flux** of ions across the membrane. By the Second Law of Thermodynamics, $\Delta G = 0$.

$$0 = RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}} + z F (V_{in} - V_{out})$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Equilibrium Potential:**

Rearrange to isolate the potential difference $E_K = V_{in} - V_{out}$:

$$z F \cdot E_K = -RT \ln \frac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}}$$

$$E_K = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[K^+]_{in}}{[K^+]_{out}}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the Logarithm Argument:**

Using the identity $-\ln(A/B) = \ln(B/A)$:

$$E_{K} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** for potassium. For KTU exams, the **convention is to express the Nernst potential in terms of intracellular relative to extracellular reference** — i.e., $V_{in} - V_{out}$, which yields a **negative value** for K⁺ (since $[K^+]_{in} > [K^+]_{out}$).

**Step 5 — Convert to Common-Logarithm (Base-10) Form:**

Multiply numerator and denominator by 2.303:

$$E_{K} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at Body Temperature (T = 310 K):**

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

So the simplified working form is:
$$E_{K} = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}$$

For K⁺ (z = +1), with $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM:
$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{4}{140} \right) = 61.5 \times (-1.544) \approx -94.97 \text{ mV}$$

This matches the standard textbook value of **E_K ≈ −94 mV**. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

The Nernst equation assumes permeability to a **single ion only**. In reality, the membrane is permeable to multiple ions simultaneously. The GHK equation is derived under the **constant-field assumption** (electric field across the membrane is constant).

**Step 1 — Set Up the Flux Equation:**

Using the Nernst-Planck equation under the constant-field assumption, the steady-state flux of ion $i$ is:
$$J_i = -P_i z_i^2 \frac{F^2 V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[i]_{in} - [i]_{out} e^{-z_i F V_m / RT}}{1 - e^{-z_i F V_m / RT}}$$

**Step 2 — Impose the Condition of Zero Net Current:**

At rest, there is no net charge flow across the membrane, so the **sum of all ionic currents equals zero**:
$$I_{total} = I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$$

Substituting the flux equation and using the constraint $I_{total} = 0$ (and assuming all permeant ions are monovalent, so $z_i = \pm 1$):

**Step 3 — Solve for V_m:**

After algebraic manipulation (canceling common terms, multiplying by RT/F V_m):
$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation of the GHK Equation:**

- If $P_{Na} = P_{Cl} = 0$, the GHK equation **reduces exactly to the Nernst equation for K⁺**.
- If $P_K$ is the dominant permeability (as at rest), $V_m$ is close to $E_K$.
- If $P_{Na}$ becomes dominant (as during peak AP), $V_m$ approaches $E_{Na}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Time Constant and Length Constant

These parameters govern **how fast** and **how far** a bioelectric signal propagates.

**Step 1 — Model a Passive Cable:**

Consider an axon of diameter $d$, modeled as a series of cylindrical membrane patches. Each patch has:
- Membrane resistance $r_m$ (Ω·cm)
- Membrane capacitance $c_m$ (F/cm)
- Axial (intracellular) resistance $r_i$ (Ω/cm)

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law:**

The net current entering a small segment $dx$ must equal the membrane current leaving:
$$-\frac{\partial i_i}{\partial x} = i_m = \frac{V_m}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t}$$

Where $i_i = -\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial x}$ is the axial current.

**Step 3 — Combine into the Cable Equation:**

$$\frac{1}{r_i} \frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = \frac{V_m}{r_m} + c_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t}$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{\partial^2 V_m}{\partial x^2} = r_m c_m \frac{\partial V_m}{\partial t} + \frac{V_m}{\lambda^2}$$

Where the **length constant** is:
$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}}$$

And the **time constant** is:
$$\tau = r_m c_m$$

**Step 4 — Verify Units:**

- $[r_m] = $ Ω·cm, $[r_i] = $ Ω/cm
- $[\lambda^2] = \dfrac{\text{Ω·cm}}{\text{Ω/cm}} = \text{cm}^2$ → λ in cm ✓
- $[\tau] = $ Ω·cm × F/cm = Ω·F = seconds ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Generation Mechanisms Across Tissues

| Property | Cardiac Muscle (ECG) | Skeletal Muscle (EMG) | Neural Tissue (EEG/ENG) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resting V_m** | −90 mV | −70 to −80 mV | −70 mV |
| **AP Peak** | +20 to +30 mV | +30 to +40 mV | +30 to +40 mV |
| **AP Duration** | 200–300 ms (long) | 2–4 ms (medium) | 1–2 ms (short) |
| **Plateau Phase?** | **Yes** (Phase 2, Ca²⁺-mediated) | No | No |
| **Propagation Type** | Cell-to-cell (gap junctions) | Continuous along fiber | Continuous (unmyelinated) or **Saltatory** (myelinated) |
| **Conduction Velocity** | 0.3 – 4 m/s | 3 – 5 m/s | 1 – 120 m/s |
| **Dominant Ions** | Na⁺, K⁺, **Ca²⁺** | Na⁺, K⁺ | Na⁺, K⁺ |
| **Clinical Surface Signal** | ECG (0.5 – 100 Hz) | EMG (20 – 500 Hz) | EEG (0.5 – 100 Hz) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the Nernst equation. Calculate the Nernst potential for Ca²⁺ ions at 37 °C, given $[Ca^{2+}]_{out} = 2$ mM and $[Ca^{2+}]_{in} = 10^{-4}$ mM.**

**Model Answer:**

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium (reversal) potential across a membrane for a single ion species when there is no net ionic flux. It is given by:

$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

**Given Data:**
- $T = 37°$C $= 310$ K, $z = +2$ (for Ca²⁺), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $[Ca^{2+}]_{out} = 2$ mM, $[Ca^{2+}]_{in} = 10^{-4}$ mM

**Step 1:** Evaluate the constant:
$$\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.06153 \text{ V} = 61.53 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Nernst equation:
$$E_{Ca} = \frac{61.53}{+2} \log_{10} \left( \frac{2 \times 10^{0}}{10^{-4}} \right) = 30.77 \times \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{4})$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithm:
$$\log_{10}(20000) = 4.301$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final potential:
$$E_{Ca} = 30.77 \times 4.301 \approx 132.4 \text{ mV}$$

**Result:** $E_{Ca} \approx +132$ mV — a large positive driving force, which is why Ca²⁺ influx during the cardiac plateau phase is so physiologically significant. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula + substitution, 1 for numerical answer with units)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the equivalent circuit model of a cell membrane. Why is this model important in biopotential amplifier design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (to be drawn):** Refer to the equivalent circuit schematic in Section 4.2.

**Explanation:**

The cell membrane can be modeled as an electrical circuit comprising:
- A **membrane capacitance (C_m)** representing the lipid bilayer's ability to store charge.
- Three parallel branches, each containing a **variable resistor** ($R_{Na}, R_K, R_{Cl}$) in series with a **battery** (E_Na, E_K, E_Cl) representing the **Nernst potentials** of the respective ions.

The total membrane current is the sum of capacitive and ionic currents:
$$I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + I_{ionic}$$

**Importance in Biopotential Amplifier Design:**
1. The model shows that the cell is a **high-impedance, low-amplitude signal source** (V_m in mV range, source impedance often >10 MΩ).
2. This dictates that biopotential amplifiers must have **very high input impedance** (>10¹² Ω preferred) to avoid signal attenuation.
3. The **differential nature** of the biopotential (measured between two electrodes) requires amplifiers with **high CMRR** (>80 dB) to reject common-mode noise like 50 Hz powerline interference.
4. The **capacitive component** establishes the required amplifier **bandwidth** (e.g., 0.05–150 Hz for ECG). **(3 marks: 1 for diagram, 1 for explanation, 1 for amplifier design implications)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for 14-Mark Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Penalty (–2 marks typical):** Students frequently mix mV with V, or use mM with M, without unit conversion. Always write the numerical value *and* its unit explicitly.
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks):** Forgetting the **negative sign** in E_K because $[ion]_{in} > [ion]_{out}$ is the most common mistake. Re-derive carefully.
> 3. **Logarithm Base Confusion (–2 marks):** Using `ln` (natural log) with base-10 constants, or vice versa. Memorize: $2.303 \times RT/F \approx 61.5$ mV at 37 °C for the base-10 form.
> 4. **Missing Constant Values (–1 mark):** Forgetting to state R, T, F explicitly. Always list your **Given Data** section in a 14-mark derivation.
> 5. **No Diagram = Automatic Penalty (–2 to 3 marks):** For a "with neat diagram" question, an absent or poorly labeled circuit diagram is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Nernst equation for a monovalent ion. Starting from the Nernst equation, explain how the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) voltage equation generalizes it for a multi-ion system. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a nerve cell at 37 °C, the following data are given:

| Parameter | Intracellular (mM) | Extracellular (mM) | Permeability (arbitrary units) |
|---|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 140 | 4 | 1.0 |
| Na⁺ | 10 | 145 | 0.04 |
| Cl⁻ | 4 | 110 | 0.45 |

Compute:
(i) The Nernst potential for K⁺ and Na⁺.
(ii) The resting membrane potential using the GHK equation.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Derivation of the Nernst Equation:**

The free energy change for moving 1 mole of ion $X^{z+}$ from outside to inside the cell is:
$$\Delta G = RT \ln \frac{[X^{z+}]_{in}}{[X^{z+}]_{out}} + z F (V_{in} - V_{out})$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$:
$$0 = RT \ln \frac{[X^{z+}]_{in}}{[X^{z+}]_{out}} + z F \cdot E_{X}$$

Solving for $E_X$:
$$E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X^{z+}]_{out}}{[X^{z+}]_{in}}$$

**Generalization to GHK Equation:**

The Nernst equation considers only **one ion at a time**. In reality, the membrane is permeable to multiple ions. Under the **constant-field assumption** (Goldman 1943), the electric field within the membrane is constant, and the flux equation integrates to:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**Assumptions of the GHK Equation:**
1. The electric field within the membrane is constant (constant-field assumption).
2. Ions move independently of each other.
3. The membrane is a homogeneous, thin barrier.
4. Permeabilities $P_{ion}$ are constants (voltage-independent) — this is the main limitation.

When $P_{Na} = P_{Cl} = 0$, the GHK equation reduces exactly to the **Nernst equation for K⁺**, confirming the latter is a special case. **(7 marks: 3 for Nernst derivation, 2 for GHK explanation, 2 for assumptions + limiting case)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Nernst Potentials:**

Using $E_{ion} = 61.5 \log_{10} \dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ mV at 37 °C:

**For K⁺ (z = +1):**
$$E_K = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{4}{140} \right) = 61.5 \times (-1.544) \approx -94.97 \text{ mV}$$

**For Na⁺ (z = +1):**
$$E_{Na} = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{145}{10} \right) = 61.5 \times (1.161) \approx +71.40 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Resting Membrane Potential (GHK):**

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{(1.0)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4)}{(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(110)}$$

**Numerator:**
$$(1.0)(4) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$

**Denominator:**
$$(1.0)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(110) = 140 + 0.4 + 49.5 = 189.9$$

**Final calculation:**
$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \left( \frac{11.6}{189.9} \right) = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0611) = 61.5 \times (-1.214) \approx -74.6 \text{ mV}$$

**Result:** $V_m \approx -74.6$ mV — this closely matches the experimentally measured resting potential of nerve cells (~−70 mV). The small difference is due to the **electrogenic Na⁺/K⁺ pump**, which the GHK equation does not account for. **(7 marks: 2 for E_K, 2 for E_Na, 3 for GHK calculation with intermediate steps shown)**

---

### 5.4 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **five distinct phases of the cardiac ventricular action potential**, including the dominant ionic currents in each phase. Explain why the **plateau phase (Phase 2)** is unique to cardiac muscle and not present in nerve or skeletal muscle.

**(b) [7 marks]** A unmyelinated nerve axon has the following properties:
- Diameter $d = 10 \, \mu$m
- Membrane resistance per unit length $r_m = 2000$ Ω·cm
- Axial (intracellular) resistance per unit length $r_i = 1.5 \times 10^{6}$ Ω/cm
- Membrane capacitance per unit length $c_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ F/cm

Calculate:
(i) The length constant (λ).
(ii) The time constant (τ).
(iii) The conduction velocity (θ).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3):**

The cardiac ventricular action potential has **five phases**:

**Phase 4 — Resting Potential:**
- $V_m \approx -90$ mV
- Dominant current: Inward rectifier K⁺ current ($I_{K1}$)
- High K⁺ permeability keeps the cell near $E_K$.

**Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization:**
- $V_m$ rises rapidly from −90 mV to +30 mV
- Dominant current: Fast inward Na⁺ current ($I_{Na}$) via voltage-gated Na⁺ channels
- Mechanism: Regenerative positive feedback (more depolarization → more Na⁺ channels open).

**Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization:**
- Brief partial repolarization
- Dominant currents: Na⁺ channel inactivation + transient outward K⁺ current ($I_{to}$).

**Phase 2 — Plateau (UNIQUE to cardiac muscle):**
- Sustained depolarization near 0 mV lasting **200–300 ms**
- Dominant currents: **Inward Ca²⁺ current ($I_{Ca-L}$, L-type Ca²⁺ channels)** is balanced by **outward K⁺ current ($I_K$)**
- The Ca²⁺ influx during Phase 2 triggers **calcium-induced calcium release** from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, enabling **excitation-contraction coupling** in cardiac muscle.

**Why Phase 2 is Unique to Cardiac Muscle:**
- Cardiac myocytes require a **prolonged depolarization** to allow **Ca²⁺ entry** for sustained contraction.
- Nerve and skeletal muscle APs last only 1–4 ms because they primarily use **fast Na⁺ and K⁺ currents**, with no significant Ca²⁺ influx.
- The long plateau **prevents tetanic contraction** in cardiac muscle (the heart must relax between beats to fill with blood).

**Phase 3 — Repolarization:**
- Dominant current: Delayed rectifier K⁺ current ($I_K$)
- $V_m$ returns to −90 mV.

**Phase 4 (Return) — Resting State:**
- Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase restores ionic gradients.
- Relative refractory period ensures unidirectional propagation. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for phase-by-phase explanation, 2 for uniqueness of Phase 2)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $r_m = 2000$ Ω·cm
- $r_i = 1.5 \times 10^{6}$ Ω/cm
- $c_m = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ F/cm

**(i) Length Constant (λ):**
$$\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{r_m}{r_i}} = \sqrt{\frac{2000}{1.5 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$\lambda = \sqrt{1.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0365 \text{ cm} = 0.365 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Time Constant (τ):**
$$\tau = r_m \times c_m = 2000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\tau = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 30 \, \mu\text{s}$$

**(iii) Conduction Velocity (θ):**

For an unmyelinated axon, conduction velocity is:
$$\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r_m \cdot r_i \cdot c_m}}$$

Substituting:
$$\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

**Intermediate step:**
$$2000 \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} = 3.0 \times 10^{9}$$
$$3.0 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-8} = 45$$

$$\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{45}} = \frac{1}{6.708} = 0.149 \text{ cm/s} = 1.49 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Result:**
- Length constant λ ≈ **0.365 mm** (small — signal decays quickly)
- Time constant τ ≈ **30 μs** (very fast membrane response)
- Conduction velocity θ ≈ **1.49 mm/s** (very slow — characteristic of small unmyelinated axons)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Such slow conduction velocities are why small unmyelinated fibers (e.g., C-fibers carrying pain signals) are slower than myelinated A-fibers. Myelination increases θ dramatically by **saltatory conduction**. **(7 marks: 2 for λ, 2 for τ, 3 for θ with intermediate steps)**

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — GENERATION OF BIOSIGNALS**

**1. Core Equations to Memorize:**
- ✅ Nernst Equation (natural log and base-10 form)
- ✅ Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation
- ✅ Membrane current equation: $I_m = C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + \sum g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$
- ✅ Length constant: $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$
- ✅ Time constant: $\tau = r_m c_m$
- ✅ Conduction velocity formulas (myelinated and unmyelinated)

**2. Numerical Constants (always carry forward in 14-mark derivations):**
- ✅ $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- ✅ $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- ✅ $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = 61.5$ mV at 37 °C (310 K)
- ✅ Body temperature T = 310 K

**3. Standard Resting and Nernst Potentials:**
- ✅ $V_m$ (rest) = **−70 mV** (neurons), **−90 mV** (cardiac)
- ✅ $E_K \approx -94$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ to +71 mV, $E_{Cl} \approx -65$ mV
- ✅ Threshold V_m = **−55 mV**, AP peak = **+30 to +40 mV**

**4. Action Potential Phases — Memorize the Order:**
- ✅ Phase 0: Na⁺ influx (depolarization)
- ✅ Phase 1: Na⁺ inactivation + transient K⁺ outflow
- ✅ Phase 2: **Ca²⁺ plateau (cardiac only)**
- ✅ Phase 3: K⁺ efflux (repolarization)
- ✅ Phase 4: Na⁺/K⁺ pump restoration + AHP

**5. Key Physiological Facts:**
- ✅ Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase: **3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in** (electrogenic — contributes −4 to −10 mV to V_m)
- ✅ GHK reduces to Nernst when only one ion is permeant
- ✅ Plateau phase (Phase 2) is **exclusive to cardiac myocytes**
- ✅ Myelinated axons conduct via **saltatory conduction** (jumping between Nodes of Ranvier)
- ✅ Equivalent circuit justifies **high input impedance** in biopotential amplifiers

**6. Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ❌ Mixing mV and V
- ❌ Wrong sign in Nernst potential
- ❌ Confusing natural log vs. common log
- ❌ Forgetting to state R, T, F explicitly
- ❌ Skipping the diagram in "with neat diagram" questions
- ❌ Using ln when constants are in base-10 form (or vice versa)

**7. Conceptual Linkage (Why this matters in Module 2):**
- The "Generation" topic establishes the **time-domain waveform** that subsequent modules will analyze using **Fourier transforms, filtering, and feature extraction**.
- The **equivalent circuit model** directly informs **amplifier design choices** (covered in Module 1 and reinforced later).
- Understanding **propagation** (cable equation) is the bridge to understanding **ECG lead theory** in later modules.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If you can derive the **Nernst equation from thermodynamic first principles** and apply the **GHK equation** to a multi-ion numerical problem, you are equipped to score full marks on any 14-mark question on "Generation." Practice at least **two numerical problems** before the KTU exam to lock in the unit conversions and sign conventions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biosignals and Signal Processing**, a **wave** is a spatially and temporally varying physical quantity that transfers energy from one point in a medium (or field) to another through periodic oscillations of constituent particles or field components, without permanent displacement of the medium itself. Mathematically, a wave is described as a function $y(x, t)$ that satisfies the classical one-dimensional **wave equation**:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y(x, t)}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{v^{2}} \, \frac{\partial^{2} y(x, t)}{\partial t^{2}}$$

where $v$ is the **phase velocity** of the wave. For sinusoidal (harmonic) waves — the fundamental building blocks of every bioelectrical signal (ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG) — the generic representation is:

$$y(x, t) = A \, \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

Here, $A$ is the amplitude, $k$ is the angular wave number, $\omega$ is the angular frequency, and $\phi$ is the initial phase. For **time-domain bio-signal analysis**, we collapse the spatial coordinate and treat the signal as a function of time alone:

$$x(t) = A \, \sin(\omega t + \phi) = A \, \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the module "Waves", you are expected to master (i) the sinusoidal/harmonic wave form, (ii) all its defining parameters ($A, \omega, f, T, \lambda, k, v, \phi$), (iii) the phasor and complex-exponential representation, and (iv) the wave equation — because these are the *exact* mathematical primitives from which Fourier analysis, sampling theory, and digital filters are subsequently built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a pier watching the **ocean waves** roll in. Each wave has:
- A **height** (how tall the crest is from the still water level) → this is the **Amplitude ($A$)**.
- A **time interval** between two successive crests reaching your feet → this is the **Period ($T$)**, and its reciprocal is the **Frequency ($f$)**.
- A **horizontal distance** between two consecutive crests → this is the **Wavelength ($\lambda$)**.
- A **delay** (the moment the wave crest first reaches you relative to when it was generated out at sea) → this is the **Phase ($\phi$)**.

In the human body, **bioelectric waves** behave identically:
- The **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** is a quasi-periodic wave with $f \approx 1\text{–}2\,\text{Hz}$.
- The **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** consists of waves classified by their frequency bands: Delta ($0.5\text{–}4\,\text{Hz}$), Theta ($4\text{–}8\,\text{Hz}$), Alpha ($8\text{–}13\,\text{Hz}$), Beta ($13\text{–}30\,\text{Hz}$), Gamma ($>30\,\text{Hz}$).
- **EMG (Electromyogram)** signals extend up to several kHz.

Thus, every bioelectric signal is, at its core, a **superposition of sinusoidal waves** — making the study of waves a foundational prerequisite for ALL of signal processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Metrics to Memorize:**
> - Frequency $f$ → **Hertz (Hz)**
> - Angular frequency $\omega$ → **radians/second (rad/s)**
> - Period $T$ → **seconds (s)**
> - Wavelength $\lambda$ → **metres (m)**
> - Wave number $k$ → **radians/metre (rad/m)**
> - Velocity $v$ → **metres/second (m/s)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Sinusoidal Wave

A complete sinusoidal signal is defined by exactly **seven independent parameters**. We now dissect each one with its physical meaning, mathematical role, and engineering utility.

#### 2.1.1 Amplitude ($A$)
- **Definition:** The peak (maximum) displacement of the wave from its equilibrium (zero) value.
- **For bio-signals:** In ECG, amplitude is measured in **millivolts (mV)**; in EEG, in **microvolts ($\mu$V)**.
- **Peak-to-Peak Amplitude ($A_{pp}$):** Distance from the highest crest to the lowest trough: $A_{pp} = 2A$.
- **RMS (Root-Mean-Square) Amplitude:** $A_{rms} = \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$. This is the DC-equivalent power measure.

#### 2.1.2 Frequency ($f$) and Angular Frequency ($\omega$)
- **Frequency** $f$ → number of complete oscillations per second, measured in **Hz**.
- **Angular Frequency** $\omega$ → rate of change of phase in **radians per second**.

The conversion is:

$$\omega = 2\pi f \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

#### 2.1.3 Period ($T$)
- **Definition:** Time taken for one full cycle.

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

- **For ECG:** $T \approx 0.83$ s (at 72 bpm).
- **For EEG Alpha waves:** $T \approx 0.08$ – $0.125$ s.

#### 2.1.4 Wavelength ($\lambda$) and Wave Number ($k$)
- **Wavelength** $\lambda$ → spatial period (distance over which the wave pattern repeats).
- **Wave Number** $k$ → spatial angular frequency (radians per metre).

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k}$$

#### 2.1.5 Phase ($\phi$)
- **Definition:** The initial angular offset of the waveform at $t=0$.
- A wave with $\phi = 0$ starts at zero, going *up*. A wave with $\phi = \pi/2$ starts at its **positive peak**.

#### 2.1.6 Phase Velocity ($v$)
- The speed at which a point of constant phase (e.g., a wave crest) propagates.

$$v = f \lambda = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

- For **electromagnetic waves** (and hence all bioelectric potentials propagating along nerves): $v \approx c$ in vacuum and $v = c/n$ in tissue (where $n$ is the refractive index).
- For **sound in tissue:** $v \approx 1540$ m/s (used in medical ultrasound).

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Parameter

- **Why amplitude matters:** Determines signal strength and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
- **Why frequency matters:** Determines which physiological source generated the signal (e.g., alpha vs delta EEG rhythms).
- **Why phase matters:** Critical in coherent detection, beamforming, and inter-channel correlation analysis (e.g., coherence between left/right hemispheres in EEG).
- **How waves propagate:** Energy is transferred via **oscillation of local field/potential**, not bulk matter transport.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sinusoidal Signal | $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | V or mV | Instantaneous amplitude |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | Rate of phase change |
| Frequency | $f = 1/T$ | Hz | Cycles per second |
| Period | $T = 2\pi / \omega$ | s | Time per cycle |
| Wave Number | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | rad/m | Spatial angular frequency |
| Wavelength | $\lambda = v / f$ | m | Spatial period |
| Phase Velocity | $v = f \lambda = \omega / k$ | m/s | Crest propagation speed |
| Wave Equation | $\partial^{2} y / \partial x^{2} = (1/v^{2}) \, \partial^{2} y / \partial t^{2}$ | — | Spatiotemporal relation |
| Peak-to-Peak Amplitude | $A_{pp} = 2A$ | V | Crest-to-trough distance |
| RMS Amplitude | $A_{rms} = A / \sqrt{2}$ | V | DC-equivalent amplitude |
| Complex Exponential | $x(t) = A e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}$ | V | Euler/Phasor form |
| Phasor | $\mathbf{X} = A e^{j\phi} = A \angle \phi$ | V | Time-invariant complex amplitude |
| Energy Density | $u = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \omega^{2} A^{2}$ | J/m³ | Energy per unit volume |
| Average Power | $P_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2} A^{2} / R$ | W | Dissipated power across $R$ |
| Superposition | $y(t) = y_1(t) + y_2(t)$ | — | Linear additivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every linear time-invariant (LTI) system in biomedical engineering — from simple RC filters used in ECG front-ends to digital FIR filters removing 50 Hz powerline interference — is characterized by its **frequency response** (gain vs. frequency) and **phase response**, both of which are direct consequences of treating signals as superposed sinusoidal waves.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Generic Sinusoid to All Derived Parameters

We start with the general travelling wave in 1D:

$$y(x, t) = A \, \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the period in time.**  
For a fixed location $x = x_0$, $y$ must return to its original value when $t$ increases by $T$:

$$A \sin(kx_0 - \omega(t+T) + \phi) = A \sin(kx_0 - \omega t + \phi)$$

This requires the phase to increase by $2\pi$:

$$-\omega T = -2\pi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the period in space (wavelength).**  
For a fixed time $t = t_0$, $y$ must repeat when $x$ increases by $\lambda$:

$$A \sin(k(x+\lambda) - \omega t_0 + \phi) = A \sin(kx - \omega t_0 + \phi)$$

This requires the phase to increase by $2\pi$:

$$k \lambda = 2\pi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the phase velocity.**  
A point of constant phase (e.g., a crest) satisfies $kx - \omega t + \phi = \text{const}$. Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$k \, \frac{dx}{dt} - \omega = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{k}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Express velocity in terms of $f$ and $\lambda$.**  
Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $k = 2\pi / \lambda$:

$$v = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f \lambda$$

$$\boxed{\,v = f \lambda\,}$$

This is the **universal wave-speed equation**, valid for *all* waves — sound, light, ocean, and bioelectric action potentials.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Wave Equation (D'Alembert's Equation)

We start from the travelling wave solution $y(x,t) = f(x - vt)$. Compute the partial derivatives needed.

**Step 1 — First derivative with respect to $t$:**

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = -v \, f'(x - vt)$$

**Step 2 — Second derivative with respect to $t$:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial t^{2}} = v^{2} \, f''(x - vt)$$

**Step 3 — First derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = f'(x - vt)$$

**Step 4 — Second derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(x - vt)$$

**Step 5 — Form the ratio:**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y / \partial t^{2}}{\partial^{2} y / \partial x^{2}} = \frac{v^{2} f''(x-vt)}{f''(x-vt)} = v^{2}$$

Rearranging gives the canonical **1-D wave equation**:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{v^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial t^{2}}\,}$$

This PDE is linear, second-order, and admits *any* sum of forward and backward travelling waves as a solution. In bioelectric contexts (Hodgkin–Huxley cable equation, nerve impulse propagation), the wave equation is the starting point of all action-potential models.

### 3.3 Derivation: RMS Value of a Sinusoidal Wave

The RMS (root-mean-square) value of a periodic signal $x(t)$ of period $T$ is defined as:

$$x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x^{2}(t) \, dt}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$:**

$$x_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} A^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Use the power-reduction identity** $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} \left(1 - \cos(2\theta)\right)$:

$$x_{rms}^{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral** (note that $\int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t) \, dt = 0$ because $2\omega T = 4\pi$):

$$x_{rms}^{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$\boxed{\,x_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \, A\,}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Energy and Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

Consider a sinusoid $v(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ applied across a resistor $R$. The instantaneous power is $p(t) = v^{2}(t)/R$.

| **Quantity** | **Expression** | **Value for $A=10$ V, $R=1\,\Omega$** |
|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous Voltage $v(t)$ | $A \sin(\omega t)$ | $10 \sin(\omega t)$ V |
| Peak Voltage $V_{peak}$ | $A$ | **10 V** |
| Peak-to-Peak $V_{pp}$ | $2A$ | **20 V** |
| Instantaneous Power $p(t)$ | $A^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) / R$ | $100 \sin^{2}(\omega t)$ W |
| Maximum Power $P_{max}$ | $A^{2} / R$ | **100 W** |
| Average Power $P_{avg}$ | $A^{2} / (2R) = (A_{rms})^{2} / R$ | **50 W** |
| RMS Voltage $V_{rms}$ | $A / \sqrt{2}$ | **7.071 V** |
| Energy over one period | $P_{avg} \cdot T$ | $50T$ J |

### 3.5 Derivation: Euler's Identity and Phasor Representation

A sinusoidal signal can be elegantly recast using **Euler's formula**:

$$e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$$

**Step 1 — Express $\cos$ and $\sin$ in terms of complex exponentials:**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}, \qquad \sin\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$:**

$$x(t) = \frac{A}{2} \left[e^{j(\omega t + \phi)} + e^{-j(\omega t + \phi)}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Define the phasor** $\mathbf{X} = A e^{j\phi}$:

$$x(t) = \text{Re}\left\{\mathbf{X} \, e^{j\omega t}\right\}$$

This **phasor representation** collapses the time dependence, leaving a single complex number $\mathbf{X}$ that encodes both amplitude $A$ and phase $\phi$. It is the cornerstone of AC circuit analysis, Fourier transforms, and coherent demodulation of biosignals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip:** In the phasor domain, differentiation becomes multiplication by $j\omega$ and integration by $1/(j\omega)$, which dramatically simplifies LTI system analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of a Sinusoidal Wave

The following diagram visually decomposes a single sinusoid into its constituent parameters. Every node is given a clean alphanumeric identifier and a plain-text label (no markdown formatting inside labels, per the engine's safeguard protocol).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["SINUSOIDAL WAVE x of t"]
    nodeB["AMPLITUDE A"]
    nodeC["FREQUENCY f in HZ"]
    nodeD["ANGULAR FREQ OMEGA = 2PI F"]
    nodeE["PERIOD T = 1 OVER F"]
    nodeF["WAVELENGTH LAMBDA in M"]
    nodeG["WAVE NUMBER K = 2PI OVER LAMBDA"]
    nodeH["PHASE PHI in RADIANS"]
    nodeI["PHASE VELOCITY V = F LAMBDA"]
    nodeJ["WAVE EQUATION D2Y DX2 = 1 OVER V2 TIMES D2Y DT2"]
    nodeK["PHASOR FORM X = A EXP J PHI"]
    nodeL["RMS VALUE A OVER ROOT 2"]
    nodeM["ENERGY DENSITY U = 1 OVER 2 RHO OMEGA2 A2"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeA --> nodeI
    nodeA --> nodeJ
    nodeA --> nodeK
    nodeA --> nodeL
    nodeA --> nodeM

    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeB --> nodeL
    nodeB --> nodeM
    nodeH --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wave Processing in a Bioelectric Front-End

This block diagram illustrates how the theoretical wave concepts of **amplitude, frequency, phase, and velocity** are operationalized inside a typical biomedical signal-acquisition system (e.g., ECG machine).

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["BIOELECTRIC SOURCE HEART OR BRAIN"]
    electrode["ELECTRODES OR SENSORS"]
    ampStage["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER GAIN SET BY A"]
    filtLP["LOW PASS FILTER CUTOFF F MAX"]
    filtHP["HIGH PASS FILTER CUTOFF F MIN"]
    filtNotch["NOTCH FILTER 50 OR 60 HZ POWERLINE"]
    adc["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER SAMPLING FS"]
    proc["DIGITAL PROCESSOR FFT PHASE ANALYSIS"]
    disp["DISPLAY OR DIAGNOSIS"]

    src -->|"WAVE PROPAGATES VELOCITY V"| electrode
    electrode -->|"AMPLITUDE A MICROVOLTS TO MILLIVOLTS"| ampStage
    ampStage --> filtHP
    filtHP --> filtLP
    filtLP --> filtNotch
    filtNotch -->|"BANDWIDTH LIMITED SIGNAL"| adc
    adc -->|"DIGITAL WAVE ARRAY"| proc
    proc -->|"SPECTROGRAM PHASE MAP"| disp
```

### 4.3 ASCII Visualisation of Wave Parameters (Supplementary)

Although Mermaid is the primary diagrammatic tool, a textual visualisation of one full cycle helps cement the parameter definitions:

```
      A (Amplitude)
      │       ╭───╮
      │      ╱     ╲
      │     ╱       ╲
      │    ╱         ╲
──────┼───╱───────────╲─────────── Equilibrium
      │  ╱             ╲
      │ ╱               ╲
      │╱                 ╲
      └────┬─────┬─────┬─────► time t (s)
           0    T/2    T
           │◄──►│
            T/4
      ◄──── T (Period) ────►
      ◄──────── λ (Wavelength, spatial analog) ──────►
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often write frequency in rad/s without converting to Hz (or vice-versa). Always state the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Phase confusion:** $\sin$ and $\cos$ differ by a $90°$ phase shift. Don't blindly equate $A\sin(\omega t)$ to $A\cos(\omega t)$.
> 3. **Sign error in wave equation:** The 1-D wave equation is $\partial^{2} y / \partial x^{2} = (1/v^{2}) \, \partial^{2} y / \partial t^{2}$, *not* the other way round.
> 4. **Phasor $j$ vs. $i$:** Engineers use $j = \sqrt{-1}$ to avoid confusion with current $i$. Losing this convention costs a mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the $2\pi$:** Converting $f$ to $\omega$ requires a factor of $2\pi$. This is the **single most common arithmetic error** in KTU answer scripts.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define a sinusoidal wave. List any four characteristic parameters of a sinusoidal wave.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A sinusoidal wave is a periodic, smooth, oscillating waveform described mathematically by the function $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ or $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. It is the fundamental building block of all linear signal-processing theory.

The four characteristic parameters are:
1. **Amplitude ($A$)** — peak displacement from equilibrium (Volts).
2. **Frequency ($f$)** — number of cycles per second (Hertz).
3. **Period ($T = 1/f$)** — time for one complete cycle (seconds).
4. **Phase ($\phi$)** — initial angular offset at $t = 0$ (radians).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for parameters with units.)*

---

#### **Q2. State the one-dimensional wave equation and explain the significance of its solution.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The 1-D wave equation is:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y(x,t)}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{v^{2}} \, \frac{\partial^{2} y(x,t)}{\partial t^{2}}$$

**Significance:** The general solution is $y(x,t) = f(x - vt) + g(x + vt)$, representing a **forward-travelling wave** and a **backward-travelling wave** moving at speed $v$ without distortion. It models how disturbances propagate in time and space — fundamental to understanding nerve-impulse transmission, acoustic ultrasound, and electromagnetic bioelectric signals.

*(3 marks — 1 for equation, 1 for solution, 1 for significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship $v = f \lambda$ for a sinusoidal wave, clearly defining each term. Show that the wave function $y(x,t) = A\sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$ satisfies the 1-D wave equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG signal has a fundamental frequency of $1.2$ Hz. Calculate (i) its angular frequency, (ii) its period, and (iii) if the wave is observed propagating along the heart's conduction tissue at $4$ m/s, find the wavelength. The peak amplitude is $1.5$ mV — find the RMS value. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define each term.**
- $v$ = phase velocity (m/s)
- $f$ = frequency (Hz)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $A$ = amplitude
- $k$ = wave number (rad/m)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $\phi$ = phase constant (rad)

**Step 2 — Derive $v = f \lambda$.**

From the wave $y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$, a point of constant phase satisfies $kx - \omega t = \text{const}$. Differentiating:

$$k \frac{dx}{dt} - \omega = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $k = 2\pi / \lambda$:

$$v = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f \lambda \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3 — Verify the wave equation.** Compute partial derivatives of $y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = A k \cos(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial x^{2}} = -A k^{2} \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = -A \omega \cos(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial t^{2}} = -A \omega^{2} \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$

Form the ratio:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y / \partial x^{2}}{\partial^{2} y / \partial t^{2}} = \frac{-A k^{2} \sin(\cdot)}{-A \omega^{2} \sin(\cdot)} = \frac{k^{2}}{\omega^{2}} = \frac{1}{v^{2}}$$

Hence the wave satisfies:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{v^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} y}{\partial t^{2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f = 1.2$ Hz, $v = 4$ m/s, $A = 1.5$ mV.

**(i) Angular frequency:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2 \pi (1.2) = 2.4\pi \approx 7.5398 \,\text{rad/s}$$

**(ii) Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1.2} \approx 0.8333 \,\text{s}$$

**(iii) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{4}{1.2} \approx 3.3333 \,\text{m}$$

**RMS amplitude:**

$$A_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.0607 \,\text{mV}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **phasor representation** of a sinusoidal wave. Starting from Euler's identity, show that $A\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \text{Re}\{A e^{j\phi} e^{j\omega t}\}$. State two engineering advantages of phasor representation in biomedical instrumentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EEG alpha wave has $f = 10$ Hz and $A = 50 \,\mu$V. The signal passes through an amplifier of gain $100$. Calculate (i) the amplified amplitude, (ii) the period, and (iii) the wavelength if the wave is an electromagnetic wave propagating through brain tissue with $n = 1.4$ (refractive index). Take $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Euler's identity:**

$$e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $A e^{j\phi}$ and take the real part:**

$$A e^{j(\omega t + \phi)} = A \cos(\omega t + \phi) + j A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Taking the real part:

$$\text{Re}\left\{A e^{j\phi} \, e^{j\omega t}\right\} = A \cos(\omega t + \phi) \quad \blacksquare$$

The complex number $\mathbf{X} = A e^{j\phi}$ is called the **phasor**, and it compactly encodes the amplitude and phase.

**Step 3 — Engineering advantages:**
1. **Algebraic simplification:** Time-domain differentiation $d/dt$ becomes multiplication by $j\omega$ in the phasor domain, allowing ordinary algebraic (rather than differential) analysis of LTI systems such as bioelectric amplifiers and filters.
2. **Direct measurement of amplitude & phase:** Coherent detectors (lock-in amplifiers used in evoked-response EEG/ERPs) can extract both magnitude and phase of a buried bio-signal, dramatically improving SNR.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f = 10$ Hz, $A = 50 \,\mu$V, gain $G = 100$, $n = 1.4$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**(i) Amplified amplitude:**

$$A_{out} = G \cdot A = 100 \times 50 = 5000 \,\mu\text{V} = 5 \,\text{mV}$$

**(ii) Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \,\text{s}$$

**(iii) Wavelength:**

First compute the velocity in tissue: $v = c / n = (3 \times 10^{8}) / 1.4 \approx 2.1429 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

Then:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{2.1429 \times 10^{8}}{10} = 2.1429 \times 10^{7} \,\text{m} = 21{,}429 \,\text{km}$$

*(Note: This astronomically large wavelength is why EM bioelectric fields can be treated as quasi-static at EEG frequencies — a key point in volume-conduction theory.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **sinusoidal wave** is the simplest periodic signal: $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$.
- [x] **Amplitude $A$** = peak deviation; **RMS** = $A/\sqrt{2}$; **Peak-to-Peak** = $2A$.
- [x] **Frequency $f$ (Hz)** and **Angular frequency $\omega$ (rad/s)** are related by $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- [x] **Period** $T = 1/f$; **Wavelength** $\lambda = v/f$; **Wave number** $k = 2\pi / \lambda$.
- [x] **Phase velocity** $v = f\lambda = \omega / k$ — universal for all wave types.
- [x] The **1-D wave equation** is $\partial^{2} y/\partial x^{2} = (1/v^{2}) \, \partial^{2} y/\partial t^{2}$, with general solution $y = f(x-vt) + g(x+vt)$.
- [x] **Euler's identity** converts sines/cosines to complex exponentials: $\cos\theta = \text{Re}\{e^{j\theta}\}$.
- [x] **Phasor** $\mathbf{X} = A e^{j\phi}$ — encodes amplitude and phase in a single complex number.
- [x] The **principle of superposition** allows complex bio-signals to be decomposed into sinusoidal components — the foundation of Fourier analysis.
- [x] **EEG bands to remember:** Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (>30 Hz).
- [x] **Speed of sound in tissue** $\approx 1540$ m/s (used in ultrasound imaging).
- [x] **Speed of EM wave in tissue** $= c/n$ where $n \approx 1.4$ for brain.
- [x] Average power of a sinusoid across $R$ is $P_{avg} = A^{2}/(2R)$.
- [x] Always **state units** in KTU numerical answers — missing units = lost marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Duration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biosignals and Signal Processing**, **Duration** refers to the *temporal extent* — that is, the length of the time interval over which a biosignal is defined, non-zero, or clinically significant. Mathematically, the duration $D_x$ of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$D_x = T_2 - T_1$$

where $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the starting and ending time instants of the signal, respectively. For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$, the duration is expressed in terms of number of samples $N$:

$$D_x = N \cdot T_s$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period (in seconds) and $N$ is the total number of samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In biosignal processing, "Duration" is not a single parameter — it is a **class of measurements** applied to physiological events. For instance, ECG (electrocardiogram) uses QRS duration, PR interval, QT interval; EEG (electroencephalogram) uses epoch duration; EMG (electromyogram) uses burst duration. Each has diagnostic value.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **recording a song** on your phone. The "duration" of the song is the time from the moment the first note plays to the moment the last note fades out — say, **3 minutes 45 seconds**. Now think of your **heartbeat** as a "song" — the duration of one heartbeat is the time from one R-peak to the next R-peak (called the **R-R interval**), typically around **0.8 to 1.0 seconds** for a healthy adult at rest. Biosignal duration works the same way: it measures **how long** a particular biological event lasts on the timeline.

## 1.3 Classification of Duration

| Classification | Description | Example in Biosignals |
|---|---|---|
| **Finite Duration** | Signal is non-zero only over a finite interval $[T_1, T_2]$ | A single ECG QRS complex (~80–120 ms) |
| **Infinite Duration** | Signal extends indefinitely in time | Continuous EEG recording, heart rate variability (HRV) trends |
| **Compact Support** | Signal is non-zero on a closed, bounded interval and zero elsewhere | Windowed EMG burst |
| **Periodic Duration** | Signal repeats with period $T_0$ | Respiratory cycle (~4–5 seconds) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Constants (Adult Resting Values):**
> - **Heart Rate (HR):** 60–100 bpm → R-R duration = 0.6–1.0 s
> - **QRS Duration:** 80–120 ms
> - **PR Interval:** 120–200 ms
> - **QT Interval:** 350–450 ms
> - **EEG Alpha Rhythm Period:** ~100 ms (frequency ~10 Hz)
> - **Respiratory Cycle Duration:** 4–5 s
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations of Signal Duration

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time Signal Duration

A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is said to have **finite duration** if there exist finite real numbers $T_1 < T_2$ such that:

$$x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad t < T_1 \quad \text{and} \quad t > T_2$$

The duration is then computed as:

$$D = T_2 - T_1 \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time Signal Duration

For a discrete signal $x[n]$ sampled at sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$:

$$D = (N-1) \cdot T_s = \frac{N-1}{f_s} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

where $N$ is the total number of samples in the recording window.

### 2.1.3 Duration of Energy vs Power Signals

This is a **high-yield KTU concept**:

- An **energy signal** has finite total energy $E < \infty$ AND zero average power $P = 0$. Almost all finite-duration signals are energy signals.
- A **power signal** has finite average power $P > 0$ and infinite energy $E \to \infty$. Periodic signals and infinitely long recordings are power signals.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for biosignals?** A 10-second ECG strip is a finite-duration energy signal. A 24-hour Holter recording is treated as a power signal (or segmented into finite-duration epochs for analysis).

## 2.2 Duration of Key Biosignal Components

### 2.2.1 ECG Duration Metrics

| Parameter | Definition | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P-wave duration** | Atrial depolarization time | 80–110 ms | Atrial enlargement if prolonged |
| **PR interval** | AV nodal conduction time | 120–200 ms | AV block if prolonged |
| **QRS duration** | Ventricular depolarization time | 80–120 ms | Bundle branch block if >120 ms |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular activity | 350–450 ms | Risk of arrhythmia if prolonged |
| **R-R interval** | One complete cardiac cycle | 0.6–1.0 s | Heart rate = 60/R-R (in seconds) |

### 2.2.2 Duration in EEG

EEG is analyzed in **epochs** — fixed-duration windows (commonly **2 seconds, 4 seconds, or 10 seconds**). The epoch duration must be chosen to satisfy:

$$T_{\text{epoch}} \geq \frac{1}{f_{\min}}$$

where $f_{\min}$ is the lowest frequency of interest. For delta waves (0.5–4 Hz), the epoch must be at least 2 seconds to capture at least one full cycle.

### 2.2.3 Duration in EMG

EMG signals are characterized by:
- **Burst duration:** The time during which muscle is actively contracting (typically 100 ms to several seconds).
- **Muscle action potential duration:** 2–10 ms for motor unit action potentials (MUAPs).

## 2.3 Duration in Signal Transformation Domains

The duration of a signal is closely related to its **bandwidth** through the **time-frequency uncertainty principle**:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

This means a **shorter duration** signal has a **wider bandwidth**, and vice versa. For example, the sharp QRS complex (~80 ms) has frequency content up to ~150 Hz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Duration analysis is the first step in **windowing**, **segmentation**, **feature extraction**, and **event detection** in any biosignal processing pipeline. Choosing the correct window duration is critical — too short and you lose information; too long and you compromise stationarity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = T_2 - T_1$ | Continuous-time signal duration | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $D = (N-1)T_s$ | Discrete-time signal duration | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $D = (N-1)/f_s$ | Duration in terms of sampling frequency | seconds (s) |
| 4 | $\text{HR} = 60/D_{RR}$ | Heart rate from R-R duration | beats per minute (bpm) |
| 5 | $E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Energy of finite-duration signal | joules (J) |
| 6 | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Power of infinite-duration signal | watts (W) |
| 7 | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | Time-frequency uncertainty relation | dimensionless product |
| 8 | $T_{\text{epoch}} \geq 1/f_{\min}$ | Minimum epoch duration for spectral analysis | seconds (s) |
| 9 | $N_{\text{cycle}} = D \cdot f_0$ | Number of cycles in duration $D$ at frequency $f_0$ | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heart Rate from R-R Interval Duration

**Given:** An ECG recording shows a QRS peak at $t_1 = 1.20$ s and the next QRS peak at $t_2 = 2.00$ s. Calculate the heart rate in bpm.

**Step 1: Identify the duration.**
The R-R interval is the duration between two consecutive R-peaks:

$$D_{RR} = t_2 - t_1$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$D_{RR} = 2.00 - 1.20 = 0.80 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the heart rate formula.**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{D_{RR}}$$

The factor 60 converts beats per second to beats per minute.

**Step 4: Compute the result.**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{0.80} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 5: Interpret clinically.**
A heart rate of **75 bpm** falls within the **normal resting range of 60–100 bpm**. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Epoch Duration for EEG Spectral Analysis

**Given:** We wish to perform FFT analysis on an EEG signal to study the delta rhythm (0.5–4 Hz). The lowest frequency of interest is $f_{\min} = 0.5$ Hz. Determine the minimum epoch duration required.

**Step 1: State the principle.**
To capture at least **one full cycle** of the lowest frequency, the epoch must satisfy:

$$T_{\text{epoch}} \geq \frac{1}{f_{\min}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $f_{\min} = 0.5$ Hz.**

$$T_{\text{epoch}} \geq \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: For better frequency resolution, we typically use 5–10 cycles.**

$$T_{\text{recommended}} = \frac{5}{f_{\min}} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Conclude.**
A **2-second** epoch is the theoretical minimum, but a **10-second** epoch is practically recommended for reliable delta-band power estimation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy of a Rectangular ECG Pulse

**Given:** A simplified ECG R-wave is modeled as a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A = 1$ mV, starting at $t_1 = 0$ s and ending at $t_2 = 0.08$ s. Calculate its total energy.

**Step 1: Write the energy formula for a finite-duration signal.**

$$E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2: Substitute $x(t) = A$ over $[0, 0.08]$ s.**

$$E = \int_{0}^{0.08} A^2 \, dt = A^2 \cdot (0.08 - 0)$$

**Step 3: Substitute $A = 1$ mV $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ V.**

$$E = (1 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 0.08 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.08 = 8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ V}^2 \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 4: Result.**

$$E = 8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J (normalized to 1 Ω)}$$

**Step 5: Interpret.**
The finite duration ($D = 80$ ms) ensures the energy is finite — confirming the pulse is an **energy signal** (not a power signal).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Samples in a Given Duration

**Given:** A biosignal is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. The signal duration is $D = 10$ s. Find the total number of samples $N$.

**Step 1: Use the duration formula.**

$$D = (N-1) T_s = \frac{N-1}{f_s}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $N$.**

$$N - 1 = D \cdot f_s$$
$$N = D \cdot f_s + 1$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$N = 10 \times 500 + 1 = 5000 + 1 = 5001 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4: Verify.**
At 500 samples per second, in 10 seconds we expect $500 \times 10 = 5000$ intervals. Since samples are counted at endpoints, we get 5001 samples. ✅

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Duration vs Other Signal Properties

| Property | If Duration is Short | If Duration is Long |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | Wide (uncertainty principle) | Narrow |
| **Energy** | Low (for fixed amplitude) | High |
| **Power** | Approaches zero (energy signal) | Approaches constant (power signal) |
| **Frequency Resolution** (in FFT) | Poor | Good |
| **Time Resolution** | High | Low |
| **Suitability for Stationarity Analysis** | Better (assumed stationary) | Worse (may need segmentation) |
| **Storage Requirement** | Low | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Duration Measurement Pipeline for ECG

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal x_t] --> B[Bandpass Filtering 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B --> C[R-Peak Detection Algorithm]
    C --> D{R-Peak Found?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Store Timestamp t_n]
    D -- No --> C
    E --> F[Compute R-R Duration D_RR = t_n - t_n-1]
    F --> G[Compute Heart Rate HR = 60 / D_RR]
    G --> H[Compute QRS Duration]
    H --> I[Clinical Decision: Normal or Abnormal]
    I --> J[Output: Duration Metrics and HR]
```

**Description of the flow:**
- The raw ECG is first filtered to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise.
- The Pan-Tompkins or similar algorithm detects R-peaks.
- Each consecutive pair of R-peaks yields the **R-R duration**.
- Heart rate, QRS duration, and other interval metrics are computed and passed to a clinical decision module.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: EEG Epoch Duration Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous EEG Recording] --> B{Choose Frequency Band of Interest}
    B --> C[Delta: 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    B --> D[Theta: 4 to 8 Hz]
    B --> E[Alpha: 8 to 13 Hz]
    B --> F[Beta: 13 to 30 Hz]
    C --> G[Compute T_epoch = 5 / f_min = 10 s]
    D --> H[Compute T_epoch = 5 / 4 = 1.25 s]
    E --> I[Compute T_epoch = 5 / 8 = 0.625 s]
    F --> J[Compute T_epoch = 5 / 13 = 0.385 s]
    G --> K[Segment EEG into 10s Epochs]
    H --> L[Segment EEG into 1.25s Epochs]
    I --> M[Segment EEG into 0.625s Epochs]
    J --> N[Segment EEG into 0.385s Epochs]
    K --> O[Apply Windowing and FFT]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Spectral Power per Band]
```

**Insight:** Lower frequency analysis requires **longer epoch durations**. This is a direct consequence of the time-frequency uncertainty principle.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Finite vs Infinite Duration Signals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Finite["Finite Duration Signal (Energy Signal)"]
        A1[Time = 0] --> A2[Signal Active for D seconds]
        A2 --> A3[Time = D, Signal = 0]
    end
    subgraph Infinite["Infinite Duration Signal (Power Signal)"]
        B1[Time to Negative Infinity] --> B2[Signal Continues Forever]
        B2 --> B3[Time to Positive Infinity]
    end
    subgraph Periodic["Periodic Signal (Power Signal)"]
        C1[One Period T0] --> C2[Repeats Every T0]
        C2 --> C3[Infinite Repetition]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Safeguard Note

All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `Finite`) and **none** use reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold, italics, or HTML formatting inside the quoted strings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the duration of a signal. Differentiate between finite duration and infinite duration signals with a biosignal example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The duration of a signal is the time interval over which the signal is defined or non-zero, given by $D = T_2 - T_1$.
- **Finite duration (1 mark):** A signal that is non-zero only over a finite interval $[T_1, T_2]$ and zero elsewhere. *Example:* A single QRS complex in ECG (~80–120 ms).
- **Infinite duration (1 mark):** A signal that extends indefinitely in time. *Example:* A continuous 24-hour Holter ECG recording or an ongoing EEG stream.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**An ECG signal is sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz. A 10-second recording is taken. Calculate (a) the sampling period and (b) the total number of samples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (a) Sampling period: $T_s = 1/f_s = 1/360 = 2.778$ ms **(1 mark)**
- (b) Total samples: $N = D \cdot f_s + 1 = 10 \times 360 + 1 = 3601$ samples **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Define the duration of a continuous-time and a discrete-time biosignal. Derive the relationship between duration, number of samples, and sampling frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The R-R interval in an ECG is measured as 0.75 s. Two other consecutive R-R intervals are 0.80 s and 0.85 s. Calculate (i) the heart rate for each interval, and (ii) the average heart rate. Comment on the clinical significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions and derivation (7 marks):**

For a continuous-time signal $x(t)$, duration is the interval over which the signal is non-zero:

$$D = T_2 - T_1 \quad \text{where} \quad x(t) \neq 0 \text{ for } t \in [T_1, T_2]$$

For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ sampled at $f_s = 1/T_s$ Hz, the time between sample $n_1$ and sample $n_2$ is:

$$D = (n_2 - n_1) \cdot T_s$$

For a complete recording of $N$ samples (from $n=0$ to $n=N-1$):

$$D = (N-1) T_s = \frac{N-1}{f_s}$$

Solving for $N$:

$$N = D \cdot f_s + 1$$

This relationship is fundamental because it links **time-domain duration** with **storage and processing requirements** in digital biosignal systems. **(Full 7 marks: 2 for definitions + 5 for derivation)**

**(b) Heart rate calculations (7 marks):**

Using the formula $\text{HR} = 60 / D_{RR}$:

(i) **Interval 1: $D_{RR} = 0.75$ s**
$$\text{HR}_1 = \frac{60}{0.75} = 80 \text{ bpm}$$

**Interval 2: $D_{RR} = 0.80$ s**
$$\text{HR}_2 = \frac{60}{0.80} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$

**Interval 3: $D_{RR} = 0.85$ s**
$$\text{HR}_3 = \frac{60}{0.85} = 70.59 \text{ bpm}$$

(ii) **Average heart rate:**

$$\text{HR}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{80 + 75 + 70.59}{3} = \frac{225.59}{3} = 75.20 \text{ bpm}$$

**Clinical comment (1 mark):**
All three heart rates fall within the normal resting range (60–100 bpm). However, the **gradual decrease** from 80 → 75 → 70.59 bpm suggests **heart rate variability (HRV)**, which is a marker of healthy autonomic nervous system function. The standard deviation of R-R intervals (SDNN) can be computed for further HRV analysis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the time-frequency uncertainty principle. Using this, justify why a shorter duration biosignal has a wider bandwidth. Give one example from ECG or EEG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an EEG study of the alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz, center frequency 10 Hz), determine (i) the minimum epoch duration to capture at least one full cycle, (ii) the recommended epoch duration for 5 cycles, and (iii) the number of samples per epoch if the sampling rate is $f_s = 256$ Hz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Time-frequency uncertainty principle (7 marks):**

The Heisenberg uncertainty principle, adapted to signals and systems, states that the product of the **effective duration** ($\Delta t$) and the **effective bandwidth** ($\Delta f$) of a signal has a lower bound:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

**Justification of short duration → wide bandwidth (5 marks):**

Since the product $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ cannot be arbitrarily small, if $\Delta t$ is small (short duration), then $\Delta f$ must be large to satisfy the inequality. Conversely, a long-duration signal can have a narrow bandwidth.

**Example:** The QRS complex in ECG has a duration of ~80 ms, which is very short. Consequently, it has a wide frequency spectrum extending up to 150 Hz. This is why ECG is typically sampled at ≥ 250 Hz to preserve the sharp QRS features. In contrast, the slower T-wave (~160 ms) has lower frequency content (~1–7 Hz).

**(b) EEG epoch duration calculations (7 marks):**

Given: $f_{\text{center}} = 10$ Hz, $f_{\min} = 8$ Hz (lowest in alpha band), $f_s = 256$ Hz.

(i) **Minimum epoch for 1 full cycle at $f_{\min} = 8$ Hz:**

$$T_{\min} = \frac{1}{f_{\min}} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \text{ s} = 125 \text{ ms}$$

(ii) **Recommended epoch for 5 cycles at $f_{\min} = 8$ Hz:**

$$T_{\text{rec}} = \frac{5}{f_{\min}} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625 \text{ s}$$

*(Alternatively, if cycles are at the center frequency 10 Hz: $T_{\text{rec}} = 5/10 = 0.5$ s — either interpretation is acceptable, but the boundary frequency is the safe choice.)*

(iii) **Number of samples per epoch:**

$$N = T_{\text{rec}} \cdot f_s + 1 = 0.625 \times 256 + 1 = 160 + 1 = 161 \text{ samples}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $N$ with $N-1$:** Students often write $N = D \cdot f_s$ instead of $N = D \cdot f_s + 1$. For short durations this matters; for long durations it's negligible, but the examiner expects the correct formula.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always state whether the duration is in seconds, milliseconds, or samples. Unit errors can cost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up energy vs power signals:** A finite-duration signal is an *energy signal* (finite E, zero P). An infinite-duration periodic signal is a *power signal* (zero E, finite P). This is a **favorite KTU question**.
> 4. **Heart rate formula sign error:** HR = 60/D_RR (when D_RR is in seconds). Some students invert it. ❌
> 5. **Not using $f_{\min}$ for epoch duration:** Always use the **lowest** frequency in the band of interest when computing the minimum epoch, not the center frequency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Duration** = the time interval over which a signal is defined or non-zero: $D = T_2 - T_1$.
- For **discrete signals**: $D = (N-1)/f_s$ and $N = D \cdot f_s + 1$.
- **Finite-duration signals** are **energy signals**; **infinite-duration periodic signals** are **power signals**.
- **ECG duration metrics:** P-wave (80–110 ms), PR (120–200 ms), QRS (80–120 ms), QT (350–450 ms), R-R (0.6–1.0 s).
- **Heart Rate** = 60 / R-R duration (in seconds), result in bpm.
- **EEG epoch duration** must satisfy $T_{\text{epoch}} \geq 1/f_{\min}$; recommended = 5 to 10 cycles.
- **Time-frequency uncertainty:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq 1/(4\pi)$ — short duration ↔ wide bandwidth.
- **Sampling theorem reminder:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist criterion) — duration alone doesn't determine $f_s$, but bandwidth does.
- Always carry **units** through every calculation; the examiner awards partial credit for correct units.
- The **uncertainty principle** is the bridge between time-domain duration and frequency-domain bandwidth — a **high-yield KTU concept**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intervals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intervals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2: Intervals in Biosignal Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Biosignals & Signal Processing** (specifically Electrocardiography), an **Interval** refers to a precisely defined temporal segment measured between two specific morphological fiducial points (reference landmarks) on a biosignal waveform. Unlike a simple "duration" between arbitrary points, an interval in the KTU 2024 syllabus context is anatomically and physiologically meaningful, representing the time taken for a specific electrophysiological event to occur within the cardiac cycle.

The standard cardiac intervals defined by the **American Heart Association (AHA)** and the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC 60601-2-25)** are:
- **RR Interval** ($T_{RR}$): Time between two successive R-peaks.
- **PR Interval** ($T_{PR}$): Time from the onset of the P-wave to the onset of the QRS complex.
- **QRS Duration** ($T_{QRS}$): Time from the onset of the Q-wave to the offset of the S-wave.
- **QT Interval** ($T_{QT}$): Time from the onset of the QRS complex to the offset of the T-wave.
- **ST Segment**: The isoelectric portion between the end of the S-wave and the beginning of the T-wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the OEEBT613 syllabus, "Intervals" is covered under Module 2 (ECG Signal Analysis). Students must distinguish between **segments** (which are isoelectric, e.g., ST segment) and **intervals** (which always contain at least one waveform).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **musical conductor's beat pattern** to understand cardiac intervals. As the heart beats, it does not produce a single "thump" — it produces a structured sequence of sounds. If you record the time between the **first beat of bar 1** and the **first beat of bar 2**, that is your **RR interval** (the dominant period of the signal).

Now, within that bar, the conductor gives a small preparatory gesture (**P-wave — atrial preparation**), a strong downbeat (**QRS complex — main ventricular contraction**), and then a sustained, fading note (**T-wave — ventricular recovery**). The time from the start of the preparatory gesture to the strong downbeat is the **PR interval**. The time from the strong downbeat to the fading note's end is the **QT interval**. By measuring these sub-events, a cardiologist can tell whether the orchestra (the heart) is playing in rhythm or if one section (atria, AV node, ventricles) is delayed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Clinical Metrics (Adult, Resting, 60-100 bpm):**
> - Normal Heart Rate: **60–100 bpm**
> - Normal PR Interval: **120–200 ms**
> - Normal QRS Duration: **80–120 ms**
> - Normal QT Interval: **350–450 ms**
> - Paper Speed (Standard): **25 mm/s** (i.e., **1 small box = 0.04 s**, **1 large box = 0.20 s**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Cardiac Intervals

The cardiac cycle is composed of distinct electrophysiological events. Each interval captures a specific portion of this cycle:

### A. RR Interval ($T_{RR}$)
- **Definition:** The temporal distance between the peaks of two successive R-waves.
- **Physiological Correlate:** Represents the duration of one complete cardiac cycle.
- **Primary Use:** Heart Rate computation and Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis.
- **Typical Range:** **0.6 s to 1.2 s** at rest.

### B. PR Interval ($T_{PR}$)
- **Definition:** From the beginning of the P-wave to the beginning of the QRS complex (regardless of whether the Q-wave is present; if absent, the start of the R-wave is used).
