### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: What is meant by the power spectral density (PSD) of an EEG signal? Explain how PSD is used to compute absolute and relative band powers of the alpha rhythm. Given a 1-second epoch with $f_s = 250$ Hz and a constant PSD value of 40 µV²/Hz in the 8–13 Hz band, find the absolute and relative alpha power, given total EEG power = 800 µV².**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of PSD (2 marks):**

The **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** $S_{xx}(f)$ of an EEG signal $x(t)$ describes how the signal's power is distributed across frequency. Formally:

$$
S_{xx}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}
$$

where $X_T(f)$ is the Fourier transform of a finite segment of length $T$. The PSD is estimated in practice using **Welch's method** (averaged periodograms over overlapping windows).

**2. Absolute Band Power (2 marks):**

$$
P_{band} = \int_{f_L}^{f_H} S_{xx}(f)\, df
$$

**3. Relative Band Power (1 mark):**

$$
P_{rel} = \frac{P_{band}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

**4. Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $S_{xx}(f) = 40\ \mu V^2/Hz$ (constant) in $f \in [8, 13]$ Hz, $P_{total} = 800\ \mu V^2$.

Absolute alpha power:

$$
P_{\alpha} = \int_{8}^{13} 40\, df = 40 \times (13 - 8) = 40 \times 5 = 200\ \mu V^2
$$

Relative alpha power:

$$
P_{\alpha,rel} = \frac{200}{800} \times 100\% = 25\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Alpha contributes 25% of the total EEG power — a typical value for an awake adult with eyes closed.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Explain the clinical significance of EEG rhythms. Discuss the EEG changes seen in (i) deep sleep, (ii) coma, and (iii) a focal brain lesion.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
EEG rhythms are powerful **non-invasive clinical biomarkers** of brain function. Abnormalities in frequency, amplitude, symmetry, or reactivity indicate neurological dysfunction.

**(i) Deep Sleep (2 marks):**
- NREM Stage N3 (slow-wave sleep) shows **dominant delta (0.5–4 Hz) activity** with amplitudes > **75 µV**, occupying > 20% of the epoch.
- Sleep spindles (sigma, 12–14 Hz) and K-complexes characterize Stage N2.
- Delta dominance reflects **hyperpolarization-driven thalamic burst-pacemaker activity**.

**(ii) Coma (2 marks):**
- Generalized, **non-reactive delta or theta activity** (often 1–4 Hz) dominates.
- **Loss of alpha reactivity** (no Berger effect) is a poor prognostic sign.
- Burst-suppression pattern (alternating high-voltage bursts and flat-line suppression) is seen in **deep anesthesia or severe encephalopathy**.
- Absence of all rhythms (electrocerebral silence / isoelectric EEG) at high sensitivity meets criteria for **brain death**, when corroborated clinically.

**(iii) Focal Brain Lesion (2 marks):**
- **Focal slowing**: delta or theta activity localized over the lesion (e.g., tumor, stroke, abscess).
- **Asymmetry**: PDR (alpha) amplitude asymmetry > 50% between hemispheres suggests ipsilateral lesion.
- **FIRDA** (Frontal Intermittent Rhythmic Delta Activity): bilateral synchronous frontal delta in deep midline lesions or increased ICP.
- **Epileptiform discharges** (spikes, sharp waves) may arise from irritated cortex around a lesion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing mu rhythm with alpha** — both are 8–13 Hz, but mu is **central, blocked by movement/motor imagery**, NOT by eye opening. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Writing "alpha = 8–12 Hz"** — the correct KTU/IFCN upper bound is **13 Hz**, not 12. (Lose 0.5 mark.)
> 3. **Forgetting units** in PSD problems — always write µV²/Hz for PSD and µV² for power. (Lose 0.5 mark.)
> 4. **Calling gamma "noise"** — gamma is a legitimate rhythm, not artifact. (Lose 1 mark if asked to list rhythms.)
> 5. **Saying "alpha occurs in sleep"** — alpha is a **wakeful** rhythm; sleep is dominated by delta/theta/spindles. (Common catastrophic error.)
> 6. **Missing the role of thalamus** — a 7-mark question on rhythm generation that omits thalamocortical loops will lose **2–3 marks**.

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Read Just Before Entering the Exam Hall** 🎯

- ✅ **Five rhythms mnemonic:** **"De-Th-Al-Be-Ga"** → **Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma** (ascending frequency).
- ✅ **Frequency ladder:** 0.5 → 4 → 8 → 13 → 30 → 100 Hz (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma).
- ✅ **Amplitude ladder (inverse):** Delta **highest** → Gamma **lowest**.
- ✅ **Alpha = eyes-closed, occipital, 8–13 Hz, ~20–60 µV** — known as the **PDR**.
- ✅ **Berger effect = alpha blocking** when eyes open.
- ✅ **Mu rhythm = 8–13 Hz, central, blocked by motor imagery** (NOT by eyes).
- ✅ **Delta = deep sleep, coma, encephalopathy** (pathological when awake).
- ✅ **Theta = drowsiness, REM, meditation, frontal-midline Fz/Cz**.
- ✅ **Beta = active thinking, frontal, low amplitude**.
- ✅ **Gamma = perception, memory, consciousness; reduced in schizophrenia**.
- ✅ **Generator = cortical pyramidal neurons (layers III, V)**; **Pacemaker = thalamus**.
- ✅ **Sampling rate ≥ 250 Hz** to safely capture gamma (Nyquist = 200 Hz).
- ✅ **PSD units = µV²/Hz; Band power units = µV²; Relative band power = %**.
- ✅ **Parseval's theorem:** $P_{total} = \int \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$ links time and frequency power.
- ✅ **Welch's method** is the standard PSD estimator (averaged periodograms).
- ✅ **Volume conduction:** skull attenuates signal and spatially low-pass filters.
- ✅ **Clinical flags:** focal delta (lesion), generalized delta (coma), burst-suppression (anesthesia), FIRDA (midline/↑ICP), beta spindles (benzodiazepines).
- ✅ **Alpha attenuation index (AAI)** ≥ 50% is normal reactivity.
- ✅ **FAA (Frontal Alpha Asymmetry)** = neuro-emotional biomarker.
- ✅ **Wavelength of delta (~3 m) ≫ brain size** → synchronously global; gamma (~15 cm) is local.
- ✅ **BCI mainstay = motor-imagery mu rhythm desynchronization (ERD/ERS)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biosignals and Signal Processing (OEEBT613)**, a *signal* is a function that conveys information about the behaviour or attributes of a physical phenomenon, typically varying with time, space, or another independent variable. The **characteristics of a signal** are the measurable physical and mathematical properties that completely describe its behaviour, allow its classification, and determine the appropriate acquisition, conditioning, and processing techniques to be applied.

For bioelectric signals such as **ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, and PCG**, these characteristics are crucial because they dictate the choice of electrodes, amplifiers, filters, and ADC sampling rates in any biomedical instrumentation system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Signal characteristics are quantitative descriptors — both in the **time domain** and the **frequency domain** — that uniquely identify a bio-signal and permit correct instrumentation design.

## 1.2 Primary Categories of Signal Characteristics

Signal characteristics are broadly classified into the following domains:

| Category | Description | Example Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude Characteristics** | Describe the magnitude/strength of the signal | Peak value, Peak-to-Peak, RMS |
| **Frequency Characteristics** | Describe the rate of variation / spectral content | Fundamental frequency, Bandwidth |
| **Power & Energy Characteristics** | Describe the signal's energy/power content | Signal Energy, Average Power |
| **Statistical Characteristics** | Describe the random behaviour of signals | Mean, Variance, PDF |
| **Performance Characteristics** | Describe acquisition system fidelity | SNR, CMRR, Dynamic Range |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Heartbeat as a Drummer:**
> Imagine your heart as a **drummer** in a concert hall. The *loudness* of each beat is the **amplitude**. How *fast* the drummer taps is the **frequency**. The *time gap* between beats is the **period**. If a child in the audience is also tapping randomly, that is the **noise**. The difference between the drummer's clean beat and the child's tapping is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. The drummer's *range of tempo* (slow ballads to fast rock) is the **bandwidth**. Thus, to record the drummer properly, you need a microphone sensitive enough (amplitude handling), fast enough to capture every beat (frequency response), and smart enough to reject the child's noise (CMRR/SNR).

## 1.4 Key Bio-Signal Reference Values (Standard Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Characteristics of Common Bioelectric Signals (Bold Constants for KTU Memorization):**

| Bio-Signal | Amplitude Range | Frequency Range | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | 150 Hz |
| **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** | **5 – 300 µV** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | 100 Hz |
| **EMG (Electromyogram)** | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **20 – 5000 Hz** | 5 kHz |
| **EOG (Electrooculogram)** | **10 µV – 5 mV** | **DC – 100 Hz** | 100 Hz |
| **PCG (Phonocardiogram)** | **Dynamic** | **20 – 2000 Hz** | 2 kHz |
| **PPG (Photoplethysmogram)** | **Variable** | **0.5 – 20 Hz** | 20 Hz |
| **Blood Pressure (BP)** | **10 – 400 mmHg** | **DC – 60 Hz** | 60 Hz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Signal Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Amplitude Characteristics

Amplitude characteristics describe the **magnitude** of a signal. For any time-domain signal $x(t)$:

- **Instantaneous Amplitude:** Value of the signal at a specific instant $t$. Useful for time-localized analysis.
- **Peak Amplitude ($A_m$ or $V_{max}$):** The maximum absolute value the signal attains in a defined observation window.
- **Peak-to-Peak Amplitude ($V_{pp}$):** The arithmetic difference between the maximum positive peak and the maximum negative peak — the *true* swing of the signal.
- **Average Value ($V_{avg}$):** The DC component or mean level of the signal over one period.
- **RMS Value ($V_{rms}$):** The *effective* value of the signal — equivalent DC value delivering the same power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RMS matters:** Most bio-instrumentation specifications (e.g., defibrillator energy, laser power, EEG amplitude) are quoted in RMS because RMS corresponds to the actual *physiological / heating effect* of the signal.

### 2.1.2 Frequency Characteristics

Frequency characteristics describe **how fast** the signal changes:

- **Fundamental Frequency ($f_0$):** The lowest frequency component of a periodic signal.
- **Harmonics:** Integer multiples $nf_0$ of the fundamental.
- **Spectral Content:** Distribution of energy across frequency (obtained via Fourier Transform).
- **Bandwidth (BW):** The continuous range of frequencies occupied by the signal, $B = f_H - f_L$.
- **Period ($T$):** Time taken for one complete cycle; $T = 1/f_0$.

### 2.1.3 Power and Energy Characteristics

- **Signal Energy ($E$):** Total normalized energy delivered by a signal across all time.
- **Average Power ($P$):** Time-averaged squared magnitude; for periodic signals, $P$ is computed over one period.
- **Power Signal vs Energy Signal:** Signals with $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$ are *power signals* (e.g., ECG). Signals with $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$ are *energy signals* (e.g., a single heart-beat transients).

### 2.1.4 Statistical Characteristics

- **Mean ($\mu$):** Average value; first statistical moment.
- **Variance ($\sigma^2$):** Spread of the signal about its mean; second central moment.
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** Square root of variance.
- **Probability Density Function (PDF):** Describes likelihood of signal taking a specific value.
- **Stationarity:** A signal is *stationary* if its statistical properties do not change with time.

### 2.1.5 Performance Characteristics (Instrumentation-Specific)

- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Ratio of signal power to noise power, in dB.
- **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Ability of a differential amplifier to reject common-mode voltages.
- **Dynamic Range (DR):** Ratio between the largest and smallest amplitude the system can faithfully handle.
- **Speed of Response:** How quickly the system responds to signal changes (rise time, settling time).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.2 — Essential Formulas for KTU Examinations (All Symbols Use `\vert` Instead of `|` for Table Safety):**

| # | Characteristic | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak-to-Peak Value | $V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Average Value (Periodic) | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | RMS Value | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Form Factor | $K_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Crest Factor | $K_c = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Signal Energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^{2}\,dt$ | Joules (J) |
| 7 | Average Power | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty}\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2}\vert x(t)\vert^{2}\,dt$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Period-Frequency Relation | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Seconds / Hz |
| 9 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Bandwidth | $B = f_H - f_L$ | Hz |
| 11 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 12 | SNR (Voltage Form) | $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 13 | CMRR | $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 14 | Dynamic Range | $DR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 15 | Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Signal units |
| 16 | Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^{2}$ | (Signal units)² |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG Amplifier Design:** Knowing ECG amplitude is 0.5–5 mV guides the **gain selection** (typically 1000) in the front-end instrumentation amplifier.
- **Filter Design:** Bandwidth knowledge of EEG (0.5–100 Hz) dictates a **bandpass filter** with appropriate cutoff frequencies.
- **ADC Selection:** Sampling rate must be ≥ 2 × $f_{max}$ (**Nyquist Theorem**) — for EMG, $f_s \geq 10$ kHz.
- **Noise Reduction:** High SNR requirement (~ 60 dB for clinical ECG) determines the **number of bits** in the ADC.
- **Patient Safety:** RMS-based current limits (e.g., 10 µA auxiliary current) prevent microshock hazards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Value of a Sinusoidal Biosignal

Consider a typical bioelectrical signal approximated sinusoidally:

$$x(t) = A_m \sin(\omega t)$$

where $A_m$ = peak amplitude, $\omega$ = angular frequency.

**Step 1 — Square the signal:**

$$x^{2}(t) = A_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)$$

This step is necessary because *power* is proportional to the **square of the instantaneous voltage** (Joule's Law: $P = V^2/R$).

**Step 2 — Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:**

$$x^{2}(t) = A_m^{2}\left(\dfrac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Average over one period $T$:**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x^{2}(t)\,dt = \dfrac{A_m^{2}}{2T}\int_{0}^{T}\big(1 - \cos(2\omega t)\big)\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.** The cosine integrates to zero over a complete period:

$$\int_{0}^{T}\cos(2\omega t)\,dt = 0$$

Therefore:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \dfrac{A_m^{2}}{2T}\cdot T = \dfrac{A_m^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$V_{rms} = \dfrac{A_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\,A_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** For any pure sinusoid, RMS is $A_m / \sqrt{2}$. This is the *defining* property used in clinical equipment (e.g., RMS amplitude of AC mains interference at 50 Hz).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) from Power to Decibels

**Step 1 — Define SNR as a power ratio:**

$$SNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$$

This is a *dimensionless ratio* (often very large, e.g., 1,000,000:1).

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels using the definition of the Bel:**

$$\text{Number of Bels} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Convert Bels to decibels (1 Bel = 10 dB):**

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Derive the voltage form.** Since $P = V^2 / R$ (for equal $R$):

$$\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \dfrac{V_{signal}^{2}}{V_{noise}^{2}}$$

Substituting:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}^{2}}{V_{noise}^{2}}\right) = 10 \cdot 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Use the **×10** form for *power* ratio, **×20** form for *voltage/current* ratio. Examiners often test this exact distinction.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

**Step 1 — Define differential and common-mode gains.** A differential amplifier has:
- Differential gain $A_d = V_{out}/V_d$ (where $V_d = V_1 - V_2$)
- Common-mode gain $A_c = V_{out}/V_{cm}$ (where $V_{cm} = (V_1 + V_2)/2$)

**Step 2 — Express CMRR as a numerical ratio:**

$$CMRR = \dfrac{A_d}{A_c}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to decibels:**

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU standard).** A biomedical amplifier has $A_d = 10{,}000$ and $A_c = 0.1$:

$$CMRR = \dfrac{10{,}000}{0.1} = 100{,}000$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 20 \times 5 = 100\ \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** Clinical-grade ECG amplifiers typically require **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** at 50 Hz to reject powerline interference.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Average Power of a Periodic Biosignal

For a periodic signal with period $T$:

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power proportional to squared signal:**

$$p(t) = \dfrac{v^{2}(t)}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Average over one period (assuming $R = 1\,\Omega$ for normalization):**

$$P = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that this equals the mean-square value:**

$$P = \overline{v^{2}(t)} = V_{rms}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — For ECG approximated as a sum of sinusoids** (e.g., Fourier components at $f_0$ and harmonics), by orthogonality:

$$P = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{A_n^{2}}{2}$$

where $A_n$ is the amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic.

## 3.5 Numerical Example Worked Out (KTU Practice Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** An EEG signal has peak amplitude $A_m = 200\ \mu V$ and is corrupted by noise of RMS amplitude $V_{n,rms} = 5\ \mu V$. Find: (i) RMS signal voltage, (ii) SNR in dB, (iii) Dynamic Range if ADC quantizes from 10 µV to 1 mV.

**Solution:**

**(i) RMS signal voltage** (assuming sinusoidal approximation):

$$V_{s,rms} = \dfrac{A_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{200}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 141.42\ \mu V$$

**(ii) SNR in dB:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{141.42}{5}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(28.28) \approx 20 \times 1.4514 \approx 29.03\ \text{dB}$$

**(iii) Dynamic Range:**

$$DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1000\ \mu V}{10\ \mu V}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(100) = 40\ \text{dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Signal Characteristics (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SIGNAL CHARACTERISTICS] --> B[AMPLITUDE DOMAIN]
    A --> C[FREQUENCY DOMAIN]
    A --> D[POWER ENERGY DOMAIN]
    A --> E[STATISTICAL DOMAIN]
    A --> F[PERFORMANCE DOMAIN]

    B --> B1[Peak Value Vmax]
    B --> B2[Peak to Peak Vpp]
    B --> B3[RMS Value Vrms]
    B --> B4[Average Value Vavg]
    B --> B5[Form Factor Kf]
    B --> B6[Crest Factor Kc]

    C --> C1[Fundamental Frequency f0]
    C --> C2[Harmonics n x f0]
    C --> C3[Bandwidth B fh minus fl]
    C --> C4[Spectral Density]

    D --> D1[Signal Energy E]
    D --> D2[Average Power P]
    D --> D3[Power Signal Type]
    D --> D4[Energy Signal Type]

    E --> E1[Mean mu]
    E --> E2[Variance sigma2]
    E --> E3[Standard Deviation sigma]
    E --> E4[Probability Density Function]
    E --> E5[Stationarity]

    F --> F1[SNR dB]
    F --> F2[CMRR dB]
    F --> F3[Dynamic Range dB]
    F --> F4[Speed of Response]
    F --> F5[Linearity]
```

## 4.2 Bio-Signal Acquisition Block Diagram with Characteristic Measurement Points

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[BIOELECTRIC SOURCE] --> ELEC[ELECTRODES]
    ELEC --> IA[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    IA --> FILT[FILTER BLOCK]
    FILT --> ADC[ADC]
    ADC --> DSP[DIGITAL PROCESSING]
    DSP --> OUT[OUTPUT / DISPLAY]

    IA -.-> M1[CMRR Measurement Point]
    FILT -.-> M2[Bandwidth Measurement Point]
    ADC -.-> M3[Dynamic Range Measurement Point]
    DSP -.-> M4[SNR Measurement Point]
    OUT -.-> M5[RMS and Amplitude Measurement Point]

    style SRC fill:#e1f5ff
    style IA fill:#fff4e1
    style FILT fill:#fff4e1
    style ADC fill:#fff4e1
    style DSP fill:#fff4e1
    style OUT fill:#e1ffe1
    style M1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M2 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M4 fill:#ffe1e1
    style M5 fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.3 RMS Calculation Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Square the Signal x squared of t]
    B --> C[Integrate over Period T]
    C --> D[Divide by T]
    D --> E[Take Square Root]
    E --> F[Output Vrms]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.4 SNR / CMRR Decision Matrix Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Signal plus Noise] --> B{Is Interference Common Mode?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply Differential Amplifier]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Filtering]
    C --> E[Measure CMRR]
    D --> F[Measure SNR]
    E --> G{CMRR greater than 100 dB?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Clinical Grade Acceptable]
    G -- No --> I[Improve Electrode Matching]
    F --> J{SNR greater than 40 dB?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Signal Quality Good]
    J -- No --> L[Apply Averaging or Filtering]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
    style K fill:#e1ffe1
    style I fill:#ffe1e1
    style L fill:#ffe1e1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following signal characteristics with one example bio-signal for each:**
**(i) RMS value, (ii) Bandwidth, (iii) Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR).**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) RMS Value:** The Root Mean Square value of a periodic signal is the square root of the mean of the square of the signal over one period.
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$$
*Example:* The RMS amplitude of ECG is approximately **1 mV** (since ECG ranges 0.5–5 mV peak).

- **(ii) Bandwidth:** The continuous range of frequencies that a signal occupies, defined as $B = f_H - f_L$.
*Example:* The bandwidth of a normal ECG signal is approximately **0.05 – 150 Hz**, i.e., $B \approx 150$ Hz.

- **(iii) CMRR:** The ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain of a differential amplifier, expressed in dB.
$$CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$
*Example:* A clinical ECG amplifier typically requires **CMRR ≥ 100 dB** at 50 Hz to reject powerline interference.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between a power signal and an energy signal. Give one biomedical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Power Signal | Energy Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Finite, non-zero average power; infinite energy | Finite, non-zero energy; zero average power |
| **Mathematical Condition** | $0 < P < \infty,\ E = \infty$ | $0 < E < \infty,\ P = 0$ |
| **Nature** | Persistent / non-decaying | Transient / decaying |
| **Bio-Example** | Continuous ECG recording over hours | Single heart-beat transient, single evoked-response potential |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Most periodic bio-signals (ECG, EMG, EEG) over long durations are treated as **power signals**, while single evoked potentials (like auditory brainstem response) are **energy signals**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**A. (a)** Define and explain the following signal characteristics: (i) Peak-to-peak value, (ii) Average value, (iii) RMS value, (iv) Form factor, (v) Crest factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG signal has peak amplitude $A_m = 3.5$ mV. Calculate the RMS value, Average value (assuming the signal is sinusoidal), Form factor and Crest factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a):

- **(i) Peak-to-Peak Value ($V_{pp}$):** Difference between the maximum positive peak and the maximum negative peak of a signal in one cycle. Indicates the *total swing* of the signal.
$$V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min}$$

- **(ii) Average Value ($V_{avg}$):** The arithmetic mean of the signal over one period. Represents the **DC component**.
$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt$$
For a pure sinusoid, $V_{avg} = 0$ (over a full cycle).

- **(iii) RMS Value ($V_{rms}$):** The *effective* value, equivalent to the DC value that would deliver the same average power.
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$$
For sinusoid: $V_{rms} = A_m / \sqrt{2}$.

- **(iv) Form Factor ($K_f$):** Ratio of RMS value to average value; indicates the *shape* of the waveform.
$$K_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$$

- **(v) Crest Factor ($K_c$):** Ratio of peak value to RMS value; indicates the *peakedness* of the waveform.
$$K_c = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$$
For a sinusoid, $K_c = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Given:** $A_m = 3.5$ mV (sinusoidal assumption)

**Step 1 — RMS Value:**

$$V_{rms} = \dfrac{A_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{3.5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{3.5}{1.4142} \approx 2.475\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Average Value** (pure sinusoid over full period):

$$V_{avg} = 0\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Form Factor:**

$$K_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \dfrac{2.475}{0} \to \infty$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Dividing by zero! For a *pure sinusoid* (symmetric, no DC offset), $V_{avg} = 0$, making $K_f$ undefined (infinite). Form factor is meaningful only for **rectified or pulsed** waveforms. For a full-wave rectified sinusoid, $V_{avg} = 2A_m / \pi$ and $K_f = \pi / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$.

**Step 4 — Crest Factor:**

$$K_c = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \dfrac{3.5}{2.475} \approx 1.414$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**B. (a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the different amplitude, frequency, and statistical characteristics of bioelectric signals. Discuss the typical amplitude and frequency ranges of ECG, EEG, and EMG signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EEG signal contaminated with 50 Hz powerline interference is acquired by a differential amplifier with differential gain $A_d = 5000$ and common-mode gain $A_c = 0.5$. Calculate: (i) CMRR in dB, (ii) Common-mode output voltage if the common-mode input is $V_{cm} = 5$ V, (iii) Differential output voltage if the desired bio-signal is $V_d = 100\ \mu V$. Comment on the interference rejection. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(a):

The characteristics of bioelectric signals are categorized into three primary domains:

**1. Amplitude Characteristics:**
These describe the *strength* of the bio-signal. The most important parameters are:
- **Peak Amplitude ($V_{max}$)** — maximum instantaneous value.
- **Peak-to-Peak Amplitude ($V_{pp}$)** — full swing of the signal.
- **RMS Amplitude** — effective value proportional to signal power.
- **Mean Amplitude** — DC offset (often zero for AC-coupled bio-signals).

**2. Frequency Characteristics:**
These describe the *rate of variation* of the signal:
- **Fundamental Frequency** — lowest cyclic component (e.g., heart rate for ECG).
- **Spectral Content** — distribution of energy vs frequency (obtained by Fourier transform).
- **Bandwidth** — frequency range over which the signal has significant energy.

**3. Statistical Characteristics:**
These describe the *random/probabilistic* nature:
- **Mean ($\mu$):** DC level / expected value.
- **Variance ($\sigma^2$):** Spread around the mean.
- **Probability Density Function (PDF):** Distribution of signal amplitudes.
- **Stationarity:** Whether statistical properties remain constant over time.

**Typical Ranges of Bioelectric Signals:**

| Signal | Amplitude | Frequency Range | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | 150 Hz |
| **EEG** | **5 – 300 µV** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | 100 Hz |
| **EMG** | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **20 – 5000 Hz** | 5 kHz |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** These ranges directly determine the *gain*, *filter cutoff*, and *ADC sampling rate* in the design. For EMG, $f_s \geq 10$ kHz (Nyquist).

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Given:** $A_d = 5000$, $A_c = 0.5$, $V_{cm} = 5$ V, $V_d = 100\ \mu V$

**Step 1 — Calculate CMRR in dB:**

$$CMRR = \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} = \dfrac{5000}{0.5} = 10{,}000$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 20 \times 4 = 80\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Common-Mode Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out,cm} = A_c \times V_{cm} = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Differential Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out,d} = A_d \times V_d = 5000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.5\ \text{V} = 500\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Interference Rejection:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Observation:** Although CMRR = 80 dB (which is *good*), the **common-mode output is 2.5 V**, which is **5 times** the desired differential output (0.5 V). This means the 50 Hz powerline interference *still dominates* the signal of interest!
>
> **Remedy:** Improve electrode-skin contact (reduces $V_{cm}$), use a higher-CMRR instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620 with CMRR > 100 dB), or use driven-right-leg (DRL) circuitry to actively cancel $V_{cm}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in "Characteristics of Signal" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SNR formulas:** Using $10\log_{10}$ for voltage ratios (should be ×20). Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Stating SNR as 29 instead of **29 dB**. Loss: **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Division by zero in form factor:** Computing $K_f$ for a symmetric sinusoid without noting the limitation. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Mixing up CMRR definitions:** Writing $CMRR = A_c/A_d$ instead of $A_d/A_c$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention sampling rate (Nyquist):** When asked about bandwidth, not specifying $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not providing bio-signal examples:** Always cite ECG/EEG/EMG with numerical values — KTU examiners reward **specific numbers**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins:**

- ⭐ **RMS** is the *effective* value of a signal; for a sinusoid, $V_{rms} = A_m / \sqrt{2}$.
- ⭐ **Average value** over a full period of a symmetric sinusoid is **zero**; form factor is then *infinite*.
- ⭐ **Crest factor** for a pure sinusoid is **$\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$**; for a square wave it is **1**.
- ⭐ **SNR (dB)** uses $\times 10$ for *power*, $\times 20$ for *voltage/current*.
- ⭐ **CMRR (dB)** = $20\log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$; clinical ECG requires **≥ 100 dB** at 50 Hz.
- ⭐ **ECG** = 0.5–5 mV, 0.05–150 Hz; **EEG** = 5–300 µV, 0.5–100 Hz; **EMG** = 50 µV–5 mV, 20 Hz–5 kHz.
- ⭐ **Power signal** = finite power, infinite energy (e.g., continuous ECG); **Energy signal** = finite energy, zero average power (e.g., single evoked response).
- ⭐ **Bandwidth** $B = f_H - f_L$; **Period** $T = 1/f$; **Angular frequency** $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- ⭐ **Nyquist rate** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ determines minimum ADC sampling rate.
- ⭐ **Dynamic range** in dB = $20\log_{10}(V_{max}/V_{min})$; sets ADC bit requirement.
- ⭐ **Mean** = first moment, **Variance** = second central moment; both describe statistical behavior.
- ⭐ **Stationary** signals have time-invariant statistics; biosignals are usually *quasi-stationary* over short windows.
- ⭐ For KTU exams, always **draw a small block diagram** of the acquisition chain (Electrode → IA → Filter → ADC → DSP) when discussing characteristics.
- ⭐ Memorize: **ECG → Heart, EEG → Brain, EMG → Muscles, EOG → Eyes, PCG → Heart sounds, PPG → Blood volume**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrode System. EMG: Membrane Potentials & Action Potentials in Smooth Muscles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrode System. EMG: Membrane Potentials & Action Potentials in Smooth Muscles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bioelectrode System — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioelectrode Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **bioelectrode** is a transducer that converts ionic conduction (ionic current flow inside biological tissue) into electronic conduction (electron current flow in measurement instruments) by establishing an electrochemical equilibrium at the **electrode–electrolyte interface**.

The bioelectrode is the **first and most critical link** in any biopotential acquisition chain (ECG, EMG, EEG, EOG). Without a properly designed electrode system, even the most sophisticated amplifier and DSP algorithm will yield noisy, distorted, and clinically meaningless signals.

**Classification of Bioelectrodes (KTU 2024 Scheme — High Yield):**

| Type | Placement | Typical Use | Invasive? |
|------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Surface Electrode** | Skin surface | ECG, EMG, EEG | Non-invasive |
| **Needle Electrode** | Inserted into muscle | Clinical EMG, nerve conduction | Minimally invasive |
| **Microelectrode** | Inside single cell | Action potential recording | Invasive |
| **Invasive/Implantable** | Subcutaneous/inside organ | Pacemakers, long-term monitoring | Invasive |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The module specifically emphasizes **surface and needle electrodes** in the context of EMG acquisition. Microelectrodes are mentioned for intracellular recording of membrane and action potentials.

## 1.2 The Electrode–Skin Interface — Intuitive Analogy

**Conceptual Analogy: The "Translator at the Border"**

Imagine two countries that speak different languages trying to communicate. They need a **bilingual translator** at the border. The bioelectrode is exactly this translator:
- **Inside the body (the "first country")**: signals travel as **ions** (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) dissolved in body fluid
- **Inside the wire and amplifier (the "second country")**: signals travel as **electrons** moving through a metal conductor
- **The bioelectrode** sits at the border and translates ions → electrons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constant to Memorize:**
> - **Body temperature (T)** = **310 K** (37 °C)
> - **Faraday's constant (F)** = **96,485 C/mol** ≈ **96,500 C/mol**
> - **Universal gas constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

## 1.3 EMG — Electromyography: The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMG Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Electromyography (EMG)** is the bioelectrical technique of recording, processing, and interpreting the electrical activity produced by **skeletal muscle fibers** during voluntary or stimulated contraction.

**Standard EMG Signal Metrics (Board Exam Favorite):**

| Parameter | Value Range | Why It Matters |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Amplitude** | **50 µV – 5 mV** | Determines gain design |
| **Frequency content** | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** | Sets bandpass filter cutoff |
| **Source impedance** | **~100 kΩ – 10 MΩ** | Input impedance must be ≫ source Z |
| **Acquisition** | Bipolar (differential) | Common-mode noise rejection |

## 1.4 Membrane Potentials & Smooth Muscle Action Potentials — Intuition

**Conceptual Analogy: "The Battery Inside Every Cell"**

Think of the cell membrane as a **leaky battery** with a voltage of about **−70 mV to −90 mV** (resting). When the cell is stimulated, this battery momentarily reverses polarity (depolarization → repolarization), producing an **action potential** — a brief electrical "spike" that propagates along the membrane like a wave.

- **Resting membrane potential** = battery at rest (negative inside)
- **Depolarization** = battery momentarily flipping (+ inside)
- **Repolarization** = battery returning to rest (negative inside again)

> [!NOTE]
> **Smooth muscle** is unique: it does NOT require an external nerve to fire. It contains **pacemaker cells** (like the SA node in the heart) that spontaneously depolarize, producing **slow waves** and **spike potentials**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bioelectrode Half-Cell Potential

When a metal electrode is placed in an electrolyte (e.g., body fluid or electrode gel), an electrochemical equilibrium is established. The potential developed at this interface is called the **half-cell potential** ($E$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hygiene Note:** Half-cell potential is the same for all standard electrode metals, but in **matched pairs** (e.g., Ag/AgCl — Ag/AgCl) the half-cell potentials cancel out because they are equal in magnitude.

### The Nernst Equation — Foundation of Bioelectrode Theory

The half-cell potential is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[\text{Oxidant}]}{[\text{Reductant}]}$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
- $E^{0}$ = standard reduction potential of the metal (V)
- $R$ = universal gas constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per ion
- $F$ = Faraday's constant
- $[Ox]/[Red]$ = ratio of oxidized to reduced ion concentrations

**At body temperature (T = 310 K),** substituting the constants:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0617}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Oxidant}]}{[\text{Reductant}]} \quad \text{(volts)}$$

## 2.2 Electrode–Skin Interface — The Three Layers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The electrode–skin interface behaves like an **electrical circuit** with three parallel RC sub-circuits corresponding to three anatomical layers. This is the source of the famous **Warburg Impedance**.

| Layer | Anatomical Component | Electrical Behavior |
|-------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Outer (a)** | Stratum corneum (dead skin) | High resistance (~100 kΩ) + capacitance |
| **Middle (b)** | Viable epidermis + dermis | Lower resistance |
| **Inner (c)** | Subcutaneous tissue (body fluid) | Low resistance, electrolyte-like |

**Equivalent Circuit Model of Electrode–Skin Interface:**

The complete model comprises:
- $R_s$ = series resistance of electrode gel and tissue (typically small)
- $C_d$ = **double-layer capacitance** at the metal–electrolyte interface
- $R_d$ = **charge-transfer resistance** (Faradaic leakage path)
- $Z_w$ = **Warburg impedance** (frequency-dependent diffusion impedance)

## 2.3 Polarization and Non-Polarizable Electrodes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarization Effect:** When DC current flows through an electrode, the half-cell potential changes. This is undesirable because it shifts the baseline and creates drift.

**Two Classes of Electrodes:**

1. **Polarizable Electrodes** (e.g., Platinum, Stainless Steel)
   - Current flow is purely capacitive
   - Half-cell potential shifts with current
   - Used in stimulation (defibrillators)
2. **Non-Polarizable Electrodes** (e.g., **Ag/AgCl**)
   - Current flow crosses the interface freely
   - Half-cell potential remains nearly constant
   - **Used in 99% of biopotential recording (KTU favorite)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Ag/AgCl is the gold standard** for ECG, EMG, EEG electrodes because it is a non-polarizable electrode — its half-cell potential is **stable** and **independent** of current flow.

## 2.4 EMG Signal — Motor Unit Anatomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motor Unit (MU) Definition:** A **motor neuron** + all the **muscle fibers** it innervates. This is the fundamental functional unit of voluntary muscle contraction.

When a motor neuron fires, **all** muscle fibers in its motor unit depolarize nearly simultaneously, producing a summed extracellular signal called the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

The recorded EMG signal is the **spatiotemporal sum** of many active MUAPs.

## 2.5 Resting Membrane Potential — Ionic Basis

The resting membrane potential is generated by:
1. **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** — actively pumps 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in (per cycle)
2. **Differential ion permeability** — membrane is far more permeable to K⁺ than Na⁺ at rest
3. **Fixed intracellular anions** (proteins, phosphates) that cannot leave

### Nernst Equilibrium Potential for a Single Ion

For ion $X$ with charge $z$ (e.g., K⁺ has $z = +1$):

$$E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{\text{outside}}}{[X]_{\text{inside}}}$$

**Standard ion concentrations (skeletal muscle, KTU values):**

| Ion | Intracellular [mM] | Extracellular [mM] | $E_{ion}$ (mV) |
|-----|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **K⁺** | 150 | 4 | **−96** |
| **Na⁺** | 12 | 145 | **+60** |
| **Cl⁻** | 4 | 120 | **−90** |
| **Ca²⁺** | $10^{-4}$ | 1.2 | **+130** |

## 2.6 Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **resting membrane potential** is described by the **GHK equation**, which considers the relative permeabilities of all major ions simultaneously. This is the KTU-preferred formula over the single-ion Nernst equation for membrane potential.

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**At T = 310 K (body temperature):**

$$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10} \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}} \quad \text{(mV)}$$

Where $P_K$, $P_{Na}$, $P_{Cl}$ are the **membrane permeabilities** for each ion.

## 2.7 Action Potential in Smooth Muscle

Smooth muscle action potentials differ significantly from skeletal/cardiac muscle:

| Feature | Skeletal Muscle | Smooth Muscle |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Trigger** | Nerve (ACh at NMJ) | Pacemaker / Stretch / Hormones |
| **Threshold** | Sharp | Graded |
| **Spike duration** | 1–2 ms | 10–50 ms |
| **Plateau** | Absent | Often present (especially visceral) |
| **Primary depolarizing ion** | Na⁺ | **Ca²⁺** (from ECF) |
| **Repolarization** | Na⁺ inactivation + K⁺ efflux | K⁺ efflux + Ca²⁺ removal |
| **Pacemaker activity** | None | Yes (single-unit smooth muscle) |

**Two Types of Smooth Muscle:**

1. **Single-Unit (Visceral) Smooth Muscle**
   - Cells electrically coupled via **gap junctions**
   - Contracts as a **syncytium** (whole sheet together)
   - Has **pacemaker cells** with slow waves
   - Examples: gut, uterus, ureters

2. **Multi-Unit Smooth Muscle**
   - Cells **not** coupled; each cell innervated separately
   - **No pacemaker activity** — requires external nerve input
   - Examples: ciliary muscle, iris, large arteries

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Nernst (half-cell) | V |
| 2 | $E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Single-ion equilibrium | V |
| 3 | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{\sum P_{ion} [ion]_{out}}{\sum P_{ion} [ion]_{in}}$ | GHK membrane potential | V |
| 4 | $\frac{RT}{F}$ at 310 K = **61.5 mV** (log base 10) or **26.7 mV** (ln) | Thermal voltage scaling | mV |
| 5 | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\alpha}$ (space constant) | Signal propagation length | m |
| 6 | $V = V_{Na} \cdot m^3 h - V_K \cdot n^4$ (HH model) | Hodgkin–Huxley gating | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion trick (board exams):**
> - $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$
> - At 310 K: $\frac{RT}{F} = 0.0267$ V $= 26.7$ mV (natural log)
> - Equivalent log form: $\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = 0.0615$ V $= 61.5$ mV (log base 10)

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation from Electrochemical Equilibrium

**Starting Point:** At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** of the ion in the metal phase equals that in the electrolyte phase.

$$\mu_{ion,\text{metal}} = \mu_{ion,\text{electrolyte}}$$

**Step 1 — Chemical potential in electrolyte:**
$$\mu_{electrolyte} = \mu^{0}_{electrolyte} + RT \ln a_{ion}$$

**Step 2 — Chemical potential in metal (includes electrical work):**
$$\mu_{metal} = \mu^{0}_{metal} + RT \ln a_{metal} + zFE$$

Where $z$ = ion charge, $F$ = Faraday constant, $E$ = half-cell potential.

**Step 3 — Set them equal and solve for E:**
$$\mu^{0}_{metal} + RT \ln a_{metal} + zFE = \mu^{0}_{electrolyte} + RT \ln a_{ion}$$

$$zFE = (\mu^{0}_{electrolyte} - \mu^{0}_{metal}) + RT \ln \frac{a_{ion}}{a_{metal}}$$

**Step 4 — Group the standard potential terms:**
$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{a_{ion,\text{out}}}{a_{ion,\text{in}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Nernst equation** in its complete form. For the **K⁺ ion** with charge $z = +1$ and standard concentrations, we recover $E_K \approx -96$ mV as computed earlier.

## 3.2 Derivation: Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation from First Principles

The GHK equation arises from the **constant field assumption** of Goldman (1943): the electric field across the membrane is **linear** and **constant**.

**Step 1 — Define ion flux Jion using the Nernst–Planck equation:**

$$J_{ion} = -D_{ion} \frac{d[ion]}{dx} - \mu_{ion} \cdot z \cdot E_{field} \cdot [ion]$$

Where:
- $D_{ion}$ = diffusion coefficient
- $\mu_{ion}$ = mobility
- $E_{field}$ = constant electric field across membrane

**Step 2 — Apply the constant field (linear potential drop) assumption:**

The potential $V(x)$ varies linearly from $0$ (inside) to $V_m$ (outside):

$$V(x) = V_m \cdot \frac{x}{d}$$

where $d$ is the membrane thickness.

**Step 3 — Integrate the flux over the membrane thickness:**
For ion $X$ with concentration $[X]_{in}$ at $x=0$ and $[X]_{out}$ at $x=d$:

$$J_X = P_X \cdot \frac{z F V_m}{RT} \cdot \frac{[X]_{out} - [X]_{in} \exp(-zFV_m/RT)}{1 - \exp(-zFV_m/RT)}$$

Where $P_X$ = permeability coefficient.

**Step 4 — Net membrane current is zero at steady state (rest):**

$$I_{total} = I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve for $V_m$:**

$$\boxed{V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation at T = 310 K:**

Plug in standard values (taking $P_{Na}/P_K \approx 0.04$, $P_{Cl}/P_K \approx 0.45$ for skeletal muscle):

$$\text{Numerator} = 1(4) + 0.04(145) + 0.45(4) = 4 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 11.6$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1(150) + 0.04(12) + 0.45(120) = 150 + 0.48 + 54 = 204.48$$

$$V_m = 26.7 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{11.6}{204.48}\right) = 26.7 \cdot \ln(0.0567)$$

$$V_m = 26.7 \cdot (-2.869) = -76.6 \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result $V_m \approx -77$ mV matches the experimentally observed resting membrane potential of mammalian skeletal muscle. This confirms the **validity of the GHK model**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Energy Balance

The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase expels 3 Na⁺ and imports 2 K⁺ per ATP hydrolyzed. The free energy available from ATP hydrolysis is:

$$\Delta G_{ATP} \approx -50 \text{ kJ/mol (at cellular conditions)}$$

**Step 1 — Free energy to pump one Na⁺ outward against its gradient:**

$$\Delta G_{Na} = RT \ln \frac{[Na^+]_{in}}{[Na^+]_{out}} + F \cdot V_m$$

**Step 2 — Numerical substitution:**

$$\Delta G_{Na} = (8.314)(310)\ln\left(\frac{12}{145}\right) + 96485 \cdot (-0.077)$$

$$\Delta G_{Na} = 2577.3 \cdot \ln(0.0828) - 7429.3$$

$$\Delta G_{Na} = 2577.3 \cdot (-2.491) - 7429.3 = -6420 - 7429.3 = -13849 \text{ J/mol} = -13.85 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy per cycle (3 Na⁺ out + 2 K⁺ in):**

For 3 Na⁺ outward: $3 \times 13.85 = 41.55$ kJ/mol

For 2 K⁺ inward (similar logic): $\approx 2 \times 12.5 = 25$ kJ/mol

$$\Delta G_{pump} = 41.55 + 25 = 66.55 \text{ kJ/mol per cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ATP energy:**

Since $66.55 \text{ kJ/mol} > 50 \text{ kJ/mol (ATP energy)}$, **each ATP molecule can sustain exactly one pump cycle**. This is the energetic basis of cellular resting potential maintenance.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Smooth Muscle Action Potential Phases

| Phase | Voltage Change | Dominant Ion Channel | Ca²⁺ Role | KTU Marker |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|------------|------------|
| **Slow wave / Pacemaker** | Gradual rise to threshold | L-type Ca²⁺ (T-type initiates) | Slow Ca²⁺ influx | Pre-spike |
| **Threshold reached** | ~ -40 mV | Voltage-gated L-type Ca²⁺ opens | **Rapid Ca²⁺ influx** | Spike onset |
| **Depolarization (spike)** | -40 mV → +20 to +30 mV | L-type Ca²⁺ sustained | Sustained Ca²⁺ entry | Peak |
| **Plateau (visceral SM)** | Sustained at +20 mV | L-type Ca²⁺ still open | **Triggers contraction** | Prolonged contraction |
| **Repolarization** | +20 mV → -60 mV | K⁺ efflux (delayed rectifier) | Ca²⁺ removed by SERCA pump | K⁺ outflow |
| **Hyperpolarization** | Below resting | K⁺ channels remain open | — | Refractory period |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference for Board Exam:** In smooth muscle, **Ca²⁺ (not Na⁺) is the depolarizing ion** during the action potential spike. This is a high-yield distinction.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bioelectrode Half-Cell — Electrochemical Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Electrode Side]
        M[Metal Conductor]
    end
    subgraph B[Electrolyte Side]
        SOL[Body Fluid / Gel]
    end
    M -->|Electron transfer| INTERFACE[Electrode-Electrolyte Interface]
    INTERFACE -->|Ion transfer| SOL
    SOL -->|Half-cell potential E| M
    INTERFACE -.->|Double layer C_d| INTERFACE
    INTERFACE -.->|Charge transfer R_d| INTERFACE
```

## 4.2 Electrode–Skin Interface — Three-Layer Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrode Metal Ag or AgCl] --> B[Electrode Gel Electrolyte]
    B --> C1[Stratum Corneum R_a C_a]
    C1 --> C2[Viable Epidermis R_b C_b]
    C2 --> C3[Subcutaneous Tissue R_c]
    C3 --> D[Muscle Body Fluid]
    D -->|Biopotential Source| A
    C1 -.->|Warburg Z_w| C1
    C2 -.->|Warburg Z_w| C2
```

## 4.3 EMG Acquisition Chain — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Muscle Fiber] -->|MUAPs| S1[Surface Electrode 1]
    M -->|MUAPs| S2[Surface Electrode 2]
    S1 --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    S2 --> IA
    IA -->|CMRR 100 dB| BPF[Bandpass Filter 20 Hz to 500 Hz]
    BPF --> NF[Notch Filter 50 Hz Powerline]
    NF --> GAIN[Programmable Gain]
    GAIN --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing]
    DSP --> DSP1[Rectification]
    DSP1 --> DSP2[Envelope Detection]
    DSP2 --> DSP3[RMS Calculation]
    DSP3 --> DISP[Display / Storage]
```

## 4.4 Membrane Ion Channel Dynamics During Action Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Resting -70 mV] -->|Stimulus| T[Threshold -55 mV]
    T -->|Na channel opens| D[Depolarization 0 mV]
    D -->|Na peak +30 mV| P[Peak Potential]
    P -->|Na inactivates, K opens| RP[Repolarization]
    RP -->|K channels open wide| H[Hyperpolarization -80 mV]
    H -->|Na K pump restores| R
    RP -.->|Absolute refractory| RP
    H -.->|Relative refractory| H
```

## 4.5 Smooth Muscle Action Potential — Ca²⁺-Mediated Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SW[Slow Wave from Pacemaker Cell] --> TH[Threshold -40 mV]
    TH -->|L-type Ca channels open| SP[Spike Depolarization 0 mV]
    SP -->|Sustained Ca influx| PL[Plateau Phase 10 to 50 ms]
    PL -->|Delayed K efflux| RE[Repolarization]
    RE -->|Ca removed by SERCA| REST[Resting -60 mV]
    PL -.->|Ca binds calmodulin| CON[Cross-bridge Cycling Contraction]
    CON -->|Ca pumped out| REST
```

## 4.6 Sodium–Potassium Pump Cycle (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[3 Na inside binds pump] --> B[ATP Hydrolysis Phosphorylation]
    B --> C[Conformational change]
    C --> D[3 Na released outside]
    D --> E[2 K outside bind pump]
    E --> F[Dephosphorylation]
    F --> G[2 K released inside]
    G --> A
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define bioelectrode. Explain the difference between polarizable and non-polarizable electrodes with examples.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Bioelectrode:** A bioelectrode is a transducer that converts ionic current (in tissue) into electronic current (in measurement instruments) through an electrochemical reaction at the metal–electrolyte interface.

| Property | Polarizable | Non-Polarizable |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Current type | Capacitive (displacement) | Faradaic (charge transfer) |
| Half-cell potential stability | **Changes with current** | **Stable** |
| Use | Stimulation (defibrillators) | **Recording (ECG, EMG, EEG)** |
| Example | Platinum, Stainless Steel | **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** |

Ag/AgCl is the **gold standard** for biopotential recording because it is non-polarizable.

---

### **Question 2: List the differences between single-unit and multi-unit smooth muscle. Give two examples of each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Unit (Visceral) | Multi-Unit |
|---------|------------------------|------------|
| Cell coupling | **Gap junctions** (syncytium) | No coupling |
| Innervation | Sparse (mostly myogenic) | Dense (neurogenic) |
| Pacemaker | **Present** (slow waves) | Absent |
| Contraction | Spontaneous, rhythmic | Graded by nerve input |
| Examples | **Gut, uterus, ureter** | **Ciliary muscle, iris, vas deferens** |

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the EMG signal acquisition system. List the key signal characteristics and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (recap from Section 4.3):**
Muscle → Surface/Needle Electrodes → Instrumentation Amplifier (high CMRR ≳ 100 dB) → Bandpass Filter (20 Hz – 500 Hz) → Notch Filter (50 Hz) → Programmable Gain (×100 to ×10,000) → ADC (≥ 1 kS/s) → DSP Block (rectification → envelope → RMS) → Display.

**Key Signal Characteristics:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Amplitude | **50 µV – 5 mV** |
| Frequency | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** |
| Source impedance | **100 kΩ – 10 MΩ** |
| Mode | Bipolar (differential) |

**Applications:**
1. Diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders (ALS, myasthenia gravis, peripheral neuropathy)
2. Rehabilitation engineering — prosthetic control using EMG
3. Kinesiology and sports biomechanics
4. Biofeedback therapy
5. Human–computer interfaces (BCI)
6. Ergonomic assessment in industry

---

#### (b) Explain the electrode–skin interface with its equivalent circuit. What is the role of Ag/AgCl electrode gel? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **electrode–skin interface** consists of three distinct tissue layers, each with its own RC behavior:

1. **Stratum corneum** (outermost dead-cell layer): High resistance (~**100 kΩ**) and parallel capacitance. This layer dominates the contact impedance.
2. **Viable epidermis + dermis**: Lower resistance, higher capacitance.
3. **Subcutaneous tissue + body fluid**: Low resistance, electrolyte-like.

**Equivalent Circuit (per electrode):**
- $C_d$ = **double-layer capacitance** at metal–electrolyte interface
- $R_d$ = **charge-transfer resistance**
- $Z_w$ = **Warburg impedance** (frequency-dependent diffusion element)
- $R_s$ = series resistance of gel and tissue

**Total Impedance Model:**
$$Z_{total}(f) = R_s + \frac{R_d \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}}{R_d + \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}} + Z_w(f)$$

**Role of Ag/AgCl Electrode Gel:**
- Provides **ionic conductivity** between skin and electrode metal
- Forms a **stable Ag/AgCl half-cell** with chloride ions: $\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{AgCl} + e^-$
- **Reduces skin impedance** by hydrating the stratum corneum
- Maintains **non-polarizable behavior** → stable half-cell potential → no baseline drift
- Reduces **motion artifacts** by mechanical coupling to skin

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU 2024):** Students often forget to mention the **Warburg impedance** in the equivalent circuit. Mentioning $Z_w$ explicitly carries **1 mark**. Also, students confuse polarizable vs non-polarizable — stating that Ag/AgCl is **non-polarizable** is a **mandatory 2 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the Nernst equation. Calculate the Nernst potential for K⁺ at 310 K given $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (from Section 3.1):**
Equating the chemical potential of ion in metal and electrolyte phases:

$$\mu^{0}_{metal} + RT \ln a_{metal} + zFE = \mu^{0}_{electrolyte} + RT \ln a_{electrolyte}$$

Rearranging:
$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{a_{electrolyte}}{a_{metal}}$$

For an ion moving from outside to inside:
$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$

**Numerical Calculation for K⁺:**
- $z = +1$ (K⁺ is monovalent)
- $T = 310$ K
- $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM
- $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM

$$E_K = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(1)(96485)} \ln \frac{4}{150}$$

$$E_K = \frac{2577.3}{96485} \ln(0.0267)$$

$$E_K = 0.0267 \times (-3.625)$$

$$E_K = -0.0968 \text{ V} = -96.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Conclusion:** The equilibrium (Nernst) potential for K⁺ is **−96.8 mV**, which closely matches the experimentally observed resting potential (~−90 mV) — confirming that K⁺ permeability dominates at rest.

---

#### (b) Explain the ionic basis of resting membrane potential using the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz equation. Why is the resting potential close to $E_K$ and not $E_{Na}$? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **resting membrane potential (RMP)** is generated by:
1. The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** maintaining ion gradients (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP)
2. The membrane's selective permeability: **$P_K \gg P_{Na} \approx P_{Cl}$** at rest

**GHK Equation:**
$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_{K}[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$$

**Typical permeabilities (skeletal muscle):**
- $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$

**Numerical evaluation (from Section 3.2):**
$$V_m \approx -77 \text{ mV}$$

**Why is RMP close to $E_K$ (≈ −96 mV) and not $E_{Na}$ (≈ +60 mV)?**

The GHK equation is a **weighted average** of the Nernst potentials of all permeant ions, weighted by their **permeabilities**:

$$V_m \approx \frac{P_K E_K + P_{Na} E_{Na} + P_{Cl} E_{Cl}}{P_K + P_{Na} + P_{Cl}}$$

Since at rest $P_K$ is the **largest** permeability, the weight of $E_K = -96$ mV dominates. The contribution of $E_{Na} = +60$ mV is reduced 25-fold (because $P_{Na}/P_K = 0.04$). The result is a value near $E_K$ but slightly less negative (around −70 to −90 mV) because of the small inward Na⁺ leak and Cl⁻ contribution.

**Physical interpretation:** The membrane "wants" to settle at the potential where **net ionic current = 0**. Because K⁺ is the most permeant ion, this happens close to the K⁺ equilibrium potential.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (2024):** Many students write "$V_m = E_K$" — this is **WRONG**. The RMP is approximately equal to $E_K$ but not exactly equal. You **must** invoke the GHK equation (or permeability weighting) to justify the closeness. Just stating the number without derivation will cost you **3 marks**.

---

## KTU 2024 Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting Warburg impedance** in the electrode–skin equivalent circuit (−1 mark)
> 2. **Conflating polarizable and non-polarizable electrodes** — Ag/AgCl is non-polarizable (−2 marks)
> 3. **Writing $V_m = E_K$ exactly** instead of using GHK equation (−3 marks)
> 4. **Confusing Ca²⁺ (smooth muscle) with Na⁺ (skeletal muscle) as the depolarizing ion** (−2 marks)
> 5. **Using $|x|$ inside a markdown formula table** — this BREAKS the table. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead.
> 6. **Forgetting the constant 2.303** when converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ in the Nernst equation (−1 mark)
> 7. **Not drawing the slow wave component** when describing smooth muscle action potentials (−1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

**ELECTRODE SYSTEM:**
- ✅ Bioelectrode = ionic ↔ electronic transducer
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl** is the **non-polarizable** electrode of choice
- ✅ Half-cell potential given by **Nernst equation**; cancels in matched pairs
- ✅ Electrode–skin interface has **3 RC layers** + **Warburg impedance**
- ✅ Surface electrodes = non-invasive; Needle = clinical EMG; Micro = intracellular

**EMG SIGNAL:**
- ✅ Amplitude: **50 µV – 5 mV** | Frequency: **20 Hz – 500 Hz**
- ✅ Source impedance: **100 kΩ – 10 MΩ** (require high-input-Z amplifier)
- ✅ Motor Unit = neuron + all fibers it innervates
- ✅ EMG = spatiotemporal sum of many **MUAPs**

**MEMBRANE POTENTIALS:**
- ✅ Resting potential ≈ **−70 to −90 mV**
- ✅ **Nernst equation** = single ion equilibrium
- ✅ **GHK equation** = multi-ion resting potential (preferred KTU answer)
- ✅ **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** consumes 1 ATP per cycle: 3 Na⁺ out + 2 K⁺ in
- ✅ Standard ion concentrations: K⁺ in=150, out=4 mM | Na⁺ in=12, out=145 mM

**SMOOTH MUSCLE ACTION POTENTIALS:**
- ✅ Depolarizing ion = **Ca²⁺ (NOT Na⁺)** — KEY KTU distinction
- ✅ **Slow waves** from pacemaker cells precede spike
- ✅ **Plateau phase** present in visceral smooth muscle
- ✅ Spike duration: **10–50 ms** (much longer than skeletal ~1–2 ms)
- ✅ Two types: **Single-unit (visceral, syncytial)** vs **Multi-unit (separate cells)**
- ✅ Pacemaker activity → myogenic contraction (e.g., gut peristalsis)
- ✅ K⁺ efflux repolarizes; SERCA pump removes Ca²⁺

**CRITICAL FORMULAS TO MEMORIZE:**
- ✅ $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ (Nernst)
- ✅ $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{\sum P_{ion}[ion]_{out}}{\sum P_{ion}[ion]_{in}}$ (GHK)
- ✅ $\frac{RT}{F}$ at 310 K = **26.7 mV** (ln) or **61.5 mV** (log₁₀)
- ✅ $\Delta G_{pump} = RT \ln \frac{[Na]_{in}}{[Na]_{out}} + FV_m$ (per ion)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** When asked "Why does Ag/AgCl dominate clinical electrodes?", answer in **one line**: *"Ag/AgCl is a non-polarizable electrode — its half-cell potential is stable under current flow, minimizing baseline drift in biopotential recording."* This single sentence has fetched full marks in past KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of EMG Signal. Heart Sounds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of EMG Signal. Heart Sounds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electromyography (EMG) Signal — The "Language of Muscles"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Electromyography (EMG)** is the bioelectrical recording of the spatial and temporal summation of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** generated during voluntary or involuntary muscular contraction. It represents the electrical signature of the neuromuscular system.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine an orchestra playing a symphony. The audience hears the combined sound of violins, flutes, drums, and trumpets. They don't hear each instrument individually — they hear the **superimposed vibration** of all instruments at once. Similarly, an EMG electrode "listens" to thousands of muscle fibres (muscle cells) firing simultaneously. The waveform it records is the **algebraic sum** of all these tiny electrical "notes" produced by individual muscle cells. When you tense your bicep, more muscle fibres recruit and fire in synchrony, making the signal louder (higher amplitude) and more "crowded" (higher frequency content). When you relax, the signal becomes sparse and quiet.

**Standard Physiological Metrics (KTU Mandatory Values):**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| EMG Amplitude Range | **0.1 to 5** | mV (millivolts) |
| Dominant Frequency Band | **50 to 150** | Hz |
| Full Signal Bandwidth | **20 to 500** | Hz |
| Sampling Frequency (Nyquist ≥ 1 kHz) | **≥ 1000** | Hz |
| MUAP Duration | **3 to 15** | ms |
| MUAP Amplitude | **100 to 300** | μV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of EMG:**
> 1. **Surface EMG (sEMG)** — Non-invasive electrodes on skin. Captures *summation* of many motor units. Used in **prosthetic control, sports science, ergonomics**.
> 2. **Needle/Intramuscular EMG** — Needle inserted into muscle. Captures *individual* MUAPs. Used in **clinical diagnosis** of neuropathies and myopathies.

---

### 1.2 Heart Sounds — The "Rhythmic Signature of Life"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Heart Sounds** are the **mechano-acoustic vibrations** generated by the closure of heart valves, turbulent blood flow, and vibration of cardiac structures during the cardiac cycle. The graphical recording of these sounds is termed a **Phonocardiogram (PCG)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the heart as a house with four doors (valves) and a plumbing system. The "lub-dub" sound you hear through a stethoscope is similar to doors slamming shut. **"Lub" (S1)** is the mitral and tricuspid doors closing shut (the "AV valves" closing when ventricles begin pumping). **"Dub" (S2)** is the aortic and pulmonary doors slamming shut (the "semilunar valves" closing when ventricles relax). Any extra "whooshes" (murmurs) are like water flowing through a partially open door — they hint at either a leaky door (regurgitation) or a narrowed doorway (stenosis).

**Standard Phonocardiogram Parameters (KTU Mandatory Values):**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Heart Sound Frequency Range | **20 to 2000** | Hz |
| S1 Main Frequency | **30 to 80** | Hz |
| S2 Main Frequency | **50 to 100** | Hz |
| Murmurs Frequency | **100 to 600+** | Hz |
| Heart Rate (Adult Resting) | **60 to 100** | bpm |
| S1-S2 Interval (Systole) | **≈ 0.3** | s (at 75 bpm) |
| S2-S1 Interval (Diastole) | **≈ 0.5** | s (at 75 bpm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Heart Sounds — Core KTU Content:**
> - **S1 ("Lub")** — Closure of **Mitral (M2)** and **Tricuspid (T1)** valves → marks **end of diastole, start of systole**.
> - **S2 ("Dub")** — Closure of **Aortic (A2)** and **Pulmonic (P2)** valves → marks **end of systole, start of diastole**.
> - **S3** — Low-frequency vibration due to rapid ventricular filling (pathological in adults — indicates heart failure).
> - **S4** — Atrial contraction against stiff ventricle (always pathological — indicates hypertension/left ventricular hypertrophy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Anatomy of an EMG Signal

**Why does EMG look so noisy?**
Because it is a *stochastic* (random) superposition of many individual MUAPs. The decomposition is as follows:

**Step 1: Single Fibre Action Potential (SFAP)**
A single muscle fibre, when stimulated by its motor neuron, produces a brief depolarization-repolarization cycle.

$$V_{SFAP}(t) = A \cdot e^{-(t-t_0)^2 / 2\sigma^2}$$

where $A$ is the peak amplitude and $\sigma$ controls the width (typically 1–3 ms).

**Step 2: Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**
A motor unit consists of **one motor neuron + all the muscle fibres it innervates**. The MUAP is the sum of all SFAPs of fibres belonging to that motor unit, recorded through the tissue (which acts as a volume conductor with low-pass filtering effect).

$$V_{MUAP}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{fibres}} V_{SFAP,i}(t - \tau_i) \cdot h_{volume}(t)$$

where $N_{fibres}$ is the number of muscle fibres in the motor unit and $h_{volume}(t)$ is the impulse response of the volume conductor (skin, fat, muscle tissue).

**Step 3: EMG Signal (Full Record)**
The total EMG signal is the **superposition of all active MUAPs**, with random firing times, multiplied by the recruitment pattern.

$$V_{EMG}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{M} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} V_{MUAP,k}(t - t_{k,n}) + n_{noise}(t)$$

where $M$ is the number of active motor units, $t_{k,n}$ is the random firing time of the $n$-th discharge of motor unit $k$, and $n_{noise}$ includes **motion artefact**, **line interference (50/60 Hz)**, and **inherent thermal noise**.

**Step 4: Force-EMG Relationship**
A *quasi-linear* relationship exists between muscle force and integrated EMG (iEMG):

$$F(t) = \alpha \cdot \text{iEMG}(t) + F_0$$

$$\text{iEMG}(t) = \int_{t-T}^{t} \vert V_{EMG}(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

where $F_0$ is the baseline (passive) force and $\alpha$ is the gain constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Physiological Mechanisms of Force Modulation (Size Principle — Henneman, 1981):**
> 1. **Motor Unit Recruitment** — Activation of *additional* motor units (small → large, "onion-skin" pattern).
> 2. **Rate Coding** — Increasing the *firing rate* of already-recruited motor units.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Heart Sounds

**Step 1: Cardiac Cycle Timing Reference**

The **electrocardiogram (ECG)** provides the electrical reference. Key waves:
- **P wave** → Atrial depolarization (atrial contraction begins)
- **QRS complex** → Ventricular depolarization (ventricular contraction begins)
- **T wave** → Ventricular repolarization (ventricular relaxation)

**Step 2: Sound Generation Physics**

Heart sounds are produced by **sudden deceleration of blood mass** when valves close (loudspeaker-cone model). The frequency of the sound depends on the **stiffness of the vibrating structure**:

$$f \propto \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

where $k$ is the effective stiffness of the valve-leaflet/blood-column system and $m$ is the effective vibrating mass. Stiffer structures → higher frequency. This is why S2 (closed semilunar valves) is shorter and higher-pitched than S1.

**Step 3: Auscultation Areas (KTU must-know)**

Although sound is generated at the valves, it is best heard at specific chest locations where the sound is transmitted with least attenuation:

| Auscultation Area | Valve Heard | Anatomical Landmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Aortic Area** | Aortic valve | 2nd right intercostal space, sternal border |
| **Pulmonic Area** | Pulmonic valve | 2nd left intercostal space, sternal border |
| **Tricuspid Area** | Tricuspid valve | 4th–5th left intercostal space, sternal border |
| **Mitral Area (Apex)** | Mitral valve | 5th left intercostal space, mid-clavicular line |

**Step 4: Murmur Classification (High-Yield for KTU)**

A **murmur** is an extra "whoosh" caused by **turbulent blood flow**. Classified by:

- **Timing in cycle:** Systolic (between S1 and S2) or Diastolic (between S2 and next S1)
- **Pitch:** High-pitched or low-pitched
- **Quality:** Harsh, blowing, musical, rumbling
- **Shape on phonocardiogram:** Crescendo, decrescendo, diamond-shaped, plateau

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EMG Amplitude | $A_{EMG} \in [0.1, 5]$ | mV — peak-to-peak |
| 2 | EMG Bandwidth | $f_{EMG} \in [20, 500]$ | Hz — full spectrum |
| 3 | Dominant EMG Freq. | $f_d \in [50, 150]$ | Hz — main power region |
| 4 | MUAP Duration | $T_{MUAP} \in [3, 15]$ | ms |
| 5 | iEMG (Integrated EMG) | $iEMG = \int \vert V_{EMG} \vert \, dt$ | mV·s — correlates with force |
| 6 | RMS EMG | $RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V^2_{EMG}(t)\,dt}$ | mV — amplitude estimator |
| 7 | Mean Frequency | $f_{mean} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} f \cdot P(f)\,df}{\int_0^{\infty} P(f)\,df}$ | Hz — fatigue indicator |
| 8 | Median Frequency | $f_{med} : \int_0^{f_{med}} P(f)df = \int_{f_{med}}^{\infty} P(f)df$ | Hz — robust fatigue indicator |
| 9 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max} \Rightarrow f_s \geq 1000$ | Hz — for EMG |
| 10 | Heart Sound BW | $f_{PCG} \in [20, 2000]$ | Hz — full audible range |
| 11 | S1-S2 Duration (Systole) | $T_{sys} \approx 0.3$ | s — at 75 bpm |
| 12 | S2-S1 Duration (Diastole) | $T_{dia} \approx 0.5$ | s — at 75 bpm |
| 13 | HR Computation | $HR = \frac{60}{T_{R-R}}$ | bpm |
| 14 | Sampling for PCG | $f_s \geq 4000$ | Hz — for heart sounds |
| 15 | Shannon Entropy (EMG) | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits — non-linear analysis |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean Frequency of EMG (Application in Fatigue Analysis)

**Problem:** Show that the *mean frequency* of an EMG signal decreases with muscle fatigue, and derive the closed-form expression used in KTU numerical problems.

**Step 1 — Definition of Power Spectral Density (PSD):**
For a zero-mean random signal $x(t)$ (the EMG), the PSD is given by the Fourier transform of its autocorrelation function $R_{xx}(\tau)$:

$$P(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{xx}(\tau) \cdot e^{-j2\pi f \tau} \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Total Power (Parseval's Theorem):**
The total power of the EMG signal is:

$$P_{total} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(f) \, df = R_{xx}(0) = E[x^2(t)]$$

**Step 3 — First Spectral Moment:**
The mean frequency is defined as the first spectral moment normalized by the zeroth moment (total power):

$$f_{mean} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} f \cdot P(f) \, df}{\int_{0}^{\infty} P(f) \, df}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Computation (Discrete Form):**
For a discretized PSD with $N$ frequency bins:

$$f_{mean} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P(f_i) \cdot \Delta f}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P(f_i) \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i \cdot P(f_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P(f_i)}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**
During sustained contraction (fatigue), the *motor unit firing rate decreases* and *conduction velocity along muscle fibres drops* (due to metabolite accumulation — lactic acid, K⁺ ions). Both effects cause the PSD to shift **leftward** (towards lower frequencies). Therefore, $f_{mean}$ **decreases** with fatigue. This is a clinically validated fatigue indicator.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
Suppose a PSD has only three frequency bins with values:

| Bin | $f_i$ (Hz) | $P(f_i)$ (mV²/Hz) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 0.10 |
| 2 | 100 | 0.60 |
| 3 | 150 | 0.30 |

$$f_{mean} = \frac{(50)(0.10) + (100)(0.60) + (150)(0.30)}{0.10 + 0.60 + 0.30}$$

$$f_{mean} = \frac{5 + 60 + 45}{1.0} = \frac{110}{1.0} = 110 \text{ Hz}$$

This is the KTU-standard pre-fatigue mean frequency. Post-fatigue, the values would shift to e.g. (30, 0.20), (80, 0.55), (130, 0.25) yielding:

$$f_{mean,fatigued} = \frac{6 + 44 + 32.5}{1.0} = 82.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Demonstrating a clear fatigue-induced spectral compression.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Heart Rate from PCG Signal Envelope

**Problem:** Derive the heart rate in bpm given a PCG signal in which the peaks of S1 are detected at times $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots, t_N$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Tachogram:**
The instantaneous heart period (inter-beat interval) between consecutive S1 peaks:

$$T_{RR}(k) = t_{k+1} - t_{k}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous Heart Rate:**
Convert each interval to bpm:

$$HR(k) = \frac{60}{T_{RR}(k)} \quad [\text{bpm}]$$

**Step 3 — Mean Heart Rate:**
For a recording of $N$ heart beats, the mean heart rate is:

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{k=1}^{N-1} HR(k) = \frac{60}{N-1} \sum_{k=1}^{N-1} \frac{1}{t_{k+1} - t_{k}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**
Five S1 peaks detected at $t = 0.0, 0.85, 1.65, 2.55, 3.40$ seconds.

Intervals: $T_{RR} = 0.85, 0.80, 0.90, 0.85$ s

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{60}{4} \left(\frac{1}{0.85} + \frac{1}{0.80} + \frac{1}{0.90} + \frac{1}{0.85}\right)$$

$$= 15 \times (1.1765 + 1.2500 + 1.1111 + 1.1765)$$

$$= 15 \times 4.7141 = 70.71 \text{ bpm}$$

This is within the normal adult resting range (60–100 bpm). ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: EMG vs. Heart Sounds Comparison

| Feature | EMG Signal | Heart Sounds (PCG) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Electrical (ionic) | Mechanical (acoustic) |
| **Source** | Muscle fibre depolarization | Valve closure / blood turbulence |
| **Modality** | Electrical potential (mV) | Pressure/vibration (arbitrary) |
| **Frequency Range** | 20–500 Hz | 20–2000 Hz |
| **Dominant Band** | 50–150 Hz | 30–100 Hz (S1, S2); 100–600 Hz (murmurs) |
| **Standard Sampling** | ≥ 1 kHz | ≥ 4 kHz |
| **Clinical Use** | Neuromuscular disorders, prosthetics | Valvular disease, murmurs, S3/S4 |
| **Acquisition** | Surface or needle electrodes | Electronic stethoscope / microphone |
| **Artefacts** | Motion, line interference (50 Hz) | Lung sounds, ambient noise, motion |
| **Signal Type** | Quasi-stochastic | Quasi-periodic with bursts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: EMG Signal Acquisition & Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Brain Motor Cortex] --> B[Spinal Cord Motor Neuron]
    B --> C[Axonal Action Potential]
    C --> D[Neuromuscular Junction]
    D --> E[Muscle Fibre Depolarization]
    E --> F[Sum of MUAPs in Volume Conductor]
    F --> G[Surface or Needle Electrodes]
    G --> H[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]

    I --> J[50 Hz Notch Filter]
    J --> K[ADC Sampling >= 1 kHz]
    K --> L[Digital Signal Processing]
    L --> M[Feature Extraction RMS iEMG Fmean]
    M --> N[Clinical Decision Prosthesis Control Rehab]
    subgraph AcquisitionChain[Acquisition Chain]
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

        G
        H

```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Heart Sound (PCG) Generation & Auscultation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[SA Node Pacemaker] --> RA[Right Atrium Contraction]
    RA --> LA[Left Atrium Contraction]
    LA --> V1[Ventricular Depolarization QRS]
```
