```mermaid
    V1 --> S1B[Closure of Tricuspid Valve T1]
    S1A --> S1[Sound S1 LUB]
    S1B --> S1
    S1 --> VE[Ventricular Ejection Phase]
    VE --> S2A[Closure of Aortic Valve A2]
    VE --> S2B[Closure of Pulmonic Valve P2]
    S2A --> S2[Sound S2 DUB]
    S2B --> S2
    S2 --> VF[Ventricular Filling Phase]
    VF --> S3[Optional S3]
    S3 --> AC[Atrial Contraction]
    AC --> S4[Optional S4]
    S1 --> AUSC[Auscultation Points Chest Wall]
    S2 --> AUSC
    S3 --> AUSC
    S4 --> AUSC
    AUSC --> MIC[Electronic Stethoscope Microphone]
    MIC --> AMP[Amplifier and Filter 20 Hz to 2 kHz]
    AMP --> DSP[PCG Signal Analysis]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: EMG Signal Generation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Single Ion Channel] --> L2[Single Muscle Fibre SFAP]
    L2 --> L3[Motor Unit One Neuron plus Fibres MUAP]
    L3 --> L4[Multiple Active Motor Units]

    L5 --> L6[Recorded via Surface or Needle Electrode]
    L1 -- Depolarization --> L2
    L2 -- Volume Conduction --> L3

```

```mermaid
    Q --> S1NODE[Sound S1 Closure of AV Valves]
    S1NODE --> ST[ST Segment Systole]
    ST --> TNODE[T Wave Ventricular Rep]
    TNODE --> S2NODE[Sound S2 Closure of Semilunar Valves]
    S2NODE --> D[Diastole]
    D --> PNEXT[P Wave Next Cycle]
    S1NODE -. Audible LUB .-> S1LABEL
    S2NODE -. Audible DUB .-> S2LABEL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define EMG signal. List its standard amplitude and frequency range.*

**Model Answer:**

Electromyography (EMG) is the bioelectrical recording of the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles during contraction. It represents the spatial and temporal summation of Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs).

- **Amplitude range:** 0.1 mV to 5 mV
- **Frequency range:** 20 Hz to 500 Hz (dominant energy between 50 Hz and 150 Hz)

The signal is acquired using surface or intramuscular (needle) electrodes and is used in clinical diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders and in prosthetic control.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for amplitude, 1 for frequency)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List the four heart sounds and state the cause of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Sound | Cause | Timing |
|---|---|---|
| **S1 ("Lub")** | Closure of Mitral (M2) and Tricuspid (T1) valves | Beginning of systole |
| **S2 ("Dub")** | Closure of Aortic (A2) and Pulmonic (P2) valves | Beginning of diastole |
| **S3** | Rapid ventricular filling (physiological in children; pathological in adults) | Early diastole |
| **S4** | Atrial contraction against a stiff/non-compliant ventricle (always pathological) | Late diastole (just before S1) |

*(3 marks: 0.5 per sound + cause + timing)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Model]**

**(a)** Explain the generation of the EMG signal starting from the motor unit action potential. Discuss the parameters of the EMG signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the acquisition and processing of an EMG signal. Mention the artefacts and methods to remove them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Generation of EMG Signal — 7 marks**

The EMG signal generation is a hierarchical physiological process:

1. **Brain command (1 mark):** The motor cortex generates a voluntary command that travels down the corticospinal tract to the alpha motor neuron in the spinal cord ventral horn.

2. **Motor Unit definition (1 mark):** A *motor unit* consists of one alpha motor neuron and all the muscle fibres it innervates. When the neuron fires, *all* the fibres in that motor unit contract together.

3. **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) (2 marks):** Each muscle fibre within the motor unit produces a *Single Fibre Action Potential (SFAP)*. The MUAP is the algebraic sum of all SFAPs, propagated through the volume conductor (muscle tissue, fat, skin) which acts as a low-pass filter.

4. **EMG signal formation (2 marks):** The full EMG signal is the temporal and spatial summation of all active MUAPs from many motor units. Two physiological mechanisms control force:
   - **Motor unit recruitment** (Henneman's size principle — small units first)
   - **Rate coding** (firing rate modulation of active units)

5. **EMG parameters (1 mark):** Amplitude: 0.1–5 mV; Frequency: 20–500 Hz (dominant 50–150 Hz); MUAP duration: 3–15 ms.

**(b) EMG Acquisition & Processing — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
Motor Unit → Electrodes → Instrumentation Amplifier (Gain ~1000)
            → Bandpass Filter (20–500 Hz) → 50 Hz Notch Filter
            → ADC (f_s ≥ 1 kHz) → DSP → Features/Display
```

**Step-by-step description (2 marks):**

1. **Electrodes** — Surface (Ag/AgCl) or needle; convert ionic currents to electronic voltage.
2. **Instrumentation amplifier** — High CMRR (>100 dB) to reject common-mode noise; gain ~1000.
3. **Bandpass filter** — Removes DC offset (<20 Hz) and high-frequency noise (>500 Hz).
4. **Notch filter** — Removes 50 Hz power-line interference (Q-factor ~30).
5. **ADC** — Sampling rate ≥ 1 kHz, resolution ≥ 12-bit.
6. **DSP** — Rectification, smoothing, RMS / iEMG / Mean frequency computation.

**Artefacts and removal (1 mark):**

| Artefact | Source | Removal Method |
|---|---|---|
| 50 Hz line interference | Power mains | Notch filter at 50 Hz |
| Motion artefact | Electrode-skin movement | High-pass filter (cutoff 20 Hz) |
| ECG cross-talk | Heart's electrical field (in trunk EMG) | Adaptive filtering / blind source separation |
| Baseline drift | Electrode polarization | AC coupling, high-pass filter |
| Inherent noise | Electrode/skin impedance | Low-impedance gel, proper skin prep |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model]**

**(a)** Describe the four heart sounds, their timing, frequency content, and clinical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the Phonocardiogram (PCG) acquisition system. Discuss the importance of heart sound analysis in clinical diagnosis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Heart Sounds — 7 marks**

1. **S1 — First Heart Sound (2 marks)**
   - **Cause:** Closure of the Mitral (M2) and Tricuspid (T1) atrioventricular valves at the *start of ventricular systole*.
   - **Timing:** Just after the QRS complex of the ECG.
   - **Frequency:** 30–80 Hz (low-pitched, dull "lub").
   - **Duration:** ~0.14 s.
   - **Clinical:** Loud S1 in mitral stenosis; soft S1 in mitral regurgitation or calcified valves.

2. **S2 — Second Heart Sound (2 marks)**
   - **Cause:** Closure of the Aortic (A2) and Pulmonic (P2) semilunar valves at the *end of ventricular systole*.
   - **Timing:** Just after the T wave of the ECG.
   - **Frequency:** 50–100 Hz (higher-pitched, sharper "dub").
   - **Splitting:** A2-P2 split widens on inspiration (physiological).
   - **Clinical:** Loud P2 in pulmonary hypertension; fixed split in ASD.

3. **S3 — Third Heart Sound (1.5 marks)**
   - **Cause:** Rapid ventricular filling during early diastole (passive blood rush).
   - **Frequency:** <30 Hz (very low, often palpable as a "ventricular gallop").
   - **Clinical:** Physiological in children/young adults; pathological in adults — indicates *ventricular failure, volume overload* (e.g., CHF, mitral regurgitation).

4. **S4 — Fourth Heart Sound (1.5 marks)**
   - **Cause:** Atrial contraction forcing blood into a *stiff, non-compliant* ventricle (late diastole).
   - **Frequency:** <30 Hz (low-pitched "atrial gallop" — "Tennessee" rhythm).
   - **Clinical:** *Always pathological* — indicates left ventricular hypertrophy, hypertension, aortic stenosis, ischemic heart disease.

**(b) Phonocardiogram (PCG) Acquisition — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Heart → Chest Wall Vibration → Microphone / Electronic Stethoscope
       → Preamplifier → Bandpass Filter (20 Hz – 2 kHz)
       → 50 Hz Notch → ADC (f_s ≥ 4 kHz) → DSP/Display
```

**Step-by-step description (3 marks):**

1. **Transducer** — Electronic stethoscope (piezoelectric microphone) or accelerometer; converts chest vibrations to electrical signal. The *air-coupled* type is sensitive to ambient noise; the *contact accelerometer* is more robust.
2. **Preamplifier** — Low-noise, gain 100–1000.
3. **Bandpass filter** — 20 Hz (to reject DC and motion artefact) to 2 kHz (to capture murmurs).
4. **50 Hz notch filter** — Removes power-line interference.
5. **ADC** — Sampling rate ≥ 4 kHz (Nyquist criterion for 2 kHz max); resolution ≥ 12-bit.
6. **DSP** — Segmentation (S1, systole, S2, diastole), envelope extraction, murmur detection.

**Clinical significance (1 mark):**

- Detection of **valvular diseases** (stenosis, regurgitation) via murmur analysis.
- Detection of **S3, S4 gallops** indicating heart failure or hypertrophy.
- **Timing analysis** of systole/diastole to identify conduction abnormalities.
- **Telemedicine applications** — automated PCG analysis via AI for remote cardiac screening.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing S1 and S2 frequencies** — S1 is *lower-pitched* (30–80 Hz) and S2 is *higher-pitched* (50–100 Hz). Many students write the opposite and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the second component** of S2 — Always write "A2 and P2" together, not just "aortic valve".
> 3. **Mixing up sampling rates** — EMG: ≥ 1 kHz; PCG: ≥ 4 kHz. Many students use the same value for both.
> 4. **Stating S3 as always pathological** — It is *physiological* in children/young adults and *pathological* only in adults. This nuance fetches full marks.
> 5. **Confusing "murmur" with "gallop"** — A murmur is a *whoosh* (turbulent flow); S3/S4 are *gallops* (low-frequency vibrations).
> 6. **Not mentioning the 50 Hz notch filter** for EMG/PCG in the block diagram — examiners specifically look for it in KTU 2024 scheme answers.
> 7. **Writing EMG amplitude in μV** — It is in **mV** (0.1–5 mV). Mis-scaling the units costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMG — Critical Points:**
> - EMG = **E**lectro**M**yo**G**raphy — measures muscle electrical activity.
> - Amplitude: **0.1 to 5 mV** | Bandwidth: **20–500 Hz** | Dominant: **50–150 Hz**.
> - Sample at **≥ 1 kHz** (Nyquist for 500 Hz).
> - Generation hierarchy: **Single fibre → Motor unit → Many motor units → EMG signal**.
> - Two mechanisms of force control: **Recruitment** + **Rate coding** (Henneman's size principle).
> - Features: **RMS** (amplitude), **iEMG** (integrated), **Mean/Median frequency** (fatigue).
> - During fatigue, **$f_{mean}$ decreases** (spectral compression).
> - Artefacts: **50 Hz line interference** (use notch filter), **motion artefact** (use HPF), **ECG cross-talk** (use adaptive filtering).
> - **Surface EMG** = non-invasive, summed activity | **Needle EMG** = invasive, individual MUAPs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heart Sounds — Critical Points:**
> - "**Lub-Dub**" = S1-S2 in one cardiac cycle.
> - **S1** = AV valve closure (Mitral + Tricuspid) → marks start of **systole** → low frequency **30–80 Hz**.
> - **S2** = Semilunar valve closure (Aortic + Pulmonic) → marks start of **diastole** → higher frequency **50–100 Hz**.
> - **S3** = Rapid ventricular filling → early diastole → physiological in young, pathological in adults (CHF).
> - **S4** = Atrial kick against stiff ventricle → late diastole → **always pathological** (LV hypertrophy, hypertension).
> - **Murmurs** = whooshes from turbulent flow → frequency **100–600+ Hz**.
> - Auscultation: **Aortic** (2nd ICS right), **Pulmonic** (2nd ICS left), **Tricuspid** (4th ICS left), **Mitral** (5th ICS mid-clavicular).
> - PCG sampling: **≥ 4 kHz** (Nyquist for 2 kHz murmurs).
> - HR (bpm) = **60 / T_RR** where T_RR is the S1-to-S1 interval in seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phonocardiography. PPG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Phonocardiography (PCG)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Phonocardiography (PCG)** is the non-invasive, graphical recording of the acoustic vibrations produced by the mechanical activity of the heart (closure of valves, turbulent blood flow, and myocardial vibrations), typically obtained using an electronic stethoscope, microphone, or accelerometer placed on the chest wall, and displayed as amplitude-versus-time traces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "Phonocardiography is the technique of recording heart sounds and murmurs arising from the closure of heart valves and turbulent blood flow, by converting the acoustic vibrations into electrical signals using a suitable transducer, which are then amplified, filtered, and displayed as a function of time."

### Conceptual Analogy — The Heart as a Drum
Imagine the human heart as a **mechanical drum** inside a sealed chamber (the chest cavity). Every time a valve slams shut (mitral/tricuspid → "lub"; aortic/pulmonic → "dub"), the surrounding blood, muscle, and ribs vibrate briefly, producing a faint "thump." Just as a recording engineer places a microphone against a drum to capture its beat, a phonocardiograph places a sensitive transducer on the chest wall to **listen, amplify, and chart** these vibrations. The resulting PCG trace is essentially a **graphical drum-score** of cardiac mechanics.

### Key Standard Metrics
- **Frequency band of S1 (first heart sound):** **10 – 200 Hz** (dominant energy near **25 – 45 Hz**)
- **Frequency band of S2 (second heart sound):** **10 – 400 Hz** (dominant energy near **50 – 150 Hz**)
- **Murmurs (pathological sounds):** **20 – 2000 Hz**
- **Standard sampling rate recommended:** **≥ 4000 Hz** (to satisfy Nyquist up to 2 kHz murmurs)
- **Standard auscultation sites:** Aortic (2nd ICS, RSB), Pulmonic (2nd ICS, LSB), Tricuspid (4th ICS, LSB), Mitral (5th ICS, MCL)

> [!NOTE]
> Heart sounds are **mechanical**, not electrical. This is the fundamental distinction from the ECG, which records the *electrical* triggering of the heart. PCG records the *consequence* of that trigger.

---

## 1.2 Photoplethysmography (PPG)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Photoplethysmography (PPG)** is a non-invasive optical technique that detects volumetric changes in blood within the microvascular bed of tissue by illuminating the skin with light (typically red and/or infrared) and measuring the intensity of either transmitted or reflected light, thereby providing a continuous waveform proportional to the pulsatile arterial blood volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "Photoplethysmography is an optical measurement technique used to detect blood volume changes in the microvasculature of tissue. It operates on the principle of differential light absorption between oxygenated and deoxygenated haemoglobin, producing a waveform that contains both a pulsatile (AC) component synchronized with cardiac systole and a quasi-static (DC) baseline component."

### Conceptual Analogy — The Flashlight and the Window
Hold a **flashlight** behind your finger in a dark room. You will notice that the light passing through the finger is *red-ish* and that the brightness **pulses** in sync with your heartbeat. Why? Because each heartbeat pumps an extra surge of arterial blood into the finger, temporarily increasing the volume of light-absorbing blood and so dimming the transmitted light. A PPG sensor is exactly this — a **miniaturized flashlight + photodetector** that turns these micro-fluctuations in light intensity into a continuous waveform. The rising edge of the pulse corresponds to systole; the slower decay corresponds to diastolic run-off and vascular tone.

### Key Standard Metrics
- **Standard light wavelengths used:** **Red ≈ 660 nm**, **Infrared (IR) ≈ 940 nm**
- **AC component amplitude:** **1 – 10 %** of the total DC level
- **DC component sources:** Venous blood, non-pulsatile arterial blood, skin, bone, tissue
- **Typical sampling rate:** **100 – 500 Hz** (cardiac pulsation has energy below ~20 Hz)
- **AC/DC ratio (Perfusion Index, PI):** Typically **0.02 – 20 %**

> [!NOTE]
> PPG is the **physical-volume counterpart** of the ECG's electrical trigger — both repeat once per cardiac cycle, but the PPG carries **hemodynamic** information (pulse wave velocity, arterial stiffness, SpO₂) that the ECG cannot.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phonocardiography — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Genesis of Heart Sounds
Heart sounds arise from **transient mechanical vibrations** of cardiac structures, accelerations/decelerations of blood, and turbulent flow, set in motion by valve closure.

| Sound | Cause | Approximate Frequency (Hz) | Duration (ms) |
|-------|-------|---------------------------|---------------|
| **S1 ("lub")** | Closure of Mitral (M1) and Tricuspid (T1) valves | 10 – 200 | 100 – 200 |
| **S2 ("dub")** | Closure of Aortic (A2) and Pulmonic (P2) valves | 10 – 400 | 80 – 140 |
| **S3** | Rapid ventricular filling (early diastole) | 20 – 70 | 40 – 80 |
| **S4** | Atrial contraction against stiff ventricle | 20 – 100 | 30 – 60 |
| **Murmurs** | Turbulent flow, valve regurgitation/stenosis, septal defects | 20 – 2000 | Sustained |

### 2.1.2 PCG System Block Diagram (Logical Steps)
1. **Acoustic source** — heart sounds & murmurs reach chest wall.
2. **Transducer** — piezoelectric microphone, electret microphone, or accelerometer converts pressure/vibration into an analog electrical signal.
3. **Pre-amplifier** — low-noise amplification (gain ≈ 40 – 60 dB) using instrumentation amplifiers.
4. **Anti-aliasing filter** — low-pass filter with cutoff **≥ 2 kHz** (typically Butterworth, 4th order).
5. **Band-pass filtering** — usually **20 Hz – 2 kHz** to suppress low-frequency chest-wall motion and high-frequency noise.
6. **ADC** — sampling at **≥ 4 kHz** with at least **12-bit** resolution.
7. **Digital processing** — wavelet denoising, segmentation, feature extraction.
8. **Display / Storage** — waveform on monitor or paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are heart sounds low-frequency despite "sounds"?** The S1/S2 vibrations propagate through a **fluid-loaded, low-pass biological medium** (lung parenchyma, ribs, muscle). The high-frequency harmonics are filtered out, leaving mostly the 10 – 200 Hz "boom." Murmurs, with their high-frequency content, indicate abnormal high-velocity turbulent jets that retain energy up to 2 kHz.

### 2.1.3 Time–Frequency Analysis of PCG (Why Fourier Alone Fails)
Heart sounds are **non-stationary** — their spectral content changes within milliseconds. A plain Fourier transform averages over the whole recording and hides *when* the frequency changed. The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** and especially the **Wavelet Transform (WT)** are mandatory for PCG analysis.

The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) is defined as:

$$W(a,b) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,\psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

where $\psi(t)$ is the mother wavelet, $a$ is the scale (inverse of frequency), and $b$ is the translation (time shift). Common mother wavelets used in PCG analysis are **Daubechies (db4, db6)**, **Symlets**, and **Coiflets**, chosen for their compact support and orthogonality.

---

## 2.2 Photoplethysmography — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Physical Principle — Beer–Lambert Law
The Beer–Lambert law relates the attenuation of light to the absorbing medium's properties. For monochromatic light traversing a homogeneous absorber of path length $l$ and concentration $c$:

$$I(\lambda) \;=\; I_{0}(\lambda)\,e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l}$$

The absorbance (optical density) is:

$$A(\lambda) \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{0}(\lambda)}{I(\lambda)}\right) \;=\; \varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l$$

In tissue, the effective absorbance is the **weighted sum** of contributions from multiple absorbers (oxy-haemoglobin HbO₂, deoxy-haemoglobin HHb, water, melanin, etc.):

$$A(\lambda) \;=\; \sum_{k} \varepsilon_{k}(\lambda)\,c_{k}\,l$$

> [!NOTE]
> For PPG, the dominant absorbers that change during a cardiac cycle are **HbO₂** and **HHb** in arterial blood. The variable path length $l$ in tissue is what converts the optical signal into a *blood-volume* proxy.

### 2.2.2 Two Operating Modes
- **Transmission Mode:** Light source and photodetector are placed on *opposite* sides of the tissue (e.g., fingertip, earlobe). Best for thin appendages.
- **Reflectance (Reflection) Mode:** Source and detector are on the *same* side of the tissue (e.g., forehead, wrist, under the finger pad). Better suited for thick or curved body parts and wearable devices.

### 2.2.3 PPG Signal Anatomy — AC and DC Components
The PPG waveform $y(t)$ decomposes as:

$$y(t) \;=\; y_{\text{DC}}(t) \;+\; y_{\text{AC}}(t)$$

| Component | Physiological Origin | Frequency | Clinical Use |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------|---------------|
| **DC** | Venous blood, non-pulsatile arterial blood, skin, bone, muscle, ambient light drift | 0 – 0.5 Hz | Baseline perfusion, sensor contact |
| **AC** | Pulsatile arterial blood volume (systolic surge) | 0.5 – 5 Hz (cardiac band) | Heart rate, pulse waveform, SpO₂ |

The **Perfusion Index (PI)** is defined as:

$$\text{PI} \;=\; \frac{\text{AC}_{\text{peak-to-peak}}}{\text{DC}} \times 100\;\text{(\%)}$$

### 2.2.4 Pulse Oximetry — A SpO₂ Application
By taking the ratio $R$ of normalized AC amplitudes at two wavelengths:

$$R \;=\; \frac{\left(\dfrac{\text{AC}}{\text{DC}}\right)_{\text{Red}}}{\left(\dfrac{\text{AC}}{\text{DC}}\right)_{\text{IR}}}$$

an empirical linear calibration converts $R$ to arterial oxygen saturation:

$$\text{SpO}_{2} \;=\; A \;-\; B \cdot R$$

where $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants (typically $A \approx 110$, $B \approx 25$ for fingertip sensors).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Units | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|----------|
| 1 | $I = I_{0} e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | $I$ transmitted, $I_{0}$ incident, $\varepsilon$ molar absorptivity, $c$ concentration, $l$ path length | — | PPG optical basis |
| 2 | $A = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{0}}{I}\right) = \varepsilon c l$ | $A$ absorbance | AU | Beer–Lambert absorbance |
| 3 | $y(t) = y_{\text{DC}}(t) + y_{\text{AC}}(t)$ | PPG components | V (or a.u.) | PPG decomposition |
| 4 | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{AC}_{\text{pp}}}{\text{DC}} \times 100\,\%$ | Perfusion Index | % | Perfusion quality |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{(\text{AC}/\text{DC})_{\text{Red}}}{(\text{AC}/\text{DC})_{\text{IR}}}$ | Normalized ratio | dimensionless | Pulse oximetry |
| 6 | $\text{SpO}_{2} = A - B \cdot R$ | Empirical calibration | % | Oxygen saturation |
| 7 | $W(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | CWT of $x(t)$ | — | PCG time–frequency analysis |
| 8 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Minimum sampling for PCG | Hz | Anti-aliasing design |
| 9 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{\text{signal}}}{A_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | PCG amplifier design |
| 10 | $\text{PWV} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}$ | Pulse-wave velocity (PPG-PPG / PPG-ECG) | m/s | Arterial stiffness |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Phonocardiography**: Embedded in digital stethoscopes (3M Littmann CORE, Eko Core), automated murmur detection using CNNs, and AI-assisted screening for **valvular heart disease** in primary-care and rural telemedicine settings.
- **Photoplethysmography**: Smartwatches (Apple Watch, Fitbit, Samsung Galaxy Watch), clinical pulse oximeters, **contactless video-PPG (vPPG / rPPG)** for remote HR monitoring, and cuff-less blood-pressure estimation via pulse-transit-time (PTT) using synchronized ECG + PPG.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Beer–Lambert Law from First Principles

The Beer–Lambert law is the *physical* foundation of PPG. We derive the intensity of transmitted monochromatic light through a homogeneous absorbing medium.

### Step 1 — Define the differential attenuation
Consider a thin slab of thickness $dl$ inside a homogeneous absorber. The fractional reduction in light intensity is proportional to the concentration of absorbing molecules and to the slab thickness:

$$dI \;=\; -\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,I\,dl$$

> *Logic:* More molecules → more absorption; thicker slab → more path; intensity enters linearly.

### Step 2 — Separate variables
Rearrange to bring all intensity terms to one side and the geometric term to the other:

$$\frac{dI}{I} \;=\; -\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,dl$$

### Step 3 — Integrate across the full path length
Integrate the left side from $I_{0}$ (incident at the entry face) to $I$ (transmitted at the exit face), and the right side from $0$ to $l$:

$$\int_{I_{0}}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} \;=\; -\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c \int_{0}^{l} dl$$

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_{0}) \;=\; -\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l$$

### Step 4 — Exponentiate
Apply the exponential to recover intensity as a function of path length:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) \;=\; -\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{I}{I_{0}} \;=\; e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l}$$

$$\boxed{\,I \;=\; I_{0}\,e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to base-10 absorbance
Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides to obtain the standard **absorbance** $A(\lambda)$ used in clinical instrumentation:

$$A(\lambda) \;=\; -\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{0}}\right) \;=\; \varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l$$

> [!NOTE]
> In PPG, $\varepsilon$ and $c$ for HbO₂ / HHb are wavelength-dependent. This wavelength dependence is precisely why **two different wavelengths (Red + IR)** are needed for SpO₂ — each wavelength "sees" oxy- and deoxy-haemoglobin with a *different* relative absorption, so the ratio $R$ becomes a unique signature of oxygen saturation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PPG Pulsatile AC Component

We now derive the *change* in transmitted intensity caused by a small pulsatile change $\Delta V$ in arterial blood volume inside the illuminated tissue.

### Step 1 — Beer–Lambert expression for tissue
For a tissue sample with arterial blood concentration $c$ and effective optical path $l$:

$$I \;=\; I_{0}\,e^{-\varepsilon\,c\,l}$$

### Step 2 — Take the small-signal differential
For a small pulsatile change $\Delta(c l)$ during systole, the change in intensity is, to first order in a Taylor expansion:

$$\Delta I \;\approx\; I_{0}\,e^{-\varepsilon c l}\cdot\left[-\varepsilon\,\Delta(c l)\right] \;=\; -I \cdot \varepsilon\,\Delta(c l)$$

### Step 3 — Express the relative intensity change
The fractional change in transmitted intensity is therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I} \;=\; -\varepsilon\,\Delta(c l)$$

> *Logic:* The pulsatile volume of arterial blood added during systole increases the effective $c \cdot l$ product for that wavelength. The result is a *dip* in $I$ that the photodetector transduces into the AC component of the PPG signal.

### Step 4 — Multi-wavelength (Pulse Oximetry) form
At two wavelengths, with empirical scaling constant $S$ accounting for scattering, optical path differences, and tissue heterogeneity:

$$\text{SpO}_{2} \;=\; A \;-\; B \cdot \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{\Delta I_{\text{Red}} / I_{\text{DC,Red}}}{\Delta I_{\text{IR}} / I_{\text{DC,IR}}}\right)}_{R}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{SpO}_{2} \;=\; A \;-\; B \cdot R\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CWT-Based PCG Energy Distribution

To prove *why* the Wavelet Transform is necessary (instead of the raw Fourier spectrum), we compute the energy contribution of each wavelet scale to the total PCG signal.

### Step 1 — Define wavelet energy at scale $a$
The wavelet energy at scale $a$ is the square of the CWT magnitude integrated over time:

$$E(a) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert W(a,b) \vert^{2}\, db$$

### Step 2 — Total signal energy (Parseval-like)
The total signal energy is the sum of $E(a)$ over all scales:

$$E_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{a} E(a) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^{2}\, df$$

### Step 3 — Define the relative scale-energy (RSE)
The percentage of total energy at scale $a$:

$$\text{RSE}(a) \;=\; \frac{E(a)}{E_{\text{total}}} \times 100\,\%$$

> *Application in PCG:* A scale of $a$ corresponds to a pseudo-frequency $f \approx f_{c}/a \cdot f_{s}$ (with $f_{c}$ the wavelet center frequency, $f_{s}$ the sampling rate). The RSE-vs-scale plot therefore maps out which frequencies carry the most energy in S1, S2, and murmurs — a powerful diagnostic for clinicians and machine-learning pipelines.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PCG vs. PPG at a Glance

| Feature | Phonocardiogram (PCG) | Photoplethysmogram (PPG) |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Physical origin** | Mechanical vibration of heart | Optical absorption of blood |
| **Measured quantity** | Sound pressure / acceleration | Light intensity (transmitted / reflected) |
| **Transducer** | Microphone / accelerometer | LED + photodiode |
| **Frequency band** | 20 Hz – 2 kHz | 0.5 – 20 Hz (DC removed) |
| **Sampling rate** | ≥ 4 kHz | 100 – 500 Hz |
| **Dominant components** | S1, S2, S3, S4, murmurs | DC baseline, AC pulse, dicrotic notch |
| **Primary diagnostic** | Valvular disease, murmurs, S3/S4 gallops | HR, SpO₂, perfusion, PWV |
| **Standard analysis** | Wavelet transform, STFT, MFCC, CNN | Peak detection, derivative analysis, AC/DC ratio |
| **Major artifact** | Ambient noise, contact noise, motion | Motion artifact, ambient light |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — PCG Acquisition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heart Source\nMechanical Vibration] --> B[Chest Wall\nCoupling Medium]
    B --> C[Transducer\nPiezo / Electret / Accelerometer]
    C --> D[Pre Amplifier\nIN-AMP Gain 40 to 60 dB]
    D --> E[Band Pass Filter\n20 Hz to 2 kHz Butterworth 4th]
    E --> F[Anti Aliasing LPF\nCutoff at 2 kHz]
    F --> G[ADC\nFs at 4 kHz 12 bit]
    G --> H[Digital Processing\nWavelet Denoising Segmentation]
    H --> I[Display and Storage\nMonitor or Paper Chart]
    H --> J[Feature Extraction\nS1 S2 Murmur Timing]
    J --> K[Classifier\nCNN or Rule Based]
    K --> L[Diagnosis\nNormal Murmur Gallop]

    classDef src fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef dig fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    class A,B src
    class C,D,E,F sig
    class G,H,J dig
    class I,K,L dec
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PPG Acquisition System (Reflectance Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source\nRed 660 nm and IR 940 nm LEDs] --> B[Drive Circuit\nTime Division Multiplexing]
    B --> C[Tissue\nFingertip or Wrist]
    C --> D[Photodetector\nPhotodiode]
    D --> E[Transimpedance Amp\nConverts Current to Voltage]
    E --> F[Band Pass Filter\n0.5 Hz to 20 Hz]
    F --> G[Demultiplexer\nSeparates Red and IR]
    G --> H[ADC\nFs at 500 Hz 16 bit]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processing\nBaseline Removal Peak Detect]
    I --> J[AC DC Extraction]
    J --> K1[Heart Rate\nBeat per Minute]
    J --> K2[Perfusion Index\nAC over DC in percent]
    J --> K3[SpO2\nR Ratio Calibration]

    classDef src fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class A,B src
    class C,D,E,F opt
    class G,H,I,J sig
    class K1,K2,K3 dec
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison Architecture — PCG vs PPG

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CardiacCycle[Cardiac Cycle Source]
        H[Heart]
    end

    H --> EM[Electrical Activity]
    H --> ME[Mechanical Activity]
    H --> BL[Blood Volume Pulsation]

    EM --> ECG[ECG Signal]
    ME --> PCG[PCG Signal]
    BL --> PPG[PPG Signal]

    ECG --> E1[P Q R S T Detection]
    PCG --> P1[S1 S2 S3 S4 Murmur Detection]
    PPG --> G1[AC DC SpO2 PI Analysis]

    E1 --> F1[Heart Rate Variability]
    P1 --> F2[Valvular Disease Screening]
    G1 --> F3[Oxygen Saturation Perfusion]

    F1 --> X1[Cardiac Diagnosis]
    F2 --> X1
    F3 --> X1

    classDef src fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef ecg fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef pcg fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef ppg fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    class H src
    class EM,ECG,E1 ecg
    class ME,PCG,P1 pcg
    class BL,PPG,G1 ppg
    class F1,F2,F3,X1 out
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three signals (ECG, PCG, PPG) are *time-aligned* events of the same cardiac cycle. ECG's R-peak precedes S1 by ~30 ms, S1 precedes PPG upstroke by ~100 ms (pulse-transit-time). This temporal relationship is exploited in cuff-less blood-pressure estimation and in identifying the **first and second heart sounds** automatically (Springer-Hinds segmentation in PCG).

## 4.4 PPG Waveform Anatomy (Labeled Schematic)

```mermaid
    B --> C[Anacrotic Notch]

    D --> E[Dicrotic Notch\nAortic Valve Closure]
    E --> F[Diastolic Decay\nEnd of Cycle]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A (Short Answer):** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B (Long Answer, Internal Choice):** 1 question × 14 marks = 14 marks
> - Sub-parts typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks
> - Bloom levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Phonocardiogram Frequency Content `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q.** List the frequency ranges of the four standard heart sounds (S1, S2, S3, S4) and explain why a phonocardiograph requires a minimum sampling rate of 4 kHz.

**Model Answer (Examiner-Ready):**

| Sound | Frequency Range (Hz) |
|-------|----------------------|
| S1 | 10 – 200 |
| S2 | 10 – 400 |
| S3 | 20 – 70 |
| S4 | 20 – 100 |

**Sampling rate justification:** Murmurs (pathological) extend up to 2000 Hz. By the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, the sampling frequency $f_{s}$ must be at least twice the highest frequency of interest:

$$f_{s} \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}} \;=\; 2 \times 2000 \;\text{Hz} \;=\; 4000 \;\text{Hz}$$

Hence a minimum $f_{s} = 4$ kHz is mandated to capture murmurs without aliasing.

---

### Question 2: PPG DC vs AC `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.** With a neat sketch, distinguish between the **AC** and **DC** components of a PPG signal. State the origin of each and the typical perfusion-index range.

**Model Answer:**

```
        Amplitude
          |
   DC ----|---------------------------------- (Quasi-static baseline)
          |    AC component
          |      /\
          |     /  \    /\
          |    /    \  /  \
          |   /      \/    \___
          |__/___________________________ Time
                |S1|  |S2|
```

- **DC component:** Venous blood + non-pulsatile arterial blood + skin, muscle, bone. Frequency ≈ 0 – 0.5 Hz.
- **AC component:** Pulsatile arterial blood volume change per cardiac cycle. Frequency ≈ 0.5 – 5 Hz. Synchronized with systole.
- **Perfusion Index:** $\text{PI} = (\text{AC}_{\text{pp}}/\text{DC}) \times 100\,\%$, typically **0.02 – 20 %**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Phonocardiography (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Phonocardiograph (PCG)** system. List the typical frequency bands of S1, S2, and murmurs.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Definition:** PCG is the graphical recording of heart sounds and murmurs using a suitable transducer, amplifier, filter, and display.

2. **Block diagram (write and label in answer script):**

```
Heart → Chest Wall → Transducer → Pre-Amp → Band-Pass Filter
        (20–2000 Hz) → Anti-Alias LPF → ADC (Fs≥4 kHz) → DSP → Display
```

3. **Component description:**
   - **Transducer:** Piezoelectric / electret microphone or accelerometer.
   - **Pre-amp:** Low-noise IN-AMP, gain 40–60 dB.
   - **Band-pass filter:** 20 Hz – 2 kHz, 4th-order Butterworth.
   - **ADC:** ≥ 12-bit, sampling rate ≥ 4 kHz.
   - **DSP:** Wavelet denoising, S1–S2 segmentation, feature extraction.

4. **Frequency bands (tabular answer):**

| Signal | Frequency (Hz) |
|--------|----------------|
| S1 | 10 – 200 |
| S2 | 10 – 400 |
| S3 | 20 – 70 |
| S4 | 20 – 100 |
| Murmurs | 20 – 2000 |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** Explain the **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** and justify its use in PCG signal analysis over the standard Fourier transform.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

1. **CWT definition:**

$$W(a,b) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,\psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

2. **Symbols:**
   - $\psi(t)$ — mother wavelet (e.g., Daubechies db4, Morlet)
   - $a$ — scale (inversely related to frequency)
   - $b$ — translation (time shift)
   - $x(t)$ — PCG signal

3. **Why not plain Fourier?** Fourier gives only the *global* spectrum — it cannot tell *when* a frequency component occurs. PCG is a **non-stationary** signal (S1, S2, murmur each have their own time-localized spectrum).

4. **Why CWT is appropriate:** It provides a **time-scale (time-frequency) representation** of the signal, allowing isolation of S1 (10–200 Hz), S2 (10–400 Hz), and murmur (20–2000 Hz) *segments individually*. This is the foundation of modern automated murmur detection systems.

5. **Common mother wavelets:** Daubechies (db4, db6) for their compact support and orthogonality; Morlet for its smooth Gaussian envelope.

---

### QUESTION B — Photoplethysmography (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** State the **Beer–Lambert law** and show its application to derive the **SpO₂ formula** used in pulse oximetry.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Beer–Lambert law (statement):** The absorbance of a homogeneous medium is proportional to the molar absorptivity, concentration, and path length.

$$A(\lambda) \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{0}(\lambda)}{I(\lambda)}\right) \;=\; \varepsilon(\lambda)\,c\,l$$

2. **Two-wavelength pulse oximeter:** A pulse oximeter uses two wavelengths, **Red (~660 nm)** and **Infrared (~940 nm)**, where oxy-haemoglobin (HbO₂) and deoxy-haemoglobin (HHb) have *different* absorption coefficients.

3. **Define the normalized AC/DC ratio at each wavelength:**

$$R_{\text{Red}} \;=\; \frac{\text{AC}_{\text{Red}}}{\text{DC}_{\text{Red}}}, \qquad R_{\text{IR}} \;=\; \frac{\text{AC}_{\text{IR}}}{\text{DC}_{\text{IR}}}$$

4. **Form the ratio $R$ of these two normalized ratios:**

$$R \;=\; \frac{R_{\text{Red}}}{R_{\text{IR}}} \;=\; \frac{\text{AC}_{\text{Red}}/\text{DC}_{\text{Red}}}{\text{AC}_{\text{IR}}/\text{DC}_{\text{IR}}}$$

5. **Apply empirical linear calibration (derived from in-vivo volunteer studies):**

$$\boxed{\;\text{SpO}_{2} \;=\; A \;-\; B \cdot R\;}$$

with $A \approx 110$ and $B \approx 25$ for typical fingertip sensors.

6. **Justification:** The ratio $R$ cancels out the *common* factors (optical path, scattering, LED intensity) that are wavelength-independent, leaving a quantity that depends *only* on the relative concentrations of HbO₂ and HHb — i.e., on oxygen saturation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** Compare the **transmission-mode** and **reflectance-mode** PPG sensors. Which mode is preferred for wearable wrist devices, and why?

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

| Parameter | Transmission Mode | Reflectance Mode |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Source–detector placement | Opposite sides of tissue | Same side of tissue |
| Best body site | Fingertip, earlobe, toe | Wrist, forehead, under-finger pad |
| Signal quality | Higher SNR (clean optical path) | Lower SNR (scattered light) |
| Wearable suitability | Limited (must enclose site) | Excellent (single-sided contact) |
| Power consumption | Moderate | Slightly higher (to compensate for lower SNR) |
| Susceptibility to motion artifact | Low | Higher |

**Preferred for wrist wearables:** **Reflectance mode**, because a wristband cannot straddle the tissue; the LED and photodetector must be on the *same* side of the skin. Modern smartwatches (e.g., Apple Watch) use a multi-LED reflectance array with **multi-wavelength** and **multi-PD** geometry to recover SNR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AC vs DC of PPG** — AC is the *pulsatile* part, NOT the noise. Many students mistakenly call the AC component "noise." (Lose ~2 marks)
> 2. **Using the wrong sign in Beer–Lambert** — Always $\varepsilon c l$ inside a *negative* exponent. Writing $I = I_0 e^{+\varepsilon c l}$ is a fatal error. (Lose ~3 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the 2× factor in Nyquist** — Sampling must be at least **twice** the highest frequency, not equal. For 2 kHz murmurs, this means 4 kHz, not 2 kHz. (Lose ~2 marks)
> 4. **Calling PPG an electrical signal** — PPG is an *optical* signal; only after photodetector transduction does it become electrical. (Lose ~1 mark)
> 5. **Saying "PCG is sound"** — PCG is a *recording* of sound, i.e., an electrical signal proportional to acoustic pressure. The signal itself is **not** sound. (Lose ~1 mark)
> 6. **Missing units in formulas** — KTU examiners actively deduct for missing $\lambda$, $c$, $l$, units of Hz, %, dB. Always annotate. (Lose ~1 mark per missing unit)
> 7. **Conflating SpO₂ formula constants** — $A$ and $B$ are *empirical*, not universal; they depend on sensor site (fingertip vs forehead). (Lose ~2 marks)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this list. KTU loves testing these exact bullet points.**

- **PCG = Phonocardiogram** — graphical recording of heart sounds.
- **Standard heart sound frequencies:** S1: **10–200 Hz**, S2: **10–400 Hz**, S3: **20–70 Hz**, S4: **20–100 Hz**, Murmurs: **20–2000 Hz**.
- **PCG sampling rate:** **≥ 4 kHz** (Nyquist for 2 kHz murmurs).
- **PCG filters:** Band-pass **20 Hz – 2 kHz**, 4th-order Butterworth typical.
- **PCG analysis method:** **CWT** with Daubechies (db4) mother wavelet for time–frequency decomposition.
- **PPG = Photoplethysmogram** — optical recording of blood volume changes.
- **Standard PPG wavelengths:** **Red ≈ 660 nm**, **IR ≈ 940 nm**.
- **PPG components:** **DC** (venous, non-pulsatile) + **AC** (pulsatile arterial).
- **PPG frequency band:** 0.5 – 5 Hz (AC component).
- **Beer–Lambert law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\varepsilon(\lambda) c l}$ and $A = \varepsilon(\lambda) c l$.
- **Perfusion Index (PI):** $\text{PI} = (\text{AC}_{\text{pp}}/\text{DC}) \times 100\,\%$, range 0.02–20 %.
- **Pulse oximetry ratio:** $R = \dfrac{(\text{AC}/\text{DC})_{\text{Red}}}{(\text{AC}/\text{DC})_{\text{IR}}}$.
- **SpO₂ formula:** $\text{SpO}_2 = A - B \cdot R$ (empirical, $A \approx 110$, $B \approx 25$).
- **PPG modes:** Transmission (fingertip, earlobe) vs Reflectance (wrist, forehead) — reflectance preferred for wearables.
- **PCG + PPG + ECG triad:** These are the three *complementary* cardiac signals — electrical trigger (ECG), mechanical consequence (PCG), hemodynamic response (PPG).
- **Time alignment in cardiac cycle:** ECG R-peak → ~30 ms → S1 → ~100 ms → PPG systolic upstroke.
- **Clinical relevance:** PCG → valvular disease; PPG → SpO₂, HR, perfusion, cuff-less BP.
- **Common artifacts:** PCG — ambient noise; PPG — motion + ambient light.
- **Anti-aliasing is mandatory** for both PCG (2 kHz LPF) and PPG (20 Hz LPF) before ADC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Signal Characteristics

In the context of biomedical engineering and **Biosignal Processing**, *signal characteristics* refer to the fundamental mathematical, statistical, and physical properties that uniquely describe a biosignal. These properties dictate how the signal behaves in the **time domain**, **frequency domain**, and **amplitude domain**, and they govern the choice of subsequent processing algorithms such as filtering, feature extraction, and classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A *characteristic* of a biosignal is a measurable attribute — such as its amplitude range, frequency bandwidth, energy content, statistical nature (deterministic/random), stationarity, periodicity, and symmetry — that determines the methodology of acquisition, analysis, and interpretation of physiological events.

The principal attributes that constitute the "characteristics" of a biosignal are:

1. **Domain of definition** — Continuous-time (CT) vs. Discrete-time (DT)
2. **Nature of values** — Analog (continuous amplitude) vs. Digital (quantized amplitude)
3. **Predictability** — Deterministic vs. Random (Stochastic)
4. **Repetition behavior** — Periodic vs. Aperiodic
5. **Energy/Power classification** — Energy signal vs. Power signal
6. **Symmetry** — Even signal vs. Odd signal
7. **Stationarity** — Stationary vs. Non-stationary
8. **Dimensionality** — 1-D signal, 2-D signal (image), or multi-channel signal
9. **Linearity & Time-Invariance** — LTI vs. non-LTI behavior of the underlying generation system
10. **Physio-metric specifics** — Amplitude range (mV, µV), frequency bandwidth (Hz), SNR (dB), and dominant morphological features

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor is **listening to a patient through a stethoscope**:
- The **loudness** of what he hears relates to the *amplitude* of the biosignal.
- The **pitch (high/low tone)** relates to the *frequency* of the biosignal.
- Whether the **sound is rhythmic** (lub-dub of the heart) tells the doctor if the signal is *periodic*.
- Whether the sound is **steady or changing** tells if the signal is *stationary*.
- The **clarity of the sound** (clear vs. noisy) tells the *signal-to-noise ratio*.

In the same way, when an engineer processes an **ECG** or **EEG** signal, he examines the *amplitude* (mV/µV), *frequency* (Hz), *periodic nature* (heart cycles), *stationarity* (steady state vs. transient event), and the *SNR* to decide what kind of filter, feature extractor, or classifier to apply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Relevance:** Correctly identifying the characteristics of a biosignal is the *first and most crucial step* in designing any medical device — be it a Holter monitor, EEG-based brain-computer interface (BCI), EMG-controlled prosthetic limb, or fetal heart-rate monitor. A wrong assumption about stationarity or bandwidth can render the entire system clinically useless.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Biological Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | 0.01 Hz – 10 kHz |
| Amplitude | $A$ | **Volts (V)** or mV/µV | 1 µV (EEG) – 5 mV (ECG) |
| Period | $T$ | **seconds (s)** | 0.0001 s – 100 s |
| Energy | $E$ | **Joules (J)** | Signal-dependent |
| Power | $P$ | **Watts (W)** | Signal-dependent |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **Hz** | ≥ 2 × Highest Frequency (Nyquist) |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | SNR | **decibels (dB)** | 20 – 60 dB (clinical quality) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU 2024 board exams, quoting the *correct standard unit* along with the *typical biological range* instantly upgrades your answer from a generic reply to a syllabus-aligned, high-scoring one.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Signals

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time (CT) vs. Discrete-Time (DT) Signals

A **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is defined for every instant of time $t \in \mathbb{R}$. A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is defined only at discrete instants $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

- **Why it matters:** A clinician naturally records a *continuous* ECG waveform from the patient's chest. However, once it enters a digital Holter monitor or PC, it is *sampled* and becomes a *discrete-time* signal. The mathematical tools (Fourier Transform vs. DFT) used later depend on this distinction.

### 2.1.2 Deterministic vs. Random (Stochastic) Signals

- **Deterministic Signal:** Can be completely described by an explicit mathematical expression. Example: an ideal ECG template, $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$.
- **Random Signal:** Cannot be described exactly; only its statistical properties (mean, variance, PDF) are known. Example: background muscle noise in an EMG, or EEG during deep sleep.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Almost *all real biosignals* (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, EOG) are *random* in nature because they are influenced by unpredictable physiological and environmental factors. We use *stochastic process theory* to analyze them.

### 2.1.3 Periodic vs. Aperiodic Signals

A signal is **periodic** with period $T$ if:
$$x(t + T) = x(t) \quad \forall \, t$$

The **fundamental period** $T_0$ is the smallest positive $T$ satisfying this. The **fundamental frequency** is $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{T_0}$.

- A pure **resting ECG** is approximately periodic (R-R interval ≈ 0.8 s → 75 bpm).
- **EEG during an epileptic seizure** is *aperiodic* (chaotic bursts).

### 2.1.4 Even and Odd Signals

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(Even component)}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(Odd component)}$$

Every signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

### 2.1.5 Energy vs. Power Signals

- **Energy Signal:** Total normalized energy is finite ($0 < E < \infty$) and average power is zero.
- **Power Signal:** Average power is finite ($0 < P < \infty$) and total energy is infinite.

The classification depends on whether the signal is **absolutely integrable (square)** or has **non-decaying periodic behavior**.

### 2.1.6 Stationary vs. Non-Stationary Signals

- A signal is **strict-sense stationary (SSS)** if its statistical properties (mean, variance, PDF) are invariant under a shift in time origin.
- It is **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** if only the mean and autocorrelation are time-invariant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept:** Most biosignals (ECG, EMG, PCG) are *quasi-stationary* — i.e., they can be assumed stationary only over **short windows** (typically 10 ms to a few seconds). This is the fundamental reason why **short-time analysis** (e.g., Short-Time Fourier Transform) is used in clinical DSP.

### 2.1.7 Linearity and Time-Invariance (LTI)

A system is **Linear** if it obeys superposition: $T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a T\{x_1(t)\} + b T\{x_2(t)\}$.

A system is **Time-Invariant** if a delay in input produces an equal delay in output: $y(t - t_0) = T\{x(t - t_0)\}$.

## 2.2 Characteristics of Major Biosignals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked topic in KTU 2024 Board Exams under "Characteristics."** Memorize the frequency range and amplitude range of each biosignal — examiners award full marks instantly.

| Biosignal | Full Name | Source / Origin | Typical Amplitude | Frequency Bandwidth | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ECG** | Electrocardiogram | Electrical activity of the heart | 0.5 – 5 mV | 0.05 – 150 Hz | Quasi-periodic |
| **EEG** | Electroencephalogram | Electrical activity of brain neurons | 1 – 100 µV | 0.1 – 100 Hz | Random, non-stationary |
| **EMG** | Electromyogram | Electrical activity of skeletal muscles | 50 µV – 5 mV | 20 – 5000 Hz | Random, transient |
| **PCG** | Phonocardiogram | Heart sounds (valve closure/vibration) | Acoustic pressure | 20 – 2000 Hz | Quasi-periodic |
| **EOG** | Electro-oculogram | Corneal-retinal potential (eye movement) | 10 µV – 5 mV | DC – 100 Hz | Quasi-periodic |
| **ERG** | Electroretinogram | Electrical response of retina to light | 5 µV – 1 mV | 0.2 – 200 Hz | Transient |
| **EOG** | (continued) | – | – | – | – |
| **GSR/EDA** | Galvanic Skin Response | Skin conductance (sweat glands) | 1 µS – 50 µS | 0.01 – 5 Hz | Slow drift |
| **PPG** | Photoplethysmogram | Blood volume changes in tissue | 0.1 % – 1 % | 0.1 – 20 Hz | Quasi-periodic |
| **Body Temperature** | Core/skin temperature | Metabolic activity | 32 °C – 42 °C | DC – 0.1 Hz | Very slow drift |
| **Blood Pressure** | Arterial pressure | Cardiac cycle | 40 – 200 mmHg | DC – 60 Hz | Quasi-periodic |
| **Respiration** | Chest expansion airflow | Lungs | ± 2 cm H₂O | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Quasi-periodic |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master these 12 equations. Every derivations/numericals question in Part B of ESE 2024 will be solved using one or more of these.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Total Energy of CT signal | Energy classification |
| 2 | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Total Energy of DT signal | Energy classification |
| 3 | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Average Power of CT signal | Power classification |
| 4 | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Average Power of DT signal | Power classification |
| 5 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_\text{signal}}{P_\text{noise}}\right)$ (dB) | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Quality metric |
| 6 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_\text{signal}}{A_\text{noise}}\right)$ (dB) | SNR from amplitudes | Quality metric |
| 7 | $f_s \ge 2 f_\text{max}$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Sampling rate design |
| 8 | $R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x(t+\tau) \, dt$ | Autocorrelation (Energy signal) | Periodicity detection |
| 9 | $R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} x(t) x(t+\tau) \, dt$ | Autocorrelation (Power signal) | Periodicity detection |
| 10 | $\mu_x = E\{x(t)\} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} x(t) \, dt$ | Mean value of signal | DC offset |
| 11 | $\sigma_x^2 = E\{(x(t) - \mu_x)^2\}$ | Variance / AC power | Fluctuation measure |
| 12 | $x_\text{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T x^2(t) \, dt}$ | Root Mean Square value | Energy-based amplitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note:** In the formulas above, notice the use of `\vert x(t) \vert^2` instead of `|x(t)|^2`. The vertical bar pipe character `|` is **forbidden inside markdown table cells** because it breaks the table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing absolute-value expressions inside KTU formula tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The study of biosignal characteristics is not academic — it directly drives product design in the medical device industry:

- **Pacemaker design** → Requires exact knowledge of ECG's R-peak amplitude & QRS bandwidth to detect arrhythmias reliably.
- **Hearing aids & cochlear implants** → Rely on precise audiometric signal characteristics (PCG/auditory evoked potentials).
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** → Decode motor intention from EEG mu-rhythm (8–12 Hz) and beta-rhythm (13–30 Hz) characteristics.
- **Sleep labs (Polysomnography)** → Use EEG sub-band definitions (δ, θ, α, β) to score sleep stages.
- **EMG-controlled prosthetics** → Use 20 Hz – 5 kHz EMG bandwidth to recognize intended muscle contraction.
- **AI-based ECG analysis (e.g., Apple Watch, AliveCor)** → Uses noise characteristics to build robust deep-learning classifiers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Mandate:** All derivations in this section are written in **exhaustive step-by-step form**. No algebraic step is skipped. KTU examiners award *process marks* — you get credit for every correct intermediate step, not just the final answer.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Energy and Power of a Rectangular Pulse

**Given Signal:** A rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $T_0$, centered at $t = 0$:

$$
x(t) =
\begin{cases}
A, & -T_0/2 \le t \le T_0/2 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 1: Apply the Energy Formula.**

The total energy of a continuous-time signal is defined as the integral of the squared magnitude over all time.

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the piecewise definition.**

Since $x(t) = A$ only in the interval $[-T_0/2, T_0/2]$ and is zero elsewhere, the integral reduces to a finite interval.

$$
E = \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} A^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral.**

The integrand is a constant $A^2$, so the integral is just the constant multiplied by the length of the interval.

$$
E = A^2 \left[ t \right]_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} = A^2 \left( \frac{T_0}{2} - \left(-\frac{T_0}{2}\right) \right)
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the bracket.**

The subtraction of the two bounds gives the total length of the interval.

$$
E = A^2 \left( \frac{T_0}{2} + \frac{T_0}{2} \right) = A^2 \cdot T_0
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{E = A^2 T_0 \quad \text{[Joules]}}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the average power.**

By definition, average power is energy normalized by the time duration over which it is computed. For a finite-energy signal, the average power over an infinite time horizon is:

$$
P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the energy value.**

Since the energy is finite and concentrated in a finite window, dividing by an unbounded time $2T$ gives zero.

$$
P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2 T_0}{2T} = 0
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P = 0}
$$

**Conclusion:** A rectangular pulse is an **Energy Signal** because its energy is finite ($0 < E < \infty$) and its power is zero.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy and Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

**Given Signal:** $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 1: Apply the Energy Formula.**

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert A \sin(2\pi f_0 t) \vert^2 \, dt = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \sin^2(2\pi f_0 t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Use the trigonometric identity.**

The identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$ transforms the squared sine into a constant plus a cosine term.

$$
E = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1 - \cos(4\pi f_0 t)}{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 3: Split the integral.**

The integral of a constant over an infinite domain diverges to infinity, which immediately proves infinite energy.

$$
E = \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 1 \, dt - \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cos(4\pi f_0 t) \, dt
$$

**Step 4: Conclude that energy is infinite.**

$$
E = \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot \infty - \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot 0 = \infty
$$

**Step 5: Compute the average power over one period.**

Because the signal is periodic, we can compute the power over a single period $T_0 = 1/f_0$ and it remains the same for any horizon.

$$
P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_0^{T_0} A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f_0 t) \, dt
$$

**Step 6: Apply the identity and integrate.**

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{T_0} \int_0^{T_0} \frac{1 - \cos(4\pi f_0 t)}{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 7: Evaluate the integral over one period.**

The cosine term has an integer number of cycles in $T_0$ and integrates to zero.

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \left[ t - \frac{\sin(4\pi f_0 t)}{4\pi f_0} \right]_0^{T_0}
$$

**Step 8: Simplify the boundary terms.**

At $t = T_0$, the sine term returns to $\sin(4\pi) = 0$. At $t = 0$, both terms are zero. We are left with only $T_0 - 0 = T_0$.

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \cdot T_0 = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P = \dfrac{A^2}{2} \quad \text{[Watts]}}
$$

**Conclusion:** A pure sinusoid is a **Power Signal** (infinite energy, finite non-zero power). This is why the human heart's ECG is classified as a *quasi-power signal*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SNR Calculation for a Clinical ECG Recording

**Given:** ECG signal amplitude $A_s = 1.5$ mV, noise (muscle artifact + mains interference) amplitude $A_n = 50$ µV.

**Step 1: Recall the amplitude-based SNR formula.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{A_s}{A_n} \right) \, \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to the same unit.**

Convert the signal amplitude to microvolts: $A_s = 1.5 \text{ mV} = 1500$ µV. The ratio is then unitless.

$$
\frac{A_s}{A_n} = \frac{1500 \, \mu V}{50 \, \mu V} = 30
$$

**Step 3: Compute the logarithm.**

$$
\log_{10}(30) \approx 1.4771
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by 20.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \times 1.4771 = 29.542 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR} \approx 29.54 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 5 (Optional Validation — Power-based SNR):**

The power ratio is the square of the amplitude ratio, and the power SNR formula uses a factor of 10:

$$
\text{SNR}_\text{power} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{A_s^2}{A_n^2} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( 30^2 \right) = 20 \log_{10}(30) \approx 29.54 \text{ dB}
$$

Both formulations are mathematically equivalent, confirming the result.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Decomposition of an Arbitrary Signal into Even and Odd Parts

**Given:** $x(t) = e^{-2t} \cos(3t)$ (a non-symmetric decaying oscillation typical of a damped biosignal response).

**Step 1: Compute $x(-t)$.**

Replace $t$ with $-t$ in the original expression.

$$
x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)} \cos(3(-t)) = e^{2t} \cos(-3t)
$$

**Step 2: Use the property $\cos(-\theta) = \cos(\theta)$.**

$$
x(-t) = e^{2t} \cos(3t)
$$

**Step 3: Apply the even-component formula.**

$$
x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{-2t} \cos(3t) + e^{2t} \cos(3t)}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Factor out the common term $\cos(3t)$.**

$$
x_e(t) = \frac{\cos(3t) \left( e^{-2t} + e^{2t} \right)}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Recognize the hyperbolic cosine identity.**

The identity $\cosh(at) = \dfrac{e^{at} + e^{-at}}{2}$ allows us to simplify the bracket.

$$
x_e(t) = \cos(3t) \cdot \cosh(2t)
$$

**Step 6: Apply the odd-component formula.**

$$
x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{-2t} \cos(3t) - e^{2t} \cos(3t)}{2}
$$

**Step 7: Factor out the common term $\cos(3t)$.**

$$
x_o(t) = \frac{\cos(3t) \left( e^{-2t} - e^{2t} \right)}{2}
$$

**Step 8: Recognize the hyperbolic sine identity.**

The identity $\sinh(at) = \dfrac{e^{at} - e^{-at}}{2}$ allows the simplification of the bracket.

$$
x_o(t) = -\cos(3t) \cdot \sinh(2t)
$$

**Step 9: Verify the decomposition.**

Adding the even and odd parts must reconstruct the original signal. Apply the inverse hyperbolic identity:

$$
\cosh(2t) - \sinh(2t) = e^{-2t}
$$

$$
x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \cos(3t)[\cosh(2t) - \sinh(2t)] = e^{-2t} \cos(3t) = x(t) \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Decomposition:**

$$
\boxed{x_e(t) = \cos(3t)\cosh(2t), \qquad x_o(t) = -\cos(3t)\sinh(2t)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for Signal Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix is a high-yield revision aid for the 3-mark short questions in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the decision logic.**

| Observation | Conclusion | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$ | Energy Signal | Finite energy, zero average power |
| $E = \infty$ and $0 < P < \infty$ | Power Signal | Infinite energy, finite power |
| $x(t) = x(-t)$ for all $t$ | Even Signal | Symmetric about vertical axis |
| $x(t) = -x(-t)$ for all $t$ | Odd Signal | Anti-symmetric about origin |
| $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$ for some smallest $T_0 > 0$ | Periodic Signal | Repeats every $T_0$ |
| Mean $\mu_x(t) = \text{const}$ and $R_x(t_1,t_2) = R_x(t_2-t_1)$ | WSS Signal | Time-invariant statistics |
| Defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Continuous-Time | Continuous in time |
| Defined only for $t = nT_s$ | Discrete-Time | Sampled in time |
| $f_s \ge 2 f_\text{max}$ | Sampled without aliasing | Nyquist theorem satisfied |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B] — 3 Marks
**Define a biosignal. List any four characteristics used to classify biosignals in biomedical signal processing.**

**Model Answer:**

A **biosignal** is an electrical, mechanical, thermal, or chemical signal that originates from a living organism and carries information about a physiological process. Biosignals are the direct measurable output of biological activity and are used by clinicians and biomedical engineers for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapy.

The **four primary characteristics** used to classify biosignals in biomedical signal processing are:

1. **Domain of definition** — Continuous-time (analog) or discrete-time (digital) representation.
2. **Statistical nature** — Deterministic (can be expressed as a closed-form equation) versus random/stochastic (only statistical descriptors are available).
3. **Energy and Power classification** — Energy signal (finite energy, zero power) or power signal (finite power, infinite energy).
4. **Stationarity** — Stationary (time-invariant statistical properties) versus non-stationary (time-varying statistical properties).

*Additional acceptable characteristics include periodicity, symmetry (even/odd), and amplitude-frequency range.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A] — 3 Marks
**Compare the amplitude and frequency characteristics of ECG and EEG signals.**

**Model Answer:**

| Characteristic | ECG (Electrocardiogram) | EEG (Electroencephalogram) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Amplitude Range** | 0.5 – 5 mV (millivolts) | 1 – 100 µV (microvolts) |
| **Frequency Bandwidth** | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 0.1 – 100 Hz |
| **Nature** | Quasi-periodic, quasi-stationary | Random, highly non-stationary |
| **Dominant Components** | P wave, QRS complex, T wave | Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma rhythms |
| **Signal Strength** | Strong (mV level) | Very weak (µV level) |
| **Primary Origin** | Electrical activity of cardiac muscle | Synchronized post-synaptic potentials of cortical neurons |

The ECG is approximately **50 – 100 times larger in amplitude** than the EEG, which is why EEG acquisition requires high-gain, low-noise amplifiers with active shielding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part B question (14 marks) contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers EITHER *Question A* OR *Question B*, but not both.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify the following signals and justify with mathematical reasoning:
  1. $x_1(t) = e^{-5t} u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.
  2. $x_2(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t) + 3 \sin(50\pi t)$.
  3. $x_3[n] = (-0.8)^n u[n]$.
  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional characteristics of an EMG signal**, listing its origin, amplitude range, frequency bandwidth, and the clinical disorders diagnosed using it.
  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Signal 1:** $x_1(t) = e^{-5t} u(t)$

**Step 1: Test for energy.**

Apply the energy formula. The unit step ensures the signal is zero for $t < 0$.

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert e^{-5t} u(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-10t} \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral.**

$$
E = \left[ \frac{e^{-10t}}{-10} \right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{10}\right) = \frac{1}{10} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the power.**

For a finite-energy signal, the average power over an infinite horizon is zero.

$$
P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \cdot \frac{1}{10} = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** $x_1(t)$ is an **Energy Signal** (finite non-zero energy, zero power).

**Signal 2:** $x_2(t) = 5 \cos(100\pi t) + 3 \sin(50\pi t)$

**Step 1: Identify frequencies.**

The first term has $f_1 = 50$ Hz and the second has $f_2 = 25$ Hz.

**Step 2: Find the fundamental period.**

The fundamental period is the LCM of the individual periods $T_1 = 1/50$ s and $T_2 = 1/25$ s.

$$
T_0 = \text{LCM}\!\left(\frac{1}{50}, \frac{1}{25}\right) = \frac{1}{25} = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Conclude periodicity.**

Because the two frequencies are integer multiples ($100\pi / 50\pi = 2$), the signal is **periodic** with $T_0 = 40$ ms.

**Step 4: Conclude power-signal nature.**

A periodic non-decaying sinusoid is a **Power Signal**. Its average power is the sum of the individual powers.

$$
P = \frac{5^2}{2} + \frac{3^2}{2} = 12.5 + 4.5 = 17 \text{ W (on 1 Ω basis)}
$$

**Conclusion:** $x_2(t)$ is a **Periodic Power Signal**.

**Signal 3:** $x_3[n] = (-0.8)^n u[n]$

**Step 1: Test for energy.**

$$
E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (-0.8)^n \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.64)^n
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the geometric series.**

A geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n$ converges to $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$ if $\vert r \vert < 1$. Here $r = 0.64 < 1$, so the series converges.

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.64} = \frac{1}{0.36} = 2.78 \text{ J}
$$

**Conclusion:** $x_3[n]$ is a **Discrete-Time Energy Signal**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

The **EMG (Electromyogram)** captures the electrical activity generated by skeletal muscle fibers during contraction.

**Origin:** EMG is generated by the **sum of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs)** as motor neurons fire and depolarize the muscle fibers.

**Amplitude range:** 50 µV to 5 mV (depending on muscle type and contraction level).

**Frequency bandwidth:** 20 Hz to 5000 Hz (most clinical information resides in the 20 Hz – 500 Hz band; high-frequency content above 500 Hz is often noise).

**Sampling rate requirement:** $f_s \ge 2 \times 5000 = 10$ kHz (typical clinical EMG uses 1000 – 10000 Hz).

**Signal nature:** Random, transient, non-stationary, energy signal in short epochs.

**Block Diagram of EMG Acquisition:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Skeletal Muscle Fiber Group] --> B[Surface or Needle Electrode]
    B --> C[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000 to 10000]
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter 20 Hz to 5 kHz]
    D --> E[Notch Filter 50 or 60 Hz Mains Rejection]
    E --> F[ADC Sampling at 10 kHz or more]
    F --> G[EMG Display and Feature Extraction]
    G --> H[Clinical Diagnosis Output]
```

**Clinical disorders diagnosed using EMG:**

1. **Myopathies** — Muscle diseases causing low-amplitude, short-duration MUAPs.
2. **Neuropathies** — Nerve damage causing high-amplitude, long-duration, polyphasic MUAPs.
3. **Neuromuscular junction disorders** — e.g., Myasthenia gravis (decremental response).
4. **Radiculopathies** — Nerve root compression producing denervation patterns.
5. **Upper motor neuron lesions** — Causing spasticity and abnormal motor firing patterns.
6. **Prosthetic control** — EMG signals drive robotic limbs based on residual muscle activity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE TO Question A*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **total energy** and **average power** of the continuous-time signal $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ and hence classify it. State clearly the energy and power values in joules and watts.
  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. A clinical ECG recorder has a peak QRS amplitude of 2 mV and is corrupted by 60 µV of high-frequency muscle noise. Compute the SNR in decibels. Comment on whether the recording is of diagnostic quality given the clinical threshold of 20 dB.
  *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1: Recall the energy formula.**

The total energy of a continuous-time signal is the integral of its squared magnitude over all time.

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the signal expression.**

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt
$$

**Step 3: Apply the trigonometric identity.**

Use the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$.

$$
E = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 4: Split the integral.**

$$
E = \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 1 \, dt + \frac{A^2}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the integrals.**

The first integral diverges to infinity. The second integral is the integral of a sinusoid over an infinite time, which is bounded and **averages to zero**.

$$
E = \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot \infty + 0 = \infty
$$

**Conclusion on energy:** $E = \infty$ joules (infinite energy).

**Step 6: Compute the average power.**

By definition, the average power is the energy normalized by the time horizon. For a periodic signal, we can compute the power over a single period $T_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0$.

$$
P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_0^{T_0} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt
$$

**Step 7: Apply the identity again and integrate.**

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{T_0} \int_0^{T_0} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 8: Evaluate the integral over one period.**

The cosine term has an integer number of cycles in $T_0$ and integrates to zero.

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \left[ t + \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2\omega_0} \right]_0^{T_0}
$$

**Step 9: Substitute the limits.**

At both $t = 0$ and $t = T_0$, the sine term is zero because the argument changes by an integer multiple of $2\pi$.

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \cdot T_0 = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

**Final Classification:**

$$
\boxed{E = \infty \text{ J}, \qquad P = \dfrac{A^2}{2} \text{ W (on a 1 Ω load)}}
$$

The signal $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ is a **Power Signal** (infinite energy, finite non-zero power).

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Definition of SNR:**

The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is a quantitative measure of the relative strength of the desired signal compared to the unwanted noise. It is expressed in decibels (dB) and is one of the most important quality metrics in biomedical instrumentation.

**Mathematical form (using amplitudes):**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{A_\text{signal}}{A_\text{noise}} \right) \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 1: Extract the given values.**

Signal amplitude: $A_s = 2$ mV. Noise amplitude: $A_n = 60$ µV.

**Step 2: Convert to a common unit (microvolts).**

$$
A_s = 2 \text{ mV} = 2 \times 10^3 \text{ µV} = 2000 \text{ µV}
$$

**Step 3: Form the amplitude ratio.**

$$
\frac{A_s}{A_n} = \frac{2000 \, \mu V}{60 \, \mu V} = 33.33
$$

**Step 4: Compute the logarithm base 10.**

$$
\log_{10}(33.33) \approx 1.5229
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by 20 to get SNR in dB.**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \times 1.5229 = 30.46 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{SNR} \approx 30.46 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 6: Comment on diagnostic quality.**

The clinical threshold for diagnostic-quality ECG recording is typically **20 dB or higher**. Since the computed SNR of 30.46 dB **exceeds 20 dB**, the recording is of **acceptable diagnostic quality**. The signal is roughly **33 times stronger** than the noise, which means QRS morphology can be reliably extracted for arrhythmia classification and heart-rate computation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost in KTU 2024 Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Confusing amplitude-SNR (factor 20) with power-SNR (factor 10).** Many students write `SNR = 10 log(A_s/A_n)`, which is mathematically incorrect for amplitude ratios. Remember: *amplitudes use 20 log; powers use 10 log*.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (mV vs. µV) before computing the ratio. A common answer error is using $2/60$ directly, giving an SNR of $-29.5$ dB, which is dimensionally wrong.
> 3. **Classifying a finite-duration transient as a power signal.** A single rectangular pulse is an *energy* signal, not a power signal. Students often confuse "amplitude is constant during the pulse" with "it is a power signal."
> 4. **Missing the unit step function $u(t)$ in integrals.** For $x_1(t) = e^{-5t} u(t)$, the integral must start from $t = 0$, not $-\infty$. Wrong limits give a divergent integral and wrong classification.
> 5. **Writing $e^{-2t}$ as $e^{2t}$** while computing $x(-t)$ — sign error in time reversal.
> 6. **Quoting the EEG frequency range as "0 – 100 Hz"** without specifying the sub-bands. Examiners expect at least the alpha (8–13 Hz) and beta (13–30 Hz) bands for full marks.
> 7. **Not stating the clinical threshold** when discussing SNR — examiners look for context, not just the numeric answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A biosignal's *characteristics* are the measurable attributes (amplitude, frequency, energy, power, stationarity, periodicity, symmetry) that describe it and dictate its processing.
- **Continuous-time** $x(t)$ is defined for all $t$; **discrete-time** $x[n]$ is defined only for integer $n$.
- **Deterministic** signals have a closed-form mathematical expression; **random** signals are characterized only by statistics.
- **Periodic signal** satisfies $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$ for the smallest positive $T_0$.
- **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$. **Power signal:** $E = \infty$, $0 < P < \infty$.
- **Even signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$. **Odd signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$.
- **Every signal** can be uniquely decomposed: $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
- **Stationarity:** Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) means time-invariant mean and autocorrelation.
- **ECG:** 0.5–5 mV, 0.05–150 Hz, quasi-periodic.
- **EEG:** 1–100 µV, 0.1–100 Hz, random, non-stationary; sub-bands Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma.
- **EMG:** 50 µV–5 mV, 20 Hz–5 kHz, random, transient.
- **PCG:** 20 Hz–2 kHz, quasi-periodic (heart sound recording).
- **EOG:** DC–100 Hz, eye-movement potential.
- **PPG:** 0.1–20 Hz, blood-volume change.
- **GSR/EDA:** 0.01–5 Hz, very slow skin conductance drift.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_\text{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **SNR (amplitude):** $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(A_s / A_n)$ dB.
- **SNR (power):** $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ dB.
- **Clinical SNR threshold:** $\ge 20$ dB for diagnostic-quality ECG.
- **Average power of a sinusoid** $A\cos(\omega t)$: $P = A^2 / 2$.
- **Energy of a rectangular pulse** (amplitude $A$, width $T_0$): $E = A^2 T_0$.
- **Mean of a signal:** $\mu_x = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{2T} \displaystyle\int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\,dt$.
- **RMS value:** $x_\text{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T x^2(t)\,dt}$.
- **Autocorrelation** of a periodic signal is also periodic with the same period.
- **Most biosignals are *quasi-stationary*** — assume stationarity only over short windows (10 ms to a few seconds).
- **Most biosignals are *random*, not deterministic** — use statistical descriptors.
- **Examiners award *process marks*** — show every step of integration or series evaluation, not just the final answer.
- **Always quote the unit** (Hz, mV, µV, dB) along with the value — it is a free mark.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Topic: Characteristics.** You are now equipped with the complete theoretical foundation, derivations, and exam-ready problem-solving approach for the "Characteristics" section of OEEBT613. Practice at least three numerical problems on energy/power and two SNR problems before the ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dicrotic Notch. Introduction to Other Biopotentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
