## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the First Derivative Operator (FDO) used in ECG signal processing. Write its transfer function and state the output behavior at the R-peak of the QRS complex.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The First Derivative Operator (FDO) computes the slope between consecutive samples of a discrete-time ECG signal $x(n)$:

$$y(n) = x(n+1) - x(n) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

The transfer function in the z-domain is:

$$H_{FDO}(z) = 1 - z^{-1} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

The frequency response is $H_{FDO}(e^{j\omega}) = 2j\sin(\omega/2) e^{-j\omega/2}$, which acts as a high-pass filter that amplifies high frequencies. At the R-peak of the QRS complex, the FDO output exhibits a **zero-crossing** (changes sign from positive on the upstroke to negative on the downstroke), making it a reliable indicator of R-peak timing. **...(1 mark)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three sources of random noise in ECG and explain why the Moving Average Filter is effective in removing them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three sources of random noise in ECG are:

1. **Muscle Noise (EMG Interference):** Broadband stochastic noise from skeletal muscle activity overlapping the ECG spectrum (0–500 Hz). **(1 mark)**

2. **Electrode Contact Noise:** Transient random disturbances caused by poor electrode-skin contact, with non-Gaussian statistics. **(1 mark)**

3. **Thermal (Johnson) Noise:** Broadband Gaussian noise generated by the random motion of electrons in electronic components of the ECG amplifier. **(0.5 marks)**

4. **Instrumentation Noise:** Random electronic noise from ADC quantization and amplifier circuits. **(0.5 marks)**

The Moving Average Filter is effective because it averages $M$ consecutive samples, reducing the noise variance by a factor of $M$:

$$\text{Var}[y_{MAF}(n)] = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{M}$$

This results in an **SNR improvement of $10 \log_{10}(M)$ dB** while preserving slowly varying ECG features (P, T waves). The smoothing operation exploits the **zero-mean, uncorrelated nature** of random noise.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 — Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the transfer function and magnitude response of the **Second Derivative Operator (SDO)**. Show that it has zero phase delay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for QRS detection, highlighting the role of the derivative operator. Compare the FDO and SDO for R-peak localization. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Define the SDO in the time domain. (1 mark)**

The Second Derivative Operator is the difference of first derivatives:

$$y(n) = x(n+1) - 2x(n) + x(n-1)$$

**Step 2: Take the Z-transform. (1 mark)**

$$Y(z) = zX(z) - 2X(z) + z^{-1}X(z) = X(z)(z - 2 + z^{-1})$$

**Step 3: Derive the transfer function. (1 mark)**

$$H_{SDO}(z) = z - 2 + z^{-1} = (1 - z^{-1})^2 \cdot z$$

**Step 4: Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ to obtain the frequency response. (1 mark)**

$$H_{SDO}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{j\omega}(1 - e^{-j\omega})^2$$

**Step 5: Use Euler's identity $1 - e^{-j\omega} = 2j\sin(\omega/2) e^{-j\omega/2}$. (1 mark)**

$$H_{SDO}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{j\omega} \cdot 4 \cdot j^2 \cdot \sin^2(\omega/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega} = -4 \sin^2(\omega/2)$$

**Step 6: Conclude with the magnitude response. (1 mark)**

$$|H_{SDO}(e^{j\omega})| = 4 \sin^2(\omega/2)$$

**Step 7: Prove zero phase delay. (1 mark)**

The phase is $\angle H_{SDO}(e^{j\omega}) = \angle(e^{j\omega}) + 2\angle(1 - e^{-j\omega}) = \omega - \omega = 0$. Since the phase is identically zero for all $\omega$, the SDO has **zero phase distortion**, meaning the R-peak zero-crossing of the SDO marks the *true* location of the R-wave in time without any shift.

---

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram of the Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (3 marks):**

```
ECG → [Bandpass 5–15 Hz] → [Derivative] → [Squaring] → [Moving Window Integration] → [Adaptive Threshold] → R-peak
```

**Stage-by-Stage Explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz):** Isolates the QRS energy and suppresses baseline wander, T-waves, and high-frequency muscle noise.

2. **Derivative Operator:** Highlights the *steep slopes* of the QRS complex. The output is large wherever the signal is changing rapidly. This is the **critical stage** where the FDO emphasizes the QRS against the relatively flat P and T waves.

3. **Squaring Function:** $y(n) = [x(n)]^2$ makes all values positive and **non-linearly amplifies large values**, suppressing small differences (P, T waves) and emphasizing QRS peaks.

4. **Moving Window Integration:** $y(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k)$ smooths the squared signal over a window of N=150 ms (typical), producing a triangular pulse for each QRS.

5. **Adaptive Thresholding:** Two thresholds (high and low) adapt to the running mean of the signal. When the integrated output crosses the threshold, an R-peak is declared.

**Comparison Table: FDO vs SDO for R-peak Localization (1 mark):**

| Feature | First Derivative | Second Derivative |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase delay** | $\omega/2$ (linear, non-zero) | Zero |
| **At R-peak** | Zero-crossing | Zero-crossing (with maximum $\vert$SDO$\vert$) |
| **At QRS upstroke** | Maximum | Zero |
| **Robustness to baseline** | Moderate | High |
| **Implementation** | Simpler | Two cascaded FDO stages |

**Conclusion:** The SDO is preferred for *precise* R-peak localization due to zero phase, while the FDO is preferred for *onset/offset* detection (steepest slope) and computationally efficient real-time systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **random noise removal in ECG** using the **Moving Average Filter**. Derive the SNR improvement in decibels for an M-point MAF applied to a noisy ECG with white Gaussian noise of variance $\sigma_w^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Ensemble Averaging** for random noise removal. State two advantages and two limitations of the technique. Why is the Wiener filter considered *optimal* compared to the MAF? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Define the noisy ECG model. (1 mark)**

$$x(n) = s(n) + w(n)$$

where $s(n)$ is the clean ECG and $w(n)$ is white noise with $E[w(n)] = 0$ and variance $\sigma_w^2$.

**Step 2: Define the M-point MAF output. (1 mark)**

$$y(n) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x(n-k)$$

**Step 3: Use linearity to separate signal and noise. (1 mark)**

$$y(n) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} s(n-k)}_{s_{MAF}(n)} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w(n-k)}_{w_{MAF}(n)}$$

**Step 4: Compute the output noise variance. (1 mark)**

For uncorrelated noise samples:

$$\text{Var}[w_{MAF}(n)] = \frac{1}{M^2} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \sigma_w^2 = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{M}$$

**Step 5: Compute the output signal power. (1 mark)**

For a slowly varying signal (the MAF acts as a gain of 1):

$$\text{Var}[s_{MAF}(n)] \approx \text{Var}[s(n)] = \sigma_s^2$$

**Step 6: Derive the SNR improvement. (1 mark)**

$$\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{\sigma_s^2}{\sigma_w^2 / M} = M \cdot \text{SNR}_{in}$$

$$\Delta \text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(M) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 7: Numerical example and conclusion. (1 mark)**

For $M = 16$: $\Delta \text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(16) \approx 12.04$ dB improvement. **Conclusion:** The MAF is a simple, computationally efficient, and effective method for random noise removal in real-time ECG systems, with the trade-off that large $M$ smooths out fast QRS details.

---

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram of Ensemble Averaging (2 marks):**

```
Beat 1 (R-aligned) ─┐
Beat 2 (R-aligned) ─┤
Beat 3 (R-aligned) ├─→ [Average] ─→ y_ens(n)
   ...              │
Beat M (R-aligned) ─┘
```

**Step-by-Step Process (2 marks):**

1. **Detect R-peaks** in the noisy ECG (using a derivative-based detector or template matching).
2. **Segment the ECG** into single beats of fixed length (e.g., 600 ms) around each R-peak.
3. **Time-align** all beats to the R-peak fiducial point.
4. **Average** the M aligned beats sample-by-sample to produce $\bar{x}(n) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M} x_k(n)$.

**Variance Reduction (1 mark):** Since the noise is uncorrelated between beats but the signal is identical, the noise variance reduces by $M$:

$$\text{Var}[\bar{x}(n)] = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta \text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(M) \text{ dB}$$

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **Highest possible noise reduction** with simple linear averaging.
2. **Preserves the averaged morphology** of the dominant beat — useful for diagnostic templates.

**Two Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **Requires time-alignment** (R-peak detection must be accurate) and **stationary morphology** (fails during arrhythmias like AF, PVCs).
2. **Computationally expensive** in real-time; needs storage of multiple beats.

**Wiener Filter as Optimal (1 mark):**

The MAF treats *all frequencies equally*, but the Wiener filter adjusts gain **per frequency bin** based on the local SNR:

$$H_{Wiener}(\omega) = \frac{P_{ss}(\omega)}{P_{ss}(\omega) + P_{ww}(\omega)}$$

At frequencies with high signal power, $H \to 1$ (preserved). At frequencies with high noise power, $H \to 0$ (suppressed). This **frequency-adaptive** behavior makes it the **minimum mean square error (MMSE)** estimator — provably better than any fixed filter, including the MAF.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES IN KTU 2024 EXAMS — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing FDO zero-crossing with SDO zero-crossing:** Both occur at the R-peak for symmetric QRS, but the FDO has a **linear phase delay** of $\omega/2$ while the SDO has **zero phase**. Writing "FDO has zero phase" costs **2 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in Point Derivative:** The KTU Point Derivative is $L(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n+1) - 2x(n) + x(n-1)]$, **not** the full SDO. Omitting the 1/2 loses 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Wrong MAF variance formula:** The variance reduces as $\sigma_w^2/M$, **not** $\sigma_w^2/M^2$. The latter is a common student error.
> 
> 4. **Using "filter" and "operator" interchangeably:** The KTU syllabus distinguishes *operators* (FDO, SDO, PDO) from *filters* (MAF, Wiener). Mixing terminology loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of zero-mean noise:** In MAF and Ensemble averaging derivations, the key step is $E[w(n)] = 0$. Skipping this assumption loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Confusing Powerline Interference (50 Hz) with Random Noise:** Powerline is *deterministic* and removed by notch filters. Random noise is *stochastic* and removed by averaging. This conceptual mix-up is a **3-mark penalty** in long answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 EXAM**
> 
> **Derivative Based Operators:**
> - ☐ FDO: $y(n) = x(n+1) - x(n)$; $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$; high-pass; **zero-crossing at R-peak**.
> - ☐ SDO: $y(n) = x(n+1) - 2x(n) + x(n-1)$; $H(z) = (1 - z^{-1})^2 \cdot z$; **zero phase delay**; **maximum $\vert$SDO$\vert$ at R-peak**.
> - ☐ Point Derivative: $L(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n+1) - 2x(n) + x(n-1)]$; L1-norm used for R-peak detection.
> - ☐ Derivative operators are the **second stage of Pan-Tompkins** algorithm.
> - ☐ Derivative operators **do NOT reduce noise** — they amplify high-frequency noise.
> 
> **Random Noise Removal:**
> - ☐ Random noise = stochastic, broadband, zero-mean, uncorrelated with signal.
> - ☐ MAF: $y(n) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} x(n-k)$; **$\text{Var} = \sigma_w^2/M$**; **$\Delta \text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(M)$ dB**.
> - ☐ Ensemble Averaging: $\bar{x}(n) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{k=1}^{M} x_k(n)$; requires R-peak alignment; best for Holter data.
> - ☐ Wiener Filter: $H(\omega) = \frac{P_{ss}}{P_{ss} + P_{ww}}$; **optimal MMSE**; adaptive per frequency.
> - ☐ MAF has **linear phase delay of $(M-1)/2$** samples; for **zero-phase** use *filtfilt* (forward-backward filtering).
> 
> **Key Distinctions:**
> - ☐ Derivative operators → **QRS detection** (feature extraction).
> - ☐ MAF / Ensemble / Wiener → **Noise removal** (preprocessing).
> - ☐ Powerline interference (50/60 Hz) ≠ random noise → use **notch filter**, not averaging.
> 
> **Exam Day Formulas (Memorize):**
> 
> $$\boxed{|H_{FDO}(e^{j\omega})| = 2\left|\sin\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\right|}$$
> 
> $$\boxed{|H_{SDO}(e^{j\omega})| = 4\sin^2\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$
> 
> $$\boxed{\Delta\text{SNR}_{MAF} = 10\log_{10}(M) \text{ dB}}$$
> 
> $$\boxed{H_{Wiener}(\omega) = \frac{P_{ss}(\omega)}{P_{ss}(\omega) + P_{ww}(\omega)}}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Baseline Drift Removal. Frequency Domain Filters: Butterworth Low Pass & Low Filter. Adaptive Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Baseline Drift Removal. Frequency Domain Filters: Butterworth Low Pass & Low Filter. Adaptive Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Baseline Drift Removal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Baseline Drift (or Baseline Wander)** is a low-frequency artifact (typically **0.15 Hz to 0.5 Hz**) superimposed on biosignals such as ECG, EMG, and EEG, caused by patient respiration, body movement, electrode-skin impedance variations, and environmental factors. **Baseline Drift Removal** is the signal processing operation that eliminates this low-frequency contamination while preserving the diagnostic morphology of the biosignal.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to listen to a **violinist playing in a concert hall** (the violin's pure tone = your clean biosignal like the QRS complex). However, someone is slowly sliding a **heavy curtain rod up and down** in the background, creating a low rumbling interference (the curtain rod = baseline drift). 

- The **curtain rod's slow movement** corresponds to frequencies < **0.5 Hz**.
- Your **violin notes** correspond to frequencies between **0.5 Hz and 40 Hz** (for ECG).
- To remove the curtain rod's sound but keep the violin, you apply a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** that blocks everything below **0.5 Hz** while passing the violin's higher frequency notes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Baseline Drift Matters Clinically:**
> - It shifts the **isoelectric line** of the ECG, leading to **false ST-segment elevation/depression** (misdiagnosis of myocardial infarction).
> - It causes **false R-peak detection** in automated QRS detectors.
> - Standard diagnostic bands: ECG = **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**, EEG = **0.5 Hz – 70 Hz**, EMG = **20 Hz – 500 Hz**.

### Standard Metrics
- **Standard Cut-off Frequency for ECG Baseline Removal:** **0.05 Hz** (clinical standard per ANSI/AAMI EC13).
- **Respiration Coupling Frequency:** **0.15 Hz – 0.5 Hz**.
- **Maximum Acceptable Baseline Drift:** **± 5 mm** on a standard ECG trace.

---

## 1.2 Frequency Domain Filters: Butterworth Filter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Butterworth Filter** is a type of **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** analog/digital filter designed to have a **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband. It was first described by **Stephen Butterworth in 1930**. For a **Low Pass Filter (LPF)**, it allows frequencies below the cut-off to pass with unity gain while attenuating higher frequencies. For a **High Pass Filter (HPF)**, it does the inverse.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Butterworth Low Pass Filter** as a **smooth, frictionless ramp** rather than a sharp cliff:
- A **brick wall filter** (ideal, non-physical) would drop all frequencies above cut-off instantly to zero — like dropping off a **vertical cliff**.
- A **Butterworth filter** has a **gradual, smooth roll-off** — like a **gentle hill**. The "flatness" of the hill at the top means the passband frequencies are passed with **no ripple** (a key feature for biosignals where distortion is unacceptable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Butterworth is Preferred for Biosignals:**
> - **Maximally Flat Passband** → No ripple that could distort ECG QRS morphology.
> - **Smooth, Monotonic Roll-off** → Predictable behavior at the cut-off.
> - The trade-off is a **less steep roll-off** compared to Chebyshev or Elliptic filters, but this is acceptable for diagnostic accuracy.

---

## 1.3 Adaptive Filters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Adaptive Filter** is a self-adjusting digital filter whose **transfer function coefficients are updated iteratively** based on an optimization algorithm (commonly **LMS — Least Mean Squares** or **NLMS — Normalized LMS**). It is used when the noise characteristics are **non-stationary** (time-varying), which is the typical case in biosignal acquisition.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **bartender pouring a drink into a moving cup**:
- The cup keeps moving (non-stationary noise from breathing, motion).
- A **fixed filter** is like aiming at one spot — you spill the drink.
- An **adaptive filter** is like a **smart bartender who watches the cup's position and continuously adjusts the pour** in real time, learning from each error (spill) to improve the next pour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Applications in Biosignals:**
> - **Maternal ECG cancellation** from abdominal ECG (fetal ECG extraction).
> - **50/60 Hz powerline interference** removal when reference is available.
> - **Motion artifact cancellation** in Holter monitoring.
> - **Fetal Heart Rate (FHR)** extraction from maternal abdominal signals.

### Standard Metrics
- **LMS Step Size (μ):** typically **0.001 < μ < 0.1** (must satisfy stability: **0 < μ < 1/λmax**).
- **Filter Order (N):** typically **N = 4 to 32** taps for adaptive noise cancellation.
- **Convergence Rate:** dependent on eigenvalue spread of input autocorrelation matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Baseline Drift Removal — Methodological Breakdown

There are **five primary methods** to remove baseline drift in biosignals. Each has trade-offs in computational cost, latency, and signal distortion.

### Method 1: High-Pass Filtering (Linear Phase FIR / IIR)
- Apply a digital HPF with cut-off **f_c = 0.05 Hz** (ECG) or **f_c = 0.5 Hz** (EEG).
- **Pros:** Simple, real-time, low computation.
- **Cons:** Can distort the ST-segment if cut-off is too high; phase distortion in IIR.

### Method 2: Baseline Subtraction Using Polynomial Fitting
1. Identify **isoelectric anchor points** (e.g., PQ intervals in ECG).
2. Fit a low-order **polynomial (typically order 3–5)** through these points using **least-squares regression**.
3. Subtract the fitted polynomial from the raw signal.

### Method 3: Moving Average (MA) Filter / Moving Median Filter
- A **centered moving average** of window length **W** = sampling rate / lowest frequency of interest.
- Example: For **f_s = 360 Hz** and **f_low = 0.5 Hz**, choose **W = 720 samples**.
- Subtract MA output from raw signal to obtain high-passed result.

### Method 4: Wavelet-Based Decomposition
- Decompose the signal using **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** with a chosen mother wavelet (e.g., **db4, sym8**).
- Approximation coefficients at the **highest decomposition level** represent the baseline drift.
- **Reconstruct** the signal by setting the approximation coefficients to zero.
- **Pros:** Multi-resolution, preserves transient features like QRS.

### Method 5: Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD)
- Decompose signal into **Intrinsic Mode Functions (IMFs)**.
- The **last IMF** (or sum of last few) represents baseline drift.
- Reconstruct using remaining IMFs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** For real-time ECG monitors, the **Moving Average / HPF at 0.05 Hz** is most common. For Holter recordings (offline), **wavelet-based** is preferred for ST-segment preservation.

---

## 2.2 Butterworth Filter — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Butterworth Low Pass Filter (LPF)

The **magnitude-squared response** of an **N-th order** Butterworth LPF is:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

where:
- **Ω** = angular frequency = **2πf** (rad/s)
- **Ω_c** = **2πf_c** = cut-off angular frequency
- **N** = filter order (integer ≥ 1)

**Key Properties:**
- At **Ω = 0**: $|H(j0)|^2 = 1$ → DC gain = **1** (unity).
- At **Ω = Ω_c**: $|H(j\Omega_c)|^2 = 1/2$ → magnitude = **1/√2 ≈ 0.707** (−3 dB point).
- At **Ω → ∞**: $|H|^2 → 0$ → complete attenuation.
- The first **2N − 1 derivatives** of $|H|^2$ are **zero at Ω = 0** → maximally flat.

### 2.2.2 Butterworth High Pass Filter (HPF)

Obtained via **spectral transformation** $s → \Omega_c / s$ on the LPF prototype:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\Omega_c}{\Omega}\right)^{2N}}$$

**Key Properties:**
- At **Ω → ∞**: $|H|^2 → 1$ → passband.
- At **Ω = Ω_c**: $|H|^2 = 1/2$ → −3 dB point.
- At **Ω = 0**: $|H|^2 = 0$ → DC blocked.

### 2.2.3 Pole Locations

The Butterworth LPF has **2N poles** evenly distributed on a circle of radius **Ω_c** in the **s-plane**, at angles:

$$\theta_k = \frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, 2N-1$$

Only poles in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)** are retained for stability.

### 2.2.4 Filter Order Selection

Given specifications:
- **Passband frequency** $\Omega_p$ with maximum attenuation **A_p (dB)**
- **Stopband frequency** $\Omega_s$ with minimum attenuation **A_s (dB)**

The minimum order **N** is:

$$N \geq \frac{\log_{10}\left(\sqrt{\frac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}}\right)}{\log_{10}\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

The cut-off frequency is then:

$$\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{\left(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1\right)^{1/(2N)}}$$

### 2.2.5 Digital Butterworth (Bilinear Transformation)

To convert analog prototype **H(s)** to digital **H(z)**, apply **Bilinear Transform**:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

where **T = 1/f_s** is the sampling period. To correct for **frequency warping**, apply **pre-warping**:

$$\Omega_{analog} = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\Omega_{digital} \cdot T}{2}\right)$$

---

## 2.3 Adaptive Filters — LMS Algorithm

### 2.3.1 Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) Architecture

The standard **Widrow-Hoff ANC** has two inputs:
- **Primary input:** **d(n)** = corrupted signal = **s(n) + v(n)** (where s = signal, v = noise).
- **Reference input:** **x(n)** = noise correlated with v(n) but uncorrelated with s(n).

The adaptive filter **W(z)** processes x(n) to produce an estimate **y(n)** of the noise v(n). The error signal is:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = s(n) + v(n) - y(n) \approx s(n)$$

**y(n) is subtracted from d(n) → output e(n) ≈ clean signal s(n).**

### 2.3.2 LMS Update Equation

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu \cdot e(n) \cdot \mathbf{x}(n)$$

where:
- **w(n)** = filter weight vector at time n (length N).
- **μ** = step size (learning rate).
- **e(n)** = error signal.
- **x(n)** = reference input vector.

### 2.3.3 Stability Condition

The LMS algorithm converges (in the mean) if and only if:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

where **λ_max** is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix **R = E[x(n)x(n)ᵀ]**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off in μ:**
> - **Large μ** → fast convergence, but high steady-state error and possible instability.
> - **Small μ** → slow convergence, but low steady-state error and high stability.

### 2.3.4 Normalized LMS (NLMS)

To make the algorithm robust to input signal power variations:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \frac{\mu}{\epsilon + \|\mathbf{x}(n)\|^2} \cdot e(n) \cdot \mathbf{x}(n)$$

where **ε** is a small regularization constant (e.g., **1e-6**) to avoid division by zero.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Module 3**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Butterworth LPF magnitude | $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ | Maximally flat at $\Omega=0$ |
| 2 | Butterworth HPF magnitude | $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1+(\Omega_c/\Omega)^{2N}}$ | Unity gain at $\Omega \to \infty$ |
| 3 | -3 dB cut-off | $\vert H(j\Omega_c)\vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | Defines $\Omega_c$ |
| 4 | Butterworth order | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\sqrt{(10^{0.1A_s}-1)/(10^{0.1A_p}-1)}}{\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | $A_p, A_s$ in dB |
| 5 | Cut-off from specs | $\Omega_c = \Omega_p / (10^{0.1A_p}-1)^{1/(2N)}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Pole angles | $\theta_k = \pi/2 + (2k+1)\pi/(2N)$ | $k = 0, 1, \dots, 2N-1$ |
| 7 | Bilinear Transform | $s = (2/T)(1-z^{-1})/(1+z^{-1})$ | Analog to digital |
| 8 | Pre-warping | $\Omega_a = (2/T)\tan(\Omega_d T/2)$ | Avoids freq. warping |
| 9 | LMS update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Real-time adaptive |
| 10 | LMS stability | $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$ | Mean convergence |
| 11 | NLMS update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \dfrac{\mu}{\epsilon + \Vert\mathbf{x}\Vert^2} e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Power-normalized |
| 12 | ANC error | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n) \approx s(n)$ | Primary output |
| 13 | ECG cut-off | $f_c = 0.05$ Hz (clinical) | ANSI/AAMI EC13 |
| 14 | Baseline drift band | $0.15 - 0.5$ Hz | Respiration range |
| 15 | Moving Average window | $W = f_s / f_{low}$ | Samples |
| 16 | Excess MSE | $J_{ex} = \dfrac{\mu}{2} \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) \cdot J_{min}$ | LMS performance |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Method Used | Why |
|-------------|-------------|-----|
| **Holter ECG monitors** | Butterworth HPF at 0.05 Hz | Preserves ST segment for ischemia detection |
| **Patient monitors (ICU)** | Moving Average / IIR HPF | Real-time processing, low latency |
| **Fetal ECG extraction** | Adaptive LMS filter | Removes maternal ECG dynamically |
| **EEG acquisition** | Butterworth BPF (0.5–70 Hz) | Removes DC drift & line noise |
| **EMG preprocessing** | Butterworth HPF at 20 Hz | Removes motion artifacts |
| **Wearable ECG patches** | Wavelet decomposition | Robust to varying noise conditions |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Butterworth Filter Magnitude Response

**Goal:** Derive the Butterworth LPF magnitude response formula from first principles.

### Step 1: Start with the desired property — Maximally Flat Passband

We want a filter whose magnitude-squared response $|H(j\Omega)|^2$ has the **maximum number of zero derivatives at $\Omega = 0$** (flatness at DC).

For an N-th order filter, we require:

$$\left.\frac{d^k}{d\Omega^k} |H(j\Omega)|^2 \right|_{\Omega=0} = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N-1$$

### Step 2: Postulate a polynomial form

The simplest squared-magnitude that satisfies the flatness constraint and the asymptotic behavior ($|H|^2 → 0$ as $\Omega → \infty$) is:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + B_N(\Omega^2)}$$

where $B_N$ is a polynomial in $\Omega^2$ of degree N.

### Step 3: Choose $B_N$ for maximal flatness

To get **2N − 1 zero derivatives at $\Omega = 0$**, the polynomial must take the form:

$$B_N(\Omega^2) = (\Omega / \Omega_c)^{2N}$$

This is the **simplest monomial** with the right symmetry. Any lower-order term would either reduce flatness or violate physical realizability.

### Step 4: Substitute back

$$|H(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

### Step 5: Verify cut-off condition

At $\Omega = \Omega_c$:

$$|H(j\Omega_c)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + 1^{2N}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\Rightarrow |H(j\Omega_c)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = -3 \text{ dB}$$

This confirms $\Omega_c$ is the **−3 dB cut-off** frequency. **Derivation complete.** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Butterworth Filter Order for Given Specifications

**Given:**
- Passband edge: $\Omega_p = 2\pi (40)$ rad/s = $80\pi$ rad/s
- Stopband edge: $\Omega_s = 2\pi (100)$ rad/s = $200\pi$ rad/s
- Passband ripple: $A_p = 3$ dB
- Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 40$ dB

**Find:** Minimum filter order **N** and cut-off $\Omega_c$.

### Step 1: Define attenuation in dB

$$A(\Omega) = -10 \log_{10} |H(j\Omega)|^2 = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}\right]$$

### Step 2: Apply at passband edge

$$A_p = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}\right]$$

$$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 10^{0.1 A_p} - 1$$

### Step 3: Apply at stopband edge

$$A_s = 10 \log_{10}\left[1 + \left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}\right]$$

$$\Rightarrow \left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 10^{0.1 A_s} - 1$$

### Step 4: Divide the two equations

$$\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)^{2N} = \frac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}$$

### Step 5: Solve for N

$$2N \log_{10}\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)$$

$$N = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)}{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

### Step 6: Substitute numerical values

Numerator:

$$10^{0.1 \times 40} = 10^4 = 10000$$

$$10^{0.1 \times 3} = 10^{0.3} \approx 1.9953$$

$$\frac{10000 - 1}{1.9953 - 1} = \frac{9999}{0.9953} \approx 10046.6$$

$$\log_{10}(10046.6) \approx 4.0019$$

Denominator:

$$\frac{200\pi}{80\pi} = 2.5$$

$$\log_{10}(2.5) \approx 0.3979$$

Therefore:

$$N = \frac{4.0019}{2 \times 0.3979} = \frac{4.0019}{0.7959} \approx 5.028$$

### Step 7: Round up to integer

$$\boxed{N = 6 \text{ (minimum order to meet specs)}}$$

### Step 8: Compute actual cut-off frequency

$$\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1)^{1/(2N)}}$$

$$\Omega_c = \frac{80\pi}{(0.9953)^{1/12}}$$

Using $(0.9953)^{1/12} \approx 0.9996$:

$$\Omega_c \approx \frac{80\pi}{0.9996} \approx 80.16\pi \approx 251.7 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{f_c \approx 40.08 \text{ Hz}}$$

This is just above the passband edge of 40 Hz, satisfying the −3 dB ripple requirement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LMS Convergence Analysis (Mean Weight Behavior)

**Goal:** Show that the LMS weight vector converges to the **Wiener solution** in the mean.

### Step 1: Define the optimum Wiener solution

The Wiener filter is the optimum linear filter minimizing $E[e^2(n)]$:

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]$ = input autocorrelation matrix
- $\mathbf{p} = E[\mathbf{x}(n) d(n)]$ = cross-correlation vector

### Step 2: Express LMS update in terms of weight error

Let $\mathbf{w}(n)$ be the current weight vector. Define the weight error vector:

$$\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}$$

The LMS update is:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$$

### Step 3: Substitute $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu \left[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{x}(n)\right] \mathbf{x}(n)$$

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu \mathbf{x}(n) d(n) - 2\mu \mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^T(n) \mathbf{w}(n)$$

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \left[\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^T(n)\right] \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu \mathbf{x}(n) d(n)$$

### Step 4: Take expectation (assume independence of $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{w}$ — "independence assumption")

$$E[\mathbf{w}(n+1)] = E\left[\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^T(n)\right] E[\mathbf{w}(n)] + 2\mu E[\mathbf{x}(n) d(n)]$$

Since $E[\mathbf{x}(n) \mathbf{x}^T(n)] = \mathbf{R}$ and $E[\mathbf{x}(n) d(n)] = \mathbf{p}$:

$$E[\mathbf{w}(n+1)] = [\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{R}] E[\mathbf{w}(n)] + 2\mu \mathbf{p}$$

### Step 5: Steady-state condition

At convergence, $E[\mathbf{w}(n+1)] = E[\mathbf{w}(n)] = E[\mathbf{w}_\infty]$:

$$E[\mathbf{w}_\infty] = [\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{R}] E[\mathbf{w}_\infty] + 2\mu \mathbf{p}$$

$$2\mu \mathbf{R} E[\mathbf{w}_\infty] = 2\mu \mathbf{p}$$

$$\boxed{E[\mathbf{w}_\infty] = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{w}_{opt}}$$

**The LMS weight vector converges in the mean to the Wiener optimum.** ✓

### Step 6: Stability via diagonalization

Let $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{Q}^T$ (eigendecomposition). Define rotated weight error:

$$\mathbf{v}'(n) = \mathbf{Q}^T \mathbf{v}(n)$$

Then:

$$E[\mathbf{v}'(n+1)] = [\mathbf{I} - 2\mu \mathbf{\Lambda}] E[\mathbf{v}'(n)]$$

Each mode decays independently as:

$$E[v'_k(n)] = (1 - 2\mu \lambda_k)^n E[v'_k(0)]$$

Convergence requires $|1 - 2\mu \lambda_k| < 1$ for all k, i.e.:

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

This confirms the **stability condition** derived earlier.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Moving Average Filter as a High-Pass Filter

**Goal:** Show that subtracting a centered moving average from the signal is equivalent to a high-pass filtering operation.

### Step 1: Define centered moving average of window W

$$y_{MA}(n) = \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} x(n+k)$$

### Step 2: Define the high-pass output

$$y_{HP}(n) = x(n) - y_{MA}(n) = x(n) - \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} x(n+k)$$

### Step 3: Z-transform

$$Y_{MA}(z) = \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} z^{-k} \cdot X(z) = \frac{X(z)}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} z^{-k}$$

$$Y_{HP}(z) = X(z) - Y_{MA}(z) = X(z) \left[1 - \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} z^{-k}\right]$$

### Step 4: Transfer function

$$H_{HP}(z) = 1 - \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} z^{-k}$$

### Step 5: Evaluate at $z = 1$ (DC, $\omega = 0$)

$$H_{HP}(1) = 1 - \frac{1}{W} \cdot W = 1 - 1 = 0$$

**→ DC is blocked (zero gain at ω = 0).** ✓

### Step 6: Evaluate at $z = -1$ ($\omega = \pi$, Nyquist)

$$H_{HP}(-1) = 1 - \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=-W/2}^{W/2} (-1)^{-k}$$

For even W, the alternating sum = 0, so $H_{HP}(-1) = 1$. **→ High frequencies pass.** ✓

This proves the moving-average subtraction acts as a high-pass filter — perfect for baseline drift removal.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Filter

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| Real-time ICU ECG monitor | IIR Butterworth HPF (0.05 Hz) | Low latency, real-time, no delay |
| Holter (24-hr) offline analysis | Wavelet decomposition | Preserves ST morphology, no phase distortion |
| Fetal ECG from abdominal leads | Adaptive LMS filter | Non-stationary maternal ECG noise |
| EEG (high sample rate) | Butterworth BPF (0.5–70 Hz) | Standard clinical band |
| EMG analysis | Butterworth HPF (20 Hz) + BPF (20–500 Hz) | Removes motion + isolates MUAPs |
| Single-channel noisy ECG | Moving average subtraction | Simple, no reference needed |
| Multi-channel with reference noise | Adaptive NLMS | Optimal Wiener-like cancellation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Adaptive Noise Canceller (Widrow-Hoff ANC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Signal Source s of n] --> nodeSum1(("+"))
    nodeNoiseV[Noise Source v of n] --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 --> nodePrim[Primary Input d of n]
    nodeNoiseRef[Reference Noise x of n] --> nodeAdaptF[Adaptive Filter W z]
    nodeAdaptF --> nodeY[Filter Output y of n]
    nodeY --> nodeSum2((+))
    nodePrim --> nodeSum2
    nodeSum2 --> nodeErr[Error Signal e of n]
    nodeErr --> nodeAdaptF
    nodeErr --> nodeOut[Clean Output e n approx s of n]
    classDef src fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeS,nodeNoiseV,nodeNoiseRef src
    class nodeSum1,nodeSum2,nodePrim,nodeAdaptF,nodeY,nodeErr proc
    class nodeOut out
```

**Interpretation:** The reference noise **x(n)** is filtered by **W(z)** to produce an estimate **y(n)** of the noise. The error **e(n) = d(n) − y(n)** is fed back to adapt the filter. The output converges to the clean signal **s(n)**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Butterworth Filter Design Pipeline

```mermaid
    nodeA --> nodeC[Stopband edge Omega s]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Ripple A p dB]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Attenuation A s dB]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Step 2: Compute Order N]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 3: Compute Cutoff Omega c]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 4: Analog Prototype H of s]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 5: Pre warping for Bilinear]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 6: Bilinear Transform]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 7: Final Digital H of z]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Step 8: Verify Frequency Response]
    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef compute fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE step
    class nodeF,nodeG,nodeH,nodeI,nodeJ compute
    class nodeK,nodeL output
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart shows the complete Butterworth filter design procedure — from specifications to the final digital filter transfer function. Each block represents a key engineering step in module 3.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define baseline drift in biosignals. List any two causes and standard cut-off frequency used for its removal in ECG.**

**Model Answer:**

Baseline drift is a low-frequency artifact (typically **0.15 Hz to 0.5 Hz**) superimposed on biosignals such as ECG, caused by slow-varying interference. The clinical standard cut-off frequency for ECG baseline removal is **0.05 Hz** (per ANSI/AAMI EC13).

**Two causes:**
1. **Patient respiration** — chest motion varies electrode-skin impedance.
2. **Body movement / electrode motion** — varying contact pressure.
3. **Perspiration** — changes in skin-electrode impedance over time.

(Any two acceptable. Full credit: 3 marks)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is an adaptive filter? State the LMS update equation and the condition for stability.**

**Model Answer:**

An **adaptive filter** is a self-adjusting digital filter whose coefficients are updated iteratively using an optimization algorithm to minimize an error signal in real time. It is used for non-stationary noise cancellation in biosignals.

**LMS update equation:**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Stability condition (mean convergence):**

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Derive the magnitude-squared response of an N-th order Butterworth Low Pass Filter. Show that it is maximally flat at DC. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Statement of Design Goal**

We require a filter with:
- Unity gain at DC: $|H(0)|^2 = 1$
- Zero gain at infinity: $|H(\infty)|^2 = 0$
- **Maximal flatness** at DC: as many zero derivatives as possible at $\Omega = 0$

**Step 2: General Form**

For a stable, realizable filter, the squared magnitude is:

$$|H(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + B_N(\Omega^2)}$$

where $B_N(\Omega^2)$ is a polynomial of degree N in $\Omega^2$.

**Step 3: Apply Flatness Condition**

For $2N - 1$ zero derivatives at $\Omega = 0$:

$$\left.\frac{d^k |H(j\Omega)|^2}{d\Omega^k}\right|_{\Omega=0} = 0, \quad k = 1, \ldots, 2N-1$$

The only polynomial of degree N in $\Omega^2$ that achieves this is the pure monomial:

$$B_N(\Omega^2) = \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}$$

(Any term of lower degree would reduce the number of zero derivatives, and the constant term would violate $|H(0)|=1$.)

**Step 4: Final Magnitude-Squared Response**

$$\boxed{|H(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}}$$

**Step 5: Verification of Flatness at DC**

Differentiating:

$$|H|^2 = \left[1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}\right]^{-1}$$

$$\frac{d|H|^2}{d\Omega} = -2N \cdot \frac{\Omega^{2N-1}}{\Omega_c^{2N}} \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}\right]^{-2}$$

At $\Omega = 0$, this is **0** (since the numerator has $\Omega^{2N-1}$).

In general, the k-th derivative at $\Omega = 0$ contains a factor $\Omega^{2N-k}$, which is zero for $k < 2N$. Hence the first **$2N - 1$ derivatives vanish**, proving maximal flatness. ✓

**Step 6: −3 dB Cut-off Verification**

At $\Omega = \Omega_c$: $|H|^2 = 1/2 \Rightarrow |H| = 1/\sqrt{2} = -3$ dB. ✓

---

**(b) Design a digital Butterworth Low Pass Filter using the Bilinear Transformation method for the following specifications: (7 marks)**

- Passband edge: $f_p = 100$ Hz
- Stopband edge: $f_s = 200$ Hz
- Sampling frequency: $f_T = 1000$ Hz
- Passband ripple: $A_p = 1$ dB
- Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 20$ dB

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Pre-warp the critical frequencies**

Digital frequencies:
$$\omega_p = 2\pi f_p / f_T = 2\pi(100)/1000 = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$
$$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / f_T = 2\pi(200)/1000 = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

Pre-warped analog frequencies:
$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan(\omega_p / 2) = 2 \times 1000 \times \tan(0.1\pi) \approx 2000 \times 0.3249 = 649.84 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_s = 2 \times 1000 \times \tan(0.2\pi) \approx 2000 \times 0.7265 = 1453.09 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the minimum order**

$$N \geq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 \times 20} - 1}{10^{0.1 \times 1} - 1}\right)}{\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

Numerator:
$$10^2 - 1 = 99, \quad 10^{0.1} - 1 = 1.2589 - 1 = 0.2589$$
$$99 / 0.2589 \approx 382.39, \quad \log_{10}(382.39) \approx 2.5825$$

Denominator:
$$\Omega_s / \Omega_p = 1453.09 / 649.84 \approx 2.236, \quad \log_{10}(2.236) \approx 0.3495$$

$$N \geq \frac{2.5825}{2 \times 0.3495} = \frac{2.5825}{0.6990} \approx 3.695$$

**Step 3: Round up to next integer**

$$\boxed{N = 4}$$

**Step 4: Compute the 3-dB cut-off frequency**

$$\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1)^{1/(2N)}} = \frac{649.84}{(0.2589)^{1/8}}$$

$(0.2589)^{1/8} \approx 0.8475$

$$\Omega_c = \frac{649.84}{0.8475} \approx 766.78 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute poles on unit circle of radius $\Omega_c$**

For N = 4, angles:
$$\theta_k = \frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{8}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

- $k=0$: $\theta_0 = \pi/2 + \pi/8 = 5\pi/8 = 112.5°$
- $k=1$: $\theta_1 = \pi/2 + 3\pi/8 = 7\pi/8 = 157.5°$
- $k=2$: $\theta_2 = \pi/2 + 5\pi/8 = 9\pi/8 = 202.5°$
- $k=3$: $\theta_3 = \pi/2 + 7\pi/8 = 11\pi/8 = 247.5°$

Only LHP poles (k = 0, 1) are kept for stability:

$p_{1,2} = \Omega_c \cdot e^{j\theta_0}, \Omega_c \cdot e^{j\theta_1}$

**Step 6: Construct analog prototype $H_a(s)$**

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^4}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)(s - p_3)(s - p_4)}$$

where $p_3, p_4$ are conjugates of $p_1, p_2$.

**Step 7: Apply Bilinear Transform**

$$H(z) = H_a(s)\Big|_{s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}}$$

This yields the final digital transfer function $H(z)$ with all poles inside the unit circle (after normalization). ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) using the LMS algorithm. Derive the LMS update equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 — Adaptive Noise Canceller architecture)*

**Operation Explanation:**

The ANC system has two inputs:
- **Primary input** $d(n) = s(n) + v(n)$, where $s(n)$ is the desired biosignal and $v(n)$ is the noise.
- **Reference input** $x(n)$ is a noise signal correlated with $v(n)$ but uncorrelated with $s(n)$.

The adaptive filter $W(z)$ processes $x(n)$ to produce an estimate $y(n)$ of the noise $v(n)$. The error signal:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = s(n) + v(n) - y(n)$$

When $y(n) \approx v(n)$, the output $e(n) \approx s(n)$ (clean signal). The error is fed back to adapt $W(z)$ using LMS.

**LMS Derivation:**

**Step 1: Define Cost Function**

The instantaneous squared error:
$$J(n) = e^2(n) = [d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{x}(n)]^2$$

**Step 2: Gradient of Cost Function**

$$\nabla_w J(n) = \frac{\partial J(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2 e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3: Steepest Descent Update**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2} \nabla_w J(n)$$

**Step 4: Substitute Gradient**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2} \cdot (-2 e(n) \mathbf{x}(n))$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)}$$

(Note: Many textbooks use the notation $2\mu$ to absorb the factor of 2 from the gradient, giving the equivalent form $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$.)

**Stability condition:** $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$ (where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T]$).

---

**(b) An ECG signal sampled at 360 Hz is contaminated by baseline drift. Design a moving average filter to remove the drift. The lowest frequency of interest is 0.5 Hz. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the window length**

The moving average filter has a cut-off frequency approximately at:

$$f_c \approx \frac{f_s}{W} = \frac{0.443}{\text{normalized}}$$

For a centered moving average of length **W**, the first null occurs at $f = f_s/W$. To remove frequencies below 0.5 Hz, choose:

$$W = \frac{f_s}{f_c} = \frac{360}{0.5} = 720 \text{ samples}$$

This is equivalent to a window of **2 seconds**.

**Step 2: Filter Equation**

The moving average output:

$$y_{MA}(n) = \frac{1}{720} \sum_{k=-360}^{359} x(n+k)$$

**Step 3: High-Pass Output**

The baseline-corrected ECG:

$$y_{HP}(n) = x(n) - y_{MA}(n) = x(n) - \frac{1}{720} \sum_{k=-360}^{359} x(n+k)$$

**Step 4: Transfer Function Analysis**

Using z-transform:

$$H_{HP}(z) = 1 - \frac{1}{720} \sum_{k=-360}^{359} z^{-k}$$

At DC ($z = 1$): $H_{HP}(1) = 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓ (DC blocked)
At Nyquist ($z = -1$): Alternating sum = 0, so $H_{HP}(-1) = 1$ ✓ (high freq passes)

**Step 5: Verification — Cut-off Frequency**

For a centered MA filter, the magnitude response is:

$$|H_{HP}(f)| = \left|1 - \frac{\sin(\pi W f / f_s)}{W \sin(\pi f / f_s)}\right|$$

The first null of the MA portion (and thus where the HP response is maximum) occurs at $f = f_s / W = 360/720 = 0.5$ Hz ✓.

**Step 6: Phase Consideration**

A centered MA filter has **linear phase** (zero phase distortion if applied forward-backward), making it ideal for preserving ECG morphology, unlike IIR filters that introduce phase distortion.

**Step 7: Alternative Implementation**

For real-time operation where a 720-sample delay is unacceptable, a **causal MA filter** can be used with a smaller window (e.g., W = 200), but this introduces less sharp cut-off. The **forward-backward (filtfilt)** approach is preferred for offline Holter analysis. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the −3 dB definition:** Many students confuse the cut-off $\Omega_c$ with the passband edge. Remember: Butterworth's $\Omega_c$ is the **−3 dB** point, NOT the passband edge.
> 2. **Pole selection error:** Always retain only the **Left Half Plane (LHP)** poles for stability. Including RHP poles creates an unstable filter.
> 3. **Bilinear warping:** Forgetting to **pre-warp** critical frequencies leads to frequency response mismatch. The bilinear transform compresses the analog frequency axis non-linearly.
> 4. **LMS step size:** Using $\mu$ too large causes divergence. Always verify $\mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$ before deployment. Using $\mu$ too small causes slow convergence (sometimes taking >10,000 samples).
> 5. **Adaptive filter reference requirement:** The reference signal $x(n)$ must be **correlated with the noise** but **uncorrelated with the signal**. A wrong reference degrades the desired signal itself.
> 6. **MA window length:** Forgetting that W must be related to the lowest frequency of interest by $W = f_s / f_{low}$. Choosing W too small fails to remove drift; W too large over-smooths the QRS.
> 7. **Units consistency:** Use $\Omega$ (rad/s) for analog and $\omega$ (rad/sample) for digital. Mixing them is a common mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3**

### 🎯 Baseline Drift Removal
- ☐ Baseline drift = low-frequency artifact (**0.15 – 0.5 Hz**) from respiration, motion, impedance changes.
- ☐ Clinical cut-off for ECG: **f_c = 0.05 Hz** (ANSI/AAMI EC13).
- ☐ Five removal methods: **HPF, Polynomial Fitting, Moving Average, Wavelet, EMD**.
- ☐ MA window: $W = f_s / f_{low}$; centered MA = linear phase, ideal for ECG.
- ☐ DWT with **db4 or sym8** is preferred for ST-segment preservation in Holter.

### 🎯 Butterworth Filter
- ☐ Defined by **maximally flat** passband — all derivatives zero at DC up to order 2N−1.
- ☐ LPF magnitude: $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 = 1/[1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]$.
- ☐ HPF magnitude: $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^2 = 1/[1 + (\Omega_c/\Omega)^{2N}]$.
- ☐ −3 dB at $\Omega = \Omega_c$ by definition.
- ☐ Order formula uses **passband ripple $A_p$** and **stopband attenuation $A_s$** (both in dB).
- ☐ Poles lie on circle of radius $\Omega_c$; keep only **LHP poles** for stability.
- ☐ Analog-to-digital via **Bilinear Transform** with **pre-warping** correction.
- ☐ Smooth roll-off (less steep than Chebyshev) but **no passband ripple** — best for biosignals.

### 🎯 Adaptive Filters
- ☐ Use when noise is **non-stationary** (e.g., maternal ECG in fetal monitoring).
- ☐ **LMS update**: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$.
- ☐ **Stability**: $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$.
- ☐ **NLMS** uses $\mu / (\epsilon + \|\mathbf{x}\|^2)$ for power-independent convergence.
- ☐ **Convergence rate** ∝ eigenvalue spread of **R** — large spread = slow convergence.
- ☐ Applications: fetal ECG extraction, 50/60 Hz cancellation, motion artifact removal.
- ☐ Mean-converges to **Wiener optimum** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$.

### 🎯 Examiner's Key Takeaways
- ☐ Always state **units** (Hz, rad/s, rad/sample).
- ☐ Always **draw block diagrams** in design questions — KTU awards separate marks.
- ☐ For filter order problems, **show the full calculation** with logs.
- ☐ For LMS problems, mention the **stability condition** explicitly.
- ☐ For baseline drift, distinguish **real-time (HPF)** vs **offline (Wavelet)** approaches.
- ☐ In adaptive filters, always note the **requirement on reference signal** correlation properties.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cancellation of 50 Hz Signal in ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cancellation of 50 Hz Signal in ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Power Line Interference (PLI)** is the most ubiquitous and persistent noise corrupting Electrocardiogram (ECG) recordings. In India, Europe, Africa, and most of Asia, the AC mains electrical supply operates at a fundamental frequency of **f₀ = 50 Hz**. This interference manifests in the ECG trace as a steady, periodic sinusoidal contamination that rides on top of the true cardiac bioelectric signal.

**Cancellation of the 50 Hz signal in ECG** is therefore defined as the process of estimating the parameters (amplitude and phase) of the 50 Hz interference component contaminating the measured ECG and subtracting it out, using either a **fixed-coefficient notch filter** or an **adaptive interference cancelling architecture** (typically the Widrow-Hoff LMS adaptive filter), thereby restoring the diagnostic integrity of the cardiac waveform.

The 50 Hz PLI problem is aggravated because:
- The **QRS complex** (the most diagnostically important feature of the ECG, representing ventricular depolarization) contains spectral energy that overlaps with 50 Hz.
- The human body acts as an **antenna** capacitively coupled to the 50 Hz mains field.
- Stray leakage currents through poorly grounded equipment inject 50 Hz directly into the patient cable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The two most examination-relevant techniques for 50 Hz cancellation are (1) the **IIR Notch Filter** designed using the **Bilinear Transform (BLT)** with frequency warping, and (2) the **Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC)** using the **LMS algorithm**. Both are high-weightage topics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are recording a beautiful song in a studio, but an old refrigerator in the corner is humming a constant "hmmm" sound at a fixed pitch. Your recording contains the song (the real signal) plus that constant hum (the noise). 

- **Method 1 (Notch Filter):** This is like an equalizer that surgically cuts out *only* the exact "hmmm" frequency and everything around it, leaving the rest of the song untouched. It works perfectly as long as the hum stays at exactly the same pitch (50 Hz) and never drifts.

- **Method 2 (Adaptive Filter):** This is like placing a separate microphone near the refrigerator that records *only* the hum (not the song). A smart little audio processor then learns the exact loudness and timing of the hum from this auxiliary mic, and subtracts the right amount of it from your studio recording in real time. Even if the hum gets louder or softer, the adaptive system keeps adjusting itself.

In the ECG world:
- The **"refrigerator"** is the AC mains supply at 50 Hz.
- The **"song"** is the true ECG waveform (P-QRS-T).
- The **"equalizer / smart processor"** is the notch or adaptive filter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in 50 Hz Cancellation:**
> - **Line Frequency (India/Europe):** 50 Hz
> - **Line Frequency (USA/Japan-west):** 60 Hz
> - **Required Attenuation at f₀:** ≥ **40 dB** (clinical standard)
> - **3 dB Bandwidth (BW):** Typically **1–5 Hz** around 50 Hz
> - **Quality Factor Q = f₀ / BW** must be high (≥ 10) to preserve QRS spectral content.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of 50 Hz Interference in ECG

The 50 Hz contamination enters the ECG acquisition chain through three primary physical mechanisms:

1. **Capacitive Coupling (Electrostatic Induction):** The patient's body, treated as a floating conductor, picks up the 50 Hz electric field surrounding every live mains wire. Displacement current is injected into the body, and because the electrode-skin impedance is finite (not zero), a 50 Hz voltage appears at the electrode terminals.
2. **Inductive Coupling (Electromagnetic Induction):** Closed loops formed by the patient cable and the patient body act as single-turn secondary windings of a hypothetical power transformer. The time-varying magnetic field from mains wiring induces a 50 Hz EMF proportional to the loop area.
3. **Resistive / Conductive Leakage:** Poor grounding of biomedical equipment, faulty isolation amplifiers, and broken earth pins on power sockets allow small AC leakage currents (typically limited to **10 µA** by safety standards such as IEC 60601-1) to flow through the patient.

A fourth mechanism, **mains pickup through the neutral/ground reference electrode**, is technically a special case of (3) but is often mentioned separately because it is the dominant source in many clinical setups.

## 2.2 The Two Engineering Approaches

### Approach A — IIR Notch Filter (Fixed Coefficient)
A **notch filter** is a **band-stop filter** with an extremely narrow stopband centered on **f₀ = 50 Hz**, and an otherwise flat passband. Because the bandwidth is tiny, the filter's **quality factor Q is very high**.

For a digital IIR notch filter designed from an analog prototype using the **Bilinear Transform (BLT)**, the procedure is:
- Define analog pole-zero locations in the s-plane on a circle of radius Ω₀ = 2π·50 rad/s.
- Apply frequency pre-warping: Ω_a = 2/T · tan(ω₀/2) to compensate the non-linear frequency mapping of the BLT.
- Map s-plane to z-plane using z = (1 + sT/2)/(1 − sT/2).
- Obtain the digital transfer function H(z) = B(z)/A(z).

### Approach B — Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) using LMS
This is a Widrow-Hoff adaptive filter architecture. The setup requires:
- A **primary input** d(n) = ECG(n) + 50 Hz interference.
- A **reference input** x(n) that is highly correlated with the interference but uncorrelated with the ECG. Often x(n) is generated internally as a pure 50 Hz sine and cosine: x(n) = [sin(2π·50·nT), cos(2π·50·nT)]ᵀ.
- An adaptive FIR filter of order M whose weights w(n) are updated using the **LMS (Least Mean Squares)** algorithm to minimize the mean-square error E[e²(n)].
- The **error signal e(n) = d(n) − y(n)** is the cleaned ECG output.

The LMS update equation is:
- y(n) = wᵀ(n) · x(n)
- e(n) = d(n) − y(n)
- w(n+1) = w(n) + 2μ · e(n) · x(n)

where **μ** is the step-size that controls convergence speed and stability.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Line frequency (India) | **f₀ = 50 Hz** | ω₀ = 2π·50 = 100π rad/s |
| 2 | Digital angular frequency | **ω₀ = 2π f₀ T** | T = sampling interval (s) |
| 3 | Pre-warped analog freq. | **Ω_a = (2/T) tan(ω₀/2)** | BLT warping, rad/s |
| 4 | IIR Notch H(z) numerator (B(z)) | (1 − 2 cos ω₀ z⁻¹ + z⁻²) | Zeros ON unit circle at ±ω₀ |
| 5 | IIR Notch H(z) denominator (A(z)) | (1 − 2r cos ω₀ z⁻¹ + r² z⁻²) | Poles INSIDE unit circle, r < 1 |
| 6 | Pole radius r for given Q | **r = 1 − (π · BW)/(f_s)** | BW = −3 dB bandwidth (Hz) |
| 7 | Quality factor | **Q = f₀ / BW** | Higher Q → narrower notch |
| 8 | −3 dB BW for given r | **BW = (1 − r) f_s / π** | f_s = sampling rate |
| 9 | LMS output | **y(n) = wᵀ(n) x(n)** | Inner product of weights & input |
| 10 | LMS error | **e(n) = d(n) − y(n)** | Cleaned ECG ≈ e(n) |
| 11 | LMS weight update | **w(n+1) = w(n) + 2μ e(n) x(n)** | μ = step-size |
| 12 | LMS stability bound | **0 < μ < 1 / (M · P_x)** | P_x = input power |
| 13 | Notch depth (clinical goal) | **≥ 40 dB** | i.e. attenuation by 100× |
| 14 | Notch bandwidth (clinical) | **≈ 1 Hz to 5 Hz** | Must not clip QRS spectrum |
| 15 | Sampling rate (typical ECG) | **f_s = 250 to 1000 Hz** | Nyquist requires f_s ≥ 2·f₀ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** When asked to design a notch filter, you must explicitly show the frequency warping step (Ω_a = 2/T tan(ω₀/2)). Skipping the pre-warping in BLT is the #1 reason KTU examiners deduct marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Diagnostics:** Reliable ECG interpretation depends on removing 50 Hz so the ST segment, T wave, and tiny P wave are not masked.
- **Holter Monitors & Wearables:** 24-hour ambulatory ECG recorders cannot rely on shielded hospital-grade rooms; adaptive 50 Hz cancellation is essential.
- **Defibrillator Safety:** Patient isolation analysers monitor leakage currents whose measurements are themselves contaminated by 50 Hz.
- **Telemetry & IoMT (Internet of Medical Things):** Wireless ECG patches running on battery cannot use expensive Faraday cages; signal-processing-based cancellation is the only practical solution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IIR Notch Filter Transfer Function (Bilinear Transform Method)

### Step 1 — Define the digital notch specifications
Given:
- Sampling frequency **f_s = 250 Hz**, hence T = 1/250 = 0.004 s.
- Notch (centre) frequency **f₀ = 50 Hz**.
- −3 dB bandwidth **BW = 2 Hz** (very narrow so the QRS is not damaged).
- Quality factor **Q = 50/2 = 25**.

### Step 2 — Compute the digital angular frequency
The normalized digital frequency (in radians/sample) corresponding to 50 Hz is:
$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \, \frac{f_0}{f_s}$$

Substituting f₀ = 50 Hz and f_s = 250 Hz:
$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \, \frac{50}{250} = \frac{2\pi}{5} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the pole-radius r from the bandwidth
Using the standard relation for a narrow-band IIR notch:
$$r = 1 - \frac{\pi \, \text{BW}}{f_s}$$

Substituting BW = 2 Hz and f_s = 250 Hz:
$$r = 1 - \frac{\pi \cdot 2}{250} = 1 - \frac{6.2832}{250} = 1 - 0.02513 = 0.97487$$

The pole is placed very close to the unit circle (r → 1) so the notch is sharp and the Q is high.

### Step 4 — Place zeros exactly on the unit circle at angles ±ω₀
The zeros are placed precisely on the unit circle at z = e^{±jω₀} so that the magnitude response is **exactly zero** at f = f₀. This forces infinite attenuation (ideal notch) at the line frequency.
$$z_{1,2} = e^{\pm j \, 0.4\pi}$$

The numerator polynomial is therefore:
$$B(z) = (1 - e^{j\omega_0} z^{-1})(1 - e^{-j\omega_0} z^{-1}) = 1 - 2\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$

### Step 5 — Place poles inside the unit circle at the same angles
The poles are placed at the same angles ±ω₀ but at radius r < 1 to ensure stability:
$$p_{1,2} = r \, e^{\pm j \, 0.4\pi}$$

The denominator polynomial is:
$$A(z) = (1 - r e^{j\omega_0} z^{-1})(1 - r e^{-j\omega_0} z^{-1}) = 1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + r^{2} z^{-2}$$

### Step 6 — Assemble the digital transfer function
$$H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \frac{1 - 2\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + r^{2} z^{-2}}$$

### Step 7 — Numerically evaluate the coefficients
Compute the constant 2cos(0.4π):
$$2\cos(0.4\pi) = 2 \times 0.309017 = 0.618034$$

And r²:
$$r^{2} = (0.97487)^{2} = 0.95037$$

Final numerical transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.618034 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.602532 \, z^{-1} + 0.95037 \, z^{-2}}$$

The difference equation is therefore:
$$y(n) = 0.602532 \, y(n-1) - 0.95037 \, y(n-2) + x(n) - 0.618034 \, x(n-1) + x(n-2)$$

This filter will attenuate the 50 Hz component by approximately **40 dB** while leaving all other ECG frequencies essentially untouched (passband ripple < 0.5 dB).

## 3.2 Derivation of the LMS Adaptive 50 Hz Canceller

### Step 1 — Define the two-channel architecture
- **Primary input:** d(n) = s(n) + i(n), where s(n) is the true ECG and i(n) is the 50 Hz interference.
- **Reference input:** x(n) = [x₁(n), x₂(n)]ᵀ = [A sin(ω₀ nT), A cos(ω₀ nT)]ᵀ, an internally generated pure 50 Hz reference that is correlated with i(n) but **uncorrelated** with s(n).

### Step 2 — Express the interference in vector form
The actual interference at the primary input is:
$$i(n) = A_{1} \sin(\omega_0 nT) + A_{2} \cos(\omega_0 nT) = \mathbf{x}^{T}(n) \, \mathbf{w}^{*}$$

where w* = [A₁, A₂]ᵀ are the unknown true amplitudes of the in-phase and quadrature interference components.

### Step 3 — Adaptive filter output
The adaptive FIR filter with weight vector w(n) = [w₁(n), w₂(n)]ᵀ produces:
$$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n) = w_1(n) \sin(\omega_0 nT) + w_2(n) \cos(\omega_0 nT)$$

### Step 4 — Error signal (cleaned ECG estimate)
$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = s(n) + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{w}^{*} - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$
$$e(n) = s(n) + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\left[\mathbf{w}^{*} - \mathbf{w}(n)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Cost function (instantaneous squared error)
$$J(n) = e^{2}(n)$$

We minimize J(n) using stochastic gradient descent. The gradient with respect to w is:
$$\nabla J(n) = \frac{\partial e^{2}(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2 \, e(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

### Step 6 — LMS weight update
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2} \nabla J(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu \, e(n) \, \mathbf{x}(n)$$

Writing it out in scalar form for the two-tap case:
$$w_1(n+1) = w_1(n) + 2\mu \, e(n) \, \sin(\omega_0 nT)$$
$$w_2(n+1) = w_2(n) + 2\mu \, e(n) \, \cos(\omega_0 nT)$$

### Step 7 — Convergence and stability condition
For mean-square stability, the step-size μ must satisfy:
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{M \, P_x}$$

where M is the filter order (here M = 2) and P_x is the average power of the reference input x(n). For our 50 Hz reference with amplitude A, P_x = A²/2.

If A = 1, then P_x = 0.5 and the bound is 0 < μ < 1/(2 × 0.5) = 1. A common safe choice is **μ = 0.05** for ECG work.

### Step 8 — Steady-state behaviour
As n → ∞, w(n) → w* and the error e(n) → s(n). In other words, the adaptive filter has perfectly reconstructed and cancelled the 50 Hz interference, leaving the ECG clean.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Performance Criterion | IIR Notch Filter | LMS Adaptive Filter |
|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Computational cost | Very low (5 multiplies/sample) | Moderate (~2M+2 multiplies/sample) |
| Handles drift in f₀? | No (fixed at 50 Hz) | Yes (if reference tracks line) |
| Handles amplitude variation? | Only partial (deep notch clips it) | Yes, fully adaptive |
| Convergence / transient | None (instantaneous) | Requires ~N/2μ samples to settle |
| Output delay | 0 (causal, real-time) | M/2 samples |
| Sensitivity to coefficient quantisation | High (poles near unit circle) | Low |
| Suitable for Holter/ambulatory? | Yes (cheap, low power) | Yes if DSP available |
| Non-linear ECG-QRS preservation | Risk of ringing at sharp transients | Excellent (slow adaptation) |
| Typical KTU mark weightage | 7 marks (Part B sub-question) | 7 marks (Part B sub-question) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Adaptive Noise Canceller for 50 Hz ECG

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[50 Hz Mains Field] -->|Capacitive & Inductive Coupling| B(Patient Body / ECG Electrodes)
    B -->|Corrupted ECG d_n| C[Summing Junction]
    D[Local Oscillator 50 Hz] --> E[Sine Generator sin w0 nT]
    D --> F[Cosine Generator cos w0 nT]
    E --> G[Adaptive FIR Filter Weights w1, w2]
    F --> G
    G -->|Filter Output y_n| C
    C -->|e_n = d_n minus y_n| H[LMS Algorithm]
    H -->|Weight Update w_n_plus_1| G
    C -->|Cleaned ECG e_n| I[ECG Display / Diagnosis]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — IIR Notch Filter Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ECG Input x_n] --> B[Delay z^-1]
    A --> M1[Multiplier b0 = 1]
    B --> M2[Multiplier b1 = minus 2cos w0]
    B2[Delay z^-1] --> M3[Multiplier b2 = 1]
    M1 --> S1((+))
    M2 --> S1
    M3 --> S1
    S1 --> C[Output y_n]
    S1 --> D1[Delay z^-1]
    D1 --> M4[Multiplier a1 = 2r cos w0]
    S1 --> D2[Delay z^-1]
    D2 --> M5[Multiplier a2 = minus r2]
    M4 --> S2((-))
    M5 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Pole-Zero Diagram of the Notch Filter (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Unit Circle in z-plane] --> Z1[Zero at e to the power j 0.4 pi on Unit Circle]
    O --> Z2[Zero at e to the power minus j 0.4 pi on Unit Circle]
    O --> P1[Pole at r e to the power j 0.4 pi Inside Unit Circle r=0.975]
    O --> P2[Pole at r e to the power minus j 0.4 pi Inside Unit Circle r=0.975]
    Z1 --> R[Zeros sit ON unit circle at angles corresponding to 50 Hz]
    Z2 --> R
    P1 --> Q[Poles sit CLOSE to unit circle same angles giving sharp notch and high Q]
    P2 --> Q
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Full ECG Acquisition Chain with 50 Hz Cancellation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Heart] --> B[ECG Electrodes Ag or AgCl]
    B --> C[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    C --> D[Analog Anti-alias Filter]
    D --> E[ADC Sampling f_s = 250 to 1000 Hz]
    E --> F{Digital Domain}
    F --> G1[Option 1 IIR Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    F --> G2[Option 2 LMS Adaptive Canceller]
    G1 --> H[Baseline Wander Removal 0.5 Hz HPF]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[QRS Detection & Heart Rate]
    I --> J[Display or Holter Storage]
```
