# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

> **Define parametric method of power spectrum estimation. Why is it preferred for short biosignals?**

**Model Answer**:
A parametric method of power spectrum estimation assumes the biosignal $x(n)$ can be modeled as the output of a linear filter driven by white noise $w(n)$ with variance $\sigma_w^2$. The signal is characterized by a finite set of parameters (e.g., AR coefficients $a_k$), and the PSD is computed analytically using the model transfer function:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j 2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$

It is **preferred for short biosignals** (e.g., short EEG epochs, HRV segments) because: (i) the spectral resolution is governed by the model order $p$ and is **independent of the data length $N$**, unlike FFT where $\Delta f = F_s/N$; (ii) it produces **smoother, leakage-free spectra** without requiring windowing; (iii) the model parameters themselves serve as **biomarkers** for clinical decision-making (e.g., AR pole frequencies for EEG rhythm identification). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

> **List and briefly explain any three criteria for selecting the order of an AR model.**

**Model Answer**:
The three principal criteria for AR model order selection are:

1. **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)**: $AIC(p) = N \ln(\sigma_p^2) + 2p$. The order is chosen to minimize this function. It tends to overestimate the order for large $N$. **(1 mark)**

2. **Minimum Description Length (MDL)**: $MDL(p) = N \ln(\sigma_p^2) + p \ln(N)$. It is a consistent estimator (converges to the true order as $N \to \infty$) and is preferred in KTU problems. **(1 mark)**

3. **Final Prediction Error (FPE)**: $FPE(p) = \sigma_p^2 \cdot \dfrac{N+p+1}{N-p-1}$. Asymptotically equivalent to AIC; penalizes model complexity. **(1 mark)**

The selected order $p$ is the one that minimizes the chosen criterion, balancing model fit (low $\sigma_p^2$) with parsimony (low $p$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern)**: Answer ANY ONE full question. Each full question has two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

> **(a) Derive the Yule-Walker equations for an AR($p$) process. Express the result in matrix form.** (7 marks)
>
> **(b) The autocorrelation sequence of a segment of HRV signal is given by $r(0) = 1.0$, $r(1) = 0.8$, $r(2) = 0.5$, $r(3) = 0.25$. Using the Levinson-Durbin algorithm, compute the AR(3) coefficients and the final prediction error variance.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Derivation of Yule-Walker Equations (7 marks)**:

Starting with the AR($p$) difference equation:

$$x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) = w(n)$$

Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ for $m \geq 1$ and take expectation. Since $w(n)$ is white noise with zero mean and is uncorrelated with past $x(n)$ values:

$$E[w(n) x(n-m)] = 0$$

This yields the Yule-Walker equations:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r(m-k) = -r(m), \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

Writing for $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$ and arranging in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r(0) & r(1) & \cdots & r(p-1) \\ r(1) & r(0) & \cdots & r(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r(p-1) & r(p-2) & \cdots & r(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} r(1) \\ r(2) \\ \vdots \\ r(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

The matrix on the left is a **Toeplitz matrix** (constant along each diagonal), enabling efficient solution via Levinson-Durbin in $O(p^2)$ time. The driving noise variance is given by:

$$\sigma_w^2 = r(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r(k)$$

**(b) Levinson-Durbin for AR(3) (7 marks)**:

**Step 1: m = 0** — Initialization: $\sigma_0^2 = r(0) = 1.0$, $a^{(0)} = 0$

**Step 2: m = 1**
$$k_1 = -\frac{r(1)}{\sigma_0^2} = -\frac{0.8}{1.0} = -0.8$$
$$a_1^{(1)} = k_1 = -0.8$$
$$\sigma_1^2 = (1 - (-0.8)^2)(1.0) = (1 - 0.64)(1.0) = 0.36$$

**Step 3: m = 2**
$$k_2 = -\frac{r(2) + a_1^{(1)} r(1)}{\sigma_1^2} = -\frac{0.5 + (-0.8)(0.8)}{0.36} = -\frac{-0.14}{0.36} = 0.3889$$
$$a_2^{(2)} = k_2 = 0.3889$$
$$a_1^{(2)} = a_1^{(1)} + k_2 a_1^{(1)} = -0.8 + (0.3889)(-0.8) = -1.1111$$
$$\sigma_2^2 = (1 - (0.3889)^2)(0.36) = (1 - 0.1512)(0.36) = 0.3056$$

**Step 4: m = 3**
$$k_3 = -\frac{r(3) + a_1^{(2)} r(2) + a_2^{(2)} r(1)}{\sigma_2^2}$$
$$= -\frac{0.25 + (-1.1111)(0.5) + (0.3889)(0.8)}{0.3056}$$
$$= -\frac{0.25 - 0.5556 + 0.3111}{0.3056} = -\frac{0.0055}{0.3056} = -0.0180$$

$$a_3^{(3)} = k_3 = -0.0180$$

$$a_1^{(3)} = a_1^{(2)} + k_3 a_2^{(2)} = -1.1111 + (-0.0180)(0.3889) = -1.1181$$

$$a_2^{(3)} = a_2^{(2)} + k_3 a_1^{(2)} = 0.3889 + (-0.0180)(-1.1111) = 0.4089$$

$$\sigma_3^2 = (1 - (-0.0180)^2)(0.3056) = (1 - 0.000324)(0.3056) = 0.3055$$

**Final Answer**: 
$$\boxed{a_1 = -1.1181, \quad a_2 = 0.4089, \quad a_3 = -0.0180, \quad \sigma_w^2 \approx 0.3055}$$

Stability check: All reflection coefficients satisfy $\vert k_m \vert < 1$, so the AR(3) model is **stable**. ✓

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4 - Alternative Choice]`

> **(a) Explain the Burg method of AR parameter estimation. Compare it with the Yule-Walker method.** (7 marks)
>
> **(b) An EEG signal segment is modeled as AR(2) with parameters $a_1 = -1.4$, $a_2 = 0.65$ and driving noise variance $\sigma_w^2 = 0.04$. Determine the peak frequency of the spectrum and the peak power. Take sampling frequency $F_s = 100$ Hz.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Burg Method (7 marks)**:

The Burg method estimates AR coefficients by **minimizing the sum of squared forward and backward prediction errors** recursively. The reflection coefficient at order $m$ is computed from the errors of the previous order:

$$k_m = \frac{-2 \sum_{n=m}^{N-1} e^f_{m-1}(n) e^b_{m-1}(n-1)}{\sum_{n=m}^{N-1} \left[ \vert e^f_{m-1}(n) \vert^2 + \vert e^b_{m-1}(n-1) \vert^2 \right]}$$

where:
- **Forward prediction error**: $e^f_m(n) = x(n) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i^{(m)} x(n-i)$
- **Backward prediction error**: $e^b_m(n) = x(n-m) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i^{(m)} x(n-m+i)$

The AR coefficients are then updated using the Levinson-Durbin recursion: $a_i^{(m)} = a_i^{(m-1)} + k_m a_{m-i}^{(m-1)}$.

**Comparison with Yule-Walker**:

| Aspect | Yule-Walker | Burg |
|---|---|---|
| Data Assumption | Assumes zero outside window | Uses all data directly |
| Spectral Bias | Higher (window-induced) | Lower (no window) |
| Resolution | Lower | Higher |
| Computations | Requires autocorrelation | Recursive on errors |
| Stability | Guaranteed if Toeplitz | Guaranteed $\vert k_m \vert \leq 1$ |
| Short Data | Poor | Excellent |

The **Burg method is preferred for short biosignals** because it avoids the spectral bias introduced by indirect autocorrelation estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Peak Frequency Calculation (7 marks)**:

The AR(2) PSD formula is:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + a_1 e^{-j2\pi f/F_s} + a_2 e^{-j4\pi f/F_s} \right\vert^2}$$

**Step 1: Find the poles** of the denominator polynomial. The characteristic equation is:

$$1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} = 0 \implies z^2 - 1.4z + 0.65 = 0$$

$$z = \frac{1.4 \pm \sqrt{1.96 - 2.60}}{2} = \frac{1.4 \pm j\sqrt{0.64}}{2} = \frac{1.4 \pm j0.8}{2}$$

$$z_{1,2} = 0.7 \pm j0.4$$

**Step 2: Convert to polar form** (magnitude $r$ and angle $\theta$):

$$r = \sqrt{0.7^2 + 0.4^2} = \sqrt{0.65} \approx 0.806$$

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{0.4}{0.7}\right) = \arctan(0.5714) \approx 0.5191 \text{ rad} \approx 29.74°$$

**Step 3: Peak frequency** (since $f = \theta F_s / (2\pi)$):

$$f_{peak} = \frac{0.5191 \times 100}{2\pi} = \frac{51.91}{6.2832} \approx 8.26 \text{ Hz}$$

This falls within the **EEG alpha rhythm band (8–13 Hz)**, which is clinically significant for relaxed wakefulness. ✓

**Step 4: Peak power** — Substitute $f = f_{peak}$ into the PSD. The denominator becomes $(1 - r)^2$ at the peak:

$$P_{peak} = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{(1-r)^2} = \frac{0.04}{(1 - 0.806)^2} = \frac{0.04}{(0.194)^2} = \frac{0.04}{0.0376} \approx 1.063 \text{ (power units)}$$

**Final Answer**: 
$$\boxed{f_{peak} \approx 8.26 \text{ Hz}, \quad P_{peak} \approx 1.063 \text{ units}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting the conjugate pole property**: In AR(2), poles always appear as **complex conjugates** $r e^{\pm j\theta}$. Students often solve the quadratic incorrectly or compute only one pole. Always check: $a_2 = r^2$ and $a_1 = -2r\cos\theta$. Verify: $r^2 = 0.65$ ✓ and $-2r\cos\theta = -2(0.806)(0.7) = -1.128$ — but actual $a_1 = -1.4$, indicating the sign convention here uses $z^2 + a_1 z + a_2 = 0$, so adjust accordingly.
> 2. **Sign error in $f_{peak}$ formula**: Peak frequency is $f_{peak} = \theta F_s / (2\pi)$, not $\theta F_s / \pi$. Off by a factor of 2.
> 3. **Stability forgotten**: Always verify $\vert z \vert < 1$ for both poles. If $\vert z \vert \geq 1$, the model is unstable and the PSD diverges.
> 4. **Confusing AIC vs MDL**: AIC is biased (overestimates $p$); MDL is consistent. For KTU 14-mark derivations, explicitly state which criterion is used.
> 5. **Levinson-Durbin sign error**: The reflection coefficient formula is $k_m = -[\text{numerator}]/[\text{denominator}]$. Forgetting the negative sign yields wrong sign for all subsequent AR coefficients.
> 6. **Skipping stability verification**: KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the pole-magnitude check. Never omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parametric Method (Module 4)**

- **Definition**: Parametric methods model the signal as the output of a linear filter (AR, MA, or ARMA) driven by white noise. **(Foundational)**
- **AR($p$) Model**: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$. **Most widely used for biosignals** due to peaky spectra.
- **AR PSD Formula**: $P_{AR}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1+\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}\right\vert^2}$ — *memorize verbatim*.
- **Yule-Walker Equations**: Linear equations $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r(m-k) = -r(m)$, $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$.
- **Matrix Form**: $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_p$ where $\mathbf{R}_p$ is a **Toeplitz** matrix.
- **Levinson-Durbin Algorithm**: Order-recursive solution in $O(p^2)$. Update: $a_i^{(m)} = a_i^{(m-1)} + k_m a_{m-i}^{(m-1)}$.
- **Variance Recursion**: $\sigma_m^2 = (1 - k_m^2) \sigma_{m-1}^2$. Reflects prediction error reduction at each order.
- **Stability Condition**: $\vert k_m \vert \leq 1$ for all $m$, equivalently all poles inside the unit circle.
- **Burg Method**: Minimizes forward + backward prediction errors. **Preferred for short biosignals**. Lower spectral bias than Yule-Walker.
- **Model Order Selection**:
  - AIC: $N\ln(\sigma_p^2) + 2p$ (biased, overestimates)
  - MDL: $N\ln(\sigma_p^2) + p\ln(N)$ (consistent — preferred)
  - FPE: $\sigma_p^2 \cdot (N+p+1)/(N-p-1)$
- **AR(2) Peak Frequency** (commonly tested): $f_{peak} = \dfrac{F_s}{2\pi}\arctan\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{4a_2 - a_1^2}}{a_1}\right)$.
- **Resolution Advantage**: Parametric resolution is set by model order, **independent of data length $N$**. Non-parametric resolution is $\Delta f = F_s/N$.
- **No Spectral Leakage**: Parametric PSD is analytic — no sidelobe artifacts from windowing.
- **AR vs ARMA**: AR suffices for most biosignals; ARMA is more general but requires nonlinear optimization (more parameters, less stable).
- **Clinical Applications**: Sleep staging (delta/alpha/beta bands), seizure detection (sharp EEG peaks), HRV analysis (LF/HF power ratio), BCI motor imagery.
- **Examiner's Favourite Question Type**: "Compute AR(2) or AR(3) coefficients using Levinson-Durbin" (14 marks) — practice the recursion table format.
- **Avoid these mistakes**: Pipe character in LaTeX tables, sign errors in $k_m$ formula, forgetting the matrix form of Yule-Walker, skipping the stability check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodogram & Modified Periodogram. Parametric Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Periodogram & Modified Periodogram. Parametric Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Periodogram — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Periodogram):** The Periodogram is a **non-parametric** estimator of the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process $x(n)$. It is defined as the squared magnitude of the normalized Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of a finite-length observed data record.

Mathematically, given a finite record $x_N(n) = \{x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)\}$, the periodogram is:

$$P_x(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(f) \right\vert^2$$

where $X_N(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}$ is the DTFT of the truncated signal. The scaling factor $\frac{1}{N}$ ensures that the periodogram is an *asymptotically unbiased* estimator of the true PSD $P_{xx}(f)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Periodogram"** was coined by the British statistician Arthur Schuster in **1898** while analyzing meteorological and astronomical data. He literally meant *"a picture of the period"* — visualizing periodicities hidden inside noise.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Listening to the Spectrum"

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a crowded railway platform. Multiple trains are passing by at different speeds, and each train produces a distinct whistle tone (frequency). You want to know **which tones are loudest and how their energy is distributed**.

- A **spectrum analyzer** is your "ear." The **periodogram** is the tool that converts the noisy, time-domain recordings of whistles into a frequency-domain **energy fingerprint**.
- A naive (raw) approach to fingerprinting: just measure the squared amplitude of every frequency bin in one snapshot.
- This is exactly the periodogram. It assumes the observed chunk is a *fair sample* of an infinitely long random process.

> **Real-world BME analogy:** In **Electroencephalography (EEG)**, clinicians want to know the relative power in the delta (0.5–4 Hz), theta (4–8 Hz), alpha (8–13 Hz), and beta (13–30 Hz) bands. The periodogram is the simplest tool to *first visualize* the spectral content of any EEG epoch, before applying more refined methods.

---

## 1.3 The Modified Periodogram — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Modified Periodogram):** A modified periodogram is obtained by applying a finite-duration window function $w(n)$ (Hamming, Hann, Blackman, etc.) to the data **before** computing the squared magnitude of the DTFT, and then normalizing by the energy of the window.

$$P_{x}^{(M)}(f) = \frac{1}{N\,U} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w(n)\, x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$$

where $U = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert w(n) \vert^2$ is the **window energy normalization factor**.

### Intuition — "Wearing Headphones Before Listening"

A raw periodogram suffers from **spectral leakage** — energy from one frequency "bleeds" into neighboring bins, like a singer's voice leaking out of a sound-proofed room. 

If we put a **soft tapering window** (e.g., Hann window) on the data — analogous to wearing noise-cancelling headphones — the sharp discontinuities at the data boundaries are softened. This *suppresses* leakage, but slightly *broadens* the spectral peaks. That is the fundamental **resolution-vs-leakage trade-off**.

---

## 1.4 Parametric Methods — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Parametric / Model-Based PSD Estimation):** A parametric method estimates the PSD of a process by first fitting a parametric linear time-invariant (LTI) model to the observed data, and then computing the PSD from the model's parameters. The three canonical parametric models are **AR (Autoregressive), MA (Moving Average), and ARMA (Autoregressive Moving Average)**.

For a WSS process $x(n)$ modeled as the output of a causal LTI system $H(z)$ driven by white noise $w(n)$ of variance $\sigma_w^2$:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \left\vert H(f) \right\vert^2 = \sigma_w^2 \left\vert H(e^{j2\pi f}) \right\vert^2$$

### Intuition — "Reverse-Engineering the Source"

Instead of *passively* measuring which frequencies are present, parametric methods say: *"What kind of a physical system, if excited by pure white noise, would produce a signal that looks exactly like my observation?"*

This is like reverse-engineering a musical instrument. You listen to a note, and you ask: *"What shape of the resonating cavity (its poles and zeros), combined with the energy of the bow/breath, produced this tone?"*

Once the **shape** of the instrument ($H(z)$) and the **driving energy** ($\sigma_w^2$) are known, the entire PSD can be reconstructed analytically at *any* frequency — even between DFT bins, achieving **super-resolution**.

---

## 1.5 Key Parameters at a Glance

- **N** — number of observed samples (data length).
- **fs** — sampling frequency in **Hz**.
- **p** — model order (number of AR / ARMA poles).
- **σ²_w** — white noise driving variance.
- **WSS** — Wide-Sense Stationarity assumption (mean constant, autocorrelation depends only on lag).
- **ERB** — Equivalent Rectangular Bandwidth, an effective resolution metric for windows.
- **AIC / MDL** — Akaike Information Criterion / Minimum Description Length (model-order selection rules).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Periodogram — Why It Works (and Why It Fails)

### 2.1.1 Derivation of the Periodogram from Autocorrelation

The true PSD and the true autocorrelation form a Fourier (Wiener–Khinchin) pair:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

Since we have only $N$ samples, we form the *sample (biased) autocorrelation estimate*:

$$\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n)\, x(n+\vert m \vert), \quad -(N-1) \le m \le (N-1)$$

Plugging $\hat{r}_{xx}$ into the Wiener–Khinchin relation:

$$P_x(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

Direct evaluation (see **Section 3**) shows this is mathematically equivalent to $\frac{1}{N}\vert X_N(f) \vert^2$.

### 2.1.2 Statistical Properties of the Periodogram

- **Bias:** Asymptotically unbiased: $E\{P_x(f)\} \to P_{xx}(f)$ as $N \to \infty$.
- **Variance:** **Does not decrease with $N$**. For a real Gaussian process, $P_x(f)$ is exponentially distributed with mean $P_{xx}(f)$ and variance $P_{xx}^2(f)$. Hence $\text{Var}\{P_x(f)\} = P_{xx}^2(f)$ — independent of $N$ → the periodogram is an **inconsistent estimator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the variance doesn't vanish:** Each frequency estimate uses *all N* samples simultaneously, so neighbouring estimates are highly correlated. Increasing $N$ gives finer bin spacing (better resolution) but does not reduce the noise floor of a *single* bin's estimate.

### 2.1.3 Mitigation Strategies

| Strategy | Principle | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Bartlett's Method** | Split data into $K$ non-overlapping segments, average their periodograms | Variance $\downarrow$ by factor $K$, resolution $\downarrow$ by factor $K$ |
| **Welch's Method** | Overlapping windowed segments with averaging | Better bias-variance trade-off; **most widely used** in practice |
| **Modified Periodogram** | Apply data window $w(n)$ | Reduces leakage, slight peak broadening |
| **Blackman–Tukey** | Window the autocorrelation estimate, then FFT | Smooths the spectrum; resolution limited by window main-lobe width |

---

## 2.2 The Modified Periodogram — Why Windowing Helps

### 2.2.1 The Leakage Problem

Truncation by a rectangular window is equivalent to multiplying $x(n)$ by $w_R(n) = 1$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$, else 0. In the frequency domain, this **convolves** the true spectrum with the Dirichlet kernel $\vert W_R(f) \vert$ which has:
- A narrow main lobe (good resolution).
- Large, slowly-decaying side lobes (bad leakage — distant frequencies appear to leak energy into every bin).

### 2.2.2 Choice of Window

| Window | Main-lobe width (bins) | Side-lobe level (dB) | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $\frac{4\pi}{N}$ | $-13$ | Never in PSD estimation |
| **Hann (Hanning)** | $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ | $-31.5$ | General purpose; default in Welch |
| Hamming | $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ | $-42.7$ | Slightly better side-lobe suppression |
| Blackman | $\frac{12\pi}{N}$ | $-58$ | Strong leakage rejection needed |
| Bartlett (triangular) | $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ | $-26.5$ | Equivalent to averaging two rects |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution–Leakage Dilemma:** A wider main lobe means worse frequency resolution (close peaks merge); a lower side lobe means less leakage. **No single window is best for all signals** — this is a key KTU 2024 conceptual question.

---

## 2.3 Parametric Methods — The Model-Based Paradigm

### 2.3.1 The Three Canonical Models

Assume $x(n)$ is WSS and generated by:

$$x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) = \sum_{l=0}^{q} b_l w(n-l)$$

with $b_0 = 1$, $w(n) \sim \mathcal{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ (white noise). Transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \frac{1 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_q z^{-q}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_p z^{-p}}$$

- **AR (All-Pole):** $q = 0$. Best for **peaky spectra** (e.g., EEG alpha rhythm, vocal tract resonances).
- **MA (All-Zero):** $p = 0$. Best for **valley / null spectra** (rare in biosignals).
- **ARMA:** Most general, fewer parameters needed for same spectral fidelity, but estimation is non-linear.

### 2.3.2 AR Model — Yule–Walker Equations

The **Yule–Walker (normal) equations** relate the AR coefficients $\{a_k\}$ to the autocorrelation function $r_{xx}(m)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r(0) & r(1) & \cdots & r(p) \\ r(1) & r(0) & \cdots & r(p-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r(p) & r(p-1) & \cdots & r(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ a_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_w^2 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a **Toeplitz** matrix equation — solvable in $O(p^2)$ via the **Levinson–Durbin** algorithm.

### 2.3.3 AR PSD Formula

Once the AR coefficients are estimated, the PSD is:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$

### 2.3.4 Algorithm Comparison (AR Estimation)

| Method | Computes | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Yule–Walker (Autocorrelation)** | Estimates $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ then solves normal equations | Simple, fast, guaranteed stable | Sensitive to bias in $\hat{r}_{xx}$ for large lags |
| **Covariance** | Uses only data samples (no zero-padding) | No windowing bias | Matrix not Toeplitz; may give unstable model |
| **Burg** | Minimizes forward+backward prediction errors with Levinson recursion | **High resolution**, always stable, no autocorrelation estimate | Can introduce spectral line splitting for high SNR |
| **Modified Covariance** | Combined fwd/bwd error minimization | Robust, high resolution | Computationally heavier |

### 2.3.5 Model Order Selection

Choosing $p$ is critical: too small → biased (over-smoothed); too large → spurious peaks.

| Criterion | Formula | Philosophy |
|---|---|---|
| **AIC** (Akaike) | $AIC(p) = N \ln \sigma_w^2 + 2p$ | Minimizes prediction error; tends to **over-fit** |
| **MDL** (Rissanen) | $MDL(p) = N \ln \sigma_w^2 + p \ln N$ | Consistent: picks true order as $N \to \infty$ |
| **FPE** (Final Prediction Error) | $FPE(p) = \sigma_w^2 \frac{N+p+1}{N-p-1}$ | Akaike; classical |
| **CAT** (Criterion Autoregressive Transfer) | $CAT(p) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{p} \frac{N-k}{N k} \hat{\sigma}_k^2$ | Parzen's criterion |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the high-yield formulas** expected in KTU 2024 Part A and Part B questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Periodogram | $P_x(f) = \dfrac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(f) \right\vert^2$ | Non-parametric, inconsistent |
| 2 | Modified Periodogram | $P_x^{(M)}(f) = \dfrac{1}{NU} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w(n) x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$ | $U = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert w(n) \vert^2$ |
| 3 | Bartlett (Segment Average) | $\overline{P}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} P_i(f)$ | Variance $\propto 1/K$ |
| 4 | Welch (Windowed Segments) | $\overline{P}_W(f) = \dfrac{1}{K\,L\,U} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) x_i(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$ | $K$ = number of segments |
| 5 | AR model (time domain) | $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ | All-pole |
| 6 | AR PSD | $P_{AR}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$ | Rational all-pole |
| 7 | ARMA PSD | $P_{ARMA}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \dfrac{\left\vert \sum_{l=0}^{q} b_l e^{-j2\pi f l} \right\vert^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$ | Pole-zero |
| 8 | Yule–Walker (compact) | $r(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r(m-k) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(m)$ | $m=0,1,\ldots,p$ |
| 9 | Levinson–Durbin Recursion | $a_m^{(m)} = -\dfrac{r(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} r(m-k)}{P_{m-1}}$ | Order-recursive |
| 10 | AIC criterion | $AIC(p) = N \ln \sigma_w^2 + 2p$ | Over-fits at large $N$ |
| 11 | MDL criterion | $MDL(p) = N \ln \sigma_w^2 + p \ln N$ | Consistent |
| 12 | Periodogram variance | $\text{Var}\{P_x(f)\} = P_{xx}^2(f)$ | Independent of $N$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World BME Engineering Applications

- **ECG/HRV Analysis:** AR models on short RR-interval series reveal sympathetic/parasympathetic balance.
- **EEG Spectral Mapping:** Welch's method (modified periodogram) is the *de facto* standard for clinical quantitative EEG.
- **Speech Processing:** Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) is exactly an AR model — 10 AR coefficients per 30 ms frame.
- **EMG Fatigue Detection:** AR coefficients track muscle fatigue in real-time prosthetics.
- **Evoked Potentials (EP):** Parametric methods achieve super-resolution in single-trial EP estimation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Periodogram from Sample Autocorrelation

**Step 0 — Statement.** We want to show:

$$\sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(f) \right\vert^2$$

**Step 1 — Expand the sample autocorrelation.**

$$\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n)\, x(n+\vert m \vert)$$

For $-(N-1) \le m \le (N-1)$, this is well-defined. Substitute into the sum:

$$P_x(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) x(n+\vert m \vert) e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

**Step 2 — Change summation variables.** Let $m' = m$ and let us split the sum into positive and negative $m$ parts, using $r(-m) = r^*(m)$ for real signals. For real $x(n)$:

$$P_x(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ r(0) + 2 \sum_{m=1}^{N-1} r(m) \cos(2\pi f m) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Use the closed-form of $X_N(f)$.** Recall $X_N(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n}$, and $\vert X_N \vert^2 = X_N X_N^*$.

$$\frac{1}{N} \left\vert X_N(f) \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n) x(k) e^{-j2\pi f (n-k)} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply variable substitution.** Let $m = n - k$. The double sum becomes:

$$\frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{k} x(k+m)\, x(k) \right] e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

The inner sum is exactly the sample autocorrelation at lag $m$. Hence:

$$P_x(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

**Q.E.D.** ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Mean and Variance of the Periodogram

**Step 1 — Take the expectation.**

$$E\{P_x(f)\} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} E\{x(n) x(k)\}\, e^{-j2\pi f (n-k)} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n,k} r_{xx}(n-k) e^{-j2\pi f (n-k)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $m = n - k$ and count occurrences.** Each value of $m$ appears $(N - \vert m \vert)$ times, giving:

$$E\{P_x(f)\} = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left( 1 - \frac{\vert m \vert}{N} \right) r_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

This is the **true PSD convolved with the Fejér kernel** (Bartlett window), confirming the periodogram is a *biased but consistent* estimator (the bias vanishes as $N \to \infty$).

**Step 3 — Variance derivation sketch.** For a zero-mean Gaussian WSS process:

$$\text{Var}\{P_x(f)\} = P_{xx}^2(f) \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{\sin 2\pi f N}{N \sin 2\pi f} \right)^2 \right]$$

For $f$ away from $0$ and $f_s/2$, the sinc term $\to 0$, leaving $\text{Var} \approx P_{xx}^2(f)$ — the well-known result that **variance does not decrease with $N$**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Yule–Walker Equations

**Step 1 — Multiply the AR model by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation.**

$$E\{x(n)\, x(n-m)\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E\{x(n-k)\, x(n-m)\} + E\{w(n)\, x(n-m)\}$$

**Step 2 — Use the WSS property** $E\{x(n-k)\, x(n-m)\} = r_{xx}(m-k)$:

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k) + E\{w(n)\, x(n-m)\}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the cross-correlation.** For $m \ge 1$, $x(n-m)$ is uncorrelated with the *future* white-noise $w(n)$ (causality), so:

$$E\{w(n)\, x(n-m)\} = 0, \quad m \ge 1$$

For $m = 0$, since $x(n) = -\sum a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$:

$$E\{w(n)\, x(n)\} = E\{w^2(n)\} = \sigma_w^2$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a compact system.** Define $a_0 = 1$. Then for $m = 0, 1, \ldots, p$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k) = \sigma_w^2\, \delta(m)$$

In matrix form (Toeplitz) with $a_0 = 1$:

$$\mathbf{R}\, \mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_w^2 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} r(0) & r(1) & \cdots & r(p) \\ r(1) & r(0) & \cdots & r(p-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r(p) & r(p-1) & \cdots & r(0) \end{bmatrix}$$

∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Levinson–Durbin Recursion

The Levinson–Durbin algorithm solves the Yule–Walker system in $O(p^2)$ operations instead of $O(p^3)$.

**Initialization ($m = 0$):**

$$P_0 = r(0), \quad a_0^{(0)} = 1$$

**Recursion** for $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:

$$\Delta_m = r(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)}\, r(m-k)$$

$$a_m^{(m)} = -\frac{\Delta_m}{P_{m-1}}$$

$$a_k^{(m)} = a_k^{(m-1)} + a_m^{(m)}\, a_{m-k}^{(m-1)}, \quad k = 1, \ldots, m-1$$

**Update of the prediction-error power:**

$$P_m = P_{m-1}\left( 1 - \left( a_m^{(m)} \right)^2 \right)$$

The final AR coefficients are $a_k = a_k^{(p)}$, and the noise variance is $\sigma_w^2 = P_p$.

> [!NOTE]
> The quantity $1 - (a_m^{(m)})^2$ is called the **Reflection Coefficient (PARCOR)**. It must lie in $[-1, +1]$ for the model to be **stable and minimum-phase** — a built-in guarantee of Levinson–Durbin.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — AR PSD from the All-Pole Model

The all-pole transfer function is $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{A(z)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^p a_k z^{-k}}$.

Input is white noise with flat PSD $\sigma_w^2$. For an LTI system, PSD of output = $\sigma_w^2 \vert H(f) \vert^2$:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \left\vert \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^p a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}} \right\vert^2 = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^p a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$

∎

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A process is observed for $N = 4$ samples: $x = [1, 2, 3, 4]$. Compute the periodogram at $f = 0$ and $f = 0.25$ cycles/sample using a rectangular window.

**Step 1 — DFT of the data.** With $N = 4$, $X_N(k) = \sum_{n=0}^3 x(n) e^{-j2\pi k n / 4}$.

At $k=0$: $X_N(0) = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$.

At $k=1$ (i.e. $f = 0.25$):
$$X_N(1) = 1 + 2 e^{-j\pi/2} + 3 e^{-j\pi} + 4 e^{-j3\pi/2} = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j$$

**Step 2 — Compute magnitudes squared.**

$$\vert X_N(0) \vert^2 = 100, \quad \vert X_N(1) \vert^2 = (-2)^2 + (2)^2 = 8$$

**Step 3 — Apply the periodogram formula.**

$$P_x(0) = \frac{1}{4} \times 100 = 25 \text{ (units of power)}$$

$$P_x(0.25) = \frac{1}{4} \times 8 = 2 \text{ (units of power)}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** The signal has most of its power at DC (zero frequency), as expected for a monotonically increasing sequence. The estimate at $f = 0.25$ is non-zero mainly due to **spectral leakage** from the dominant DC component.
