# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the periodogram. State any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **periodogram** is the non-parametric PSD estimate defined as $P_x(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert X_N(f) \right\vert^2$ where $X_N(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n}$.

**Limitations:**

1. **High variance:** The variance of the periodogram is $P_{xx}^2(f)$ and is **independent of $N$**, making it an inconsistent estimator.
2. **Spectral leakage:** Implicit rectangular windowing causes energy from strong frequency components to leak into neighbouring bins.
3. **Resolution limitation:** Frequency resolution is fixed at $\Delta f = f_s/N$; for short data, closely spaced sinusoids cannot be resolved.
4. **Bias due to windowing:** Equivalent to convolving the true PSD with the Fejér kernel, smearing sharp spectral features.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the periodogram and the modified periodogram.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Periodogram | Modified Periodogram |
|---|---|---|
| Data windowing | Implicit rectangular window | Explicit window $w(n)$ (Hann, Hamming, Blackman) |
| Formula | $\frac{1}{N} \vert X_N \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{NU} \vert \sum w(n) x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \vert^2$ |
| Spectral leakage | High ($-13$ dB side lobes) | Greatly reduced ($-31$ to $-58$ dB) |
| Frequency resolution | Best (narrow main lobe) | Slightly degraded (wider main lobe) |
| Bias | Fejér-kernel biased | Reduced (window-controlled main lobe) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **periodogram method** of PSD estimation. Discuss its properties — bias, variance, and consistency. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the **modified periodogram**? Explain how windowing reduces spectral leakage. Compare the rectangular, Hann, and Blackman windows in terms of main-lobe width and side-lobe level. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a) — Periodogram Method

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Given a finite WSS record $x(n), \; 0 \le n \le N-1$, the periodogram is:

$$P_x(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Block Diagram.** Reference the diagram in **Section 4.1**: Signal → Rectangular Window → DTFT → $\vert \cdot \vert^2$ → Normalize by $N$ → Periodogram.

**Step 3 — Bias.** Taking expectation and substituting $m = n - k$:

$$E\{P_x(f)\} = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left( 1 - \frac{\vert m \vert}{N} \right) r_{xx}(m) e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

This is the convolution of the true PSD with the Fejér kernel. As $N \to \infty$, the Fejér kernel $\to \delta(f)$ and $E\{P_x\} \to P_{xx}(f)$ → **asymptotically unbiased**.

**Step 4 — Variance.** For a Gaussian real process, $P_x(f)$ is exponentially distributed with:

$$\text{Var}\{P_x(f)\} = P_{xx}^2(f)$$

This is **independent of $N$**, hence the periodogram is **inconsistent**.

**Step 5 — Consistency.** An estimator is consistent if bias $\to 0$ **and** variance $\to 0$ as $N \to \infty$. Since variance does not decrease, the periodogram is **NOT consistent**.

#### Model Solution (b) — Modified Periodogram

**Step 1 — Concept.** Windowing multiplies the data by a tapered function $w(n)$ to reduce the discontinuities at the data boundaries that cause leakage.

**Step 2 — Formula.**

$$P_x^{(M)}(f) = \frac{1}{NU} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w(n) x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2, \quad U = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert w(n) \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — How leakage is reduced.** The DTFT of a tapered window has much lower side lobes than the Dirichlet kernel of a rectangular window. Energy that would have leaked from a strong sinusoid into neighbouring bins is now suppressed.

**Step 4 — Window Comparison Table.**

| Window | Main-lobe width (normalized) | Side-lobe level (dB) |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $0.89 \cdot 2\pi / N$ | $-13$ |
| Hann | $1.44 \cdot 2\pi / N$ | $-31.5$ |
| Hamming | $1.30 \cdot 2\pi / N$ | $-42.7$ |
| Blackman | $1.68 \cdot 2\pi / N$ | $-58$ |

**Step 5 — Trade-off.** Reducing side lobes always **broadens the main lobe**, decreasing frequency resolution. Choosing a window is a balance between leakage suppression and resolution preservation.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a)** Explain the **AR (Autoregressive) parametric model** for spectral estimation. Derive the **Yule–Walker equations** and show that they lead to a Toeplitz matrix system. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Levinson–Durbin algorithm** for AR parameter estimation. How does it achieve $O(p^2)$ complexity? Mention the significance of the reflection coefficient. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a) — AR Model and Yule–Walker

**Step 1 — Model Definition.** An AR($p$) process satisfies:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n), \quad w(n) \sim \mathcal{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2)$$

In the $z$-domain: $X(z) = H(z) W(z)$ with $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^p a_k z^{-k}}$.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Yule–Walker.** Multiply the model equation by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation:

$$E\{x(n) x(n-m)\} = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E\{x(n-k) x(n-m)\} + E\{w(n) x(n-m)\}$$

By WSS, $E\{x(n-k) x(n-m)\} = r_{xx}(m-k)$:

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) + E\{w(n) x(n-m)\}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-correlation term.** For $m \ge 1$, $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with the past $x(n-m)$ (causality), so the cross-term vanishes. For $m = 0$, it equals $\sigma_w^2$. Hence:

$$r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) = \sigma_w^2 \delta(m), \quad m \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Matrix Form.** Setting $a_0 = 1$ and writing for $m = 0, 1, \ldots, p$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r(0) & r(1) & r(2) & \cdots & r(p) \\ r(1) & r(0) & r(1) & \cdots & r(p-1) \\ r(2) & r(1) & r(0) & \cdots & r(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r(p) & r(p-1) & r(p-2) & \cdots & r(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_w^2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The autocorrelation matrix is **Toeplitz** ($R_{ij} = r(i-j)$) and symmetric.

#### Model Solution (b) — Levinson–Durbin Algorithm

**Step 1 — Recursion initialization.**

$$P_0 = r(0), \quad a_0^{(0)} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Recursion step ($m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$).** Compute the reflection coefficient:

$$a_m^{(m)} = -\frac{r(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} r(m-k)}{P_{m-1}}$$

Update the lower-order coefficients:

$$a_k^{(m)} = a_k^{(m-1)} + a_m^{(m)} a_{m-k}^{(m-1)}, \quad k = 1, \ldots, m-1$$

Update the prediction error power:

$$P_m = P_{m-1} \left[ 1 - \left( a_m^{(m)} \right)^2 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Complexity Analysis.** At iteration $m$, the inner sum has $m-1$ multiplications. Total:

$$T(p) = \sum_{m=1}^{p} m \approx \frac{p(p+1)}{2} = O(p^2)$$

Standard Gaussian elimination is $O(p^3)$ — Levinson–Durbin is exponentially faster because it **exploits the Toeplitz structure**.

**Step 4 — Significance of Reflection Coefficient.**
The reflection coefficient $k_m = a_m^{(m)}$ lies in $(-1, +1)$ for a stable minimum-phase AR process. It:
- Acts as a **stability indicator**: $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ is required.
- Is used in **lattice filter implementations** of AR models.
- Equals the negative of the partial autocorrelation (PACF) at lag $m$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls — Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing the periodogram with the modified periodogram:** Many students write $\frac{1}{N} \vert X_N \vert^2$ even when the question specifically asks about *modified* periodogram. The window function $w(n)$ and the normalization $U$ **must** appear.
> 2. **Forgetting that the periodogram is inconsistent:** Although asymptotically unbiased, the variance does **not** vanish with $N$ — explicitly state $\text{Var}\{P_x\} = P_{xx}^2(f)$.
> 3. **Mixing up AIC and MDL:** AIC uses $2p$; MDL uses $p \ln N$. AIC over-fits; MDL is consistent.
> 4. **Yule–Walker derivation errors:** Forgetting that the cross-correlation $E\{w(n) x(n-m)\} = 0$ for $m \ge 1$ — this is the **causality condition** that makes the system Toeplitz.
> 5. **Stating "parametric = AR"** — parametric includes AR, MA, and ARMA. Always mention the general form first, then specialize.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention $U$ in the modified periodogram formula** — this normalization ensures the modified periodogram is asymptotically unbiased.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Periodogram** is the simplest PSD estimator: $\frac{1}{N}\vert X_N(f) \vert^2$. It is **asymptotically unbiased** but **inconsistent** (variance $\propto P_{xx}^2$, independent of $N$).
- **Modified Periodogram** applies a data window $w(n)$ before the DTFT. Hann and Blackman windows are the most common choices; the rectangular window should *never* be used in practice for PSD estimation.
- **Bartlett's method** averages $K$ non-overlapping periodograms to reduce variance by factor $K$.
- **Welch's method** is the **industry standard** for non-parametric PSD estimation: windowed, overlapping segments averaged.
- **Parametric methods** model the signal as the output of an LTI filter driven by white noise. The three models are **AR (all-pole), MA (all-zero), ARMA (pole-zero)**.
- **AR models** are dominant in **biosignal analysis** because biomedical spectra are usually peaky (e.g., EEG alpha, ECG QRS, vocal tract formants).
- **Yule–Walker equations** link AR coefficients to the autocorrelation. The matrix is **Toeplitz** and is solved via the **Levinson–Durbin algorithm** in $O(p^2)$ time.
- **Reflection coefficient** $k_m = a_m^{(m)}$ is the **PARCOR** coefficient; must lie in $(-1, +1)$ for stability.
- **AR PSD formula:** $P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2$.
- **Model order selection:** **AIC** ($2p$) is biased upward; **MDL** ($p \ln N$) is consistent. Final Prediction Error (FPE) is Akaike's earlier alternative.
- **Resolution advantage:** Parametric AR methods can resolve frequencies **closer than the DFT bin spacing** (super-resolution) — useful for short biomedical records.
- **Biomedical relevance:** Welch's method for routine PSD display, AR modelling for feature extraction in EEG/ECG/EMG/HRV, and LPC in speech and cochlear-implant signal processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARMA Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARMA Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA)** model is a cornerstone parametric linear time-series modeling framework that fuses the predictive memory of an **Autoregressive (AR)** component with the smoothing behavior of a **Moving Average (MA)** component. In the context of biosignal processing, the ARMA(p, q) model expresses a discrete-time biosignal sample $x[n]$ as a weighted linear combination of its $p$ past samples plus a weighted linear combination of $q$ past white-noise innovations $\varepsilon[n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **ARMA(p, q)** model is a stochastic difference equation of the form:
> $$x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, x[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, \varepsilon[n-k]$$
> where $p$ is the order of the AR part, $q$ is the order of the MA part, $\phi_k$ are the AR coefficients, $\theta_k$ are the MA coefficients, and $\varepsilon[n]$ is zero-mean white Gaussian noise with variance $\sigma^2$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are predicting **tomorrow's heart rate** of a patient in an ICU:

- The **AR (Autoregressive) part** says: *"Today's heart rate depends heavily on yesterday's and the day-before-yesterday's heart rate"* — just like how your mood today is influenced by your mood yesterday. Past values **directly** shape the present.
- The **MA (Moving Average) part** says: *"But also account for the unexpected shocks from the past two days"* — like an unexpected fever spike, a sudden stress event, or a medication dose. These are **random disturbances** whose effects linger briefly.

Combining both, the ARMA model says: *"Predict tomorrow's heart rate using a memory of past heart rates (AR), but also correct for lingering effects of past random shocks (MA)."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Biosignal Mapping:**
> - **AR component** → captures the inherent quasi-periodic structure of ECG and EEG signals.
> - **MA component** → captures transient noise bursts, muscle artifacts, and motion disturbances.
> - **ARMA combined** → provides a compact, parsimonious parametric model for **spectral estimation** of biosignals.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology and Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Metric / Value |
|------|--------|-------------------------|
| Model Order (AR part) | $p$ | Typically **2 – 12** for EEG/ECG |
| Model Order (MA part) | $q$ | Typically **2 – 6** for biosignals |
| Innovation Variance | $\sigma^2$ | Estimated from residuals |
| Akaike Information Criterion | $AIC$ | Model selection metric |
| Minimum Description Length | $MDL$ | Alternative selection metric |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The total number of parameters in an ARMA(p, q) model is **$(p + q + 1)$** — a key figure for computational efficiency questions in exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars: AR, MA, and ARMA

### 2.1.1 Autoregressive (AR) Model of Order $p$ — "The Memory Model"

The current sample is a **linear regression on its own past**:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, x[n-k] + \varepsilon[n]$$

- **How it works:** Predict $x[n]$ using a weighted sum of previous $p$ samples, plus a fresh shock $\varepsilon[n]$.
- **Why it works:** Many biosignals (EEG, ECG envelopes) exhibit strong temporal correlation — the next sample is *physically tied* to the previous ones.
- **Pole-Zero Nature:** **All-pole** system. Stability requires all roots of the characteristic polynomial to lie **inside the unit circle**.

### 2.1.2 Moving Average (MA) Model of Order $q$ — "The Shock-Response Model"

The current sample is a **linear combination of past white-noise shocks**:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, \varepsilon[n-k]$$

- **How it works:** Each random shock propagates forward for $q$ time-steps with diminishing weights.
- **Why it works:** Captures short-lived transients — exactly what we see in EMG bursts, motion artifacts, and QRS complexes in ECG.
- **Pole-Zero Nature:** **All-zero** system. Always stable because it has only finite impulse response (FIR) behavior.

### 2.1.3 ARMA(p, q) Model — "The Unified Framework"

$$x[n] = \underbrace{\sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, x[n-k]}_{\text{AR part (p poles)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, \varepsilon[n-k]}_{\text{MA part (q zeros)}} + \varepsilon[n]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backshift Operator Form (Operator Notation):**
> $$\phi(B) \, x[n] = \theta(B) \, \varepsilon[n]$$
> where $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 - \ldots - \phi_p B^p$ and $\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1 B + \theta_2 B^2 + \ldots + \theta_q B^q$.

## 2.2 Stability, Causality, and Invertibility

| Property | AR Part | MA Part |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Causality | Always causal | Always causal |
| Stability | Requires poles inside unit circle | Always stable (FIR) |
| Invertibility | Always invertible (if stable) | Requires zeros inside unit circle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In KTU exam problems, the most commonly asked question is to **check stability** by computing the roots of $\phi(B)$ and verifying $\vert \text{root} \vert < 1$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of ARMA questions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Use Case |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p}\phi_k x[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{q}\theta_k \varepsilon[n-k]$ | ARMA(p, q) difference equation | Model definition |
| 2 | $\phi(B) x[n] = \theta(B) \varepsilon[n]$ | Operator form | Z-domain analysis |
| 3 | $H(z) = \dfrac{\theta(z)}{\phi(z)} = \dfrac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q}\theta_k z^{-k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p}\phi_k z^{-k}}$ | Transfer function | Pole-zero plot |
| 4 | $R_{xx}[m] = \sum_{k=1}^{p}\phi_k R_{xx}[m-k] + \sigma^2 \psi_m$ | Yule-Walker for ARMA | ACF computation |
| 5 | $P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 \dfrac{\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q}\theta_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2}{\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p}\phi_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2}$ | ARMA Power Spectral Density | Spectral estimation |
| 6 | $AIC(p,q) = N \ln(\sigma^2) + 2(p+q+1)$ | Akaike Information Criterion | Model order selection |
| 7 | $MDL(p,q) = N \ln(\sigma^2) + (p+q+1) \ln N$ | Minimum Description Length | Model order selection |
| 8 | $\sum_{k=0}^{p}\phi_k R_{xx}(k-m) = -\theta_{-m} \sigma^2$ | Extended Yule-Walker (with $\theta_0 = 1$) | Joint ARMA estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** Absolute value brackets are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. In handwritten exams, use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Biosignal | Why ARMA? |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Fetal ECG extraction | FECG | Suppresses maternal ECG + noise |
| Sleep stage classification | EEG | Captures rhythmic oscillatory patterns |
| Heart rate variability | HRV series | Models slow/fast autonomic dynamics |
| Muscle fatigue detection | EMG | Models decay of burst energy |
| Seizure prediction | EEG | Captures pre-ictal spectral shifts |
| Evoked potential estimation | ERP | Averages out stochastic background |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The module specifically emphasizes ARMA modeling for **parametric spectral estimation** of biosignals — a high-weightage topic in Part B 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ARMA Power Spectral Density (PSD) Formula

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Scheme. The examiner will test this in Part B.

### Step 1: Start with the ARMA Difference Equation

We begin with the standard ARMA(p, q) form:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, x[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, \varepsilon[n-k]$$

**Reasoning:** This is the time-domain definition. Our goal is to find the PSD of $x[n]$.

### Step 2: Apply the Z-Transform to Both Sides

Taking the bilateral Z-transform of both sides (assuming initial rest):

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, z^{-k} X(z) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, z^{-k} E(z)$$

**Reasoning:** The Z-transform converts the time-domain shift $x[n-k]$ into $z^{-k} X(z)$, which is the standard time-shift property.

### Step 3: Collect $X(z)$ on the Left-Hand Side

Move all AR terms to the LHS:

$$X(z) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, z^{-k} X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, z^{-k} E(z)$$

Factor out $X(z)$:

$$X(z) \left[ 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, z^{-k} \right] = E(z) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, z^{-k} \right]$$

**Reasoning:** Algebraic rearrangement so that we can isolate the ratio $X(z)/E(z) = H(z)$.

### Step 4: Derive the Transfer Function $H(z)$

Solving for $X(z)/E(z)$:

$$H(z) = \frac{X(z)}{E(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \, z^{-k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, z^{-k}}$$

Writing it more compactly with $\theta_0 = 1$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k \, z^{-k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, z^{-k}}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the rational transfer function of the ARMA model — it has $p$ poles (from the AR denominator) and $q$ zeros (from the MA numerator).

### Step 5: Substitute $z = e^{j2\pi f}$ for the Unit Circle

To compute the PSD, evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle:

$$H(e^{j2\pi f}) = \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k}}$$

**Reasoning:** The frequency response of a discrete-time system is obtained by evaluating the transfer function on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega} = e^{j2\pi f}$ (where $f$ is normalized frequency).

### Step 6: Apply the PSD Formula $P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 \vert H(e^{j2\pi f}) \vert^2$

Since $\varepsilon[n]$ is white noise with variance $\sigma^2$, the output PSD is:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 \, \vert H(e^{j2\pi f}) \vert^2$$

**Reasoning:** The PSD of a filtered white-noise process equals the input PSD ($\sigma^2$) multiplied by the squared magnitude of the frequency response. This is a fundamental theorem of linear systems.

### Step 7: Final Closed-Form ARMA PSD

Combining Steps 5 and 6:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{xx}(f) &= \sigma^2 \left\vert \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k}} \right\vert^2 \\[6pt]
&= \sigma^2 \, \frac{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}{\left\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Since $\vert A/B \vert^2 = \vert A \vert^2 / \vert B \vert^2$, we can split the magnitude into a numerator (zeros/MA part) and a denominator (poles/AR part). The poles create spectral peaks; the zeros create spectral nulls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Derivation.** This is the master formula — memorize and understand it for KTU ESE 2024.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Yule-Walker Equations for AR Part

For an AR(p) process, the autocorrelation function $R_{xx}[m]$ satisfies a recurrence.

### Step 1: Multiply Both Sides by $x[n-m]$ and Take Expectation

Starting from the AR equation $x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k x[n-k] + \varepsilon[n]$, multiply by $x[n-m]$:

$$x[n] x[n-m] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k x[n-k] x[n-m] + \varepsilon[n] x[n-m]$$

**Reasoning:** We want to derive a relationship between the autocorrelation $R_{xx}[m]$ and the AR coefficients $\phi_k$.

### Step 2: Take the Expectation $E[\cdot]$

$$E[x[n] x[n-m]] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k E[x[n-k] x[n-m]] + E[\varepsilon[n] x[n-m]]$$

**Reasoning:** Expectation is linear, so it distributes over the sum.

### Step 3: Apply Stationarity and Whiteness

By wide-sense stationarity: $E[x[n] x[n-m]] = R_{xx}[m]$.
Since $\varepsilon[n]$ is white noise uncorrelated with past $x$ samples: $E[\varepsilon[n] x[n-m]] = 0$ for $m \geq 1$.

$$R_{xx}[m] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k R_{xx}[m-k], \quad m \geq 1$$

**Reasoning:** For $m \geq 1$, the noise term vanishes because $\varepsilon[n]$ is independent of all past samples.

### Step 4: Yule-Walker System in Matrix Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-1) \\
R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R_{xx}(p-1) & R_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & R_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \\ \vdots \\ \phi_p \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} R_{xx}(1) \\ R_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ R_{xx}(p) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** This Toeplitz system of $p$ equations in $p$ unknowns can be efficiently solved using the **Levinson-Durbin recursion** in $O(p^2)$ time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Yule-Walker equations form a **Toeplitz matrix** (constant along diagonals) — this property is often asked in viva and short questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Summary

| Feature | AR(p) | MA(q) | ARMA(p, q) |
|---------|-------|-------|------------|
| Parameters | $p$ coefficients | $q$ coefficients | $p + q$ coefficients |
| Pole-Zero | $p$ poles, no zeros | No poles, $q$ zeros | $p$ poles, $q$ zeros |
| System Type | All-pole (IIR) | All-zero (FIR) | Pole-zero (IIR) |
| Stability condition | Roots of $\phi(B)$ inside unit circle | Always stable | Roots of $\phi(B)$ inside unit circle |
| ACF behavior | Infinite, geometric decay | Finite ($q$ lags) | Infinite, damped sinusoidal |
| PACF behavior | Cuts off after lag $p$ | Tails off | Tails off |
| Parameter estimation | Yule-Walker (linear) | Nonlinear | Nonlinear (suboptimal methods) |
| Spectral shape | Sharp peaks | Broad nulls | Combined flexibility |
| Typical use in KTU | ECG rhythm analysis | EMG smoothing | HRV and EEG modeling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARMA Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional signal flow** of an ARMA(p, q) generator. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid syntax.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[White Noise Generator epsilon n]:::source
    A1[Delay z-1]:::delay
    A2[Delay z-2]:::delay
    A3[Delay z-q]:::delay
    B1[AR Path x n-1]:::arpath
    B2[AR Path x n-2]:::arpath
    B3[AR Path x n-p]:::arpath
    C1[AR Weight phi 1]:::weight
    C2[AR Weight phi 2]:::weight
    C3[AR Weight phi p]:::weight
    D1[MA Weight theta 1]:::weight
    D2[MA Weight theta 2]:::weight
    D3[MA Weight theta q]:::weight
    E1[Summing Junction]:::sum
    F1[Output x n]:::output
    G1[Feedback to AR path]:::feedback

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> D3
    A0 --> D1
    A0 --> D2
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> B1
    G1 --> B2
    G1 --> B3

    classDef source fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef delay fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000
    classDef arpath fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
    classDef weight fill:#e3fafc,stroke:#0c8599,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#eebefa,stroke:#7048e8,color:#000
```

### Diagram Interpretation

| Block | Role in ARMA Model |
|-------|---------------------|
| **White Noise Generator** | Provides innovation input $\varepsilon[n]$ with variance $\sigma^2$ |
| **Delay Chain $z^{-1}, z^{-2}, \ldots, z^{-q}$** | Generates $\varepsilon[n-1], \varepsilon[n-2], \ldots, \varepsilon[n-q]$ for the MA part |
| **MA Weights $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_q$** | Scale the delayed noise terms |
| **AR Path $x[n-1], \ldots, x[n-p]$** | Tapped-delay line of the past output |
| **AR Weights $\phi_1, \ldots, \phi_p$** | Scale the delayed output samples |
| **Summing Junction** | Adds all weighted contributions to form $x[n]$ |
| **Output $x[n]$** | The modeled biosignal sample |
| **Feedback Loop** | Feeds the output back into the AR delay line — this is what makes the model "autoregressive" |

## 4.2 Pole-Zero Plot for a Typical ARMA(2, 2) Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ZC[Complex z-plane Unit Circle]:::plane
    P1[Pole at 0.8 exp j pi 8]:::pole
    P2[Pole at 0.8 exp -j pi 8]:::pole
    Z1[Zero at 0.6 exp j pi 3]:::zero
    Z2[Zero at 0.6 exp -j pi 3]:::zero
    UC[Unit Circle boundary]:::circle

    ZC --- P1
    ZC --- P2
    ZC --- Z1
    ZC --- Z2
    ZC --- UC

    classDef plane fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#495057,color:#000
    classDef pole fill:#ffa8a8,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
    classDef zero fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef circle fill:none,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pole-Zero Interpretation:**
> - **Poles** (red) → determine **resonant frequencies** and decay rates of spectral peaks.
> - **Zeros** (green) → determine **spectral nulls** (frequencies where the PSD vanishes).
> - **Both poles inside the unit circle** → system is **stable** and **causal**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the ARMA(p, q) Model
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **ARMA(p, q) model** is a parametric linear stochastic model for a stationary time series $x[n]$ defined as:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k x[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \varepsilon[n-k]$$

where $p$ is the **autoregressive order**, $q$ is the **moving average order**, $\phi_k$ are AR coefficients, $\theta_k$ are MA coefficients, and $\varepsilon[n]$ is **white Gaussian noise** with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2$. The AR part models memory of past signal values (poles), and the MA part models the influence of past random shocks (zeros). It is widely used for **parametric spectral estimation of biosignals** such as ECG, EEG, and HRV.

### Question 2: Distinguish Between AR, MA, and ARMA Models
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | AR(p) | MA(q) | ARMA(p, q) |
|---------|-------|-------|------------|
| Equation | $x[n] = \sum \phi_k x[n-k] + \varepsilon[n]$ | $x[n] = \sum \theta_k \varepsilon[n-k]$ | Combination of both |
| System type | All-pole (IIR) | All-zero (FIR) | Pole-zero (IIR) |
| Spectral shape | Sharp peaks | Broad nulls | Combined peaks and nulls |
| Estimation | Linear (Yule-Walker) | Nonlinear | Nonlinear |
| Parameters | $p$ | $q$ | $p + q + 1$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of an ARMA(p, q) process. Explain the role of poles and zeros in shaping the spectrum. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A biosignal $x[n]$ is modeled by the ARMA(2, 1) process:
$$x[n] = 0.5 x[n-1] - 0.3 x[n-2] + \varepsilon[n] + 0.8 \varepsilon[n-1]$$
where $\varepsilon[n]$ is white noise with $\sigma^2 = 1$. **(7 Marks)**
- (i) Write the transfer function $H(z)$.
- (ii) Find the poles and zeros.
- (iii) Check stability.
- (iv) Plot the PSD expression.

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of ARMA PSD (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Start from the ARMA difference equation:
$$x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k x[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k \varepsilon[n-k]$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Apply Z-transform:
$$X(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k z^{-k} X(z) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} \theta_k z^{-k} E(z)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Derive the transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{X(z)}{E(z)} = \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k z^{-k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k z^{-k}}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Evaluate on the unit circle $z = e^{j2\pi f}$:
$$H(e^{j2\pi f}) = \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k e^{-j2\pi f k}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k e^{-j2\pi f k}}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Apply the input-output PSD relationship:
$$P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 \vert H(e^{j2\pi f}) \vert^2$$

**Step 6 (2 Marks):** Final expression and role of poles/zeros:
$$P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 \frac{\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} \theta_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2}{\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2}$$

- **Poles** (denominator) → create **spectral peaks** at resonance frequencies. Their proximity to the unit circle determines peak sharpness.
- **Zeros** (numerator) → create **spectral nulls**. Their angular position determines the frequency of the nulls.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$$\phi_1 = 0.5, \quad \phi_2 = -0.3, \quad \theta_1 = 0.8, \quad \sigma^2 = 1$$

**(i) Transfer Function $H(z)$ (2 Marks):**
$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2}}$$

**(ii) Poles and Zeros (2 Marks):**
- **Zeros:** Solve $1 + 0.8 z^{-1} = 0 \Rightarrow z = -0.8$
- **Poles:** Solve $1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2} = 0$. Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 - 0.5 z + 0.3 = 0$
$$z = \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 - 1.2}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \pm j\sqrt{0.95}}{2} = 0.25 \pm j \cdot 0.4873$$
- Magnitude: $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.2375} = \sqrt{0.3} \approx 0.5477$

**(iii) Stability Check (2 Marks):**
Both poles have magnitude $0.5477 < 1$, so **they lie inside the unit circle**. The system is **stable** and causal.

**(iv) PSD Expression (1 Mark):**
$$P_{xx}(f) = \frac{\vert 1 + 0.8 e^{-j2\pi f} \vert^2}{\vert 1 - 0.5 e^{-j2\pi f} + 0.3 e^{-j4\pi f} \vert^2}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the Yule-Walker equations for AR models and their use in biosignal parameter estimation. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** For an AR(2) model $x[n] = 0.75 x[n-1] - 0.5 x[n-2] + \varepsilon[n]$: **(7 Marks)**
- (i) Write the Yule-Walker equations.
- (ii) Solve for $\phi_1, \phi_2$ in terms of $R_{xx}(0), R_{xx}(1), R_{xx}(2)$.
- (iii) Show the system is stable.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Yule-Walker Equations (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):** Yule-Walker equations are a set of $p$ linear equations that relate the AR coefficients $\phi_k$ to the autocorrelation function $R_{xx}(m)$ of the process.

**Derivation Sketch (3 Marks):** Starting from the AR equation and multiplying by $x[n-m]$ and taking expectation, we get:

$$R_{xx}(m) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} \phi_k R_{xx}(m-k) \quad \text{for } m \geq 1$$

**Matrix Form (2 Marks):** The system in matrix form is:
$$
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) \\
R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} R_{xx}(1) \\ R_{xx}(2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Use in Biosignal Estimation:** Yule-Walker enables efficient **linear estimation** of AR parameters from autocorrelation estimates computed from ECG, EEG, or HRV recordings.

#### Part (b) — AR(2) Problem (7 Marks)

**(i) Yule-Walker Equations (3 Marks):**
For AR(2): $R_{xx}(m) = \phi_1 R_{xx}(m-1) + \phi_2 R_{xx}(m-2)$ for $m \geq 1$.

- For $m = 1$: $R_{xx}(1) = \phi_1 R_{xx}(0) + \phi_2 R_{xx}(-1) = \phi_1 R_{xx}(0) + \phi_2 R_{xx}(1)$
- For $m = 2$: $R_{xx}(2) = \phi_1 R_{xx}(1) + \phi_2 R_{xx}(0)$

**(ii) Solving for $\phi_1, \phi_2$ (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_1 &= \frac{R_{xx}(1)[R_{xx}(0) - R_{xx}(2)]}{R_{xx}(0)^2 - R_{xx}(1)^2} \\
\phi_2 &= \frac{R_{xx}(0) R_{xx}(2) - R_{xx}(1)^2}{R_{xx}(0)^2 - R_{xx}(1)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Stability Check (2 Marks):**
Characteristic polynomial: $\phi(z) = 1 - 0.75 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2} = 0$.
Multiply by $z^2$: $z^2 - 0.75 z + 0.5 = 0$
$$z = \frac{0.75 \pm \sqrt{0.5625 - 2.0}}{2} = \frac{0.75 \pm j\sqrt{1.4375}}{2} = 0.375 \pm j \cdot 0.5995$$
Magnitude: $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.1406 + 0.3594} = \sqrt{0.5} \approx 0.7071 < 1$. **Stable.**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in ARMA Exam Answers — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $\theta_0 = 1$** in the numerator of $H(z)$ — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Conjugating $R_{xx}(-m)$ to $R_{xx}(m)$** incorrectly during Yule-Walker derivation — costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not verifying the stability condition** $\vert \text{pole} \vert < 1$ — costs **1 mark** in numerical problems.
> 4. **Confusing ACF and PACF** behavior — ARMA has both tailing off (no cutoff) — costs **1 mark** in conceptual questions.
> 5. **Writing PSD with vertical bar pipes** (e.g., $|H(f)|^2$) in tables — this breaks markdown in some renderers. Always use $\vert H(f) \vert^2$ in formatted text.
> 6. **Skipping the Z-transform step** in PSD derivation — examiners require this intermediate step — costs **2 marks**.
> 7. **Forgetting units / normalized frequency** ($f \in [0, 0.5]$) in PSD formulas — minor but adds up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- **Definition:** ARMA(p, q) = AR(p) + MA(q). Equation: $x[n] = \sum \phi_k x[n-k] + \sum \theta_k \varepsilon[n-k]$.
- **Special Cases:** ARMA(p, 0) = AR(p); ARMA(0, q) = MA(q); ARMA(0, 0) = White noise.
- **Transfer Function:** $H(z) = \theta(z) / \phi(z)$ — $p$ poles in denominator, $q$ zeros in numerator.
- **Stability Rule:** All poles of $H(z)$ must satisfy $\vert z_{\text{pole}} \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle).
- **Invertibility Rule:** All zeros of $H(z)$ must satisfy $\vert z_{\text{zero}} \vert < 1$.
- **PSD Master Formula:** $P_{xx}(f) = \sigma^2 \vert 1 + \sum \theta_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2 \, / \, \vert 1 - \sum \phi_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2$.
- **Yule-Walker:** Toeplitz matrix relating $\phi_k$ to $R_{xx}(m)$ — solve via Levinson-Durbin.
- **Model Selection:** Use AIC or MDL; ARMA is preferred over pure AR for parsimony.
- **Total Parameters:** ARMA(p, q) needs $(p + q + 1)$ parameters to be estimated.
- **Biosignal Applications:** ECG rhythm modeling, EEG spectral peaks, HRV dynamics, EMG bursts.
- **Comparison Memory Hook:** "**P**oles = **P**eaks; **Z**eros = nu**Z**eros (nulls)".
- **Stationarity Requirement:** The input biosignal must be **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** — preprocess with detrending if needed.
- **Whiteness Assumption:** $\varepsilon[n] \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$ — i.i.d. zero-mean Gaussian.
- **Operator Form:** $\phi(B) x[n] = \theta(B) \varepsilon[n]$ — useful for compact notation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Based PSD Estimation. Spectrum Analysis of Bio Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Based PSD Estimation. Spectrum Analysis of Bio Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of PSD Estimation

**Power Spectral Density (PSD)** estimation is the process of estimating the distribution of signal power over frequency from a finite, often noisy, observation of a (bio)signal. The PSD, denoted $P_x(f)$ or $S_x(f)$, characterizes a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) random process $x(n)$ in the frequency domain and is mathematically defined as the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of its autocorrelation function $r_{xx}(\tau)$, expressed via the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (KTU Syllabus Anchor):**
> The autocorrelation sequence and the power spectral density form a Fourier-transform pair.
> $$P_x(f) = \sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **PSD vs. Fourier Magnitude — A Vital Distinction:**
> * The **magnitude spectrum** $\vert X(f) \vert$ shows *amplitude* at each frequency.
> * The **PSD** $P_x(f)$ shows *average power* per unit bandwidth (in **W/Hz** or **dB/Hz**).
> * For bio-signals (which are stochastic, not deterministic), PSD is the only meaningful frequency descriptor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a **rainforest river**. You cannot see individual raindrops, but you can hear the *roar* of the river. The roar is a mixture of many frequencies — low rumbles and high splashes. The **PSD is the "audio fingerprint" of the river** — it tells you how much acoustic power lies in the bass (low frequencies) vs. the hiss (high frequencies). 

In the same way, an **ECG signal** is a stochastic (random-like) mixture of cardiac rhythms. Its PSD tells us:
* How much power sits around the heart-rate fundamental (~1.2 Hz)?
* How much power lies in respiratory modulation (~0.25 Hz)?

Just as a **musician uses an equalizer** to boost or cut frequency bands, a *biomedical engineer uses PSD* to isolate clinically relevant bands (e.g., QRS, alpha rhythm).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Sampling rate for EEG (clinical) | **256 Hz – 512 Hz** |
| Sampling rate for ECG (clinical) | **250 Hz – 1000 Hz** |
| Heart rate frequency range | **0.7 Hz – 3.0 Hz** |
| EEG band (delta + theta + alpha + beta + gamma) | **0.5 Hz – 60 Hz** |
| Standard reference frequency for ECG analysis | **50 Hz / 60 Hz** (line noise) |
| Welch default window | **Hann (Hanning)** |
| Welch default segment overlap | **50 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus Mapping:**
> This module explicitly groups two ideas: (1) *Parameter-based* (parametric) PSD estimation, and (2) *Spectrum analysis* of bio-signals such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG. The notes below follow the same KTU chapter order.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of PSD Estimation Methods

PSD estimators fall into two broad families, both of which are **high-yield KTU exam topics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Family 1 — Non-Parametric (Direct/Indirect) Methods**
> Make no assumption about the underlying data-generating model. Operate directly on the data.
> Examples: *Periodogram, Bartlett, Welch, Blackman–Tukey*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Family 2 — Parametric (Model-Based) Methods**
> Assume the signal is the output of a known rational-system model (AR / MA / ARMA) driven by white noise. Estimate model parameters, then derive the PSD analytically.
> Examples: *Yule–Walker AR, Burg AR, Covariance / Modified Covariance, MA & ARMA via Durbin*.

### 2.2 Non-Parametric Method 1 — The Periodogram (Schuster, 1898)

The simplest, direct estimator. For an $N$-point data block $x(n)$ with DTFT $X(f)$:

**Operational Steps:**
1. Acquire a finite record $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$.
2. Compute the FFT: $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$.
3. Square the magnitude and normalize by $N$.

**Why?** Because of Parseval's theorem, averaging $\vert X(f) \vert^2$ over the ensemble gives the true power. For a single record, the periodogram is the *only* unbiased estimator — but its **variance is large and does not decrease as $N$ grows**.

### 2.3 Non-Parametric Method 2 — Bartlett's Method

To reduce variance, Bartlett proposed **averaging periodograms of disjoint segments**.

* Split $x[n]$ into $K$ non-overlapping segments of length $L = N/K$.
* Compute periodogram $P_i(f)$ for each segment.
* Average: $P_B(f) = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} P_i(f)$.

**Variance reduction factor: $K$**, but resolution worsens because each segment is shorter.

### 2.4 Non-Parametric Method 3 — Welch's Method (1970) — *Most Used in Practice*

Welch combined two ideas:
* **Windowing** (to reduce spectral leakage).
* **Overlapping segments** (to keep total power constant while allowing averaging).

For a window $w[n]$ of length $L$ with energy $U = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2[n]$, each modified periodogram is:

$$\tilde{P}_i^{(L)}(f) = \frac{1}{U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x[n + iD]\, w[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

where $D$ is the segment shift. The final Welch PSD:

$$P_W(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \tilde{P}_i^{(L)}(f)$$

**Why Welch works so well on bio-signals:** 50 % overlap recovers the lost variance reduction due to windowing, while Hann tapering suppresses the strong 50/60 Hz mains leakage that pollutes ECG/EEG recordings.

### 2.5 Non-Parametric Method 4 — Blackman–Tukey (Indirect / Correlogram)

Computes the autocorrelation first, applies a lag-window $w[\tau]$ to reduce estimator variance, then Fourier-transforms:

$$P_{BT}(f) = \sum_{\tau=-(M-1)}^{M-1} \hat{r}_{xx}[\tau]\, w[\tau]\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}$$

where the maximum lag $M \le N/10$ to control noise.

### 2.6 Parametric Method 1 — Autoregressive (AR) Model — *Yule–Walker*

A $p$-th order AR process:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + e[n], \quad e[n] \sim WN(0, \sigma_e^2)$$

Its PSD is:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right|^2}$$

The parameters $\mathbf{a} = [a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p]$ and $\sigma_e^2$ are found by solving the **Yule–Walker normal equations**:

$$\mathbf{R}\, \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$$

where $r_{xx}[k]$ is the biased autocorrelation estimate, and the **Levinson–Durbin recursion** solves the Toeplitz system in $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ time.

### 2.7 Parametric Method 2 — Burg's Method (Maximum-Entropy)

Burg minimises the **forward and backward prediction errors** under the constraint that the AR coefficients satisfy the Levinson recursion, producing:
* High spectral resolution on short data records (vital for HRV, EEG epochs).
* A numerically stable estimate that always yields a **minimum-phase** model (stable poles).

The reflection coefficient estimate at stage $m$ is:

$$k_m = \frac{-2 \sum_{n=m}^{N-1} f_{m-1}[n]\, b_{m-1}[n-1]}{\sum_{n=m}^{N-1} \left( \vert f_{m-1}[n] \vert^2 + \vert b_{m-1}[n-1] \vert^2 \right)}$$

where $f_m[n]$ and $b_m[n]$ are forward and backward prediction errors.

### 2.8 Parametric Method 3 — Moving Average (MA) and ARMA

* **MA(q):** $x[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e[n-k]$, giving a PSD with *troughs* (zeros) — useful for notch-like biomedical interference.
* **ARMA(p,q):** combines poles and zeros; PSD has both peaks and troughs.
* Estimated via **Durbin's method** (a 3-stage decomposition: long-AR → MA, then simultaneous refinement).

### 2.9 Model Order Selection (KTU-Favourite)

Choosing $p$ in AR models is non-trivial. Common criteria:

* **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):** $AIC(p) = N \ln \sigma_e^2 + 2p$.
* **Minimum Description Length (MDL):** $MDL(p) = N \ln \sigma_e^2 + p \ln N$.

> Both penalise complexity, but MDL is consistent (asymptotically correct), while AIC may over-fit.

### 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Method | Key Equation | Variance Behaviour | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Periodogram | $P(f) = \dfrac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$ | Constant in $N$ (poor) | Best (length $N$) |
| 2 | Bartlett | $P_B(f) = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_i P_i(f)$ | $\propto 1/K$ | $\propto 1/L$ |
| 3 | Welch (Hann, 50% ov) | $P_W(f) = \dfrac{1}{K U} \sum_i \vert W X_i(f) \vert^2$ | $\propto 1/K$ (improved) | $\propto 1/L$ |
| 4 | Blackman–Tukey | $P_{BT}(f) = \sum_{\tau} \hat r[\tau] w[\tau] e^{-j2\pi f \tau}$ | $\propto M/N$ | $\propto 1/M$ |
| 5 | AR (Yule–Walker) | $P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2$ | Lower for fixed $N$ | Higher (smooth) |
| 6 | AR (Burg) | Same $P_{AR}$ with reflection $k_m$ | Lowest among AR | Highest for short records |
| 7 | MA(q) | $P_{MA}(f) = \sigma_e^2 \vert 1 + \sum b_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2$ | Moderate | Troughs (zeros) |
| 8 | ARMA(p,q) | $P(f) = \sigma_e^2 \dfrac{\vert B(f) \vert^2}{\vert A(f) \vert^2}$ | Moderate–low | Peaks + troughs |

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Record length (EEG epoch) | $N$ | **2 s – 30 s** |
| Segment length (Welch) | $L$ | **256 – 1024** samples |
| Overlap | overlap | **50 %** |
| Window | $w[n]$ | **Hann / Hamming** |
| Max lag (BT) | $M$ | **$N/10$** |
| AR model order (EEG) | $p$ | **6 – 20** |
| AR model order (ECG HRV) | $p$ | **8 – 16** |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for absolute values inside markdown tables.

### 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Sleep-stage classification:** Welch PSD of an EEG epoch quantifies the relative power in delta (0.5–4 Hz), alpha (8–13 Hz) etc., enabling automated polysomnography.
* **Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis:** Parametric (AR) PSD on RR-interval tachogram resolves the LF/HF peaks far better than the periodogram, allowing autonomic-nervous-system assessment.
* **Event-related potentials (ERP):** Welch PSD over short windows isolates induced gamma-band activity.
* **Detection of ventricular fibrillation:** Spectral centroid + spectral entropy (features derived from PSD) are used in automated external defibrillators (AEDs).
* **PCG (heart-sound) classification:** PSD concentrates murmur energy in 100–600 Hz, separating innocent from pathological murmurs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Periodogram from the Wiener–Khinchin Theorem

> **Goal:** Show that the periodogram is a *natural* estimator of the autocorrelation's DTFT, and hence of the true PSD.

**Step 1 — Definition of the true autocorrelation for an ergodic process:**

For a WSS, ergodic random process, the time average equals the ensemble average:

$$r_{xx}[\tau] = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x[n]\, x[n+\tau]$$

**Step 2 — Biased sample autocorrelation from a finite record $x[0],\dots,x[N-1]$:**

$$\hat r_{xx}[\tau] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert \tau \vert} x[n]\, x[n+\tau], \quad \tau = -(N-1), \dots, (N-1)$$

The factor $1/N$ (instead of $1/(N-\vert \tau \vert)$) makes $\hat r$ a *biased but PSD-positive* estimator — crucial for KTU exam.

**Step 3 — Insert $\hat r_{xx}[\tau]$ into the Wiener–Khinchin sum:**

$$P_x(f) = \sum_{\tau=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat r_{xx}[\tau] e^{-j2\pi f \tau}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the definition of $\hat r$ and swap summations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_x(f) &= \sum_{\tau} \left( \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n} x[n]\, x[n+\tau] \right) e^{-j2\pi f \tau} \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \sum_{\tau=-(N-1)}^{N-1} x[n+\tau] e^{-j2\pi f \tau} \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{j2\pi f n} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] e^{-j2\pi f m} \quad (\text{with } m = n+\tau) \\
&= \frac{1}{N}\, X^*(f)\, X(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result — The Periodogram:**

$$P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

This is the **fundamental identity** of all non-parametric PSD estimators. Every Bartlett, Welch, and Blackman–Tukey equation descends from it.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bias of the Periodogram

> **Goal:** Prove that the expected periodogram equals the *true* PSD convolved with the Fejér kernel (a bias of order $\mathcal{O}(1/N)$).

**Step 1 — Expected value of the periodogram:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\{\, P_{\text{per}}(f)\,\} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \mathbb{E}\{ x[n] x[m] \}\, e^{-j2\pi f (n-m)} \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} r_{xx}[n-m]\, e^{-j2\pi f (n-m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Change of variable $k = n - m$ and introduce the Bartlett window $w_B$:**

$$= \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left(1 - \frac{\vert k \vert}{N}\right) r_{xx}[k]\, e^{-j2\pi f k}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the triangular window as the Fejér kernel $K_N(f)$:**

$$K_N(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left( \frac{\sin(\pi N f)}{\sin(\pi f)} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Convolve with true PSD:**

$$\mathbb{E}\{\, P_{\text{per}}(f)\,\} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu)\, K_N(f - \nu)\, d\nu$$

**Conclusion:** The periodogram is an **asymptotically unbiased** but **biased for finite $N$** estimator. The bias is the *smearing* (spectral leakage) produced by the implicit rectangular window in the periodogram — this is exactly why Welch *windows the data* before squaring.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Levinson–Durbin Recursion (AR Parameter Solution)

> **Goal:** Obtain the $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ algorithm that the Yule–Walker equations demand.

**Step 1 — Write Yule–Walker in matrix form (Toeplitz system):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
r[0] & r[1] & \cdots & r[p-1] \\
r[1] & r[0] & \cdots & r[p-2] \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r[p-1] & r[p-2] & \cdots & r[0]
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1^{(p)} \\
a_2^{(p)} \\
\vdots \\
a_p^{(p)}
\end{bmatrix}
=
-\begin{bmatrix}
r[1] \\
r[2] \\
\vdots \\
r[p]
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the $m$-th order solution $(a_1^{(m)}, \dots, a_m^{(m)})$ is known, and let the prediction-error variance be $E_m$.**

**Step 3 — Order-update formulas (the heart of Levinson–Durbin):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_m &= -\frac{ \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} r[m-k] }{ E_{m-1} }, \quad a_0^{(m)} = 1 \\
a_k^{(m)} &= a_k^{(m-1)} + k_m\, a_{m-k}^{(m-1)}, \quad 1 \le k \le m-1 \\
a_m^{(m)} &= k_m \\
E_m &= E_{m-1} (1 - k_m^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the $k_m$ (reflection coefficients) stability condition $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ to guarantee an all-pole model with poles *inside* the unit circle.**

**Why this matters for KTU bio-signal work:** Levinson–Durbin is the algorithm embedded in *every* ECG and EEG HRV analyzer. Burg's method uses exactly the same recursion but estimates $k_m$ directly from the data (not from $\hat r$), giving better resolution for short, noisy segments.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mean and Variance of the Welch Estimate (Sketch)

Let $K$ overlapping Welch segments, each producing an estimate $\tilde P_i(f)$ with the same mean $P_{xx}(f)$ and (approximately) uncorrelated samples.

**Step 1 — Mean of Welch PSD:**

$$\mathbb{E}\{\, P_W(f)\,\} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^K \mathbb{E}\{\tilde P_i(f)\} = P_{xx}(f) \circledast W(f)$$

where $W(f) = \vert W(f) \vert^2 / U$ is the normalized window spectrum.

**Step 2 — Variance of Welch PSD (asymptotic, well-behaved windows):**

$$\mathrm{Var}\{\, P_W(f)\,\} \approx \frac{1}{K} P_{xx}^2(f)$$

**Step 3 — Trade-off: Increasing $K$ (shorter segments) reduces variance but worsens resolution (broader $W(f)$).**

**Step 4 — For ECG sampled at 256 Hz, KTU-recommended values:** $L = 256$ samples (1 s windows), 50 % overlap → $K \approx 2N/L - 1$ segments, providing stable HRV spectra in 5-minute recordings.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Spectrum of a Synthetic ECG (Worked Numerical)

Given a simplified ECG model sampled at 200 Hz, $N = 1000$ points, comprised of:
* 60 bpm sinus rhythm (fundamental at 1 Hz),
* 0.25 Hz respiration modulation (HF band).

**Step 1 — Compute the periodogram (Equation of Derivation 1).**
**Step 2 — Apply Welch averaging** ($L = 200$, Hann, 50 % overlap, $K = 9$).
**Step 3 — Estimate peak frequencies** by locating the PSD maxima.

$$f_{\text{peak}_1} = 1.0 \pm 0.05\ \text{Hz}, \quad f_{\text{peak}_2} = 0.25 \pm 0.04\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute LF/HF power ratio** for HRV:

$$\mathrm{LF/HF} = \frac{\int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_W(f)\, df}{\int_{0.15}^{0.40} P_W(f)\, df}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This numerical procedure is the **gold-standard** in HRV bio-signal analysis. KTU expects students to be able to outline these computational steps.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix (KTU-Mapped Concept Map)

| Concept | Periodogram | Bartlett | Welch | Blackman–Tukey | AR (YW) | AR (Burg) | ARMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bias | Smoothed | Smoothed | Smoothed (W) | Window-controlled | Negligible (p large) | Negligible | Lowest |
| Variance | High ($\sim P^2$) | Reduced (1/K) | Reduced (1/K) | Reduced (1/M) | Lower | Lowest | Low |
| Resolution (short $N$) | Poor | Poor | Good | Moderate | Excellent | **Best** | Excellent |
| Suitable for | Teaching | Quick test | General EEG/ECG | Smooth spectra | HRV, EEG | HRV, ERP | PCG |
| KTU exam weight | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define Power Spectral Density. State the Wiener–Khinchin theorem.** *(Module 4 — 3 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
> **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** is a frequency-domain function $P_x(f)$ that describes how the *average power* of a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) random signal is distributed across frequency. Its unit is **W/Hz** (or **dB/Hz** in logarithmic form).
> **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem** states that the PSD and the autocorrelation sequence $r_{xx}[\tau]$ of a WSS process form a **Fourier-transform pair**:
> $$P_x(f) = \sum_{\tau=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}[\tau]\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}, \quad r_{xx}[\tau] = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_x(f)\, e^{j2\pi f \tau}\, df$$
> It is the foundational link between time-domain (autocorrelation) and frequency-domain (PSD) descriptions of random signals.

**Q2. List any two advantages and one disadvantage of the Welch PSD estimator over the simple periodogram.** *(3 marks)* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
> * **Advantage 1 — Reduced variance:** Averaging over $K$ overlapped, windowed segments reduces the variance by approximately a factor of $K$ compared to the periodogram, which has constant (high) variance independent of $N$.
> * **Advantage 2 — Leakage suppression:** Multiplying each segment by a tapered window (e.g., Hann) before the FFT reduces spectral leakage, which is critical when bio-signals contain narrowband interference (50/60 Hz mains).
> * **Disadvantage — Reduced frequency resolution:** Because each segment is shorter (length $L < N$), the main lobe is wider, leading to poorer frequency resolution.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of the **periodogram** from the Wiener–Khinchin theorem and discuss its **bias–variance trade-off** in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **Welch's method** of PSD estimation with a block diagram. Compare it with the Bartlett and Blackman–Tukey methods. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a)

> The periodogram is defined for a finite $N$-point record $x[0], \dots, x[N-1]$ by computing its DTFT $X(f)$ and scaling the squared magnitude by $1/N$.
> **Derivation from Wiener–Khinchin (write the steps from Section 3.1 above):**
> * Start from $P_x(f) = \sum_{\tau} r_{xx}[\tau] e^{-j2\pi f \tau}$.
> * Substitute the biased autocorrelation $\hat r_{xx}[\tau] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n} x[n]x[n+\tau]$.
> * Swap summations, change variable $m = n + \tau$.
> * Recognise the inner sum as $X^*(f)$ and the outer as $X(f)$.
> * Conclude $P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$.
>
> **Bias–Variance Analysis:**
> * **Bias:** $\mathbb{E}\{P_{\text{per}}(f)\} = P_{xx}(f) \circledast K_N(f)$, where $K_N$ is the Fejér kernel of width $\propto 1/N$. As $N \to \infty$, bias $\to 0$ (asymptotically unbiased). For finite $N$, the smearing (leakage) causes bias, especially for sharp peaks (e.g., QRS spectrum, alpha rhythm).
> * **Variance:** $\mathrm{Var}\{P_{\text{per}}(f)\} \approx P_{xx}^2(f)$ — does *not* decrease with $N$. The periodogram is **inconsistent**.
> * **Trade-off:** Increasing $N$ improves resolution (less bias) but does *not* stabilise the estimate. Hence, we need **averaging** (Bartlett, Welch) or **modelling** (AR, Burg).

#### Model Solution (b)

> **Welch's Method — Procedural Steps:**
> 1. Segment $x[n]$ into $K$ overlapping blocks of length $L$, with shift $D$. Typical 50 % overlap.
> 2. Multiply each block $x_i[n]$ by a tapered window $w[n]$ (Hann, Hamming).
> 3. Compute the FFT of each windowed block.
> 4. Compute the modified periodogram $\tilde P_i(f) = \frac{1}{U} \vert X_i(f) \vert^2$ where $U = \sum w^2[n]$.
> 5. Average: $P_W(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_i \tilde P_i(f)$.
>
> **Comparison:**
> * **Vs. Bartlett:** Welch *adds* windowing (lower leakage) and *allows overlap* (more averages for the same record length → further variance reduction). Bartlett uses no overlap and a rectangular window.
> * **Vs. Blackman–Tukey:** Welch operates in the frequency domain directly; Blackman–Tukey first estimates $\hat r[\tau]$, lags it with $w[\tau]$, and then takes the DTFT. Welch is computationally cheaper (FFT-based) and avoids the bias introduced by estimating noisy $\hat r$ at high lags.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> * Many students **omit the $1/U$ normalisation** in Welch — this changes the absolute PSD scale and is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> * Confusing Welch's "$K$ segments" with the periodogram's "$N$ samples": a typical mistake is to claim resolution $\propto 1/N$; it is actually $\propto 1/L$ where $L$ is the *segment length*.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain **parametric (model-based) PSD estimation** using an AR($p$) model. State the Yule–Walker equations and explain the Levinson–Durbin recursion. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the application of PSD estimation in **ECG, EEG, and HRV analysis** with relevant frequency bands and clinical interpretations. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a)

> **AR(p) Model:**
> $x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] + e[n]$, where $e[n]$ is white noise with variance $\sigma_e^2$. The model is an all-pole linear system driven by $e[n]$.
> **PSD of AR(p):**
> $$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$
> **Yule–Walker Equations (write the matrix form from Section 3.3):**
> The autocorrelation $r[k]$ of the process satisfies $r[k] + \sum_{m=1}^{p} a_m r[k-m] = \sigma_e^2 \delta[k]$ for $k \ge 0$.
> **Levinson–Durbin Recursion (write order-update formulas):**
> $k_m = -\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} r[m-k] / E_{m-1}$, $a_m^{(m)} = k_m$, and $E_m = E_{m-1}(1 - k_m^2)$.
> **Burg's refinement:** Estimates each $k_m$ directly from the data using forward/backward prediction errors, avoiding the noisy estimate of $r[\tau]$. Better for short bio-signal records.

#### Model Solution (b)

> **ECG Spectrum Analysis:**
> * Energy concentrated in **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz**; dominant QRS power lies between **10 – 25 Hz**.
> * Welch PSD on RR-interval tachogram yields **HRV spectrum** with two peaks:
>     * **LF (0.04 – 0.15 Hz)** — sympathetic + baroreflex activity.
>     * **HF (0.15 – 0.4 Hz)** — parasympathetic (respiratory sinus arrhythmia).
> * LF/HF ratio is a clinical index of **autonomic balance**.
>
> **EEG Spectrum Analysis:**
> * Five canonical bands:
>     * **Delta (0.5 – 4 Hz)** — deep sleep, anaesthesia.
>     * **Theta (4 – 8 Hz)** — drowsiness, memory.
>     * **Alpha (8 – 13 Hz)** — relaxed wakefulness, eyes-closed.
>     * **Beta (13 – 30 Hz)** — active thinking.
>     * **Gamma (30 – 60 Hz)** — perception, cognition.
> * Welch PSD on 2 – 5 s epochs quantifies *band powers* for sleep staging.
> * AR (Burg) PSD on 1 s epochs detects **epileptic spikes** and seizure onset (sharp spectral peaks).
>
> **PCG Spectrum Analysis:**
> * S1 and S2 sounds: **20 – 200 Hz**.
> * Murmurs: extend up to **100 – 600 Hz** (continuous, machinery-like).
> * PSD separates innocent from pathological murmurs (e.g., aortic stenosis, mitral regurgitation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> * Students often quote **0.5 – 30 Hz** as the "EEG range" — full credit only if the five sub-bands (delta/theta/alpha/beta/gamma) are listed.
> * Many students confuse **LF and HF** of HRV: HF is **respiratory** (0.15 – 0.4 Hz), NOT the cardiac fundamental.
> * The Burg method estimate *requires* verifying $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for stability — forgetting this loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4)**

* **PSD** = DTFT of autocorrelation (Wiener–Khinchin). Unit: **W/Hz** or **dB/Hz**.
* **Periodogram:** $P(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$. Unbiased asymptotically, but variance does **NOT** vanish as $N \to \infty$.
* **Bartlett:** $K$ disjoint segments, average their periodograms. Variance $\downarrow$ by $K$, resolution $\downarrow$ by $1/L$.
* **Welch:** $K$ **overlapped, windowed** segments; variance $\downarrow$, leakage $\downarrow$. Default: **Hann, 50 % overlap**.
* **Blackman–Tukey:** window the autocorrelation before FT; $\mathrm{Var} \propto M/N$, resolution $\propto 1/M$.
* **AR(p) model:** $P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_e^2 / \vert 1 + \sum_k a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \vert^2$.
* **Yule–Walker:** $R \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$ (Toeplitz system).
* **Levinson–Durbin:** $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ solver; reflection coeff $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for stability.
* **Burg:** Direct estimation of $k_m$ from forward/backward errors; best for **short records** (HRV, EEG epochs).
* **AIC / MDL:** $AIC(p) = N \ln \sigma_e^2 + 2p$; $MDL(p) = N \ln \sigma_e^2 + p \ln N$. MDL is consistent.
* **ECG band:** 0.5 – 40 Hz; QRS energy 10 – 25 Hz; HRV LF 0.04 – 0.15 Hz, HF 0.15 – 0.4 Hz.
* **EEG bands:** Delta 0.5–4, Theta 4–8, Alpha 8–13, Beta 13–30, Gamma 30–60 Hz.
* **EMG band:** 20 – 500 Hz; effort-dependent.
* **PCG band:** S1/S2 at 20–200 Hz, murmurs 100–600 Hz.
* **Resolution vs. Variance:** Longer segments → better resolution; more segments → lower variance. Welch balances the two.
* **Clinical use-cases:** HRV (LF/HF), sleep staging (band ratios), seizure detection (AR spectral peaks), murmur detection (PSD shape), AED rhythm analysis (spectral centroid/entropy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectrum Estimation of ECG - Power Spectrum Estimation of Heart Rate Variability Data. Spectrum Estimation of EEG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT613 - BIOSIGNALS & SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spectrum Estimation of ECG - Power Spectrum Estimation of Heart Rate Variability Data. Spectrum Estimation of EEG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spectrum Estimation** is the mathematical procedure used to characterize the frequency-domain distribution of signal power (or energy) of biomedical signals such as the Electrocardiogram (ECG), the derived Heart Rate Variability (HRV) tachogram, and the Electroencephalogram (EEG). In the context of **OEEBT613 – Biosignals & Signal Processing**, spectrum estimation refers to the estimation of the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**, denoted $P_x(f)$ or $P_x(\omega)$, which describes how the power of a biosignal is distributed across frequency.

For each bio-signal:

- **ECG Spectrum Estimation**: Decomposes the ECG into its constituent frequency components (QRS complex, P-wave, T-wave, baseline wander) to identify dominant rhythms and noise interference.
- **HRV Power Spectrum Estimation**: Computes the PSD of the RR-interval (RR tachogram) series to quantify autonomic nervous system (ANS) modulation across very low, low, and high frequency bands.
- **EEG Spectrum Estimation**: Estimates the PSD of multichannel EEG to characterize oscillatory brain rhythms (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) and their clinical correlates.

The two principal estimation paradigms are:
1. **Non-parametric methods** — Periodogram, Welch's averaged periodogram, Blackman–Tukey.
2. **Parametric methods** — Autoregressive (AR), Moving Average (MA), and Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Spectrum estimation is treated as an essential preprocessing/analysis tool for non-stationary and quasi-stationary biosignals. Students must know both classical (FFT-based) and modern (model-based) approaches and be able to justify the choice of method for a given bio-signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are listening to an **orchestra playing a single sustained chord**. Your ears decompose that chord into individual instruments (violins, cellos, flutes). The **spectrum estimator acts as your "mathematical ear"** — it separates a complex biomedical waveform (e.g., the EEG) into a "score sheet" of frequency components, telling you how much "loudness" (power) each frequency contributes.

**Real-world analogy for ECG spectrum**: Think of an ECG trace as a piece of music with three "instruments" — the P-wave (soft note, ~10 Hz), the QRS complex (loud cymbal crash, ~10–40 Hz with most energy ~10–25 Hz), and the T-wave (gentle hum, ~1–7 Hz). Spectrum estimation tells you exactly how loud each instrument plays.

**Real-world analogy for HRV**: Consider your heartbeat intervals as a drummer tapping at slightly irregular intervals. A **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** on these intervals produces a "tempo map" showing which rhythms dominate — slow drifts (VLF), sympathetic modulations (LF), or parasympathetic breathing rhythms (HF).

**Real-world analogy for EEG**: Visualize brain activity as the **ocean waves** crashing on a shore. By placing a frequency analyzer at the shore, you identify whether the waves are slow rolling swells (delta, deep sleep), medium surf (alpha, relaxed wakefulness), or quick choppy ripples (beta, active thinking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember (Bold):**
> - **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** — typically **500 Hz** for ECG, **256–512 Hz** for EEG.
> - **Frequency Resolution ($\Delta f = f_s / N$)** — depends on window length $N$.
> - **Nyquist Frequency ($f_N = f_s / 2$)** — maximum estimable frequency.
> - **Total Power (TP)** — variance of the RR series.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Spectrum Estimation

The Power Spectral Density of a discrete-time biosignal $x[n]$ is formally defined as the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of its autocorrelation sequence $r_{xx}[k]$:

$$P_x(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}[k]\, e^{-j\omega k}$$

For a finite-length biosignal of length $N$, direct computation of the DTFT is impossible. We therefore resort to **estimators** $\hat{P}_x(\omega)$.

#### 2.1.1 Why ECG, HRV, and EEG Require Special Handling

| Biosignal | Stationarity | Sampling Rate | Key Frequencies | Preferred Method |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| ECG | Quasi-stationary (beat-to-beat) | 250–1000 Hz | 0.5–40 Hz | Welch / AR |
| HRV (RR series) | Non-stationary | 1–4 Hz resampled | 0.0001–0.5 Hz | Welch / Lomb–Scargle |
| EEG | Highly non-stationary | 128–1024 Hz | 0.5–100 Hz | Welch / Multitaper / AR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HRV data is intrinsically **non-uniformly sampled** (RR intervals are beat-triggered). The standard FFT fails because it requires uniform sampling. The **Lomb–Scargle Periodogram** is the gold-standard remedy.

### 2.2 Non-Parametric Spectrum Estimation Methods

#### 2.2.1 Periodogram (Schuster, 1898)

The simplest estimator. Given $N$ samples $x[0], x[1], \dots, x[N-1]$:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega)\vert^2$$

**Why use it:** Computationally trivial, unbiased in expectation.
**Why avoid it alone:** Variance does not decrease with $N$ — it is **inconsistent**. For HRV with short data, this is a serious problem.

#### 2.2.2 Welch's Averaged Periodogram (1967)

To reduce variance, Welch proposed:
1. Segment $x[n]$ into $K$ overlapping windows of length $L$.
2. Apply a taper window $w[n]$ (Hann, Hamming, Blackman).
3. Compute the periodogram of each segment.
4. Average the periodograms.

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L \cdot U} \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w[n]\, x[n + kD]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1}\vert w[n]\vert^2$ is the window energy and $D$ is the segment shift.

**Trade-off:** Variance drops by factor $K$, but frequency resolution drops to $\Delta f = f_s / L$.

#### 2.2.3 Blackman–Tukey Method

Computes the PSD as the Fourier transform of a windowed autocorrelation estimate:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{BT}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}[k]\, w[k]\, e^{-j\omega k}, \quad M \ll N$$

### 2.3 Parametric Spectrum Estimation Methods

#### 2.3.1 Autoregressive (AR) Model

Models the biosignal as the output of an all-pole filter driven by white noise:

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + e[n], \quad e[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_e^2)$$

The PSD becomes:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_e^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k}\right\vert^2}$$

**Why AR is preferred for ECG/EEG/HRV:** It produces **smooth spectra** even with short data, has superior frequency resolution, and yields **physically meaningful poles** corresponding to dominant rhythms.

#### 2.3.2 Yule–Walker Equations

The AR parameters are found by solving the linear system:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}[0] & r_{xx}[1] & \cdots & r_{xx}[p] \\ r_{xx}[1] & r_{xx}[0] & \cdots & r_{xx}[p-1] \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{xx}[p] & r_{xx}[p-1] & \cdots & r_{xx}[0] \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ a_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_e^2 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Efficiently solved using the **Levinson–Durbin recursion** in $O(p^2)$.

### 2.4 Lomb–Scargle Periodogram (HRV Special Case)

For unevenly sampled RR intervals $t_i$ with values $RR_i$:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{LS}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left\{ \frac{\left[ \sum_i (RR_i - \mu)\, \cos(\omega(t_i - \tau)) \right]^2}{\sum_i \cos^2(\omega(t_i - \tau))} + \frac{\left[ \sum_i (RR_i - \mu)\, \sin(\omega(t_i - \tau)) \right]^2}{\sum_i \sin^2(\omega(t_i - \tau))} \right\}$$

where $\tau = \frac{1}{2\omega}\tan^{-1}\!\left( \frac{\sum_i \sin(2\omega t_i)}{\sum_i \cos(2\omega t_i)} \right)$ is the time-offset that makes the periodogram invariant to time shifts.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|--------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | DTFT-based PSD definition | $P_x(\omega) = \sum_k r_{xx}[k] e^{-j\omega k}$ | Power / Hz | Theoretical definition |
| 2 | Periodogram | $\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \tfrac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega)\vert^2$ | ms²/Hz | ECG / EEG |
| 3 | Welch PSD | $\hat{P}_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \tfrac{1}{KLU}\sum_k \vert X_k(\omega)\vert^2$ | ms²/Hz | Short ECG / EEG |
| 4 | Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = f_s / N$ | **Hz** | All biosignals |
| 5 | Nyquist limit | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | **Hz** | All biosignals |
| 6 | AR model PSD | $\hat{P}_{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \sigma_e^2 \big/ \vert 1 + \sum_k a_k e^{-j\omega k}\vert^2$ | ms²/Hz | ECG / HRV / EEG |
| 7 | Yule–Walker | $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$ | — | AR parameter estimation |
| 8 | Levinson–Durbin | $a_{k,k} = -\tfrac{r_{xx}[k] + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} a_{k-1,j}\, r_{xx}[k-j]}{E_{k-1}}$ | — | Fast AR solver |
| 9 | HRV LF band | **0.04 – 0.15 Hz** | Hz | Sympathetic + baroreflex |
| 10 | HRV HF band | **0.15 – 0.40 Hz** | Hz | Parasympathetic (RSA) |
| 11 | HRV VLF band | **0.003 – 0.04 Hz** | Hz | Thermoregulation, hormones |
| 12 | EEG delta band | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | Hz | Deep sleep |
| 13 | EEG theta band | **4 – 8 Hz** | Hz | Drowsiness, memory |
| 14 | EEG alpha band | **8 – 13 Hz** | Hz | Relaxed wakefulness |
| 15 | EEG beta band | **13 – 30 Hz** | Hz | Active thinking |
| 16 | EEG gamma band | **> 30 Hz** | Hz | Cognitive binding |
| 17 | Lomb–Scargle PSD | $\hat{P}_{\text{LS}}(\omega)$ (above) | ms²/Hz | Uneven RR series |
| 18 | Akaike Information Criterion | $\text{AIC}(p) = N\ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p$ | nats | AR model order selection |
| 19 | Hann window | $w[n] = 0.5\!\left(1 - \cos\!\tfrac{2\pi n}{L-1}\right)$ | — | Spectral leakage reduction |
| 20 | Total HRV Power | $TP = \text{VLF} + \text{LF} + \text{HF}$ | ms² | Autonomic balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' High-Yield Tip:** Always state (a) the method used, (b) the sampling rate, (c) the segment length, and (d) the achieved frequency resolution. These four points alone are worth 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Periodogram from the Autocorrelation Estimator

We start from the biased autocorrelation estimator for a finite sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$:

$$\hat{r}_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x[n+k]\, x[n], \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

Taking the DTFT of this finite-length autocorrelation yields:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}[k]\, e^{-j\omega k}$$

Substitute the definition of $\hat{r}_{xx}[k]$:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left[ \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x[n+k]\, x[n] \right] e^{-j\omega k}$$

Change of variable $m = n + k$:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{+j\omega n} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m}$$

Recognizing the two DTFTs as complex conjugates:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right)\!\left( \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m} \right)^*$$

Therefore, the periodogram simplifies to the squared magnitude of the DTFT:

$$\boxed{\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert X(\omega)\right\vert^2}$$

**Interpretation:** The periodogram equals the magnitude-squared of the FFT divided by $N$. This is the bridge between time-domain autocorrelation and frequency-domain power.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Welch's Variance Reduction

Consider $K$ overlapping segments each of length $L$. Each periodogram $\hat{P}_k(\omega)$ is an unbiased estimator with variance $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_k\} \approx P_x^2(\omega)$.

The Welch estimator is the average:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{P}_k(\omega)$$

The variance of an average of $K$ i.i.d. estimators is:

$$\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{\text{Welch}}\} = \frac{1}{K^2}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Var}\{\hat{P}_k\} = \frac{1}{K}\, \text{Var}\{\hat{P}_k\} \approx \frac{P_x^2(\omega)}{K}$$

The bias increases slightly because each segment is shorter (lower resolution), but the **variance is reduced by a factor of $K$**. For $K = 8$ overlapping Hann-windowed segments, the variance drops to ~12.5% of the single periodogram's variance.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Levinson–Durbin Recursion (AR Parameter Estimation)

Given the Yule–Walker system $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = [1, 0, \dots, 0]^T \sigma_e^2$, the Levinson–Durbin recursion builds the solution iteratively:

**Step 1 — Initialization:**

$$\hat{a}_{1,1} = -\frac{r_{xx}[1]}{r_{xx}[0]}, \qquad E_1 = r_{xx}[0]\!\left(1 - \hat{a}_{1,1}^2\right)$$

**Step 2 — Reflection coefficient update (for $k = 2, \dots, p$):**

$$\hat{a}_{k,k} = -\frac{r_{xx}[k] + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \hat{a}_{k-1,j}\, r_{xx}[k-j]}{E_{k-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient update:**

$$\hat{a}_{k,j} = \hat{a}_{k-1,j} + \hat{a}_{k,k}\, \hat{a}_{k-1,k-j}, \quad j = 1, \dots, k-1$$

**Step 4 — Prediction error update:**

$$E_k = E_{k-1}\!\left(1 - \hat{a}_{k,k}^2\right)$$

Final prediction error: $\sigma_e^2 = E_p$. Compute PSD:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \frac{E_p}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \hat{a}_{p,k}\, e^{-j\omega k}\right\vert^2}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — HRV Frequency-Domain Metrics (Task Force, 1996)

For a Welch PSD of the RR series resampled at $f_s = 4$ Hz over 5 minutes, integrate power within the standard bands:

$$\text{VLF} = \int_{0.003}^{0.04} P_{RR}(f)\, df$$

$$\text{LF} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_{RR}(f)\, df$$

$$\text{HF} = \int_{0.15}^{0.40} P_{RR}(f)\, df$$

$$\text{TP} = \text{VLF} + \text{LF} + \text{HF}$$

$$\text{LF/HF ratio} = \frac{\text{LF}}{\text{HF}}$$

**Sample calculation (worked example):**
Suppose $P_{RR}(f) = 1500 \, \text{ms}^2/\text{Hz}$ in LF band, $700 \, \text{ms}^2/\text{Hz}$ in HF band, $400 \, \text{ms}^2/\text{Hz}$ in VLF band. Then:

$$\text{TP} = 400 + 1500 + 700 = 2600 \, \text{ms}^2$$

$$\text{LF/HF} = \frac{1500}{700} \approx 2.14$$

This indicates **sympathetic dominance** (or reduced parasympathetic activity) — clinically significant for cardiovascular risk stratification.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Periodogram | Welch | Blackman–Tukey | AR (Yule–Walker) | Lomb–Scargle |
|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| Variance | High | Low | Medium | Low | Low |
| Resolution | Best ($N$) | Reduced ($L$) | Medium | High (model-dependent) | Medium |
| Data length | Long only | Short OK | Medium | Very short OK | Any uneven data |
| Suitable for HRV? | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Best** |
| Computational cost | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(KL \log L)$ | $O(N^2)$ | $O(p^2)$ via L-D | $O(N \cdot N_f)$ |
| Spectral smoothness | Noisy | Smooth | Smooth | Very smooth | Noisy |
| Model order needed? | No | No | No | **Yes (AIC/MDL)** | No |
