## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Power Spectral Density (PSD). Explain how it is estimated using the Periodogram method for an ECG signal.**

**Model Answer:**
The Power Spectral Density $P_x(f)$ of a discrete-time biosignal $x[n]$ is the DTFT of its autocorrelation sequence, indicating how signal power is distributed over frequency. For ECG, the **Periodogram** estimator is:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j2\pi f n / f_s}\right\vert^2$$

Steps: (1) Acquire ECG at $f_s = 500$ Hz, (2) Remove baseline wander (high-pass at 0.5 Hz) and 50 Hz notch, (3) Compute FFT of length $N$, (4) Compute magnitude-squared and divide by $N$. The resulting spectrum reveals QRS energy around **10–25 Hz**, T-wave around **1–7 Hz**, and noise outside the band. *Limitation:* The variance of the periodogram does not decrease with $N$ — Welch's method is preferred clinically. **(3 marks)**

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List the standard frequency bands of HRV with their physiological significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Per the Task Force of the European Society of Cardiology (1996):

| Band | Range | Physiological Meaning |
|------|-------|------------------------|
| **VLF** | **0.003 – 0.04 Hz** | Thermoregulation, hormonal modulation, vagal withdrawal |
| **LF** | **0.04 – 0.15 Hz** | Baroreflex, sympathetic activity (mixed) |
| **HF** | **0.15 – 0.40 Hz** | Respiratory sinus arrhythmia, parasympathetic (vagal) tone |

The **LF/HF ratio** is used as an index of **sympathovagal balance**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Periodogram estimator from the autocorrelation definition and discuss its limitations for HRV data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Welch averaged periodogram method with a block diagram. Apply it to a 5-minute HRV recording sampled at 4 Hz using Hann windows of length 256 samples with 50% overlap. Compute the frequency resolution and explain the choice of parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Periodogram and its Limitations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of autocorrelation estimator** (1 mark):

$$\hat{r}_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x[n+k]\, x[n], \quad 0 \le k \le N-1$$

**Step 2 — Apply DTFT to obtain PSD** (1 mark):

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}[k]\, e^{-j\omega k}$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic simplification** (2 marks): Substituting $\hat{r}_{xx}[k]$ and changing variable $m = n + k$:

$$\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} \right)\!\left( \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] e^{-j\omega m} \right)^*$$

$$\boxed{\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega)\vert^2}$$

**Step 4 — Limitations for HRV** (3 marks):
- **Variance** $\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{\text{PER}}\} \approx P_x^2(\omega)$ does **not** decrease with $N$ — the estimator is **inconsistent**.
- **Spectral leakage** — abrupt truncation introduces sidelobes that mask weak rhythms.
- **Bias–variance trade-off** — long windows reduce variance but reduce stationarity of HRV.
- HRV data is **unevenly sampled** (RR intervals), so standard FFT fails; use Lomb–Scargle instead.

**(b) Welch Method Applied to HRV (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Resample** (1 mark): RR intervals (typically 0.6–1.2 s) are resampled via cubic spline to a uniform $f_s = 4$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Window and segment** (2 marks): With 5 min × 4 Hz = **1200 samples**, $L = 256$, and 50% overlap ($D = 128$), the number of segments is:

$$K = \left\lfloor \frac{N - L}{D} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{1200 - 256}{128} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 7.375 \right\rfloor + 1 = 8 \text{ segments}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency resolution** (2 marks):

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{L} = \frac{4}{256} = 0.015625 \text{ Hz} \approx 0.016 \text{ Hz}$$

This is sufficient to resolve the **LF peak (~0.1 Hz)** and the **HF peak (~0.25 Hz)**.

**Step 4 — Welch averaging equation** (1 mark):

$$\hat{P}_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L \cdot U} \sum_{k=1}^{8} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{255} w[n]\, x[n + kD]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2$$

**Step 5 — Variance reduction and choice of parameters** (1 mark): Variance reduces by factor $K = 8$, giving smoother spectral estimate. Hann window used to suppress sidelobes. Final PSD yields LF power (integrated 0.04–0.15 Hz) and HF power (0.15–0.40 Hz) for autonomic assessment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is an Autoregressive (AR) model? Derive the Yule–Walker equations for AR parameter estimation of an EEG signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-second EEG epoch is sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz. Compute the Welch PSD using Hann windows of length 128 samples with 50% overlap. Determine the number of segments, frequency resolution, and identify which EEG rhythms are resolvable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AR Model and Yule–Walker Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — AR model definition** (2 marks):

$$x[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + e[n]$$

with $e[n] \sim \mathcal{WN}(0, \sigma_e^2)$.

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $x[n-\ell]$ and take expectation** (2 marks):

$$r_{xx}[\ell] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}[\ell - k] + \sigma_e^2\, \delta[\ell]$$

**Step 3 — Set up the Yule–Walker system** (2 marks): For $\ell = 1, 2, \dots, p$:

$$r_{xx}[\ell] + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}[\ell - k] = 0$$

In matrix form: $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$, where $\mathbf{a} = [a_1, \dots, a_p]^T$ and $\mathbf{r} = [r_{xx}[1], \dots, r_{xx}[p]]^T$.

**Step 4 — Solve via Levinson–Durbin recursion** (1 mark): Order-$p$ recursion in $O(p^2)$ to obtain $a_k$ and $\sigma_e^2$.

**(b) Welch PSD of EEG (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total samples** (1 mark): $N = 2 \times 256 = 512$ samples.

**Step 2 — Segments** (2 marks): With $L = 128$, $D = 64$:

$$K = \left\lfloor \frac{512 - 128}{64} \right\rfloor + 1 = 7 \text{ segments}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency resolution** (2 marks):

$$\Delta f = \frac{256}{128} = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Resolvable EEG rhythms** (2 marks):
- **Delta (0.5–4 Hz)** — Resolvable (bandwidth 3.5 Hz >> 2 Hz).
- **Theta (4–8 Hz)** — Resolvable.
- **Alpha (8–13 Hz)** — Resolvable, but peak location uncertain by ~2 Hz.
- **Beta (13–30 Hz)** — Partially resolvable; fine sub-bands lost.
- **Gamma (>30 Hz)** — **Not resolvable** — would need shorter $L$ or longer recording.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the periodogram formula** — many students write $\tfrac{1}{N^2}$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{N}$, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert Hz to rad/sample** — when using $\omega$, write $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$.
> 3. **Resampling HRV before FFT** — failing to mention cubic-spline resampling at 4 Hz leads to loss of 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring stationarity** — HRV is non-stationary; the model window length and Welch segmentation must be justified.
> 5. **AR model order** — students often forget to justify via **AIC** or **MDL**; AR($p$) without justification loses a mark.
> 6. **Confusing PSD units** — ECG PSD is in $\text{mV}^2/\text{Hz}$, HRV in $\text{ms}^2/\text{Hz}$, EEG in $\mu\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$. Mixing units loses credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 (Spectrum Estimation):**
>
> ✅ **PSD definition** — Fourier transform of the autocorrelation sequence.
>
> ✅ **Periodogram** — $\tfrac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega)\vert^2$; inconsistent (variance $\neq 0$ as $N \to \infty$).
>
> ✅ **Welch method** — Segment → window → periodogram → average; variance drops by $K$.
>
> ✅ **Frequency resolution** — $\Delta f = f_s / L$; smaller $L$ means better temporal resolution but worse frequency resolution.
>
> ✅ **AR model PSD** — $\sigma_e^2 / \vert 1 + \sum_k a_k e^{-j\omega k}\vert^2$; superior for short, smooth biosignals.
>
> ✅ **Yule–Walker** — Linear system $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$; solved by Levinson–Durbin.
>
> ✅ **Lomb–Scargle** — Only correct non-parametric PSD for unevenly sampled HRV.
>
> ✅ **HRV bands** — VLF 0.003–0.04 Hz, LF 0.04–0.15 Hz, HF 0.15–0.40 Hz; LF/HF ratio = sympathovagal balance.
>
> ✅ **EEG bands** — Delta 0.5–4, Theta 4–8, Alpha 8–13, Beta 13–30, Gamma > 30 Hz.
>
> ✅ **ECG dominant frequencies** — QRS ~10–25 Hz, T-wave ~1–7 Hz, P-wave ~5–12 Hz.
>
> ✅ **Window functions** — Hann/Hamming reduce spectral leakage by tapering edges.
>
> ✅ **Model order selection** — AIC $= N \ln(\sigma_e^2) + 2p$; MDL penalizes complexity.
>
> ✅ **Clinical metrics derived from PSD** — Total Power, LF, HF, LF/HF, normalized units (LF nu = LF / (TP − VLF) × 100).
>
> ✅ **Units to remember** — ECG PSD in **mV²/Hz**, HRV PSD in **ms²/Hz**, EEG PSD in **µV²/Hz**.
>
> ✅ **Examination mantra** — Always state: (1) signal type, (2) sampling rate, (3) window/segment length, (4) frequency resolution, (5) integration band, (6) clinical interpretation.
